Title: Georgia leads push to ban datacenters used to power America's AI boom | Hacker News
Summary_AI: Georgia is leading efforts to impose a moratorium on new datacenter construction due to concerns over their massive energy consumption and water usage, which strain local infrastructure and increase costs for residents. The push reflects growing tensions between rapid AI-driven datacenter expansion and the need for sustainable energy planning, with critics highlighting the environmental impact and unfair cost distribution where utilities and datacenters benefit while local ratepayers bear the burden. This pause aims to allow policymakers to reassess energy procurement, grid capacity, and environmental impacts, potentially reshaping how datacenters are integrated into regional infrastructure and influencing future AI industry growth strategies in the U.S.

Title: Sam Altman-backed Exowatt launches arm to power data centers with clean energy
Summary_AI: Exowatt, backed by Sam Altman and Andreessen Horowitz, has launched "ExoRise," a new business arm focused on providing land and clean energy infrastructure for large-scale data centers in the U.S. Southwest. Utilizing its proprietary P3 solar and battery technology, which stores energy as heat and converts it on demand, ExoRise aims to deliver reliable, around-the-clock power to hyperscale data centers while minimizing local grid impact and costs. This initiative addresses the growing power demands driven by AI expansion, supporting U.S. data center growth and energy resilience, with a pilot project expected by the end of 2026 and a backlog exceeding 90 gigawatt-hours of customer demand.

Title: Customer-Facing Resources Could Mitigate Data Center Grid Impacts: Kansas Shows Us How
Summary_AI: A landmark November 2025 settlement approved by the Kansas Corporation Commission introduces a new rate structure for large-load customers like data centers, requiring upfront deposits, long-term contracts, and demand charges while enabling voluntary investments in clean energy and demand-side management (DSM) programs. This innovative approach incentivizes data centers to reduce peak electricity demand through energy efficiency, battery storage, and demand response, mitigating grid strain, lowering costs, and reducing emissions. The settlement exemplifies how collaboration among utilities, regulators, environmental groups, and industry can balance data center growth with grid reliability, affordability, and climate goals, setting a precedent that is influencing similar policies in states like Minnesota and Texas.

These facilities consume enormous amounts of electricity to sustain our digital ecosystems and emerging artificial intelligence technologies. Electric utilities across the nation are forecasting unprecedented demand growth, driven in part by onshoring of manufacturing and electrification of vehicles but primarily from data centers. Skyrocketing energy demand is creating serious challenges to electricity affordability, grid reliability, and fossil fuel emissions. Title: TikTok Data Center Outage Triggers Trust Crisis for New US Owners | WIRED
Summary_AI: TikTok experienced a significant service outage in the US shortly after transferring control of its American operations to a majority-US investor group, including Oracle, which owns 15% of the new entity. The disruption, caused by a power outage at an Oracle data center amid severe winter weather, led to widespread user difficulties in uploading and viewing content, sparking concerns about potential censorship, especially regarding politically sensitive topics like immigration enforcement. This outage has intensified user distrust amid TikTok’s recent compliance with a 2024 US law requiring divestment from Chinese ownership, as the new TikTok USDS Joint Venture aims to manage US user data and content algorithms, raising strategic questions about content control and platform trustworthiness under its new US-based ownership.
Article_Text:
TikTok is currently experiencing a widespread service outage in the US, causing disruptions for millions of users only a few days after the company officially transferred control of its American business to a group of majority-US investors.

The technical issues led many TikTok users to speculate about whether the app’s new owners were intentionally suppressing videos about political topics, particularly content related to recent federal immigration operations in Minnesota. TikTok has denied the allegations, attributing the problems to a power outage.

TikTok users began reporting on Sunday that they were having trouble uploading videos to the app as well as viewing content that had already been posted on the platform. Others said that while they could upload videos, they were receiving far fewer views and engagement than usual.

People in Minneapolis Are Still Standing Up to ICE

According to Downdetector, which tracks real-time service disruptions, a surge of users began reporting outages on TikTok starting early yesterday morning in the US. “Our data suggests that services are not yet fully restored for all users,” reads an alert Downdetector shared on Monday.

Steve Vladeck, a professor at Georgetown University’s School of Law, said in a Bluesky post that he had “recorded a video on TikTok about why DHS’s arguments for the power to enter homes without judicial warrants in immigration cases are bunk. Nine hours later, TikTok still says my video is ‘under review,’ and can’t be shared.”

The technical glitches have also attracted the attention of US senator Chris Murphy of Connecticut. “I know it's hard to track all the threats to democracy out there right now, but this is at the top of the list,” Murphy wrote in a Bluesky post. When reached for comment, Deni Kemper, a spokesperson for Murphy, told WIRED that his office did not “have anything to share beyond the senator’s tweet.”

Title: 2026 Senate Bill 135 | South Dakota Legislature
Summary_AI: South Dakota's 2026 Senate Bill 135, known as the "Data Center Bill of Rights for Citizens," aims to protect residents from increased utility costs and shortages caused by data centers. The bill mandates that data center operators bear all electricity costs and prohibits utility providers from passing these costs onto residential customers. It also preserves local governments' authority to regulate data center development, bans tax exemptions for data centers, and requires operators to notify local water providers about projected water use, ensuring compatibility with local supplies. Semi-annual water usage reports must be submitted publicly to the Board of Water Management, emphasizing sustainable resource allocation and community protection. This legislation strategically limits financial and environmental impacts of data centers on South Dakota residents and local infrastructure.
Article_Text: 26.729.44 101st Legislative Session 135 2026 South Dakota Legislature Senate Bill 135 Introduced by: Senator Karr An Act to protect residents from increased utility costs and utility shortages caused by data centers and clarify authority to regulate data centers. Be it enacted by the Legislature of the State of South Dakota: Section 1. This Act is known as the "Data Center Bill of Rights for Citizens." Section 2. That a NEW SECTION be added to a NEW CHAPTER in title 49: For purposes of this chapter, "data center" means a centralized repository for the dissemination, management, processing, and storage of electronic data and information. Section 3. That a NEW SECTION be added to a NEW CHAPTER in title 49: The operator of a data center shall pay for all costs associated with the provision of electricity to the data center. A provider of electricity may not: (1) Increase fees, rates, or surcharges imposed on residential customers for the purpose of financing, offsetting, or subsidizing a data center's infrastructure costs, service demands, or utility consumption; or (2) Allocate to residential customers any cost associated with the distribution, generation, or transmission infrastructure, or any other cost attributable to the data center. Section 4. That a NEW SECTION be added to a NEW CHAPTER in title 49: The state may not preempt or otherwise limit the authority of the governing body of a county, municipality, or other political subdivision to adopt ordinances and resolutions limiting, prohibiting, or otherwise regulating the construction, development, or operation of data centers. Section 5. That a NEW SECTION be added to a NEW CHAPTER in title 49: The state, or any political subdivision of the state, may not authorize or grant any tax exemption to a data center. Section 6. Title: Nvidia Invests $2 Billion in CoreWeave Amid Data Center Project | PYMNTS.com
Summary_AI: Nvidia has invested $2 billion in AI hyperscaler CoreWeave to support the development of over 5 gigawatts of AI data center capacity by 2030, aiming to accelerate large-scale AI infrastructure. This partnership highlights Nvidia's strategic focus on integrating software, infrastructure, and operations to meet growing AI demand, with CoreWeave leveraging Nvidia’s computing platform for cost-effective AI inference. The investment underscores the significant funding required for expansive AI data centers, while emerging research suggests a shift toward distributed AI workloads, balancing large-scale systems with smaller, localized models for enterprise efficiency.
Article_Text: Nvidia Invests $2 Billion in CoreWeave Amid Data Center Project BY PYMNTS | JANUARY 26, 2026 | Artificial intelligence (AI) hyperscaler CoreWeave has announced an expanded partnership with tech giant Nvidia. This new collaboration is aimed at allowing CoreWeave to accelerate the development of more than 5 gigawatts of AI data centers by 2030, the companies announced Monday (Jan. 26). As part of the partnership, Nvidia has invested $2 billion in CoreWeave stock. “From the very beginning, our collaboration has been guided by a simple conviction: AI succeeds when software, infrastructure and operations are designed together,” Michael Intrator, co-founder, chairman and CEO, CoreWeave, said in a news release. “NVIDIA is the leading and most requested computing platform at every phase of AI — from pre-training to post-training — and Blackwell provides the lowest cost architecture for inference. This expanded collaboration underscores the strength of demand we are seeing across our customer base and the broader market signals as AI systems move into large-scale production.” Gigawatts are units of power often used for illustrating the capacity of an AI data center. A report on the partnership by CNBC points to data from the Energy Information Administration that 5 gigawatts would equal the yearly power consumption of 4 million American households. “The thing to remember is we’ve invested $2 billion into CoreWeave, but recognize that the amount of funding that needs to be raised yet to support that five gigawatts is really quite significant,” Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang told CNBC. “We’re investing a small percentage of the amount that ultimately has to go and be provided.” PYMNTS wrote recently about a growing body of research challenging industry assumptions about data center needs. Title: Developer eyes Conewago Township for data center - York Dispatch
Summary_AI: Conewago Township officials in York County, Pennsylvania, are reviewing proposals to change land use laws to permit data center development on two rural properties, amid growing interest from tech investors and federal attention. The proposed data centers, supported by state and bipartisan political figures, would feature buildings up to 60 feet tall on lots of at least 50 acres, with noise and environmental regulations in place. While some residents oppose the projects due to concerns over farmland loss and utility strain, developers highlight potential economic benefits, referencing similar projects in nearby townships that could generate millions in tax revenue for local schools and counties. This move reflects a strategic push to attract data centers to Pennsylvania, leveraging its energy infrastructure and open land.
Article_Text:
Conewago Township officials are considering two rural properties for data centers as they review proposals to change land use laws to absorb the massive tech hubs.
The proposal is part of a wave of super-computer facilities to be considered, following in the wake of large-scale warehouse development that overtook the landscape along I-83 in Northern York County.
Differing from the warehouse boom, though, is massive stakeholder interest — including from the White House.
“I don’t remember the federal government having a big opinion on the freight as that was happening,” said Bethany Inman, a Conewago Township zoning officer.
Meanwhile, signs opposing data center development have sprung up in the township.
A sign promoting someone's opposition to data centers being built in Conewago Township is visible on Bowers Bridge Road in the far eastern section of the township. Township planners are considering zoning changes to permit data centers. (Mark Walters)
The AI boom heard around Pennsylvania is loud, from staunch resident opposition to bipartisan political support from Gov. Josh Shapiro and President Donald Trump.

Title: How Robotics Is Re-Engineering Data Center Construction and Operations
Summary_AI: Robotics is transforming data center construction and operations by accelerating build timelines and enhancing operational efficiency. Notably, DEWALT and August Robotics have introduced fleet-capable drilling robots that completed over 90,000 holes with 99.97% accuracy, reducing construction schedules by approximately 80 weeks across ten projects. Beyond construction, robotics integrated with AI and digital twins are increasingly used for inspection, environmental sensing, and remote assistance, improving safety and ROI. This phased adoption—from construction acceleration to robotic facility design—positions robotics as essential infrastructure, enabling hyperscalers to meet growing AI-driven demand with faster, safer, and more precise data center development and management.
Article_Text: JOIN NOW LOGIN Charting the Future of Data Center, Cloud, and AI Infrastructure HYPERSCALE How Robotics Is Re-Engineering Data Center Construction and Operations Robotics is moving onto the critical path of data center construction and operations. From fleet-based drilling to perception-driven inspection and digital twins, a phased roadmap points toward more standardized, robot-ready facilities built for AI-era scale. David Chernicoff Jan. 26, 2026 13 min read Key Highlights Construction robotics, such as fleet-capable drilling systems, significantly reduce build timelines and improve precision, addressing critical bottlenecks in data center slab work. Operational robotics are increasingly integrated with monitoring systems, performing tasks like patrols, environmental sensing, and remote assistance, with a focus on low disruption and high ROI. Physical AI, including simulation, perception pipelines, and foundation models, enables robots to adapt to complex, variable environments, facilitating tasks like inspection, manipulation, and verification. A phased roadmap for robotics adoption in data centers includes construction acceleration, sensing and patrol, manipulation tasks, and future facility designs optimized for robotic work. Emerging innovations, such as robotic microfactories for e-waste recycling, highlight the expanding role of robotics across the entire data center lifecycle, emphasizing robotics as essential infrastructure. DEWALT’s fleet-capable downward-drilling robot targets one of the most schedule-critical tasks in data center construction. Currently data center development is being shaped by two converging pressures. Hyperscalers need capacity delivered faster than conventional construction labor and sequencing can reliably support; while operations teams are being asked to run higher-density, more complex facilities under tighter uptime, safety, and staffing constraints. Tina Kotek has tasked a new advisory committee with developing policy recommendations to address the impacts associated with the rapid expansion of data centers across Oregon. Kotek appointed seven energy and industry professionals to serve on the Data Center Advisory Committee, she announced Jan. 20. The group will shape policy to help Oregon leverage economic development opportunities created by new data centers and other large-load industrial enterprises while keeping the potential cost and environmental impacts of data centers manageable. The Data Center Advisory Committee will hold at least one public meeting per month starting in February. The committee’s report to the governor is due no later than October. “Oregonians have made their concerns about rising utility bills clear. Title: Data center bill clears Wisconsin Assembly despite opposition - Finance & Commerce
Summary_AI: The Wisconsin State Assembly passed Assembly Bill 840 on January 20, aiming to regulate the state's growing data center industry by addressing environmental concerns and protecting ratepayers from energy cost burdens linked to these facilities. The bill mandates that energy costs for data center-related grid expansions not be passed to other customers, requires renewable energy projects serving data centers to be located onsite, and enforces closed-loop cooling and water usage reporting to the Department of Natural Resources. Despite its passage, the bill faced opposition from Democrats and multiple construction unions over concerns about labor wages, job quality, and insufficient measures on data center energy consumption. The legislation represents a strategic effort to balance industry growth with environmental and community impacts, though debates continue over labor protections and sustainability standards.
Article_Text:
MADISON, Wis. — Republicans in the Wisconsin State Assembly passed a bill to regulate the state’s expanding data center industry that is meant to address environmental concerns and costs passed down to ratepayers living near billion-dollar facilities. However, both Democratic lawmakers and construction unions that build both data centers and state renewable energy facilities disapproved of the legislation.
Assembly Bill 840 passed the Jan. 20 floor session by a 53-44 vote with state Rep. Lindee Brill, R-Sheboygan Falls, voting “no” with Democrats and two Democrats, Steve Doyle, D-Onalaska and Jodi Emerson, D-Eau Claire, voting “yes.”
If passed, the bill would establish requirements for data centers, including requiring the Public Service Commission to make sure rate costs set by construction or expansion of the energy grid to serve data centers would not be allocated or collected from other customers, according to the Legislative Reference Bureau.
The bill will also require any renewable energy facilities that primarily serve the load of data centers to be located at the site of a data center.
Solar, wind and other clean energy projects are booming in Wisconsin — some of it powered by tax credits under the Inflation Reduction Act — and some of the state’s major utilities have agreements with construction unions to install them. The drafted bill doesn’t have a prevailing wage requirement for renewable energy projects on data center sites.
Both the construction electrician and utility worker locals of the International Brotherhood of Electrical Workers filed against the bill.
The Iron Workers District Council of the North Central States, North Central States Regional Council of Carpenters, Plumbers Local 75, Sheet Metal Air Rail Transportation Workers Local Union 18 and Wisconsin Laborers District Council have also filed in opposition.

Title: Dozens Speak Out on New Washington Bill to Regulate Data Center Growth - NewsData
Summary_AI: Washington State legislators introduced House Bill 2515 on January 15, 2026, aiming to regulate the rapid growth of energy-intensive data centers by imposing unique tariffs on facilities with 20 MW+ demand, mandating renewable energy use (80% by 2030, 100% by 2045), and requiring detailed reporting on water and energy consumption. The bill also prohibits free emissions allowances under the state’s cap-and-invest program and imposes annual fees on large data centers. Supported by environmental groups for protecting climate goals and ratepayers, the bill faces opposition from business and local officials who argue it could hinder economic development, especially in data center hubs like Quincy, where data centers significantly contribute to jobs and tax revenue. The legislation reflects Washington’s effort to balance data center growth with environmental sustainability and grid reliability.

Mathis Römer/Flickr Facebook Twitter LinkedIn Email Print Copy article link Save Washington legislators hope to regulate the rapidly growing, energy-intensive data center industry with a new bill introduced in the state House of Representatives Jan. 15. The legislation, House Bill 2515, would establish guardrails around data center growth by primarily requiring emerging large energy use facilities (ELEUFs) that have a demand of 20 MW or more to operate under a unique tariff, report water and energy use, and develop standards to improve forecasting. The bill would also mandate new or expanded facilities to use electricity from renewable or non-emitting resources for 80 percent of their needs by 2030 and 100 percent by 2045—a key provision garnering significant support from environmental groups. Among other requirements, the bill would prohibit the Washington State Department of Ecology from distributing no-cost allowances to electric utilities for ELEUFs. Under the state’s cap-and-invest program, certain “emissions-intensive, trade-exposed facilities” receive free allowances to emit greenhouse gases instead of having to purchase them in an auction. Finally, the bill would require ELEUFs pay an annual fee to the Washington State Department of Revenue. Companion legislation, SB 6171, was heard in the Senate Environment, Energy and Technology Committee on Jan. 23. Rep. Title: SoftBank ends talks over $50bn purchase of data center firm Switch - DCD
Summary_AI: SoftBank has ended negotiations to acquire US data center firm Switch for $50 billion due to financial and logistical challenges, according to Bloomberg sources. Switch, which operates major data center campuses across the US and is majority-owned by DigitalBridge, was seen by SoftBank as a strategic asset to support OpenAI’s $500 billion Stargate infrastructure project. Meanwhile, Switch is considering an IPO that could value the company at up to $60 billion, following its 2022 privatization at $11 billion. SoftBank is proceeding with a separate $3 billion acquisition of DigitalBridge itself, which manages assets including Switch valued at $108 billion. This development impacts SoftBank’s expansion in digital infrastructure and highlights the complexities of managing large-scale data center networks.

Investment grew even in the U.S., despite a sweeping rollback of government support for clean energy. Last year, investors poured $893 billion into electric vehicles, $690 billion into renewable power, and $483 billion into power grids, with additional funding going to heat pumps, battery storage, nuclear power, and other technologies that promise to curb demand for fossil fuels, according to a new report from BloombergNEF. China remains the global leader in clean energy investment, though it saw a drop in funding for renewable power last year as new regulations came into force that slowed the buildout of wind and solar. Investment in India grew by 15 percent, and in Europe by 18 percent. In the U.S., investment climbed by just 3.5 percent amid a gutting of federal support for clean energy under President Trump. While the shift to clean energy may be unstoppable, the analysis showed that it can be slowed. Before Trump returned to office, EVs were projected to reach nearly half of U.S. car sales by the end of this decade. Now, they are on course for roughly a third, according to BNEF. Analysts also warn of another trend that threatens to slow the shift to clean energy: While China currently dominates the production of solar, batteries, and EVs, investment in manufacturing is growing in the U.S. and the E.U., and Western governments are seeking to protect domestic industries by imposing tariffs on Chinese imports. Key takeaways

New financing structures are being developed to fund powered land and powered shell opportunities or to refinance stabilized assets.

What is emerging is a market opportunity characterized by innovation, scale, and a deep need for capital. Article_Text: Skip to content INDOOR OUTDOOR RETROFIT CONTROLS LEARN SUPPORT PURCHASE LEARNING CENTER Data Center Lighting Tips: Practical Guidance for Bright, Efficient Spaces Data center lighting directly impacts your operating costs and facility performance. Most operators overlook how much energy their lighting systems consume, missing significant savings opportunities. At PacLights, we’ve seen facilities cut lighting energy use by 40% or more through smarter design and controls. This guide covers practical data center lighting tips you can implement immediately to reduce costs and improve efficiency. Why LED Lighting Cuts Data Center Costs Faster Than Any Other Upgrade Immediate Energy Savings from LED Conversion LED lighting stands as the single most effective investment you can make in data center efficiency. LEDs consume 75% less energy than traditional fluorescent or incandescent fixtures, and that reduction takes effect immediately upon installation. A typical data center running 24/7 with older lighting systems cuts lighting energy costs by 40% to 60% within the first month of switching to LEDs. If your facility spends $50,000 annually on lighting, switching to LEDs saves $20,000 to $30,000 per year without any operational changes required. How LEDs Reduce Your Cooling Load The real advantage extends beyond direct energy savings. LEDs produce significantly less heat than older technologies, which means your cooling systems work less hard to maintain server temperatures. Data centers account for roughly 1% of global energy consumption according to Energy Innovation, and cooling represents 30% to 40% of total facility energy use. When you reduce heat from lighting, you lower cooling demands proportionally. A 40% reduction in lighting energy often translates to a 5% to 10% reduction in overall cooling load, depending on your facility layout and current system efficiency. Title: Amid Soaring AI Demand, the First Statewide Data Center Moratorium Is Here
Summary_AI: A Georgia lawmaker has introduced a bill that would impose the nation’s first statewide moratorium on new data center construction until March 2027, giving state and local governments time to develop regulations for an industry seen as rapidly and permanently reshaping the landscape. Supporters cite concerns over soaring energy and water use, higher utility bills, and insufficient oversight as AI-driven data center growth accelerates. The proposal reflects a broader push for stronger regulation and could become a political flashpoint as voters weigh how leaders manage data center expansion.
Article_Text:
A Georgia state lawmaker has proposed a bill that would become the first statewide moratorium on new data centers in the country. The motion seeks to prohibit the construction of data centers until March 2027.


While Maryland and Oklahoma also introduced state legislatures in the last week, Georgia is emerging as the leading entity taking a stance against the surge in data centers. The bill aims to postpone the development of data centers “to allow state, county and municipal-level officials time to set necessary policies for regulating data centers… which permanently alter the landscape of our state,” according to Ruwa Romman, the bill sponsor and state Democratic legislator.

As AI has rapidly emerged, plans to build the data centers that power it have spread just as quickly across the country. It’s unclear how many data centers there are in Georgia, but some groups estimate that the number is between 100 to 200. The groups also estimate that those facilities require the same amount of energy used to power nearly 3.9 million homes and the amount of water that could sustain Athens, Georgia for five years.

“What we’re seeing is, as communities are learning more about this aggressive industry’s presence… [they] want to have time to thoroughly investigate all potential harms,” said Seth Gladstone, a spokesperson for Food and Water Watch, to The Guardian.

Title: Wisconsin debates how to pay for the power-hungry AI boom - Canary Media
Summary_AI: Wisconsin regulators are reviewing a proposal from utility We Energies on how to allocate the costs of new power infrastructure needed to support a surge in data centers, including Microsoft’s planned AI campus in Mount Pleasant. The utility suggests data centers should bear most or all costs for new generation capacity, with two payment options under consideration, but consumer and environmental groups warn this could unfairly burden other customers and increase reliance on fossil fuels. The outcome will set a precedent for energy cost allocation amid the AI-driven data center boom, with concerns about financial risks if demand projections fall short and the potential impact on clean energy adoption in the state.
Article_Text: Account Donate Clean energy journalism for a cooler tomorrow Wisconsin debates how to pay for the power-hungry AI boom Regulators mull the first big utility plan to provide electricity to data centers flocking to the state, igniting disputes over consumer protection and clean energy. By Kari Lydersen 27 January 2026 Microsoft’s new AI data center campus in Mount Pleasant, Wisconsin (Microsoft) How much should data centers pay for the massive amounts of new power infrastructure they require? Wisconsin’s largest utility, We Energies, has offered its answer to that question in what is the first major proposal before state regulators on the issue. Under the proposal, currently open for public comment, data centers would pay most or all of the price to construct new power plants or renewables needed to serve them, and the utility says the benefits that other customers receive would outweigh any costs they shoulder for building and running this new generation. But environmental and consumer advocates fear the utility’s plan will actually saddle customers with payments for generation, including polluting natural gas plants, that wouldn’t otherwise be needed. States nationwide face similar dilemmas around data centers’ energy use. But who pays for the new power plants and transmission is an especially controversial question in Wisconsin and other “vertically integrated” energy markets, where utilities charge their customers for the investments they make in such infrastructure — with a profit, called “rate of return,” baked in. In states with competitive energy markets, like Illinois, by contrast, utilities buy power on the open market and don’t make a rate of return on building generation. Although seven big data-center projects are underway in Wisconsin, the state has no laws governing how the computing facilities get their power. Title: Meta is spending millions to convince people that data centers are cool and you like them
Summary_AI: Meta has invested $6.4 million in a nationwide ad campaign throughout 2025 to promote its data centers in Altoona, Iowa, and Los Lunas, New Mexico, highlighting their role in creating jobs and revitalizing rural communities. This effort aims to counter growing public opposition to new data center construction, driven by concerns over energy consumption, water use, and strain on local power grids, which has led to delays and cancellations of multi-billion-dollar projects across several states. Alongside Meta, other major operators like Digital Reality, QTS, and NTT Data are launching lobbying campaigns to defend data center expansion, which is crucial for powering AI technologies but faces increasing community resistance.
Article_Text:
Over the last few months of 2025, Meta spent $6.4 million on an ad campaign running in cities across the country, from Sacramento to Washington, with a clear mission: win over viewers on the construction of new data centers. As the New York Times reports, the ad campaign is anchored by short, folksy video spotlights on Meta’s data centers in Altoona, Iowa, and Los Lunas, New Mexico.
The ads make the case that Meta’s data centers create jobs, revitalizing rural communities.
However, they take a fairly idealistic tone. For instance, the Altoona ad portrays a town on the brink of disappearing, but thanks to Meta’s data center, its residents get to meet up at local diners and attend football games. The Los Lunas ad similarly shows data center employees having cookouts with their families, rather than moving away to find work.
And Meta isn’t the only company trying to convince the public to embrace the data center boom. The Financial Times reported this week that data center operators, including Digital Reality, QTS, and NTT Data, are planning a “lobbying blitz” to campaign in defense of new data centers in response to public backlash.
However, the recent winter storm that swept the country only highlighted the strain the power grid is already under, including areas around large data centers.
For Meta, and competitors like Microsoft and Google, these sprawling data centers are critical for powering AI, but public sentiment on them has been souring. Many communities are pushing back against the construction of new data centers, united across the political divide by concerns about skyrocketing energy costs and water use. These movements have caused delays and cancellations for billions of dollars’ worth of data center investments across the nation, like now-canceled buildouts in Oregon, Arizona, Missouri, Indiana, and Virginia.

Title: Public invited to data center Q&A in Fayetteville on Thursday - CityView NC
Summary_AI: Two new data centers have been proposed for Fayetteville, North Carolina, with plans for a 200-megawatt facility by Terra Nexus Ventures on Custer Avenue and a 300-megawatt project by Energy Storage Solutions LLC on Dunn Road, the latter valued at $6.4 billion but facing local opposition and uncertainty. The Fayetteville Cumberland County Economic Development Corporation is hosting a public Q&A seminar to address community concerns about data centers' environmental impact, such as electricity and water usage, and to inform leadership on future technology projects. These developments reflect Fayetteville's growing role in supporting data-intensive industries like AI and cryptocurrency mining, with existing local data centers already operating.
Article_Text:
At least two new data centers have been proposed to be built on the east side of Fayetteville across the Cape Fear River, although there are doubts about one of them.
And as the world’s insatiable maw for data centers grows to power artificial intelligence, cryptocurrency mines, and other computing needs, the Fayetteville Cumberland County Economic Development Corporation (FCEDC) is holding a seminar Thursday for the public to ask questions and learn more about them.
The information session is scheduled for 5:30 p.m. to 7 p.m in suite 401A of the Robert C. Williams Business Center at 201 Hay St., in downtown Fayetteville. Admission is free, but registration is required to guarantee a seat.
“There’s a lot of information. There’s a lot of things that are going to be viewed as positives, a lot of concerns out there,” said Robert Van Geons, the FCEDC’s president and CEO.. The seminar’s goal is to answer questions and hold an open conversation.
“And the hope is that it’ll help leadership decide how to deal with the different types of technology projects that we’re likely to be seeing in the months and years ahead,” he added.
Data centers power 21st century communications and computing technology, and can mean high-dollar property values that could make elected officials salivate for their potential revenue. But data centers have drawn criticism for their electric consumption, the amount of water they use for cooling as communities fight over drinking water access, and the noise of their equipment.

Title: East Union Twp. adopts new data center regulations - Pottsville Republican Herald
Summary_AI: East Union Township's Board of Supervisors unanimously adopted a new zoning ordinance on January 15 to regulate future data center construction within the municipality. Although no proposals have been submitted yet, the ordinance sets detailed guidelines on data center operations, including equipment use, noise, and light pollution limits, aiming to balance development with residents' quality of life. This proactive measure positions the township to better manage potential data center projects, reflecting a broader regional trend of municipalities seeking to control such developments strategically.
Article_Text:
East Union Twp. Board of Supervisors took preventative steps toward the construction of data centers in the municipality during a special meeting.
Held in the East Union Municipal Building on Jan. 15, in a meeting room packed to standing room only, the board unanimously voted to adopt a zoning ordinance, effective immediately, which would greatly regulate the construction of data centers in the township.
Though no data center construction proposals have been presented to the township yet, this “curative measure” follows a growing trend among NEPA municipalities interested in protecting themselves from the construction of data centers.
“This ordinance doesn’t make it harder, necessarily, for data centers to be built in the township,” said Kyle Mummey, Chairman of the board. “It’s very difficult for us to do something like that. What this amendment does is give us the ability to regulate any potential data centers that could try to set up shop here in the future.”

The ordinance includes language and guidelines for the data centers themselves and the attendant structures required to operate them, such as cooling blocks, emergency generators, water tanks and fencing.
Many of these guidelines cover the types of equipment specifically allowed for usage within the data centers, while others describe the tolerances the township will allow for its residents’ impacted quality of life, such as the levels of allowed noise and light pollution.

Title: Data Center NDA - Janesville Gazette
Summary_AI: Meta is constructing a $1 billion, 520-acre data center in Beaver Dam, Wisconsin, a project kept secret for over a year through nondisclosure agreements (NDAs) signed by local officials with shell companies. This data center, expected to open in 2027, is part of seven major Wisconsin data center projects collectively valued at over $57 billion, highlighting the state's growing role in data infrastructure and AI advancement. The secrecy surrounding these projects has sparked public backlash and legislative efforts in Wisconsin to ban NDAs for data center proposals, emphasizing the tension between economic development confidentiality and community transparency.
Article_Text:
BEAVER DAM — How did a $1 billion, 520-acre data center proposed by one of the world’s richest companies go unnoticed in tiny Beaver Dam, Wisconsin?
A key reason: In a city that lists “communication matters” atop its core values, officials took steps to keep the project hidden for more than a year.
Now Meta, the trillion-dollar company that owns Facebook and Instagram, is building a complex as big as 12 football fields in a city with a population of 16,000, enough to fill only a fifth of Lambeau Field.
It’s one of seven major data center projects pending in Wisconsin that combined are worth more than $57 billion.
In four of them, including Beaver Dam, local government officials kept the massive projects under wraps through confidential nondisclosure agreements (NDAs), a Wisconsin Watch investigation has found.
Secrecy also occurred in the three communities without NDAs.
In one, the Madison suburb of DeForest, officials worked behind the scenes for months before publicly announcing a proposed $12 billion data center, which residents are fighting.
The lack of public disclosure, while relatively common for typical development proposals in the planning stages, raises questions about how much time the public should have to digest projects that dramatically affect the economy, land use, energy, taxes, the environment and more.
“As soon as community leadership is contemplating, even entertaining it, I think they need to make the public aware,” said retired tech executive Prescott Balch, who is advising residents around Wisconsin where data centers are proposed.
“Even if it makes it harder, that’s the right way to do it. And nobody is doing it that way.”
Blowback from residents who have been kept in the dark has spurred a new legislative proposal that would ban data center NDAs statewide.
How Beaver Dam did it

Title: The Emerging Regulatory Framework to Power Data Centers
Summary_AI: The rapid growth of data centers in the U.S., driven by AI expansion, is straining the existing electricity regulatory framework designed for incremental load growth. The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) is considering reforms to federalize interconnection rules for large loads over 20 MW, potentially allowing data centers to connect directly to transmission systems and bypass state jurisdiction. This shift aims to reduce multi-year interconnection delays and uncertain upgrade costs, which currently hinder data center development, especially in key hubs like Northern Virginia. Recent FERC actions, including new transmission service requirements in the PJM region and the approval of the Southwest Power Pool’s High Impact Large Load framework, signal a strategic federal move to treat data centers as critical infrastructure, reshaping power access, cost allocation, and regulatory authority with significant implications for the industry’s growth and grid reliability.

Now Microsoft is privately projecting its water use will surge. By just how much is a moving target. Internal forecasts that Microsoft made last year, which were obtained by The New York Times, show the company expected its annual water needs for roughly 100 data center complexes worldwide to more than triple this decade to 28 billion liters in 2030. Title: The grid can't build fast enough - Latitude Media
Summary_AI: The rapid growth of AI data centers and offshore wind projects in the U.S. is outpacing the electric grid's capacity to connect and deliver new power, creating a critical bottleneck in build throughput. Despite strong demand and financing, projects face delays due to long equipment lead times, regulatory hurdles, permitting backlogs, and local opposition, particularly in the PJM grid region and off the East Coast. Article_Text: SUBSCRIBE TECH MARKETS DEALS PODCASTS EVENTS RESEARCH RESOURCES COMMENTARY DATA CENTERS U.S. MARKET The grid can’t build fast enough AI data centers and offshore wind are hitting the same bottleneck: build throughput. NICK ZENKIN JANUARY 27, 2026 Image credit: Lisa Martine Jenkins Two weeks ago, the Trump administration and a bipartisan group of mid-Atlantic governors urged PJM to call for an emergency procurement auction, arguing that new baseload supply should be built quickly to accommodate data center power demand. The move came as a surprise to most, but it reflected an increasingly urgent reality. Data center growth is outpacing grid capacity. BloombergNEF projects that U.S. data center power demand will reach 106 GW by 2035, more than double current levels. But meanwhile, there is potential capacity to spare. The challenge isn’t a shortage of proposed projects; it’s getting them built. Across the country, planned generation is in regulatory limbo or stuck waiting for long-lead equipment. On orders from the president, several major offshore wind projects sit frozen off the East Coast, waiting for courts to decide their fate. Offshore wind developers are facing mounting financial pressure, and several gigawatts of contracted capacity are in limbo. At first blush, these two storylines don’t seem related. One’s a demand problem; the other’s supply. But they’re both stuck on the same thing: build throughput, or how fast the grid can connect and deliver new projects — and one of the metrics that will define the U.S. market in 2026. Demand side: Data centers The AI era is turning data centers from background noise into a system-planning challenge. Large artificial intelligence data centers hit the grid as a demand shock. New campuses often need network upgrades, new substations, and transmission-level interconnection, rather than using existing capacity. And nowhere is this more visible than in PJM. Title: Eaton Expands Modular Data Center Offering for Rapid Deployment of AI Factories From Grid to Chip
Summary_AI: Eaton has expanded its modular data center offerings through a collaboration with Flexnode to accelerate the deployment of AI-optimized computing environments in the U.S. This partnership integrates Eaton’s power backup, racks, and cable management with Flexnode’s modular construction platform, reducing data center build times by 35% and supporting high-density AI workloads from 3.5 to 35 megawatts. By combining Eaton’s grid-to-chip power infrastructure with Flexnode’s scalable modules, the initiative addresses the growing demand for efficient, resilient, and rapidly deployable AI factory infrastructure, positioning both companies strategically in the evolving high-performance computing market.
Article_Text: Jan 28, 2026 8:33 AM Eastern Standard Time Eaton Expands Modular Data Center Offering for Rapid Deployment of AI Factories From Grid to Chip Share Collaboration with Flexnode in the U.S. will support delivery of resilient, high-performance computing environments optimized for AI workloads Eaton continues to help its data center customers build faster and reduce onsite labor by leveraging modular systems for both power and computing infrastructure Eaton collaboration with Flexnode supports delivery of resilient, high-performance computing environments optimized for AI workloads. PITTSBURGH & BETHESDA, Md.--(BUSINESS WIRE)--Intelligent power management company Eaton is collaborating with Flexnode, an innovative digital infrastructure company, to deliver modular, scalable rack and power infrastructure for data center compute applications. Eaton will supply critical power backup, racks and cable management technologies for Flexnode’s modules that help data centers reduce deployment schedules by 35% on average. Additionally, Eaton led Flexnode’s Series A round to further accelerate its approach to data center infrastructure. As large-scale data center buildouts approach gigawatt-level power demands, high-density power, cooling and compute infrastructure requires a more efficient and scalable approach. Eaton brings complete, future-proof systems and enables modular, cost-effective production that reduces time to build and onsite labor requirements. The company’s collaboration with Flexnode expands Eaton’s modular offering in the U.S., which includes its Fibrebond business’ pre-integrated enclosures for data center power infrastructure. “As AI, high-performance computing (HPC) and quantum workloads push rack densities beyond one megawatt, Eaton’s modular strategy provides IT and power infrastructure that’s efficient, adaptive to dynamic load profiles, and enables scalable, repeatable builds across diverse geographies,” said Linsey Miller, senior vice president and general

Title: Are creative power deals actually speeding up development? - Latitude Media
Summary_AI: Tech giants like Google, Microsoft, and Amazon are innovating with creative power deals and behind-the-meter energy solutions to accelerate data center development amid the AI boom. However, local community opposition—due to concerns over utility costs, water availability, and environmental impact—has caused significant delays, stalling $98 billion in investments across 11 states in early 2025. Despite increased data center capacity and new business models, the development timeline remains unchanged, with permitting and interconnection hurdles cited as major barriers. Companies like Octopus Energy are exploring small-scale, community-friendly energy solutions to mitigate opposition and facilitate faster project approvals, aiming to balance growth with local concerns.
Article_Text: SUBSCRIBE TECH MARKETS DEALS PODCASTS EVENTS RESEARCH RESOURCES INTERVIEW DATA CENTERS REGULATION Are creative power deals actually speeding up development? There's one wild card standing in the way of faster data center development. MAEVE ALLSUP JANUARY 27, 2026 Photo credit: Gorodenkoff / Shutterstock In the artificial intelligence race, tech giants have broken just about every traditional rule of infrastructure development. Google is moving upstream into development by acquiring Intersect Power. Microsoft is resurrecting a nuclear plant at Three Mile Island. Amazon has spent more than a year navigating federal regulatory hurdles to secure a direct connection to the Susquehanna plant in Pennsylvania. But there’s one key infrastructure truism that, no matter how much tech giants may spend on behind-the-meter power, the AI boom hasn’t yet figured out a way to innovate around: local communities pose one of the biggest and most unpredictable barriers. Despite the move towards behind-the-meter generation and energy parks, the AI boom is hitting a pretty solid wall of community opposition. According to Data Center Watch, more projects were delayed or blocked in a single quarter last year than in the previous two years combined. Between March and June alone, the group tracked $98 billion in stalled investments across 11 states. In 2025, hyperscalers withdrew plans for data centers in several states — including a high profile Google withdrawal in Indiana — after local residents raised concerns about utility costs, water availability, and changes to rural landscapes. In aggregate, those delays are neutralizing the tech and business gains meant to accelerate speed to power, said Helen Kou, head of U.S. power research and analysis at BloombergNEF. “We’ve seen a significant increase in overall data center capacity…but we also analyzed how long it takes to develop a data center,” Kou told Latitude Media at the BNEF Summit in San Francisco this week, pointing to the firm’s data

Title: Foley Report Reveals Growth in Data Center Industry Faces New Strategic Challenges as ...
Summary_AI: Foley & Lardner LLP's 2026 Data Center Development Report highlights the rapid growth of the U.S. data center industry driven by the AI boom, while revealing significant strategic challenges such as power availability, supply chain disruptions, and regulatory hurdles. Surveying 105 industry stakeholders, the report finds that 63% anticipate a market correction by 2030, with energy availability cited as the top obstacle by 54% of respondents. Despite these challenges, 95% believe rising compute demand will be met by decade’s end, emphasizing opportunities in energy efficiency and cooling technologies. The report underscores the critical need for innovative solutions in power capacity and efficiency to sustain data center expansion and capitalize on AI-driven demand.
Article_Text: Jan 28, 2026 9:00 AM Eastern Standard Time Foley Report Reveals Growth in Data Center Industry Faces New Strategic Challenges as Markets Tighten Share Survey findings provide valuable insights for developing critical infrastructure to power our digital future CHICAGO--(BUSINESS WIRE)--Foley & Lardner LLP released today its 2026 Data Center Development Report looking at the artificial intelligence (AI) boom that is propping up much of the U.S. economy and the sustainability of data center development fueling it, including critical challenges and opportunities to come. The report breaks out specific findings by stakeholder groups — including financiers and investors, developers, providers, operators, and tenants and hyperscalers — and delves into important data center development issues, from supply chain breakdowns and colocation strategies to financing snags, concerns about AI’s potential profitability, and more. The report reflects insights from U.S. experts and executives and shows that the industry is bracing for change with 63% of respondents anticipating a strategic correction in the data center market by 2030 and nearly 40% of respondents saying the current pace of data center development is unsustainable. “There is a Gold Rush mentality right now around securing power. That’s a big part of why people feel there’s a bubble,” said Daniel Farris, partner and co-lead of Foley’s Data Center and Digital Infrastructure Team. “There’s going to a period in the next two to three years where power at necessary levels is going to be really hard to come by.” Survey findings reveal that despite the anticipated market correction, 95% of respondents believe the industry will meet rising compute demand by the end of the decade. However, this collective confidence is currently being tested as the race for available power to support these facilities heats up. Title: DeForest Village Board will recommend rejecting QTS data center project - Spectrum News
Summary_AI: The DeForest Village Board in Wisconsin will recommend rejecting QTS Data Centers' proposal to develop a nearly 1,600-acre data center campus in the Town of Vienna at its February 3, 2026 meeting, citing feasibility concerns. If the recommendation is approved, QTS is expected to formally withdraw its application; otherwise, a public hearing will be held, and the village will likely deny the project amendments. The decision follows local residents' environmental and resource usage concerns, reflecting community opposition, while the village emphasizes the need for future development to support municipal services and investments.

Title: Can Advanced Nuclear Reactors Solve the Data Center Power Puzzle?
Summary_AI: The article discusses the potential of advanced nuclear reactors, specifically small modular reactors (SMRs), to address the escalating energy demands of data centers driven by AI and digital transformation. The Electric Power Research Institute (EPRI) and the Nuclear Energy Institute (NEI) released a 2025 Advanced Reactor Roadmap aiming for over 300 GW of advanced nuclear capacity by 2050, with initial SMR deployments for data centers expected in the early 2030s. These reactors offer reliable, low-carbon, and resilient power with high uptime, addressing limitations of traditional renewables and grid instability. Key challenges include regulatory approval, supply chain readiness, and financial risk, but leading tech companies have already committed to nuclear-powered data centers, signaling strategic moves toward sustainable, cost-effective energy solutions that enhance grid stability and decarbonize data center operations.

Title: New Orleans considers data center limits - Audacy
Summary_AI: The New Orleans City Council is considering a one-year moratorium on approving new data center projects following community and mayoral opposition to a proposed data center near I-10 and Read Blvd. Mayor Helena Moreno expressed strong disapproval, citing lack of prior notice and concerns about neighborhood impact. The council may direct the planning commission to hold a public hearing to establish new zoning regulations, reflecting a strategic move to control data center growth and address local stakeholder concerns. This regulatory consideration could significantly affect data center development and investment in New Orleans.

Title: Microsoft's Earnings Surge Is Overshadowed by Data Center Spending - WSJ
Summary_AI: Microsoft reported a strong second-quarter revenue of $81.3 billion and net income of $38.5 billion, surpassing expectations, driven in part by its strategic investment in OpenAI. However, the company’s shares dropped 11% due to higher-than-anticipated capital spending on data centers and AI infrastructure, alongside a slowdown in Azure cloud growth to 39%, slightly below the previous quarter. CFO Amy Hood highlighted that limited AI hardware availability is constraining Azure’s growth potential, signaling that cloud demand currently outpaces supply, which may temper growth in the near term. This spending underscores Microsoft’s commitment to expanding its AI and cloud capabilities, positioning it for long-term competitiveness despite short-term market concerns.

Title: Betting big on data centers, U.S. now leads world for new gas power development
Summary_AI: The United States now leads global development of gas-fired power capacity, with nearly 252 GW in announced, pre-construction, and construction phases, surpassing China and representing about 25% of the world's total. Over one-third of this capacity is dedicated to on-site power for data centers, driven by rising energy demands from AI and tech industries, particularly in Texas, which accounts for 80.6 GW of planned capacity. This expansion, estimated to cost over $416 billion, faces challenges such as turbine supply constraints and long grid connection queues, but reflects a strategic U.S. investment to support the growing data center sector and maintain energy reliability amid surging digital infrastructure needs.

More than one-third of this capacity is slated to directly power data centers on-site1, and many more on-grid projects are planned to meet an anticipated increase in energy demand from AI (Figure 1). According to new data in the Global Oil and Gas Plant Tracker2, the U.S. nearly tripled its gas-fired capacity in development in 2025, totalling almost 252 gigawatts (GW). If all in-development plants are built, the U.S.’ existing gas fleet would grow by nearly 50%, at an estimated cost of over US$416 billion in capital costs3. Figure 1 Global gas power pipeline increases nearly a third Globally, gas-fired power capacity in development rose 31% (+249 GW) in 2025, reaching a total of 1,047 GW. Projects set to operate through 2030 are expected to surge in the last half of the decade (Figure 2). 2026 could be a record for new gas power projects: if all planned capacity starts operation this year, it would exceed the previous record of 100 GW added in 2002 at the height of the U.S. shale boom. Summary_AI: NextEra Energy aims to deliver at least 30 gigawatts of power through data center hubs by 2035, doubling its initial goal of 15 gigawatts as part of its "15 by 35" origination channel. This strategy leverages a mix of renewables, battery storage, and gas generation to meet growing data center energy demands, emphasizing a "bring your own generation" (BYOG) model to address affordability challenges in the U.S. market. Title: Meta rises after sales, earnings beat and Q1 revenue guidance exceeds expectations
Summary_AI: Meta reported a record $59.9 billion in Q4 2025 sales and earnings per share of $8.88, surpassing Wall Street expectations, which led to a 6% stock rise in after-hours trading. For Q1 2026, Meta projects revenue between $53.5 billion and $56.5 billion, exceeding consensus estimates, despite planning aggressive capital expenditures of $115 billion to $135 billion for the year—well above analyst forecasts. This substantial investment, primarily in AI infrastructure, signals Meta's strategic focus on long-term growth, with the company expecting operating income in 2026 to surpass 2025 levels despite margin pressures and elevated spending. Investors remain attentive to how Meta’s AI investments will translate into revenue growth amid heightened scrutiny over its spending pace relative to peers.
Article_Text: Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg at WSJ Innovators, held at the Museum of Modern Art on October 29, 2025, in New York City (Lexie Moreland/Getty Images) Meta rises after sales, earnings beat and Q1 revenue guidance exceeds expectations Even with spending much higher than expected, Meta still expects operating income to increase year on year in 2026. Rani Molla 1/28/26 4:09PM MetaMETA $687.23 (-2.72%) released its fourth-quarter results after the close on Wednesday, with sales coming in at a record high of $59.9 billion, well above the $58.4 billion Wall Street had expected, and earnings per share of $8.88 also far north of the $8.19 consensus estimate. The stock jumped more than 6% in after-hours trading. For the current quarter, the Mark Zuckerberg-led social media giant expects sales of $53.5 billion to $56.5 billion, well ahead of the consensus call for $51.3 billion. However, those higher sales are coming with even more aggressive spending: Meta’s capex outlook for 2026 is $115 billion to $135 billion, exceeding the $110.6 billion estimate from analysts. Full-year estimated expenses of $162 billion to $169 billion are also much more than the consensus call for $151 billion. “Despite the meaningful step up in infrastructure investment, in 2026 we expect to deliver operating income that is above 2025 operating income,” according to the press release. Generally Meta’s capex growth has been growing faster than its revenue, something that’s been raising investor scrutiny more so than it has for the tech giant’s peers, which have cloud business revenue to more directly offset their spending on AI. On a related note, Meta’s expected profit margins have deteriorated since the end of 2024, unlike any of its megacap hyperscaler counterparts. Title: Trump, tech giants seek to change data center narrative - The Hill
Summary_AI: President Trump and major tech companies are working to reshape the narrative around data centers amid rising electricity costs linked to AI infrastructure, which have sparked local opposition and political concerns. The Trump administration, alongside governors from Pennsylvania, Maryland, and Virginia, is pressuring the regional grid operator PJM to make data centers pay more for new power sources unless they provide their own, aiming to prevent cost burdens on consumers. Tech giants like Microsoft and OpenAI have pledged to adopt community-focused approaches to mitigate electricity price impacts, signaling a strategic shift to balance rapid data center growth with consumer cost concerns and political liabilities.

While the Trump administration has been broadly supportive of data centers, including through efforts to fast-track their construction, going forward that support may have limitations. “We are going to embrace data centers, but not at the price … of raising costs for consumers,” a senior White House official told The Hill on Tuesday. “So if you want to invest and build data centers, you’ve got to do it in the right way, the way that is not going to pass that cost onto consumers,” the official said. The White House official said the administration was working with “each of the major hyperscalers and data center operators” to try to find ways to defray costs for consumers. The person declined to name specific companies. The comments come as data centers face increasing backlash, including because of their potential impacts on high electricity prices, becoming a potential liability for the GOP. Title: Bernalillo County weighs strict limits on AI data centers in New Mexico
Summary_AI: Bernalillo County in New Mexico is considering strict regulations on artificial intelligence data centers, including requirements for renewable energy use, local job creation, and water offset measures. Title: Texas, Other States Draw Data Center Developers' Interest - American Oil & Gas Reporter

Article_Text: January 2026 Exclusive Story Texas, Other States Draw Data Center Developers’ Interest As data center developers look for places with ready access to energy, many are turning to Texas, according to a survey by fuel cell manufacturer Bloom Energy. “By 2028, Texas is projected to exceed 40 GW of (data center) capacity—nearly 30% of total U.S. demand—representing a 142% increase in market share relative to today,” the survey projects. “Georgia is also gaining momentum, with market share projected to rise 75% as developers expand deeper into the Southeast.” Meanwhile, many traditional data center hubs may lose market share. “While Virginia continues to grow in absolute terms, its relative share is expected to decline as grid constraints intensify in one of the industry’s most mature markets,” Bloom reports. The company adds that California, Iowa, Oregon, and Nebraska should see their relative market share halve thanks to power constraints, permitting requirements and long interconnection timelines. “At the same time, the rest of the United States is emerging as a meaningful aggregate winner, with market share projected to increase 21% by 2028,” the survey says. “This increase reflects a shift toward a broader set of power-advantaged states, as developers pursue regions where large blocks of power can be secured more quickly. Though individual markets like Mississippi and Oklahoma remain smaller in absolute terms, their growth highlights how far developers are willing to extend siting flexibility to meet AI-driven compute demand—even when that requires trade-offs in network proximity and workload placement.” To illustrate this trend, Bloom turns to Oklahoma. The state is expected to only capture about 2% market share in the next three years, but the company estimates that will require its deployed capacity to grow sixfold. Title: Developer pulls plug on proposed New Orleans East data center after City Council moves to ...
Summary_AI: A proposed data center project in New Orleans East by developer James Ramsey has been canceled after the City Council approved a one-year freeze on all data center developments, citing community concerns. The planned site near Interstate 10 and Read Boulevard faced opposition from residents worried about residential zoning, environmental impact, and resource consumption. The council aims to define "data center" officially to prevent such projects, signaling a challenging environment for future data center investments in the city, which Ramsey warns could hinder local economic growth and job creation.
Article_Text:
A businessman who wanted to build a data center in New Orleans East says the deal is off after the City Council took the first step toward blocking such developments. WWL was first to report on the proposal, which drew criticism from residents.
Developer James Ramsey had sharp criticism for the council's decision.
"Basically, the council just announced to the world that we're not smart enough to know the difference between a toxic waste dump and a building with computers in it," Ramsey said.
The council's action effectively killed Ramsey's plans to build a data center in New Orleans East. The project was planned for a large vacant lot near Interstate 10 and Read Boulevard, across from the Warwick East subdivision.
On Wednesday, council members approved an ordinance that puts a one-year freeze on all data center projects. It also directs the City Planning Commission to come up with an official definition of what a data center actually is.
"The goal is we want to move quickly on defining this to block this," Council President J.P. Morrell said.
The council listened to residents who raised concerns about putting a data center next to their homes. They also worried that data centers could be a major drain on electricity and water.
"Portions of the land is zoned for residential homes,” said Dawn Hebert, President of the New Orleans East Neighborhood Advisory Commission. “We don't want that changed for one thing. This would just be detrimental to this community,"
Ramsey warned the council's action does not bode well for future economic development in the city.
"You look at New Orleans, we have a problem with keeping our children here because you don't have jobs,” Ramsey said. “Yet, you take the number one job creator in the world and say you know what, go put it someplace else. And, unfortunately, I'm going to listen to you and do that.”
Councilman Jason Hughes, who represents New Orleans East, said his community is open for business but put would-be developers on notice.

CATHERINE BOUDREAU JANUARY 28, 2026 Photo credit: Forgent Power For decades, the electrical equipment industry was a sleepy corner of the market. But as Forgent Power Solutions prepares for a nearly $9 billion debut on the New York Stock Exchange in February, it’s clear that era has come to an end. Forgent, which makes the transformers, switchgears, and controls in high demand from data center hyperscalers and energy developers, filed for an IPO this week, targeting an $8.8 billion valuation that would put it squarely among mid-cap publicly traded companies. It marked a rapid rise for a company that only officially launched under the Forgent name in August 2025, after a private equity firm integrated four separate equipment makers. Electrical equipment is in short supply in the U.S. Lead times can be up to four years because domestic manufacturers can’t keep up with the spike in demand, and importers face their own supply chain challenges overseas, from raw materials to labor. The squeeze is reflected in Forgent’s SEC filing, which stated that the company has a backlog of $1 billion in purchase orders and saw its revenue grow 56% between fiscal 2024 and 2025, with plans to expand manufacturing capacity this year. Forgent has marketed itself as a solution to the lengthy wait times facing data centers and power developers in two ways. First, its factories are in the U.S. and Mexico, allowing it to skip more complex overseas supply chains. Second, its custom, pre-assembled “powertrains” are equipped with all the hardware artificial intelligence data centers need to get electricity flowing. Title: HB 1030: Pushing data center development out of Colorado
Summary_AI: Colorado's House Bill 26-1030 imposes stringent requirements on data center development, including a mandate for 100% clean energy use by 2040, burdensome labor rules, and the creation of a regulatory authority to oversee compliance. These provisions, such as requiring a 3-to-1 ratio of renewable to thermal energy capacity and banning fossil-fueled backup generators after 2040, significantly increase costs and operational complexity for data centers. The bill is expected to deter investment and development in Colorado, potentially pushing data center projects to neighboring states like Wyoming and Utah, which have more flexible energy policies. This legislation favors large hyperscale companies able to absorb compliance costs, while smaller developers may be driven out, impacting Colorado’s competitiveness in attracting data center investments and related economic benefits.
Article_Text: About Us Contact Us ORIGINAL REPORTING • COMMENTARY • PODCASTS • VIDEO LOCAL ISSUES 2026 LEG SESSION CONTRIBUTORS TABOR PODCASTS VIDEOS SUPPORT LOCAL JOURNALISM HB 1030: Pushing data center development out of Colorado January 28, 2026 Sarah Montalbano Legislators in Denver are off to the races this session with a heavy-handed bill that will chase data center investment out of Colorado. House Bill 26-1030 creates a new bureaucracy, imposes burdensome labor and workforce requirements, and requires data centers to use 100% clean energy. If lawmakers believe data centers are “essential critical infrastructure,” as the bill claims, then the legislature must allow them to use whatever electricity sources they need. If the goal is to drive data center developers to Wyoming, then lawmakers should continue down this path. Mandating unreliable energy The worst part of HB 1030 is its requirement that data centers must be powered with 100% renewable and clean energy resources after 2040. Enterprise-level data centers need an uptime of 99.995 percent, which means being offline for only 26 minutes per year. They’ll prefer dispatchable power on demand, 24/7/365, and they need backup systems that will work no matter what the weather. Before 2039, data centers must be powered with a 3-to-1 ratio of nameplate capacity–or the amount of power produced under ideal conditions–between “clean” and thermal resources. That means that, for every 1 megawatt (MW) in nameplate capacity of natural gas or coal-fired energy, there will need to be 3 MW of wind, solar, or other clean energy sources. The problem is that nameplate capacity means little next to actual energy delivered. In 2023, natural gas combined-cycle plants delivered reliable, dispatchable power 49.5 percent of the time, and can ramp up to meet energy needs. Solar photovoltaics ran at a capacity factor of only 24.7 percent, while wind turbines ran at 35.7 percent capacity factor. Batteries are worse because they don’t generate energ

Title: Data Centers in PJM Grid Can Rely Solely on Generators During the Cold, DOE Rules
Summary_AI: The U.S. Department of Energy has authorized data centers within the PJM Interconnection grid, including Virginia—the global hub for data centers—to rely solely on diesel backup generators during the cold spell caused by Winter Storm Fern through January 31, 2026. This emergency measure aims to stabilize the grid amid record electricity demand but raises environmental concerns due to increased pollution from diesel generators, which are often located near residential areas. While the move helps prevent power outages for residential customers, it has sparked calls for greater regulatory oversight and transparency regarding generator use and emissions in Virginia’s densely clustered data center regions.

By Charles Paullin January 29, 2026 Share This Article Republish An aerial view shows cooling vent fans on the roof next to generators on the lower level of a Digital Realty data center in Ashburn, Virginia, in November 2025. Credit: Andrew Caballero-Reynolds/AFP via Getty Images RICHMOND, Va.—The U.S. Department of Energy used the Federal Power Act on Monday to allow data centers within the regional grid operated by PJM Interconnection, including those in Virginia, to use generators at PJM’s discretion through Jan. 31. The allowance comes amid PJM’s record forecasts and demands for electricity and heat during freezing temps. This will enable data centers in Virginia, which has more of the giant server farms than any other state or nation, to run solely on their diesel backup generators in an emergency situation during the remnants of Fern’s cold weather through Jan. 31. The state’s environmental regulators won’t necessarily be notified when those generators start up, state officials said. Currently, data centers in Virginia are allowed to run their so-called Tier II generators with pollution controls and cleaner Tier IV generators outside of emergencies for extended periods of time before reaching air quality limits. Title: County, state legislators criticize proposed Archbald data centers - Scranton Times-Tribune
Summary_AI: Local officials and community members in Archbald, Lackawanna County, strongly oppose Cornell Realty Management's proposal to build the Wildcat Ridge Data Center Campus, consisting of 14 large data centers spanning 5.66 million square feet. Concerns focus on the project's significant water (up to 3.3 million gallons daily) and power demands (1,600 megawatts), environmental impact, infrastructure strain, and lack of guaranteed end users. County Commissioner Bill Gaughan and state Rep. Kyle Mullins criticized the development for its potential to harm quality of life and called for stricter regulations or a moratorium on data centers. The borough council is conducting ongoing public hearings to decide on the conditional use application amid substantial community resistance, highlighting broader regional debates about balancing economic development with environmental and social costs.
Article_Text:
ARCHBALD — Lackawanna County Commissioner Bill Gaughan urged Archbald officials to consider the countywide impacts of data centers Wednesday night while state Rep. Kyle Mullins unequivocally opposed a proposal to build more than a dozen data centers above Business Route 6.
The local legislators joined more than 300 community members Wednesday in the Valley View High School auditorium for the first of what will likely be multiple public hearings on a conditional use application for the Wildcat Ridge Data Center Campus  — a proposal from Brooklyn-based Cornell Realty Management LLC to build 5.66 million square feet of data centers above the borough’s Sturges and Eynon sections, requiring significant amounts of water and electricity to operate the facilities.
Testimony from the campus’s project manager and attorney revealed Wildcat Ridge would get its water from Lake Scranton, that it does not currently have an end user, that it would only be built if it has an end user, and that commercial space on the site could include “Trader Joe’s, or something like that.”
Wildcat Ridge became the first data center campus proposed in Lackawanna County when company representatives approached Archbald council during January 2025 work session, and one year later, representatives for the developer sat opposite borough officials on stage in Valley View’s high school auditorium to make their case as to why council should approve their project, in light of substantial opposition from the community.
Despite proposals elsewhere in Lackawanna County, Archbald maintains the highest concentration of potential data centers, with 44 buildings planned across five campuses.
