Title: Saline Township residents frustrated after zoning vote blocks referendum on AI data center
Summary_AI: The Saline Township Board in Michigan voted to maintain agricultural zoning on over 500 acres designated for a $7 billion AI data center project backed by Oracle and OpenAI, effectively blocking a resident-led referendum against the development. Despite community opposition and legal challenges, the project by Related Digital will proceed under a consent judgment settled in October 2025, which includes $14 million in community investments and environmental protections. The board’s decision to update zoning maps aligns with the consent judgment, ensuring the data center’s development continues while limiting local residents' ability to influence land use decisions.
Article_Text:
SALINE TOWNSHIP, Mich. – The Saline Township Board voted Wednesday to maintain agricultural zoning for more than 500 acres slated for a massive AI data center, though developers say the project will proceed as planned under an existing consent judgment.
The board previously voted to rezone the property from A-1 agricultural to I-1 industrial “as ordered by the Washtenaw County Circuit Court” at a meeting on Jan.14.
The board’s decision to reverse its previous rezoning from agricultural to industrial use won’t impact the development, according to township attorney David Landry.
“Tonight was to correct that so that it is consistent with the consent judgment,” Landry said. “A court cannot order a municipal entity to zone any particular way.”
The $7 billion data center project, backed by Oracle and OpenAI, has faced significant community opposition since its proposal.
The development by Related Digital is planned for 575 acres of land north of Michigan Avenue, with about 2.2 million square feet dedicated to the facility.
The latest board decision effectively blocks residents from filing a referendum against the land use, frustrating community members who oppose the project.
“If they don’t legally have to update the map, and they don’t have to legally put the notice out for that property changing, no, we won’t be able to do a referendum,” said Tammie Bruneau with the Rural Michigan Defense Fund.
“What does it look like when residents are not able to determine what happens in their communities? What are we expected to do? Just shrug, shut up, and take it?” resident Kathryn Haushalter asked during the meeting.
Haushalter has filed a lawsuit challenging the validity of the consent judgment.
Attorney Robby Dube of the law firm Eckland & Blando said the firm is waiting for a judge to determine whether it has the right to intervene in the case.

That’s enough to power nearly 800,000 homes. The company is “adding capacity as quickly as we can,” said Amy Hood, chief financial officer for Microsoft. During its earnings call, CEO Satya Nadella highlighted its “first of its kind” AI superfactory, which it completed last year. The project links Microsoft’s data centers in Wisconsin and Atlanta, about 700 miles apart, using a high-speed fiber-optic network. By connecting these facilities through an AI Wide Area Network (AI-WAN), Microsoft can run them as a single system. This means each data center contains hundreds of thousands of Nvidia GPUs that share workloads in real time, and AI tasks can be processed more efficiently. Still, Microsoft’s demand for cloud and AI continues to exceed its supply. Microsoft reported its capital expenditures (CapEx) for the quarter ending Dec. 31, 2025, were $37.5 billion, a 66% increase year-over-year, mostly driven by data center growth. The company said it would increase expenses this year, but did not offer a specific amount. The latest iteration of the Information Age appears to have arrived in full-force, manifested as AI, the digital cloud, remote work and the mass migration from the material world into cyberspace. A couple of decades ago, when I was feeling optimistic, I envisioned this future as a Jetsons-esque world, where the noisy clang of machinery would give way to a soft electrified hum while robots and artificial intelligence performed menial and mundane tasks, freeing us to live like George Jetson, working a leisurely nine hours a week as a digital index operator at a space sprocket firm. This new era would be a vast improvement over the worn-out Industrial Age, mainly because it would come with an energy transition. We would ditch our clanky old machinery — all the smokestacks and pollution and internal combustion engines — trading them for sleek cars that, if not flying, would at least be electric, powered by cleaner, gentler and quieter forms of energy, like wind and solar. LEXI CRITCHETT—BLOOMBERG/GETTY IMAGES Utilities requested a record-high $31 billion in rate hikes in 2025 across the nation—more than twice the near record from 2024—as consumer and political backlash grows over the AI data center boom that could further contribute to cost increases going forward, according to a new report from nonprofit PowerLines released Jan. 29. While aging infrastructure, extreme weather events, and natural gas price spikes are leading contributors to electricity prices rising 40% since 2001, the growing electricity demand from the data center construction surge has begun to further drive up rates. Residential retail electricity prices increased 7% in 2025 alone, while piped gas prices rose 11% last year, according to the U.S. Department of Energy. The majority of the rate hikes requested by utilities are already approved, but nearly half remain pending going into 2026 and could receive increased scrutiny from state regulators, said Charles Hua, PowerLines executive director, especially as pivotal midterm elections loom in November. “These increases, a lot of them have not actually hit people’s wallets yet,” Hua said. “So that shows that, in 2026, the utility bills will likely continue to rise, barring some major sweeping action.” Wall Street is taking note as well. Julien Dumoulin-Smith, power and utilities analyst for Jefferies, argued that the industry’s 2026 narrative is shifting from “capex growth at all costs” to “capex growth with a customer permission slip” because of the rising public backlash. “We believe it is no longer enough for utilities to say they care about

Article_Text:
City-county councilors are considering tighter restrictions on data centers amid concerns over environmental damage, water consumption and rising utility costs.
The council’s Environmental Sustainability Committee met Jan. 28 to hear from experts and members of the public to help inform their policy decisions.
There are three active proposals for data centers in Martindale Brightwood, Pike Township and Decatur Township. All of the projects have faced intense opposition from neighborhood residents.
Councilors are weighing their options for how to regulate data centers, from enacting stricter zoning laws to beefing up reporting requirements for tech companies.
But one possibility in particular appealed to many of the residents in attendance.
“Hold off. Just stop it. Stop the games,” said William Boler, an eastsider who has experience working with artificial intelligence. “Figure out what you need to do to regulate the city.”
Boler wants to see the council pass a six-month moratorium, or temporary ban, on any new data centers in Marion County. He’s one of a growing number of residents fighting back against efforts by tech companies to build data centers here.
‘A wild west’
Data centers are locations that organizations use to store computing infrastructure and data. While not a new concept, the rise of cloud computing and artificial intelligence in recent years has led to increased demand for these energy-intensive hubs.
They strain power grids and require vast amounts of water. In fact, some data centers use as much water as a city of 50,000 people, said Rebecca TeKolste, climate and energy advisor for the Hoosier Environmental Council.
“We’re thinking about water scarcity, and the prices to ratepayers,” TeKolste said.
Her organization has concerns about a plan from Citizens Energy to draw as much as 8 million gallons of water per day from the Eagle Creek Reservoir to help power a technology park in Boone County, where Meta plans to build a 1500-acre data center.

Title: Public says no to data centers in Massena industrial zones | News, Sports, Jobs
Summary_AI: Residents of Massena, New York, strongly opposed allowing data centers in the town’s industrial zones during a January 2026 public hearing, citing concerns over environmental health impacts, water usage, and increased electricity costs. Speakers highlighted past industrial pollution, potential harm to the nearby Akwesasne community, and the lack of consultation with the St. Regis Mohawk Tribe. The opposition also emphasized the limited economic benefits, such as temporary jobs and unclear tax contributions, urging the town to reinstate a moratorium on new data centers. The town board will consider public feedback before finalizing any changes to local laws, while tribal leaders seek dialogue to address shared concerns and avoid conflict.

Title: AmberSemi Launches PowerTile to Cut Data Center Power Drain
Summary_AI: AmberSemi, a California-based semiconductor firm, has introduced PowerTile, a compact power delivery device designed to reduce board-level power distribution losses by 85% in AI data center processors. The coin-sized module, which mounts beneath server processors, supports high-current delivery for CPUs, GPUs, and FPGAs, potentially saving 225 MW of power annually in a 500 MW AI data center—equivalent to $160 million in energy costs. Targeting the growing power demands of next-generation AI processors, PowerTile aims to improve energy efficiency and address critical power bottlenecks in data centers, with initial testing planned for 2026 and shipping in 2027. This innovation could significantly impact data center operations by enabling higher power density and reducing energy waste amid rising electricity consumption concerns.

Shane Snider, Senior News Writer, Data Center Knowledge January 29, 2026 4 Min Read PowerTile.AMBERSEMI California-based fabless semiconductor firm AmberSemi said it has taped out a new power-delivery device for AI processors in data centers, claiming large power reductions in board-level power distribution losses. The coin-sized PowerTile mounts on the back of a server board directly beneath the processor, and, according to the company, can cut board-level power distribution losses by 85%. The PowerTile is a 1,000-amp vertical power device that delivers current via a vertical path rather than a traditional lateral one. Measuring about 20mm x 24mm x 1.68mm – roughly the size of a quarter – the module can be used with multiple devices to scale beyond 10,000 amps. AmberSemi said it is designed to support CPUs, GPUs, FPGAs, and other high-performance processors that require large current delivery in a minimal space. For a 500 MW AI data center, AmberSemi estimates the PowerTile can save 225 MW of power per year (worth $160 million). The company compares this to the approximate annual energy output of a small modular nuclear reactor, depending on plant capacity and capacity factor. Mark Vena, CEO and principal analyst at SmartTech Research, said AmberSemi’s PowerTile addresses a real need in the data center. “This is entirely on target,” Vena told Data Center Knowledge. “AmberSemi is basically saying the power path should go vertical, not sideways, by mounting a 1,000-amp device on the backside of the server board directly under the processor. Title: Georgia emerges as a battleground over AI data centers - Axios
Summary_AI: Georgia is becoming a key battleground over the rapid expansion of AI data centers, with growing voter concerns about affordability, energy, and water usage. Democratic state Rep. Ruwa Romman has proposed a temporary moratorium on new data center construction to allow for a thorough study of their environmental and economic impacts, aiming to inform future regulations. This issue has significant political implications, influencing recent Public Service Commission elections and gubernatorial campaigns, as both parties seek to address the costs associated with Big Tech’s data centers amid rising utility rates and local opposition.

Why it matters: Tech companies are flocking to Georgia for AI energy demands, and people across the political spectrum are pushing back. In a state where key midterm elections could rebalance power statewide and nationally, candidates are laser-focused on getting the messaging and policy solutions right. Zoom in: Democratic state Rep. Ruwa Romman's HB 1012 would place a temporary moratorium on new data center construction in Georgia. The goal is to give officials time to study water usage, energy consumption and the real revenue and economic impacts instead of just projections, said Romman, who is running for governor. Romman added that data would then inform regulation. Romman echoes Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.), though Romman said she was already looking into the idea of a moratorium when she heard the progressive standard-bearer had a similar solution in mind. "I'm actually working on an email to shoot his chief of staff to let him know that we're working on this," said Romman, contending that "any policy that happens at the federal level gets implemented first at the state level." Other Democrats are sounding more like Republicans and promising voters that data center-related costs will be paid by Big Tech. The two ideas are not at odds, according to Romman, who noted that in order for Big Tech to pay, policymakers first need to understand how much it actually costs. Title: Storing the sun: Why battery and solar is the new normal for hyperscale efficiency - DCD
Summary_AI: India’s hyperscale data center capacity, which reached 1,263MW by April 2025 and is projected to hit 4,500MW by 2030, faces challenges in ensuring continuous, sustainable power. The integration of large-scale solar energy with advanced battery energy storage systems (BESS) is emerging as a strategic solution, enabling reliable, clean, and resilient power by storing excess solar energy for use during non-sunny periods. This hybrid approach reduces reliance on diesel generators, supports regulatory compliance, lowers operational costs, and aligns with ESG goals, positioning solar plus BESS as the new baseline architecture for hyperscale data centers in India’s rapidly expanding digital infrastructure landscape.

Title: Summit Ridge data center sparks debate in Santaquin over power, water, and pollution
Summary_AI: The Summit Ridge Data Center, approved in 2024 and planned near exit 242 in Santaquin, Utah, has sparked community concerns over emissions, air quality, and resource use despite promises of low water consumption and self-generated power by developer DCPC. The project, supported by local officials and expected to create dozens of jobs, faces scrutiny from residents worried about pollution impacts near new housing developments. While no groundbreaking date is set, ongoing discussions aim to balance economic growth with environmental and public health considerations in the rapidly expanding city.
Article_Text:
SANTAQUIN, Utah — Santaquin residents packed an open house Thursday night to press city leaders and developers on the approved Summit Ridge Data Center, raising fresh concerns over emissions, air quality, and the impact on the growing community.
It’s planned to go in on the southern end of town, and while it’s already been approved, some residents have recently caught wind of it and raised concerns over the project.
Santaquin Mayor Dan Olson says he’s glad the public is taking an interest. He says this packed house comes on the heels of several public discussions they’ve had in the city council about the proposal. It was approved back in 2024.
“For posted meetings, we had between nine and 11 [about the data center] - nobody attended,” said Mayor Olson.
When the Utah-based company DCPC first approached, the mayor had his own hesitations and rejected the idea. “Power coming off the grid is not enough to support a data center, so they went away,”
But these local developers came back with a solution.
“I’m a Utah County native - I stand behind this project 100 percent,” said Robert Sumsion, DCPC’s VP of business development. “This is my backyard, too. We’re doing everything we can to be as transparent as possible.”
The company presented the new plan to the public on Thursday, which focused on lower emissions, water needs, and more. “Extremely low water usage, and they’re generating their own power, so there won’t be any blackouts to the city,” Mayor Olson said, also noting the dozens of jobs it will bring.
The ‘Summit Ridge Data Center’ will be just off the freeway near exit 242, near a newer housing development that holds some residents who aren’t quite satisfied with all the company’s answers. “Even on a natural gas generator, there’s still going to be emissions that cause issues - emissions that are not healthy,” said Ben Thornell.

Title: Local groups file suit against TeraWulf, Lansing zoning board over data center
Summary_AI: Local environmental groups have filed a lawsuit against the Town of Lansing Zoning Board of Appeals (ZBA) and TeraWulf, a Maryland-based company planning to build a $400 million AI data center on the site of a former coal-fired power plant in Lansing, New York. The suit challenges the ZBA’s December 2025 decision to classify the data center as an allowed use under the town’s industrial zoning, arguing this interpretation unlawfully broadens zoning definitions and alleging bias by the ZBA chairperson. The dispute highlights local concerns over environmental impacts, including increased electricity demand, CO2 emissions, and community disruption, while TeraWulf maintains the project’s legality and economic benefits. This legal challenge could delay or complicate the development of a significant data center investment in the region.

The suit comes after last month’s ZBA decision to interpret the data center as an allowed use in the town’s industrial zone. TeraWulf, a Maryland-based company, wants to build an AI data center at the site of a former coal-fired power plant. Those plans hit a snag when the Town of Lansing’s code enforcement officer determined that the project did not comply with the town’s zoning code. TeraWulf appealed to the ZBA, and a series of heated meetings followed. The board determined on Dec. 23 the project was a permitted use and could move forward. Now local environmental groups are challenging that decision. The lawsuit, known as an Article 78, was filed in New York State Supreme Court on Thursday afternoon. The suit seeks to annul the ZBA’s decision “based on errors of law and violations of lawful procedure.” Cayuga Lake Action Now (CLEAN) and FLX Strong, the two petitioners in the suit, said in statement that the ZBA’s decision sets an “alarming precedent.” “We will continue to educate the public as to the many serious impacts that data centers represent, both to our economy in the form of rising electric bills and strain on the electric grid, and to our communities and environment in the form of noise and light pollution

Title: Va. delegate's bill would limit where high-voltage transmission lines can be built
Summary_AI: Virginia delegates have introduced House Bill 1491 to restrict the placement of high-voltage transmission lines (over 136 kV) away from residential areas, schools, and other sensitive locations, aiming to keep them at least 500 feet distant. This legislation responds to local opposition in Loudoun County against Dominion Energy’s proposed 500 kV "Golden to Mars" line, intended to supply power to nearby data centers but criticized for potential health risks and property value declines. The bill also supports enhanced local zoning authority over data center developments to better manage environmental and community impacts, reflecting growing tensions between infrastructure needs for data centers and residential community concerns. The State Corporation Commission’s decision on the transmission line route is pending, while the bill is set for committee review.

(Photo by Charlie Paullin/Virginia Mercury) A case that is playing out in front of the State Corporation Commission regarding a controversial high-voltage transmission line in Northern Virginia has inspired legislation in the General Assembly designed to keep the powerful lines away from residential areas and schools. House Bill 1491, sponsored by Del. J.J. Singh, D-Loudoun, and Del. John McAuliff, D-Fauquier, directs the SCC, when considering the approval of transmission lines that are more powerful than 136 kv, to try to the greatest extent possible to keep the lines over 500 feet away from schools, residential properties, daycares, parks, playgrounds, recreational areas, or places of worship. It also aims to limit lines that would impede on the locality’s plan for a space. In Singh’s district, the Loudoun Valley Estates neighborhoods and county government have been rallying against Dominion Energy’s plan to build a 165 foot 500kv line, dubbed the Golden to Mars line, through the Ashburn neighborhoods to bring more power to scores of nearby data centers. Last year, the Loudoun County School Board voted against allowing the transmission lines to be built on their properties, which led to Dominion Energy to create a new route that cuts through the backyards of some of the homeowners. Title: Data Centers in the AI Era: A New Blueprint for Growth
Summary_AI: The article discusses how the surge in AI workloads is driving a rapid increase in data center power demand, prompting industry-wide shifts in grid access, cost-sharing, and power generation strategies. Texas has emerged as a key hub, implementing Senate Bill 6 to speed up grid interconnections and fairly allocate costs, enabling faster project timelines. Companies like CPS Energy and CyrusOne emphasize "speed to power" as a critical competitive advantage, adopting co-location, behind-the-meter generation, and flexible energy sources including natural gas and renewables. This new blueprint prioritizes accelerated power delivery and infrastructure innovation to align with AI hardware deployment, reshaping data center growth and sustainability goals in the AI era.

NEWSLETTER SIGN-UP ENERGY & POWER SUPPLY DATA CENTER CONSTRUCTION INVESTING AI DATA CENTERS INDUSTRY TRENDS Data Centers in the AI Era: A New Blueprint for Growth As AI accelerates data center demand, the industry is rethinking grid access, cost-sharing, and power generation, with speed to power emerging as the critical factor for growth and competitiveness. Drew Robb February 2, 2026 4 Min Read ALAMY As AI workloads proliferate, data center power needs are surging. Grid interconnections, siting decisions, and financing models are being rethought to meet timelines in months rather than years. Across utilities, developers, and operators, a single imperative has emerged: deliver power faster. Texas as a Test Bed Texas has become a leading hub for large-scale data centers, surpassing long-time strongholds like Northern Virginia. Speaking at PowerGen International in San Antonio on January 20-22, 2026, Elaina Ball, Chief Strategy Officer at CPS Energy, noted that regional grid capacity is being stretched by accelerating demand. Summary_AI: Montana's NorthWestern Energy has signed agreements with three new data centers, which will nearly double the state's electricity demand, prompting concerns about rising costs and environmental impact. PRINT THIS PAGE Karin Kirk Data centers are a hot topic. They’re drawing large amounts of electricity from the stressed grid, and their thirst for ever-more power is daunting. Where will this energy come from? Who will pay for it? We have a lot of questions, and rightly so. What we don’t have is answers. NorthWestern Energy has signed agreements with three new data centers so far. The energy demand from those three deals will require nearly double the amount of electricity that NorthWestern currently serves to Montana. NorthWestern will get to build new, expensive (and likely polluting) power plants to serve those data centers, and it all sounds like a great deal for NorthWestern’s shareholders. But what about the rest of us? Well, that remains a big question. Last month, an opinion column by the Frontier Institute’s Tanner Avery argued that we don’t need to worry about data centers. They will bring jobs and attract new local businesses, it will all be great! The author dismissed well-founded worries about water use and rising electricity bills as a “fear-driven narrative.” I admire the author’s optimism, and for that matter, his bravery in sticking up for data centers. But in many ways, I agree with Mr. Avery. Data centers shouldn’t raise prices for the rest of us. Rules should require that data centers pay their fair share so we don’t get stuck with the bill. But here’s the problem. So far in the U.S., those things are not happening. Many people are paying more for their electricity use while data centers score big discounts. Title: New legislation aims to regulate data centers to protect utility costs - WCIV
Summary_AI: South Carolina lawmakers, led by Sen. Ed Sutton, are advancing Senate Bill 784 to regulate data centers amid growing concerns over their energy consumption, water usage, and tax incentives. The bill aims to protect residents and utilities by requiring data centers to cover infrastructure costs, report water usage, eliminate tax breaks, and engage in public notification and hearings. This legislation, supported across party lines, seeks to address environmental impacts and financial burdens on communities as data center developments, such as the proposed 859-acre campus in Colleton County, face increasing public opposition. The bill is currently under Senate Judiciary Committee review with hopes for a floor vote soon.
Article_Text:
CHARLESTON, S.C. (WCIV) — As large-scale data centers continue to expand across South Carolina’s Lowcountry, state lawmakers are moving to impose new regulations aimed at protecting residents, utilities and natural resources.
Sen. Ed Sutton, a Democrat representing District 20, is sponsoring Senate Bill 784, which would place new requirements on data centers seeking to build or operate in the state.
The legislation comes as communities raise concerns about energy demands, water usage and tax incentives tied to technology-intensive facilities.
“[Data centers are] a big energy hog, a big water usage,” Sutton said. “It’s imperative that the General Assembly step in here and put these guardrails in place.”
The bill would do three main things: protect energy and utility costs for residents, require public reporting of water usage, and eliminate tax incentives currently available to data centers.
The legislation is meant to close gaps in the state’s 2025 energy bill by ensuring communities are not left to absorb the financial or environmental impact of large facilities, said Sutton.
“I strongly believe we need better guardrails in place,” Sutton said. “Quite frankly, our natural environment here in South Carolina is not being respected. As state legislators, we have a duty to make sure it’s protected.”
Under the bill, utilities would be required to charge data centers for any additional infrastructure costs needed to connect them to the power grid. Data centers would also be required to sign a minimum 15-year contract and cover any remaining costs if they terminate service early.
The legislation would also require data centers to report both surface and groundwater usage — a move Sutton says is critical for protecting fragile ecosystems.

Beaver Dam, Menomonie, Kenosha, and Janesville are the four municipalities identified by Wisconsin Watch as entering into such NDAs with prospective data center developers. Meta has been identified as the developer behind one of the largest proposed projects in Beaver Dam, which is planning an investment valued at more than $1 billion. City officials were bound by a strict NDA while negotiating with the company, which operated locally through an entity named Degas LLC. The NDA meant that officials could not name Meta publicly or discuss key aspects of the project until negotiations were substantially complete, despite the scale of the project and its potential infrastructure demands. Title: As electricity demand soars, wind energy development grinds to a halt in Iowa | The Gazette
Summary_AI: Wind energy development in Iowa, a leading U.S. state for wind power, has nearly stalled due to local opposition, the phaseout of federal tax credits, and regulatory slowdowns under the Trump administration. The Shenandoah Hills Wind Farm, completed in 2024 with 54 turbines generating 214 megawatts, exemplifies the challenges faced, including community resistance, legal battles, and political divisions. Despite its economic benefits—projected $234 million in payments to landowners and local governments—the broader industry faces declining new capacity additions, threatening the U.S.'s ability to meet rising electricity demand from sectors like AI data centers and to achieve climate goals. The slowdown in onshore wind development signals a critical obstacle for clean energy expansion nationally.
Article_Text:
SHENANDOAH, Iowa—First viewed from a pickup truck driving south on U.S. 59, the wind turbines seem small and delicate.
The newly constructed wind farm is a matter of pride for Gregg Connell, the man behind the wheel of the truck. He is a former two-term mayor of this southwestern Iowa city and an official with the local chamber of commerce.
The turbines are “beautiful,” Connell said, looking out the driver’s side window. “You’re capturing energy, you’re helping the environment.”
But the sight of a new wind farm is increasingly rare, even in Iowa, a state that trails only Texas in electricity production from wind.
Wind energy development has all but ground to a halt in the face of community opposition, a phaseout of federal tax credits and the Trump administration’s actions to slow the approval of federal permits.
Some of the administration’s most notable moves on wind power have been attacks on offshore development, including a stop-work order in December that halted construction on five projects. But land-based wind projects — often described in the industry as “onshore wind” — are a much larger contributor to the nation’s electricity supply and are struggling to make even modest gains. Without robust growth in onshore wind, the United States will be severely limited in its ability to build enough power-generation capacity to meet rising demand and combat climate change.
One of Iowa’s few new developments, the Shenandoah Hills Wind Farm, illustrates what it takes to build in the current political and regulatory environment.
The developer, Chicago-based Invenergy, faced years of blowback from residents who raised concerns about damage to the visual landscape along with concerns about potential harm to human and animal health and effects on property values. The opposition campaign dragged on into court cases and led to one county supervisor losing his re-election bid.
Though controversial, Shenandoah Hills still had the advantage of obtaining all of its federal permi

Title: Pushback against Microsoft's proposed data center in Dorr Township continues - WWMT
Summary_AI: Residents of Dorr Township, Allegan County, Michigan, are actively opposing Microsoft's proposed data center development, citing concerns over environmental impact, increased water and power usage, noise pollution, and declining property values. The opposition, organized by the group "We the People," includes local lawmakers and industrial experts who criticize state tax exemptions for data centers and question the environmental claims made by Microsoft. Article_Text:
ALLEGAN COUNTY, Mich. — The statewide movement against data centers continued Saturday in Allegan County, where Dorr Township residents encouraged others to say no to Microsoft's proposed development.
They kept up the pressure against the plans with a meeting featuring lawmakers, industrial experts and residents, all of whom believe it would destroy the small farming community
Around 100 people came to the St. Mary’s Visitation Hall with one message.
“We’re going to save Pure Michigan," Paula Caltrider said. "We’ll go to every town, we’ll go to every council, we’ll speak up.”
The group "We the People," organized on Facebook, held the meeting to answer questions and discuss ways to stop artificial intelligence computing on their land.
Expressing fears of increased water and power usage and a constant hum from its power generators, each speaker felt outside companies were taking advantage of the state’s natural resources.
“They want our Great Lakes," Chris Potter said. "They want our water. They don’t care how they do it.”
State Rep. Jim DeSana (R - MI- 29) and Rep. Joseph Fox (R - MI - 101) spoke about their opposition to data centers at the statehouse.
Companies like Microsoft point to carbon sequestering and closed-loop water cooling systems as environmentally friendly solutions for their development.
DeSana called these proposals a "scam,'" blaming the state government for passing a tax exemption on data center equipment in 2024.
“Who's going to want to come by your house if you live within a half mile to a mile of a data center?" DeSana asked. "‘You know, I'm going to go up to the next township.’ It's going to affect your market value.”
Two industrial hygienists were also called to the podium to share their thoughts about the health and environmental risks of data centers.
They predict companies will abandon data centers in the years to come as they start to build them underground, and leave communities like Dorr Township with the costs of cleanup.

Title: A Solution for the Fast Data Center Connection Challenge - Energy Institute Blog
Summary_AI: The article addresses the growing challenge of rapidly connecting data centers to the power grid amid rising electricity demand, particularly from data centers, projected to increase 17% by 2030 in the U.S. It highlights a hybrid solution combining new solar-plus-storage installations sited near existing gas plants, enabling reliable, lower-emission power supply with faster grid interconnection. This approach leverages surplus interconnection services to export excess solar energy to the grid and uses gas plants as backup during low solar output, offering competitive costs ($60-138/MWh) and significant emissions reductions. Strategically, this model supports data center growth while mitigating grid strain and environmental impact, with strong potential in sunbelt states like California, Texas, and Florida to meet all projected U.S. data center power needs through 2030.
Article_Text: HOME ABOUT CONTRIBUTORS ENERGY INSTITUTE BLOG Research that Informs Business and Public Policy POSTED 24 HOURS AGO BY DUNCAN CALLAWAY A Solution for the Fast Data Center Connection Challenge Lean in on America’s fastest growing source of electricity supply. This week’s blog is co-authored with Emilia Chojkiewicz and Amol Phadke. Taking projections of all utilities in the U.S. together, peak demand is expected to grow by 17 percent by 2030, with half of that demand growth coming from – you guessed it – data centers. At the same time, Americans are fired up about the cost of electricity. As Severin pointed out last week, in some parts of the country, electricity prices are rising fast, and there is plenty of finger pointing at data centers. With long wait times to get grid access, data centers developers are feeling the squeeze, too. Hyperscalers are discovering that “move fast and break things” doesn’t apply to interconnection queues. Image credit: http://www.vpm.org Most solutions to this squeeze fall into two broad categories: Build new “firm” generation, or use the existing grid better. (Actually, there is another: put solar panels on data centers and launch them into sun-synchronous near earth orbit, but we’re going to wait a few years to blog on that one.) There is plenty of talk and planning in the “build new firm generation” camp. There are also a lot of challenges to doing this quickly and safely. On the planning front, enhanced geothermal companies have signed power purchase agreements with a few different big tech companies. Last year, Chevron announced plans for a 2.5 GW gas generation facility in the Permian Basin to meet rising artificial intelligence demand. And as Meredith wrote about two weeks ago, nuclear generation has gotten significant attention as a potential source of clean data center power. A bill that would require data centers companies to pay more for their electricity will likely come up for a vote in the full Delaware House of Representatives after it moved out of committee on Wednesday. Sponsored by Rep. Frank Burns (D-Pike Creek), House Bill 233 comes in response to fears that a surge of energy-hungry data center projects could strain the power grid and raise residents’ bills. The two bills follow a stalled attempt by New Castle County to regulate the burgeoning data center industry, which has grown rapidly in recent years as investors chase the expected riches of artificial intelligence. Title: Pike Township data center stalls after backlash as Indy plans regulations - IndyStar
Summary_AI: American Tower Corp. has withdrawn its proposal to build a 20,000-square-foot edge data center in Pike Township, Indianapolis, following strong opposition from over 1,200 residents concerned about the facility's impact. The decision comes amid ongoing local government efforts to introduce zoning regulations and tax incentive restrictions for data centers, aiming to address community concerns and prevent residents from bearing infrastructure costs. This withdrawal marks the second of four proposed data center projects in Marion County to be halted due to public backlash, reflecting growing tensions between tech infrastructure expansion and local community priorities in Indianapolis.
Article_Text: News Sports Things To Do Opinion Real Estate Advertise Obituaries eNewspaper Legals INDIANAPOLIS Pike Township data center stalls after backlash as Indy plans regulations Jordan Smith Indianapolis Star Feb. 2, 2026Updated Feb. 3, 2026, 2:32 p.m. ET Corrections & Clarifications: An earlier version of this story misstated the size of the proposed data center. It was planned to be 20,000 square feet. Another data center bites the dust. The company planning to build a data center in Pike Township withdrew its proposal Feb. 2 following intense opposition from hundreds of residents swept up in a statewide backlash to the energy-intensive facilities. American Tower Corp. planned to build a 20,000-square-foot unmanned facility called an "edge data center" — a smaller hub that's typically located near internet users for faster processing — in northwest Indianapolis near the intersection of West 79th Street and Township Line Road. Neighbors objected to the site, where the phone and internet infrastructure company already owns a cell tower, because many homes sit on the periphery. The company withdrew the plan after learning that Indianapolis officials are considering zoning changes to better regulate data centers, a spokesperson told IndyStar in a statement. American Tower wants to let this process play out before moving forward with any proposal. "Although we are not advancing this particular proposal, our decision reflects a desire to ensure that any future initiatives align with both local connectivity needs and community priorities," the statement said. "Our existing telecommunications services at the site will continue without interruption, and we remain committed to operating responsibly and collaboratively in the community." Data centers face backlash across Indianapolis The withdrawal means that two of the four data center projects proposed in Marion County last year have been nixed after residents fought back. Title: Community forum planned to address proposed Prologis data center - The Newnan Times-Herald
Summary_AI: A community forum is scheduled for Saturday at Arnco Sergeant Elementary School in Coweta County to address concerns about the proposed Prologis data center. Hosted by Citizens for Rural Coweta, the event will feature a panel discussion with experts on environmental and infrastructure issues, including a Chattahoochee Riverkeeper representative, followed by a public Q&A session. The forum aims to challenge Prologis' projections on noise, water usage, environmental impact, and revenue, responding to community frustrations after a recent Prologis open house left many questions unanswered. This event reflects growing local scrutiny over the data center's potential environmental and construction impacts, highlighting the strategic importance of community engagement in large-scale data center developments.

Title: The Trump Administration exempts new nuclear reactors from environmental review - NPR
Summary_AI: The Trump administration has exempted new advanced nuclear reactors from the environmental review requirements of the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA), citing their inherent safety features. This policy change, announced by the Department of Energy in early 2026, aims to accelerate the construction of experimental reactors at sites like Idaho National Laboratory, with a goal to have at least three operational by July 4, 2026. The reactors, funded by private capital including Silicon Valley investors, are intended to power energy-intensive AI data centers. While supporters argue the exemption streamlines innovation and reflects the reactors' safety, critics warn that the lack of rigorous environmental and safety reviews poses potential risks to public health and the environment. This move strategically supports rapid nuclear industry growth but raises concerns about transparency and regulatory oversight.

Idaho National Laboratory The Trump administration has created an exclusion for new experimental reactors being built at sites around the U.S. from a major environmental law. The law would have required them to disclose how their construction and operation might harm the environment, and it also typically required a written, public assessment of the possible consequences of a nuclear accident. The exclusion announcement comes just days after NPR revealed that officials at the Department of Energy had secretly rewritten environmental, safety and security rules to make it easier for the reactors to be built. The Trump administration has secretly rewritten nuclear safety rules The Department of Energy announced the change Monday in a notice in the Federal Register. It said the department would begin excluding advanced nuclear reactors from major requirements of the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA). The act calls on federal agencies to consider the environment when undertaking new projects and programs. The law also requires extensive reporting on how proposed programs might impact local ecosystems. That documentation, known as an environmental impact statement, and a second lesser type of analysis, known as an environmental assessment, provide an opportunity for the public to review and comment on potential projects in their community. Title: Voltus and Octopus Energy US partner on VPPs to support data center power demand
Summary_AI: US virtual power plant operator Voltus and Octopus Energy US have partnered to develop flexible capacity portfolios supporting data center power demand across PJM, MISO, New York, and California grids starting in 2026. The collaboration combines Voltus’ load flexibility platform with Octopus’ consumer engagement technology to deliver Flexibility-as-a-Service and Bring Your Own Capacity solutions, leveraging residential devices like smart thermostats and EVs to enhance virtual power plant resources. This initiative aims to accelerate data center development by providing an alternative interconnection pathway, addressing growing grid load needs while creating a multi-billion dollar opportunity in demand-side flexibility.

Title: Data center moratoriums pile up in Michigan. No one knows if they'll work
Summary_AI: Michigan is experiencing a surge in data center development proposals, prompting at least 19 communities to enact or consider moratoriums to pause new projects amid concerns over environmental impact, energy use, and local control. The state approved significant tax breaks in late 2024 to attract these facilities, which require large land areas and substantial energy, fueling debate between economic benefits and community opposition. While local governments seek to regulate or halt developments, state leaders remain divided, with some lawmakers pushing for reforms or moratoriums and others, including Governor Whitmer, opposing restrictions to preserve job growth and investment opportunities. The controversy has become a key political issue ahead of Michigan’s 2026 gubernatorial election, reflecting broader tensions over balancing technological expansion with environmental and community interests.

Title: Virginia lawmakers look to address data center water use - WDBJ7
Summary_AI: Virginia lawmakers are considering legislation to limit water consumption by data centers, prompted by Google's plan to draw about 8 million gallons daily from Carvins Cove for a new facility in Botetourt County. Proposed measures include requiring new data centers to install rainwater harvesting systems to reduce reliance on local water sources and mandating public disclosure and reporting of water usage. These efforts aim to address environmental concerns amid drought conditions and growing data center water demands, with potential impacts on regional water resource management and infrastructure planning.

(WDBJ) - Virginia lawmakers are considering legislation to limit water consumption by data centers. It comes as Google plans to draw an estimated 8 million gallons per day from Carvins Cove for a facility it’s building in Botetourt County. Nearly 30 data center-related bills have been introduced in the General Assembly, with several targeting water use concerns that have emerged across the state. The proposed Google facility would use at least 10 times more water than the current largest customer of the Western Virginia Water Authority, the Coca-Cola bottling plant, according to estimates from local advocacy groups. A bill from Senator Creigh Deeds would require new data centers to install rainwater harvesting systems on at least 30% of their roofs. The collected water would help cool the facilities, reducing demand on local water sources. “There are already data centers in South Carolina owned by Google that are already using this type of technology. It’s doable. 30% is reasonable,” said Tyrone Jarvis, a rainwater harvest designer and installer, who spoke during a Senate subcommittee meeting about the bill. Sarah Baumgardner, Public Relations Director for the Western Virginia Water Authority, said the Water Authority has experience with rainwater collection through its reservoir system and noted that even small surfaces can collect significant amounts of water. “The people that have done that have been surprised how much water you can get off of an even small surface, just through one gutter lead or, you know,

Title: Shapiro seeks stricter standards for data center growth in Pa. - WGAL
Summary_AI: Pennsylvania Governor Josh Shapiro has proposed the GRID (Governor’s Responsible Infrastructure Development) standards in his 2026 budget to regulate the rapid growth of data centers in the state. The proposal mandates that data centers must build or fully fund their own power sources, preserve water, ensure transparency with local communities, hire Pennsylvania workers, and meet state standards for expedited permitting. This initiative aims to balance economic growth with resource management, garnering bipartisan support as a way to encourage responsible development without halting expansion. The plan also reflects concerns about the impact on the electric grid and includes AI-related protections for children.

Advertisement In the governor's proposal, Shapiro laid out “GRID,” short for the Governor’s Responsible Infrastructure Development standards. Title: More than 60 data center-related bills to be considered by Virginia's legislature this year
Summary_AI: Virginia’s legislature is set to consider over 60 data center-related bills during the 2026 session, addressing issues such as electricity regulation, planning restrictions, and tax policies. Key proposals include a temporary moratorium on new data center interconnections until July 2028, stricter local noise and water usage regulations, and adjustments to tax exemptions, which previously cost the state over $1.6 billion in lost revenue in fiscal year 2025. These legislative efforts reflect growing political concern over the economic and infrastructural impacts of data center expansion, positioning Virginia at the forefront of a nationwide push to balance industry growth with public interest and utility affordability.

Title: Proposed legislation in Tennessee requires data centers to pay for increased energy costs
Summary_AI: Tennessee legislators have proposed the "Data Center, Artificial Intelligence, and Clean Transition Tariff Accountability Act," requiring data centers to pay for increased energy and utility infrastructure costs to prevent residents and small businesses from bearing the financial burden. Sponsored by Memphis Senator London Lamar and Representative Karen Camper, the bill targets the state's 60 data centers, including two in Shelby County supporting xAI supercomputers. The legislation aims to ensure transparency through mandatory reporting and fair cost-sharing, reflecting concerns about the growing energy demands of data centers and their impact on local communities. This move could influence how data centers operate financially and engage with local governments in Tennessee.

Title: Voltus, Octopus launch 'bring-your-own-capacity' partnership | Latitude Media
Summary_AI: Voltus and Octopus Energy U.S. have partnered to expand Voltus’ virtual power plant (VPP) portfolio by integrating residential consumer devices in key U.S. markets starting in 2026, including PJM, MISO, New York, and California. This "bring-your-own-capacity" model allows data centers to finance VPP programs that deliver distributed energy resources directly to utilities, helping to address capacity constraints and bypass lengthy interconnection queues. Strategically, this partnership enhances data centers' ability to meet reliability requirements and manage energy flexibility, positioning Voltus as a key player in emerging grid reliability auctions and supporting the growing energy demands of AI infrastructure.

MAEVE ALLSUP FEBRUARY 3, 2026 Photo credit: Bardhok Ndoji Virtual power plant provider Voltus is doubling down on the bring-your-own-capacity paradigm for data centers, via a partnership announced today with Octopus Energy U.S. Starting in 2026, Octopus will supply Voltus — which has previously specialized in commercial and industrial demand response — with aggregations of residential consumer devices in key markets including PJM, MISO, New York, and California. Voltus launched the first bring-your-own-VPP program for data centers last fall, in partnership with data center infrastructure developer Cloverleaf Infrastructure. Under that framework, hyperscalers can finance a VPP program in a capacity-constrained region, and then Voltus will deliver the megawatts from distributed resources directly to the local utility to offset the data center’s load. This is an evolution of the contracting frameworks that hyperscalers have long used to transfer accredited capacity directly into a utility’s portfolio to satisfy reliability requirements. It’s designed specifically to help data centers bridge the capacity gap they’re facing in the next five years, Voltus CEO Dana Guernsey told Latitude Media in September. This out-of-market financing structure has the potential to both bypass the yearslong interconnection queues for new generation, and, according to Octopus Energy U.S. CEO Nick Chaset, could help developers mitigate the significant hurdle presented by community opposition. It’s also a workaround for the ongoing debate over what it means for data centers to be flexible, and whether grid operators can or should consider that potential in their planning. As Guernse

Title: Next-Generation Nuclear Power Can Meet Data Center Energy Demand—If Regulations Allow It
Summary_AI: Next-generation small modular reactors (SMRs) present a promising solution to meet the massive, continuous energy demands of AI data centers, as demonstrated by partnerships involving Meta, Google, and startups like Aalo Atomics. These compact nuclear plants offer steady, lower-capacity power with potential environmental benefits by reducing greenhouse gas emissions linked to data center growth. However, regulatory challenges persist due to outdated licensing frameworks designed for traditional large reactors, though the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission is working to modernize rules with a new, technology-inclusive framework expected by 2027, which could accelerate SMR deployment and transform data center energy sourcing.
Article_Text: Yahoo Finance Mail Sign in Next-Generation Nuclear Power Can Meet Data Center Energy Demand—If Regulations Allow It Next-Generation Nuclear Power Can Meet Data Center Energy Demand—If Regulations Allow It · Reason.com Ed Tarnowski Tue, February 3, 2026 at 2:40 PM MST 3 min read In this article: StockStory Top Pick META -1.31% GOOG -2.48% OKLO +14.42% AI data centers have become flashpoints in public debates over energy costs and grid capacity, leading some politicians to call for heavy restrictions on their operation. Sen. Bernie Sanders (I–Vt.) is calling for an all-out moratorium. Advanced nuclear power could provide a solution to these challenges—unless federal regulations get in the way. Data centers create near-perfect conditions for the burgeoning small modular reactor (SMR) industry: enormous, concentrated energy demand that must be met 24/7. These compact nuclear power plants are designed to deliver steady power at a lower capacity than traditional reactors, so they could be uniquely positioned to meet that demand. Indeed, Meta recently struck 20-year agreements to purchase electricity from three nuclear power plants operated by a single provider, while also joining Oklo and TerraPower in an SMR development project. Google and Kairos Power have plans to deploy an advanced nuclear plant tied to the Tennessee Valley Authority's grid. Aalo Atomics is a Texas-based startup developing factory-built SMRs designed to operate on or near large energy users—exactly the model that AI infrastructure demands. "We're talking about tens of gigawatts of power in the next five years," Aalo Atomics CEO Matt Loszak told Arena last year. "Each gigawatt is like a whole city! When in history have like 20, 30 cities been created in a matter of five or ten years?" In addition to meeting data centers' power demands, SMRs could reduce these facilities' environmental footprint. Title: As another data center stalls, Indiana lawmakers try to sweeten the pot - Axios Indianapolis

The move seeks to balance Indiana’s attractiveness for data centers, given its affordable land and tax benefits, with local governments' financial pressures following recent property tax reforms, potentially accelerating data center development in the state.

Last month, the town board passed a resolution to hold a public hearing on the matter on Feb. 11, after multiple companies contacted community leaders to see if they could use the former paper mill site which closed in 2023. The move comes as a flurry of new data centers have stressed rural communities throughout the country. Data Centers have caused well-water contamination in Mansfield, GA, stressed water resources in Utah and Alaska, increased energy bills in Baltimore, and spurred complaints of air pollution from community organizations in Memphis. Towns throughout North Carolina and the Appalachian region have taken pause. According to Canton Mayor Smathers, this decision to consider banning data centers and cryptocurrency mining was prompted by repeated inquiries from tech companies about the use of the town’s former paper mill site, which laid off over a thousand people and caused the town to lose a large chunk of tax revenue. Smathers said he worries that the potential consequences caused by these data operations outweigh the benefits of additional tax revenue. Title: Rethinking Energy: How Data Centers Are Adapting to Grid Constraints
Summary_AI: Data center operators are increasingly adopting on-site power solutions, such as gas turbines and fuel cells, to address grid constraints exacerbated by the rapid growth of AI workloads. A Bloom Energy report highlights that by 2030, one-third of hyperscalers and colocation providers plan fully self-powered campuses, driven by utility power-delivery delays of up to two years in key U.S. hubs like Northern Virginia and the Bay Area. This shift includes a move toward direct current (DC) distribution architectures for improved efficiency. While technical, regulatory, and infrastructure challenges remain—such as natural gas capacity limits and transformer supply delays—the trend marks a strategic transformation in data center energy management to ensure reliable, scalable power for future AI demands.

Summary_AI: Google projects capital expenditures (capex) of $175-185 billion for 2026, nearly doubling its 2025 spend of $91.4 billion, driven primarily by investments in AI infrastructure and data centers. Google Cloud’s revenue surpassed $70 billion annual run rate, boosted by a new partnership with Apple making Google its preferred cloud provider. This aggressive capex increase signals Google’s strategic commitment to scaling AI capabilities and cloud services to meet growing demand and maintain competitive positioning.

Title: Scoop: Hawley circulating data center costs bill - Axios
Summary_AI: Senator Josh Hawley (R-Mo.) is circulating the Guaranteeing Rate Insulation from Data Centers Act, aiming to prevent data centers' energy costs from increasing consumer electricity bills. The bill mandates new data centers to use power sources separate from the grid, prioritizes consumer access to electricity, and requires transparency and a 10-year transition period for alternative energy adoption. This legislation responds to rising political pressure to hold tech companies accountable for surging power demands amid AI data center growth, marking a significant Republican initiative alongside other bipartisan bills addressing data center energy use and pricing.

Josh Hawley (R-Mo.) is shopping a bill around Congress that would ensure the cost of data centers' energy use is not passed on to consumers, per a summary of the bill obtained by Axios. Why it matters: Hawley, who's been outspoken about AI dangers and consumer costs, wants to codify President Trump's pledge last month to not let the proliferation of AI data centers make electricity bills rise. Driving the news: Hawley is circulating and plans to introduce a bill called the Guaranteeing Rate Insulation from Data Centers Act. Per the summary, it would: Guarantee no increase in consumer utility prices from data centers. Require new data centers to use energy "from power generation sources that are separate from the grid." Guarantee consumers first priority on the electrical grid. Require a 10-year off-ramp for data centers to find alternate power sources and transparency requirements around utility usage. Between the lines: There is major political pressure on both sides of the aisle to make tech companies pay for surging power costs, rather than passing them onto customers. The big picture: Hawley would be among the first congressional Republicans to push legislation on this topic. There are other bills in the Senate addressing data centers and AI, including the Power for the People Act from Sen. Chris Van Hollen (D-Md.) and the DATA Act from Sen. Tom Cotton (R-Ark.). Title: Bill calls for Oklahoma landowners to vote on data center sites
Summary_AI: Oklahoma Representative Molly Jenkins introduced HB3095, the "Rural Landowners Protection Act," which would require landowners within five miles of proposed data center sites—and other infrastructure projects like wind farms and battery storage—to vote on their approval. The bill mandates that no permits or authorizations for such projects can be issued without two-thirds landowner approval, aiming to give rural landowners significant control over large-scale developments. This legislation could substantially impact the expansion of data centers and renewable energy infrastructure in Oklahoma by introducing a new layer of local consent and regulatory hurdles.

The bill would give the power of rejection to adjacent landowners. The Republican from Coyle filed HB3095 as a means of giving landowners near proposed data center sites the power of voting on such projects. The measure cited six kinds of projects that would be targeted under the bill including those relating to wind electric power generation, relating to electric bulk power transmission when used primarily for wind energy transmission, relating to recyclable material merchants and materials recovery facilities when used for wind-turbine component recycling or decommissioning, relating to data processing, hosting, and computing infrastructure providers when used for operation of a large-scale data center, relating to grid-scale battery storage installations and activities relating to carbon capture, carbon recapture, carbon-sequestration, or related scientific or industrial operations. Her bill would give the power to vote to owners of land within 5 miles in all directions of the “proposed covered facility or infrastructure.” She identified a landowner as the person or persons identified in county land records. The bill also puts restrictions on the government agencies involved and force government officials to provide a written notice to the landowners within 30 days of receiving notice of application for one of the projects. The project, developed by White Rose Partners, is planned to include up to three data center buildings, with construction expected to start in 2027 and finish by 2029. Kyle Schmidt, president of Project Sand Springs Alliance - a group campaigning against the project, said after the meeting: “It’s not even so much about the data center as it is the way they went about annexing the land, keeping it a secret, hiding things from the public, possibly hiding things from the council so they would vote a certain way - that’s really what it was about. “It's government overreach without citizen input, and when they were called out for it, they decided their best course of action was to plow ahead and just railroad it over the citizens.” A lawsuit remains ongoing regarding the land annexation. According to the info page dedicated to the project, the actual campus will cover less than 10 percent of the site, and 30-40 percent is planned to be preserved as vegetative or green open space. Title: New report: AI data centers could recycle excess heat - WVXU
Summary_AI: A new report by Reimagine Appalachia highlights the potential for AI data centers in Ohio and Appalachia to recycle excess heat generated during their cooling processes to benefit local communities and industries. This waste heat, ranging from 77 to 194 degrees, can be repurposed for agricultural, manufacturing, and municipal uses, such as heating greenhouses, public buildings, and supporting crop growth, thereby reducing greenhouse gas emissions, improving air quality, and lowering water use. Companies like SAIHEAT and EnergiAcres are already exploring such circular systems, which could help data centers provide long-term community benefits despite their limited job creation. The report encourages developers and communities to prioritize sustainable practices like waste heat recycling to mitigate environmental impacts as data center development expands.
Article_Text: Menu Show Search Donate WVXU All Things Considered NEXT UP: 6:00 PM Latino USA All Streams Environment How could AI data centers benefit surrounding communities? New report says recycle the excess heat Ideastream Public Media | By Zaria Johnson Published February 5, 2026 at 7:46 PM EST Facebook Twitter LinkedIn Email LISTEN • 1:05 SNEHIT PHOTO / Shutterstock A data center under construction in New Albany, Ohio on Nov. 22, 2025. A coalition of groups and individuals based in and focused on Appalachia is sharing some ideas about ways AI data centers popping up in Ohio and elsewhere can use the excess heat they generate to benefit nearby communities and industries. The report from Reimagine Appalachia finds that the heat can be reused in surrounding communities and industries. According to the report, doing so could cut greenhouse gas emissions, improve air quality and reduce overall water use. "The less energy you use, the less fossil fuels that we burn," report author Debbie Stine said. "Given that most of Appalachia we're still relying on fossil fuels, particularly natural gas, this is a good thing." Waste heat is generated as a byproduct of AI data centers cooling process and is typically released into the atmosphere, Stine said. Depending on the cooling process used and the time of year, waste heat can range between 77 and 194 degrees, according to the report. Waste heat recycling can be used in agriculture, manufacturing and chemical processing industries and others, Stine said, and is already being explored in Ohio. She cited SAIHEAT, which bills itself as "a global distributed computing power operator" that helps "energy owners address issues of local energy consumption and efficient resource utilization." It's U.S. office is in Marietta. "This is a company that also captures waste heat and they use it for greenhouse heating. And also they are looking at expanding it to residential commercial heating applications, fishery and poultry." In Mansfield, EnergiAcres

Summary_AI: Microsoft announced plans to build 15 new data centers in Mount Pleasant, Wisconsin, under its "Community First AI Infrastructure Initiative," which aims to address community concerns about energy costs, water use, job creation, and tax revenue. Earlier this year, the company announced its 'Community First AI Infrastructure Initiative' to tackle these issues. "The structure really depends on communities coming to the conclusion that the benefits outweigh the costs," said Wisconsin native and corporate vice president Rima Alaily. Alaily explained that the initiative would address water use, new jobs, tax revenue, and energy costs. "We're committing that we're going to pay our own way when it comes to electricity," said Alaily. Despite this commitment, state lawmakers emphasized the importance of laws to protect ratepayers. "Data centers will increase our energy burden regardless…trying to codify the protections for those ratepayers is critical," said Rep. Darrin Madison (D - Milwaukee). Alaily mentioned that water at Microsoft facilities would be managed through a 'closed loop system,' a method lawmakers had advocated for in recent data center legislation. "That has dramatically reduced the water we have used," said Alaily. Lawmakers praised Microsoft's initiative but urged ongoing transparency between data centers and communities. "It's coming in their backyard, and at the end of the day they should know what it is," said Rep. Nate Gustafson (R - Omro). Title: Proposed Florida Legislation Creates New Regulatory Framework for Hyperscale Data Centers
Summary_AI: Florida lawmakers have introduced several bills during the 2026 legislative session to regulate hyperscale data centers, focusing on development, operation, and environmental impacts. Key proposals include Senate Bill 484, which sets a regulatory framework for large-scale data centers (50 MW+), preserving local control and establishing utility tariff rules, and House Bill 1007, which imposes stricter siting, water use, and economic incentive restrictions on hyperscale centers (25 MW+). These bills, effective July 1, 2026, reflect contrasting approaches between the Senate's more moderate regulation and the House's restrictive stance, potentially reshaping Florida's data center industry by balancing growth with environmental and community concerns. The session concludes March 13, 2026, with ongoing committee reviews influencing final outcomes.

Title: How Data Centers Are Learning to Flex for the Power Grid - Environment+Energy Leader
Summary_AI: The article discusses the DCFlex initiative, launched in late 2024, which involves major industry players like Google, Oracle, NVIDIA, and others collaborating to test and scale real-time energy load flexibility in data centers across the U.S. and Europe. With AI-driven demand increasing data centers' electricity consumption—potentially reaching 9% of U.S. demand by 2030—DCFlex pilots innovative strategies such as workload shifting, cleaner backup fuels, and integrated control systems to reduce grid strain. These efforts aim to transform data centers from passive energy consumers into active grid assets, enabling smoother grid operation, faster interconnections, and reduced market risks, thereby supporting sustainable growth in the data center industry amid rising AI energy demands.

That’s the premise behind DCFlex, a cross-industry initiative now transitioning from concept to deployment, with field tests underway in operational facilities across the U.S. and Europe. Launched in late 2024, DCFlex brings together major players like Google, Oracle, NVIDIA, Compass Datacenters, Constellation, and National Grid, along with smaller tech companies such as Emerald AI and PADO AI. Coordinated by EPRI, the goal is pragmatic: test and scale real-time load flexibility in live environments, not just labs. The need is growing clearer. According to EPRI, U.S. data centers could make up nearly 9% of national electricity demand by 2030. As AI expands, operators face mounting scrutiny—can they grow responsibly without overwhelming the grid? Projects now underway include a mix of load-shifting, cleaner backup fuels, and integrated control systems for compute and HVAC. In Chicago and Virginia, engineers are trialing ways to shift workloads between regions during congestion. In Texas, a coordinated AI platform manages both servers and cooling systems to deliver grid services with minimal operational disruption. Meanwhile, in Dallas, a separate track is exploring renewable diesel—specifically hydrotreated vegetable oil—as an alternative to conventional backup fuels. Title: Why standards must keep pace with rising data center voltages - DCD
Summary_AI: The data center industry is shifting toward higher-voltage direct current (DC) architectures, specifically 800 VDC and beyond, to meet the growing power demands driven by AI workloads. However, existing safety and performance standards lag behind these innovations, creating risks and operational challenges. Companies like Legrand and Starline are actively working with standards bodies such as UL and NEMA to update regulations, including raising DC busway voltage limits and developing DC-specific safety protocols, to ensure reliable, scalable, and safe deployment of high-voltage systems. This proactive standards development is critical for enabling the industry’s transition to more efficient, higher-voltage power infrastructures while maintaining safety and interoperability.

Higher-voltage DC promises real benefits: reduced transmission losses, greater efficiency from fewer power conversions, improved power density, and streamlined distribution for hyperscale environments. Title: Regulators Reaffirm Approval For Microgrid Generator, Data Center In Tucker County
Summary_AI: The West Virginia Air Quality Board reaffirmed approval for Fundamental Data’s Ridgeline Facility, a microgrid generator and data center in Tucker County, despite objections from environmental groups. The facility, permitted last summer by the West Virginia Department of Environmental Protection, received a "minor pollution source" designation, which critics argue is insufficient given its size and potential emissions. While the board approved most objections, it mandated additional stack testing to ensure compliance with pollution limits once operational. This decision supports Fundamental Data’s plan to boost West Virginia’s technology economy and job creation, though environmental advocates remain concerned about the facility’s impact on local air quality and ecosystems.

Thursday’s decision came following a two-day hearing held in December at the DEP headquarters in Charleston where the citizen groups argued for the release of all redacted information in the site’s air quality permit application. Title: To Harness Power Plants' Unused Capacity, States Can Turn to 'Surplus Interconnection Service'
Summary_AI: The article discusses the Surplus Interconnection Service (SIS), an innovative approach allowing new power generation or storage resources to connect to the grid more quickly by sharing unused interconnection capacity of existing power plants. Authorized by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission in 2018, SIS enables faster deployment of low-cost solar and battery projects, bypassing the traditional multi-year interconnection queue. This method enhances energy delivery, grid reliability, and reduces land use and transmission costs, offering states a strategic tool to meet rising electricity demand efficiently while benefiting consumers and energy providers.
Article_Text: SKIP TO CONTENT Open main menu Search Menu TOPICS: STRENGTHEN STATE GOVERNMENT PROJECTS: ENERGY MODERNIZATION To Harness Power Plants’ Unused Capacity, States Can Turn to ‘Surplus Interconnection Service’ Innovative approach can quickly boost electricity production to benefit consumers Authors: Jenny Netherton Xcel Energy’s Comanche Solar facility and Comanche Generating Station stand alongside one another in Pueblo, Colorado. Surplus Interconnection Service resources are colocated with existing power plants and share grid access. RICK WILKING REUTERS Article February 9, 2026 2 min SHARE As demand for electricity continues to rise in the United States, policymakers and utilities are increasingly concerned about how to meet consumers’ needs cost-effectively, especially because it takes an average of five years for new energy projects to connect to the grid after they are proposed. But one underused approach, called Surplus Interconnection Service (SIS), can shortcut that wait by allowing new power generation or storage to be added in months instead of years. That’s because SIS resources are colocated with existing power plants and share their access to the grid. The issue with adding new power sources Interconnection is the approval process that new power plants must follow to connect to the grid and deliver power to consumers. It is often time-consuming due to both the volume of requests and the time grid operators need to conduct detailed reviews of projects. Proposed power plants join an interconnection “queue” where they wait their turn for grid operators to study the plant, determine and construct needed upgrades to power lines and infrastructure, and approve the plant for a maximum number of megawatts (MW). This process safeguards the grid but takes years. However, many existing power plants do not always operate to their maximum capacity, leaving a MW surplus that can be filled with new generation or storage. Title: Data centers are scrambling to power the AI boom with natural gas | Grist
Summary_AI: Data centers powering the AI boom are increasingly relying on natural gas, with tech giants and developers deploying gas turbines and engines across the U.S., especially in Texas, to meet surging electricity demands. Notable deals include Boom Supersonic’s $1.25 billion agreement to supply 29 jet-engine gas turbines to Crusoe for OpenAI data centers, and Meta’s use of over 800 mobile mini-turbines in El Paso. This rapid build-out of fossil fuel infrastructure, including over 1,000 gigawatts of global gas-fired power projects, risks significant climate impacts, potentially adding 44 million metric tons of CO2 emissions by 2030. While on-site natural gas power offers flexible, reliable energy, it may lock in long-term fossil fuel dependence, challenging clean energy goals amid the AI-driven data center expansion.

Founded in 2014, the company set out to make air travel dramatically faster — up to twice the speed of today’s passenger jets — while also aiming for a smaller environmental footprint. For years, Boom has focused on developing the high-performance engine technology needed to sustain supersonic flight. Though the company has not yet debuted its revolutionary jet, last year it identified a new and potentially lucrative application for its novel technology: generating electricity for the data centers powering the artificial intelligence boom. Many of these data centers want the kind of flexible, around-the-clock energy associated with combined-cycle natural gas turbines. These heavy-duty machines burn gas to spin turbines and generate electricity, then capture the associated heat and use it to spin the turbines some more. As far as fossil fuel generation goes, they are among the most efficient options for dispatchable baseload power. But with demand for these turbines surging and supply increasingly tight, developers are turning to creative alternatives. The upshot of all this creativity is clear: Much of the data center build-out is poised to be powered by natural gas — and the climate consequences that come with it. Title: Judge denies major motion from City of Sulphur Springs in data center lawsuits - KLTV.com
Summary_AI: A judge has denied the City of Sulphur Springs' motion for summary judgment in ongoing lawsuits over the construction of a large AI data center in East Texas, a project developed in partnership with MSB Global. The legal dispute involves Vistra Corp. and Luminant Mining, former landowners who claim a deed restriction prohibits energy production or storage on the site, which the city contests as vague and unenforceable.
