The City of Sulphur Springs has paired up with developer MSB Global to build what would be one of the largest AI Data Centers in the country. Construction has halted amid ongoing lawsuits surrounding the property. On one side are the lawyers for Vistra Corp. and Luminant Mining, the former owners of the site where the data center would be built. Luminant and Vistra say city officials breached a deed restriction included when the land was given to the city as the company faced bankruptcy. The restriction states that the land cannot have any means of energy production or storage. City Attorney Nate Smith has continually argued the deed was deceptive. The Texas Attorney General’s Office has sided with the city, saying Luminant has put these “restrictive” deeds all over the state. Ken Paxton signed an amicus brief in support of the City’s case this fall. Retired 173rd District Court Judge Dan Moore of Henderson County is overseeing the case after 62nd Judicial District Judge Gary Young recused himself from the case. Today, Judge Moore denied the City’s Motion for summary judgment. Smith was asking Moore to find that the deed was either null and void or unenforceable against the city. The City argued the deed has taken away their governing power, unable to do anything with the land. Superconductors offer a ‘lossless’ advantage, making power transmission more efficient. To make this possible, we need to rethink traditional power designs and how datacenters move electricity today. Title: Cincinnati considers pause on new data center permits to draft necessary regulations
Summary_AI: Cincinnati leaders are proposing a three‑month pause on new data center permits so the city can study their impacts and create proper zoning rules, since data centers are not currently defined in the zoning code. Officials are concerned about rising utility and water costs and the fact that data centers use large amounts of land while creating relatively few jobs. The pause is intended to help align future development with the city’s economic and planning goals.
Article_Text:
CINCINNATI (WKRC) - Cincinnati leaders are advocating for a three-month pause on issuing new data center permits to allow the city to study the issue and develop appropriate zoning measures.

The city already hosts nearly a dozen data centers, which often resemble ordinary buildings, according to Data Center Map.

Cincinnati leaders are advocating for a three-month pause on issuing new data center permits to allow the city to study the issue and develop appropriate zoning measures. (WKRC)

With demand for these centers increasing, the city council's Housing and Growth Committee held a special meeting to discuss the proposed pause.

Cincinnati City Council Member Mark Jeffreys explained the need for the pause.

"They're kind of warehouses and offices, how we treat them, but they're obviously not. And so, we're saying, 'Hey, let's pause.'"




The temporary halt will enable the city to draft necessary zoning regulations, as "data center" is currently absent from the zoning code. Jeffreys emphasized the importance of examining the impact of data centers on utility costs and water rates.

"If our utility costs are going up and water rates are going up and if that is in part driven by these data centers, even in the city of Cincinnati, that's helpful to know not only from a zoning standpoint but also for other potential policy choices that we may want to make," he said.

Another concern for Jeffreys is the job creation impact of data centers.

"The average data center, I think, has 20 to 30 jobs on several acres. And so, anywhere that we have several acres, that's not the best use. What we would rather have is several hundred jobs on there. So, I think that certainly is a factor," said Jeffreys.

Jeffreys believes business development in the city should prioritize job creation, as the city relies on earnings tax from people working within its limits.

Title: Geothermal Energy at Scale: A New Paradigm for Data Centers
Summary_AI: Innovative enhanced geothermal systems (EGS) technology is transforming geothermal energy into a scalable, clean, and reliable power source for data centers, overcoming traditional geographic limitations. With advancements like horizontal drilling and hydraulic fracturing, EGS enables engineered underground heat reservoirs, expanding geothermal’s potential across broader regions, particularly in the Western US. The Fervo Energy Cape Station project in Utah, launching 100 MW in 2026 with plans for 400 MW by 2028, exemplifies this shift, offering modular, 24/7 renewable power that aligns with data centers’ needs for stable, sustainable, and cost-predictable energy. This development positions geothermal as a strategic solution to meet growing data center power demands while supporting decarbonization and operational reliability.

Geothermal Energy at Scale: A New Paradigm for Data Centers Innovative geothermal technologies are unlocking clean and reliable energy at scale, offering data centers a sustainable solution to meet growing power demands. Javad Asghari,James Blake February 10, 2026 5 Min Read A geothermal power station in Iceland.IMAGE: ALAMY Securing a reliable power supply at scale continues to constrain data center developers’ ability to meet their tenants’ needs in a timely manner. In response, data center developers are increasingly re-examining their usual suite of energy solutions to overcome this constraint. Recent innovations in geothermal drilling technology have positioned geothermal energy as an increasingly viable solution in data centers’ energy supply landscape. Understanding Geothermal Energy Geothermal is a long-established energy resource that is clean, reliable, and capable of delivering 24/7 power. It harnesses the Earth’s naturally occurring heat, whether from existing underground steam and hot water or through engineered circulation in hot rock, and converts that thermal energy into electricity at the surface using conventional turbine technology. Historically, the development of geothermal power facilities has been limited to regions with rare geologic conditions that create shallow, highly permeable underground heat reservoirs. As a result, geothermal has remained a relatively niche resource, with only ~4 GW of installed capacity in the US today, the vast majority of which is concentrated in California and Nevada. Title: Town of Canton to consider pause on data centers, crypto mining after public hearing
Summary_AI: Canton, North Carolina is moving toward a temporary moratorium on data centers and cryptocurrency mining as interest grows in redeveloping the former paper mill site. Town leaders want time to study environmental, infrastructure, noise, and water impacts, noting that data centers bring limited permanent jobs relative to their heavy energy and water demands. While some residents see data centers as an economic opportunity after the mill’s closure, the mayor says the town must protect its natural resources and long‑term community character.
Article_Text:
CANTON, N.C. (WLOS) — Town leaders in Canton are moving toward a moratorium on data centers and cryptocurrency mining as interest grows in redeveloping the former paper mill site.

Canton Mayor Zeb Smathers said multiple companies have contacted the town in recent months, asking preliminary questions about infrastructure capacity, including water and electrical hookups.

Smathers said those inquiries prompted town leaders to schedule a public hearing and begin the process of placing a temporary halt on data centers while officials study potential impacts.

“We’ve had a lot of interest about data centers, everything from crypto technology to server facilities,” Smathers said. “Our ordinances just aren’t in a place to have those conversations right now.”

The Canton Town Board is expected to consider a moratorium following Wednesday night’s public hearing, which would pause any data center development while the town evaluates environmental, infrastructure and quality-of-life concerns.




Smathers acknowledged the promise of new tax revenue but said job creation is relatively low compared to the strain these facilities can place on communities.

CANTON SETS PUBLIC HEARING FOR MORATORIUM ON DATA CENTERS TO ADDRESS COMMUNITY CONCERNS

“There’s no doubt an effect on our energy, our water, our natural resources and sound,” he said. “That’s what led to this potential moratorium.”

Data centers require massive amounts of electricity and cooling water to operate rows of servers that power everything from cloud storage to artificial intelligence. Rural communities across western North Carolina have increasingly raised concerns about noise, environmental impacts and long-term infrastructure demands.

“We’re not going to sit around until it’s too late and say, ‘I wish we’d done something,’” Smathers said. “This emerging technology has consequences that are dangerous enough to our people, our natural resources and the character of our mountains to say no, at least for now.”

In Farmington, developers received local approval for a 2.5 million square foot "hyperscale" data center on land once reserved for a new school, as well as a former golf course. "If we don't pay attention to what's going on and advocate for ourselves, no one else is going to," said Kathy Johnson, a Farmington resident and founder of the Coalition for Responsible Data Center Development. "I think money is driving this and quality of life is not being considered. We have to do that. Quality of life matters to the people that live here and it matters to me." Data centers aren't new to Minnesota; a 2011 law passed by state lawmakers created incentives for major tech companies to move servers here. Their footprints, however, aren't nearly as large as what's being proposed in Farmington. Even Meta's $800 million project in Rosemount, at roughly 700,000 square feet, pales in comparison. Mo Feshami, another Farmington resident who works in tech, said he first supported the idea of bringing a new data center to Dakota County. "I thought if a data center comes in there won't be as many houses or cars or strain on the school system - until I realized this is a hyperscale data center," he lamented. "The data centers I used to work in, at most they used 10 megawatts. This is 708 megawatts. We used to have it in one or two floors of a large commercial building. Summary_AI: U.S. Senators Josh Hawley and Richard Blumenthal introduced the Guaranteeing Rate Insulation from Data Centers (GRID) Act on February 11, 2026, aiming to prevent data centers from increasing electricity costs for American consumers. The bill mandates that new data centers use independent power sources separate from the U.S. grid, with a 10-year transition period for existing centers, to avoid passing energy costs onto consumers amid rising demand driven by AI growth. The legislation also requires transparency in data centers' electricity usage and prioritizes consumer access to the grid, addressing concerns over the strain data centers place on energy infrastructure and household utility bills.

Over the last several years, data center construction has taken off, creating enormous strains on our energy infrastructure. And this AI-fueled boom shows no signs of slowing down—in fact, data centers’ demand for power is expected to double over the next four years. Title: Planning commission greenlights massive 280-acre data center in Wayne County - ClickOnDetroit
Summary_AI: Van Buren Township, Michigan approved preliminary site plans for a massive 1‑gigawatt data center on more than 280 acres after a lengthy public debate. Developers and utilities said the project will not raise residential water or electric bills and is expected to become the largest taxpayer in the township, despite community concerns about scale and the undisclosed end user. The project qualifies as “use by right” under Michigan zoning law and now moves to the Township Board for the next approval step.
Article_Text:
WAYNE COUNTY, Mich. – Following an extensive five-hour debate, the Van Buren Township Planning Commission granted preliminary site approval for a major data center project in a 5-2 vote.

The facility will be located east of Haggerty Road and north of I-94 North Service Drive.

The one-gigawatt facility, which will occupy over 280 acres, is comparable in scale to the recently announced AI data center project in Saline Township.

The development plan includes three data processing and computer center buildings, one administrative office building, and one network service building.

A seven-acre substation will also be part of the complex and will require separate site approval.

Developer Panattoni has implemented several measures to address community concerns.

Adam Kramer, head of data centers for Panattoni Development, assured residents about water usage, stating that the facility will use the Great Lakes Water Authority transmission system rather than the residential distribution system.


“These are large, oversized systems today that have an incredible amount of excess capacity,” Kramer said. “So it won’t impact water pressure, it will benefit water rates, and it helps to increase the energy efficiency of this project.”

DTE Director of Policy and Regional Affairs Dan Mahoney also gave a presentation at the meeting, assuring commissioners and residents that residential utility bills would not be affected by the project’s operations.

The project has garnered mixed reactions from community members. While some residents expressed support for the economic benefits, others raised concerns about the facility’s undisclosed end user.

“Scale matters, intensity matters, community impact matters,” said resident Laura Kurkowski during public comments. “We also know this project requires massive electrical infrastructure, including substations on the property. If we don’t know which company is the buyer, we cannot be sure what they will do with that in the future.”

Title: Data Center Tariff Bill Advances - NJBIA
Summary_AI: New Jersey's Assembly Telecommunications and Utilities Committee advanced Bill A-796, which mandates electric public utilities to create tariffs for data centers with a monthly demand of at least 100 megawatts. The bill aims to protect non-data center ratepayers from increased electricity costs and incentivizes energy efficiency through heat capture technologies. Initially opposed due to aggregation concerns, the bill was amended to include only data centers with true 100-megawatt demand, leading the New Jersey Business & Industry Association to adopt a neutral stance. This legislation strategically balances ratepayer protection with fostering innovation and growth in the state's data center industry.
Article_Text: Skip to content SIGN IN Business Resources Advocacy Events & Networking News & Media Get Started AI, TECHNOLOGY & INNOVATION Data Center Tariff Bill Advances Published February 12 - Updated February 13 SHARE A bill that would require electric public utilities in New Jersey to develop and submit to the Board of Public Utilities (BPU) a tariff for certain data centers advanced in the Assembly Telecommunications and Utilities Committee today. Bill A-796 (Bailey, D-3) applies to data centers that have a maximum monthly demand of at least 100 megawatts. Among the provisions, the bill requires the tariff to: Ensure that non-data center ratepayers are protected from any increased costs that result from increased electricity demand caused by data centers; Incentivize data centers to develop and utilize methods to increase energy efficiency through the use of technologies that capture and utilize the heat produced by the data center. Require electric public utilities to apply the tariff to each data center located in their service area one year after the bill’s enactment. NJBIA initially opposed the bill because it allowed facilities to be aggregated based on common ownership, control, or other criteria — even if those facilities operate separately and individually fall well below the 100-megawatt threshold. “For example, a company could have had 10 facilities across New Jersey, each using between eight and 10 megawatts per month,” said NJBIA Policy Research Analyst Jack Ramirez. “Individually, none of those facilities would qualify as a ‘large load’ customer. The bill, however, was amended to clarify that only data centers with a true 100 megawatt-per-month demand are included. NJBIA is now neutral on the bill. “The amended changes help clarify the policy and better protect ratepayers while still allowing innovation and business growth to move forward,” Ramirez said. Title: Council inches closer to data center ordinance - LaGrange Daily News
Summary_AI: The LaGrange City Council is advancing discussions on a new ordinance to regulate data centers, focusing on size definitions, zoning restrictions, buffer zones, noise limits, and utility requirements. The proposed rules would restrict hyperscale data centers (over 100,000 square feet) to heavy industrial zones, which are limited and mostly occupied, necessitating zoning changes and public hearings. Smaller data centers would be allowed in general industrial zones but not near residential areas, with enhanced buffers and noise controls to mitigate impact. Tommy Murphy The LaGrange City Council again discussed potential updates to its Unified Development Ordinance to regulate data centers, but an extension of the moratorium is likely. There is consensus on some aspects of the draft ordinance, but questions remain. Recommendations from the Planning Commission for the new ordinance address five issues: defining exactly what a data center is, where they can be located based on size, buffer requirements, separation requirements, and noise survey requirements. The proposed ordinance defines hyperscale data centers as those larger than 30,000 square feet. Cryptocurrency mining and incidental data centers, like the one operated by the city on Church Street, are also included in the ordinance. The current plan is to only allow hyperscale data centers in heavy industrial districts. The city only has two heavy industrial districts, which are both occupied, so any potential new hyperscale data center would require a zoning change, which would also trigger a public hearing. The debate among the council comes from where smaller data centers can be and if a special use permit should be required to trigger a public hearing. The council appears to be in consensus that smaller data centers should only be allowed in general industrial zoning and not near any residential zoning. Councilwoman Darby Pippin questioned whether the definition of hyperscale should be at 30,000 square feet. Title: Google secures 1 GW solar deal as Texas data center load surges | Electrek
Summary_AI: TotalEnergies signed its largest-ever U.S. renewable power deal, agreeing to supply Google with 1 GW of solar energy to support expanding Texas data centers through two new solar projects starting construction in 2026. The long‑term PPAs underscore how hyperscalers are contracting directly for new generation to secure power as data‑center demand surges. While the deal boosts long‑term clean energy supply, it doesn’t address near‑term grid strain or Google’s 24/7 power needs without added storage.
Article_Text:
TotalEnergies just landed a massive solar deal to power Google’s expanding Texas data centers. The company signed two long‑term power purchase agreements (PPA) totaling 1 gigawatt (GW) of capacity, or about 28 terawatt‑hours of electricity over 15 years.

The power will come from two projects now in development: the 805‑megawatt Wichita solar farm and the 195‑MW Mustang Creek site. Construction is slated to start in Q2 2026.

It’s the largest renewable PPA TotalEnergies has ever signed in the US and a clear signal of how fast data‑center power demand is rising. Rather than tapping existing supply, the contracts are tied to brand‑new generation that will feed regional grids.

The agreements also stack on top of 1.2 GW in separate PPAs recently secured by Clearway, the California‑based developer that’s 50% owned by TotalEnergies. Those projects will supply Google facilities across ERCOT, PJM, and SPP power markets.


Marc‑Antoine Pignon, TotalEnergies’ vice president of US renewables, said deals like this show how major tech companies are increasingly seeking customized clean‑power supply as they scale energy‑intensive digital infrastructure. He added that colocated projects can help address land and power‑availability constraints associated with large data centers.

Will Conkling, Google’s director of clean energy and power, said bringing new generation online is critical as the company expands, adding that the projects will increase reliable and affordable electricity supply across the region.

TotalEnergies says its US portfolio now stands at about 10 GW of gross renewable and storage capacity, including 5 GW in ERCOT and 400 MW in PJM.

Electrek’s Take
This is a big deal, but it’s also worth keeping the scale in perspective. A 1 GW solar PPA is pretty dang hefty, but Google data centers with 24/7 demand will still tap into the grid (which means gas in Texas) at night unless battery storage is added alongside these solar farms.

The El Paso City Coun­cil is tak­ing a hard look at data cen­ters crop­ping up in the area.

Dur­ing its Tues­day, Feb. 17, meet­ing, the City Coun­cil will con­sider a plan to develop a data cen­ter policy frame­work, includ­ing an assess­ment of all cur­rent 380 agree­ments for area data cen­ters.

The new policy would require data cen­ters to coordin­ate with city and county gov­ern­ments, as well as util­ity pro­viders, “to assess infra­struc­ture capa­city, long term demand, and sys­tem impacts asso­ci­ated with data cen­ter devel­op­ment,” accord­ing to a doc­u­ment accom­pa­ny­ing Tues­day’s agenda.

Addi­tion­ally, the plan would put in place a “Hire El Paso First” pro­vi­sion, cla­rify zon­ing and land use clas­si­fic­a­tions, require cor­por­ate and social respons­ib­il­ity, and “provide clear, con­sist­ent, and pre­dict­able stand­ards for data cen­ter devel­op­ment.”

The city man­ager would be required to return within 60 days with a plan of action. Dur­ing that devel­op­ment phase, a pause would be placed on new data cen­ter projects.

In 2023, U.S. data cen­ters accoun­ted for nearly 4.5% of all the elec­tri­city used in the coun­try, accord­ing to data from the Con­gres­sional Research Ser­vice. That num­ber could reach as high as 12% over the next two years.

The same research found that U.S. data cen­ters con­sume nearly 164 bil­lion gal­lons of water each year.

City Coun­cil to mull new Deck Plaza design fund­ing
Also on Tues­day, the City Coun­cil is expec­ted to approve an advanced fund­ing agree­ment with the Texas Depart­ment of Trans­port­a­tion for the design of struc­tural ele­ments related to the pro­posed Down­town I-10 Deck Plaza.

The total amount of the agree­ment is just over $10.6 mil­lion, with the city pitch­ing in $584,320 from TIRZ No. 5 funds.

“This AFA allows for the design of the struc­tural ele­ments, not the (Deck Plaza) itself or the amen­it­ies, to pro­ceed,” a doc­u­ment accom­pa­ny­ing Tues­day’s agenda states. “It ens

Title: Flexible Data Centers Soon To Run On Batteries And Renewables - Forbes
Summary_AI: The article discusses the growing demand for data centers and the challenge of powering them sustainably with renewable energy due to their continuous electricity needs and the intermittent nature of wind and solar power. It highlights the strategic importance of long-duration energy storage technologies, such as pumped hydro and flow batteries, which can provide power for eight hours or more, enabling data centers to maintain reliability and support grid stability. Co-locating these storage systems with data centers offers a faster, flexible solution to meet energy demands, reduce reliance on fossil fuels, and advance decarbonization efforts, positioning data centers as active participants in a cleaner, more resilient energy grid.
Article_Text: BUSINESS ENERGY Flexible Data Centers Soon To Run On Renewables And Energy Storage ByAnna Broughel,Contributor. I write about green energy tech that will change your life. Follow Author Feb 16, 2026, 07:00am ESTFeb 16, 2026, 01:36pm EST Data centers and renewables can work especially well when combined with long-duration energy storage. GETTY Data centers are now driving demand growth at a pace that rivals electrification in transportation and buildings. The scale of this growth has prompted an urgent question for policymakers, utilities, and technology companies alike: can data centers be powered sustainably, without undermining grid reliability or climate goals? For now, the short answer is no. "We’re not able to power data centers solely with renewable energy right now,” says Brian Nelson, an electrical engineer at ABB who works on grid infrastructure and power systems. Data centers require electricity every hour of every day, and that requirement collides with the intermittent nature of wind and solar generation. Yet focusing only on whether renewables can meet demand misses the big opportunity to make data centers more sustainable. While a single technology cannot be replacing fossil fuels overnight, the focus should be on how flexibility is increasingly becoming the grid’s most valuable resource. Even a tiny amount of flexibility from a data center can unlock capacity for other large loads to come online. “Flexibility is going to become a form of currency,” Nelson notes. The good news is that limited demand shifting, combined with storage, can delay or eliminate the need for costly grid upgrades. On-site storage allows operators to maintain power quality, ride through disturbances, and continue operating during outages. Change in Paradigm Needed for Data Centers Wind and solar are now among the lowest-cost sources of electricity in many regions, but they are not dispatchable. When the sun goes down or the wind stalls, their power output drops. For residential

Title: Data Center Expansions Push the Sector Into New Territory - CommercialSearch
Summary_AI: JLL's year-end 2025 report highlights a major shift in the data center industry, with Texas poised to surpass Virginia as the world's largest data center market by 2030 due to abundant energy and business-friendly conditions. Currently, over 35 GW of data center capacity is under construction across North America, with 64% in emerging "frontier markets" like Texas, Tennessee, Wisconsin, and Ohio, driven by AI demand and hyperscaler expansion. Despite record-low vacancy rates of 1%, 92% of new capacity is preleased, fueling rent growth and underscoring strong, sustained demand. The sector is also advancing sustainability through renewable energy and innovative cooling technologies, addressing environmental concerns while navigating electrical grid limitations. This expansion signals a strategic industry transition from infrastructure buildout to long-term value creation.
Article_Text: News Property Types Business Markets Research Data Center Expansions Push the Sector Into New Territory By Scott Baltic • February 17, 2026 Data CentersAIDevelopmentFeatured More One region is on track to overtake Virginia as the world’s largest data center market. As AI demand, hyperscaler expansion and electrical grid limitations shape the explosive recent growth of data centers, these forces are relocating the front lines of the industry’s expansion, according to JLL’s North American data center report for the year-end 2025, released today. “Texas is positioned to overtake Virginia as the world’s largest data center market by 2030,” JLL reported. “Markets like Texas, Tennessee, Wisconsin and Ohio are experiencing explosive growth as companies seek abundant energy and business-friendly environments.” The report found that 64 percent of new data center construction is taking place in “frontier markets,” outside traditional hubs like Northern Virginia and Silicon Valley. In Texas, for example, 6.5 GW of capacity is under construction. Across North America, more than 35 GW of data center capacity is under construction, which JLL calls “an extraordinary volume by historical standards.” At the same time, individual projects are getting sharply larger; JLL is tracking more than 10 projects of 1 GW or larger, currently under construction. North America data center markets. Map, tables courtesy of JLL Meanwhile, vacancy has remained at a record-low 1 percent for two straight years, and 92 percent of that 35 GW construction pipeline is already preleased or committed. Currently, JLL is tracking 39 GW of active capacity across North America, roughly half of which is leased and the other half owned by hyperscalers. Title: Lumen lightens multicloud network management, expands intercity abilities - SDxCentral
Summary_AI: Lumen Technologies has launched a new Multi-Cloud Gateway service that enables enterprises to dynamically and privately manage multicloud connectivity across on-premises, data centers, and hyperscale cloud platforms via a programmable cloud fabric. Concurrently, Lumen expanded its metro data center network in 16 major U.S. markets, supporting speeds up to 400 Gb/s at key cloud data centers, aligning with its strategic focus on enterprise digital network services. Title: San Marcos City Council revisits divided $1.5B data center plan | kvue.com
Summary_AI: San Marcos city council is again considering a controversial $1.5 billion, nearly 200‑acre data center project after the Planning and Zoning Commission recommended rezoning the site for data center use. Supporters cite jobs and future tax revenue, while opponents raise concerns about water use, power demand, noise, and industrialization of a rural area. The council will hold a public hearing Tuesday, with a final vote possible on March 3.
Article_Text:
SAN MARCOS, Texas — A $1.5 billion data center proposal that has divided San Marcos residents is back before the city council on Tuesday, marking a pivotal step in a yearslong fight over growth, water use and industrial development.

The nearly 200-acre project, planned near Francis Harris Lane, stalled last year after council members failed to reach the supermajority needed to approve a rezoning request that would allow a large data center campus on land now mapped for future development and low-density uses. The setback sent the developer back to the Planning and Zoning Commission to restart the process.


Last month, the commission voted to recommend rezoning the property for data center use, clearing the way for the proposal to return to the council agenda. The project calls for a multi-building data center campus on roughly 200 acres off Francis Harris Lane.

Developers say the facility would rely on a closed-loop, water-based cooling system that would use up to 75,000 gallons of water each day and is designed to recycle that water rather than discharge it. They also say sound from the site would be limited to about 75 decibels at the property line through the use of enclosures and other noise-mitigation measures.

Supporters – including labor groups and some local officials – argue the data center could bring construction and tech-related jobs, along with millions of dollars in future property tax revenue for San Marcos.

Opponents, many of whom live near the proposed site, say the project would strain local water supplies, increase demand on the electric grid and accelerate the industrialization of a historically rural area.

Tuesday’s meeting includes a public hearing and a first reading of the rezoning ordinance tied to the data center plan, according to city notices. If the measure advances, a second and final reading is expected on March 3, when council could ultimately approve or reject the project.

City leaders see the project as a rare opportunity to diversify a heavily residential tax base and generate construction jobs, long‑term tax revenue, and limited permanent employment. Many residents oppose the plan, citing concerns over water and power use, utility costs, light and noise impacts, transparency, and proximity to a psychiatric hospital, and argue the economic benefits are overstated.

Title: Spartanburg County Council advances controversial data center tax break - GoUpstate
Summary_AI: Spartanburg County Council voted 5-2 to advance a tax incentive package reducing the property tax rate from 10.5% to 4% for Project Spero, a proposed $3 billion data center by New York-based TigerDC. Despite public opposition over environmental concerns and potential strain on the power grid, the project is expected to generate about $250 million in local taxes over 30 years, supporting local services including schools. The final vote is scheduled for March, with proponents emphasizing economic growth and tax benefits, while opponents highlight infrastructure and environmental risks.

The council has been considering tax incentives to support the project, led by New York-based company TigerDC. They passed second reading of an ordinance that would bring TigerDC's property tax burden from the state's regular 10.5 percent rate to 4 percent. The vote was 5-2. Title: Caledonia Twp. pausing data center proposals for 6 months - WZZM13.com
Summary_AI: Caledonia Township, Michigan, has imposed a six-month moratorium on new data center proposals starting January 1, 2026, to address community concerns and develop clear design standards. The township boards plan to involve residents in defining zoning requirements and may require special use permits for future data centers to enhance local control. This pause reflects broader regional debates over data centers' environmental and resource impacts, with the moratorium subject to extension and reconsideration in June 2026.

Author: Sarah Edgecomb, 13 ON YOUR SIDE Staff Published: 7:10 AM EST February 17, 2026 Updated: 7:10 AM EST February 17, 2026 CALEDONIA, Mich. — Caledonia Township is pausing plans on any future data centers following a leadership meeting Monday night. At a joint Board of Trustees and Planning Commission meeting, township leaders agreed to place a six-month moratorium on data center proposals. During that time, the boards will work to implement design standards for any potential data centers with community input. Much of Monday's debate was about how data centers have changed over the years and how to best address residents' concerns. "We all have a different idea in our head of what a data center is. So I think there needs to be more definition in our requirements as to what the trigger points are, and I think that also relies on some input from the community, because we can't just say no," said one board member. "We have to say 'this is what you can do' and set limits. But we just can't say no arbitrarily. So we have to define this problem, I think, and make it something that we can put into our zoning, our ordinances and defend." The township boards are considering making data centers get special use permits that would give the community more control over their applications and approval. That plan will be discussed more during the moratorium. The pause dates back to Jan. 1, and will be reconsidered in June. No new data center applications will be accepted during that time. Summary_AI: Unilever has signed a five-year contract with Google Cloud to become its primary cloud infrastructure provider, supplanting Microsoft Azure, which previously handled most of its cloud operations. The partnership will focus on deploying Google’s Vertex AI to enhance AI-driven marketing, customer engagement, and enterprise applications, creating a unified, AI-first digital backbone. This strategic shift supports Unilever’s AI-driven transformation, aiming to increase agility and actionable insights across its global operations, while maintaining Microsoft tools for productivity.

The new deal with Google – which will see deployment of the cloud provider’s Vertex AI – will add to existing Google cloud provision at the company and supplant a Microsoft Azure deployment, which was described as providing “the bulk of its off-premise estate” when the partnership was announced in 2023. Unilever will continue to use Microsoft workplace productivity tools such as M365 and Copilot, but according to a Google spokesperson, “Google Cloud will become the destination for Unilever’s consolidated cloud and data platform”. According to Google’s Alex Rutter, EMEA managing director for artificial intelligence (AI), the collaboration will focus on transitioning key enterprise applications and data to Google Cloud, and building on the use of AI to enhance marketing functionality, in particular in customer-facing applications and using agentic AI. Rutter said: “The deployment will include building next-generation capabilities for future ‘agentic commerce’, where the consumer’s shopping journey is increasingly shaped by AI. Title: Sen. Jacque: Charlie Berens, Senator Jacque testify on banning secret AI data center deals
Summary_AI: Senator André Jacque and comedian Charlie Berens testified in support of Wisconsin Senate Bill 969, which aims to ban the use of non-disclosure agreements (NDAs) in the development of AI data centers. The bill responds to recent secretive billion-dollar data center projects in Wisconsin communities like Beaver Dam and Menomonie, where NDAs limited public awareness and input. SB 969 mandates transparency by prohibiting NDAs and requires local governments to reject projects that violate these rules, ensuring community involvement and safeguarding taxpayers from undisclosed risks associated with large data center developments.

With the rapid growth of artificial intelligence and other data-intensive technologies, big tech companies have dramatically increased efforts to build large data centers across the country. Title: Google Taps Geothermal Power for Nevada Data Centers
Summary_AI: Google has partnered with Ormat Technologies and NV Energy to supply up to 150 MW of geothermal power to its Nevada data centers, supporting its renewable energy goals. The agreement, pending approval from the Public Utilities Commission of Nevada in late 2026, will utilize the Clean Transition Tariff framework to ensure cost coverage and grid reliability. Commercial operations are expected to begin by 2028, with further geothermal projects planned through 2030, positioning geothermal energy as a reliable, carbon-free power source to meet growing AI-driven electricity demands in the tech sector.

James Walker,Senior Editor, Data Center Knowledge February 17, 2026 2 Min Read IMAGE: ALAMY / DATA CENTER KNOWLEDGE Google is advancing its renewable energy strategy in Nevada with a long-term partnership involving Ormat Technologies, a Reno-based geothermal developer, and NV Energy, the state's primary public utility. The agreement will support Google’s operations in the state with up to 150 MW of new geothermal capacity. Under a power purchase agreement (PPA) between Ormat and NV Energy, the partners intend to use the Clean Transition Tariff (CTT), a framework designed to help utilities and large energy users invest in clean, reliable electricity while allocating cost to participating customers. The partnership is subject to approval by the Public Utilities Commission of Nevada, which is expected to review the arrangement in the second half of 2026. If approved, Ormat plans to develop a portfolio of geothermal projects across Nevada. The first initiative is targeted to begin commercial operations as early as 2028, with additional projects anticipated through 2030. Title: Legislature considers shifting power line costs from residents to data centers
Summary_AI: Virginia’s General Assembly is considering legislation to shift certain power line and capacity auction costs from residential ratepayers to high-load customers, primarily data centers. Senate Bill 253, which passed the Senate and awaits House hearings, would allow the State Corporation Commission (SCC) to decide if data centers should cover distribution line costs and capacity auction expenses, potentially saving residential customers about $5.52 monthly. The bill also extends Dominion Energy’s undergrounding program for burying power lines to reduce outages, increasing its spending cap and extending it through 2033. Another bill, SB 339, directs the SCC to investigate whether residential customers are unfairly subsidizing transmission lines mainly used by data centers. These measures reflect growing legislative efforts to address the financial impact of expanding data center energy demands on Virginia’s power infrastructure and residential electricity bills.

(Photo by Sarah Vogelsong/Virginia Mercury) Multiple measures working through the General Assembly could put more of the costs that are driving up residential power bills onto data centers. They range from direct mandates to shift the costs of distribution lines and capacity auctions, to urging the State Corporation Commission to investigate whether ratepayers are subsidizing energy infrastructure that’s primarily used by data centers. Senate Bill 253 Senate Bill 253 by Sen. Louis Lucas, D-Portsmouth, cleared the chamber and now heads to the House for more hearings. The bill began as a proposal for Dominion Energy and Appalachian Power Company to extend weatherization programs for low-income households through 2038. Now, it contains a provision to allow the SCC to determine if it is in the public’s interest for high-load Dominion customers — namely data centers — to cover the cost of distribution lines to data centers and the capacity auction costs for the utility to purchase more power from PJM, the regional grid operator. In Dominion’s latest rate case, the utility created a new rate class called GS5 to encapsulate high-load users, which are mostly data centers and some manufacturers. If the bill passes, Gov. Abigail Spanberger signs it into law and the SCC approves the cost shift, it is estimated to save the average residential customers $5.52 on their monthly bills. “Working Virginians (will) understand that we are doing this to try to make sure that down the road… they will be able to save more money,” Lucas said. Title: Fiber Seen as Critical Infrastructure for AI-Driven Data Center Expansion
Summary_AI: A February 2026 report from the Fiber Broadband Association highlights fiber infrastructure as critical to the next phase of U.S. data center expansion driven by AI, cloud computing, and enterprise services. With traditional data center hubs facing power constraints, high land costs, and network congestion, developers are shifting focus to secondary metros, edge locations, and rural areas that offer strong fiber networks, reliable power, and available land. U.S. data center bandwidth purchases surged 330% from 2020 to 2024, and fiber route miles are projected to nearly double from 95,000 to 187,000 by 2029, supporting a network infrastructure market expected to reach $20 billion. This shift underscores fiber’s strategic role in enabling scalable, efficient data center growth amid evolving industry demands.

Summary_AI: Linn County, Iowa, has unanimously approved a comprehensive new zoning ordinance for data centers in its unincorporated areas, aiming to proactively manage potential developments amid growing interest, including from Google near the Duane Arnold Energy Center. CREDIT RICHARD PRATT Any company looking to build a large-scale data center in unincorporated areas of Linn County will now need to comply with the county’s new zoning ordinance amendment for such developments. Title: AEP Ohio says new data center tariff is working, critics aren't buying it
Summary_AI: AEP Ohio reports that its new data center tariff, implemented to require upfront financial commitments from data center developers, has significantly reduced projected new power demand from 30,000 megawatts to about 5,700 megawatts, helping the utility better plan infrastructure investments. The tariff filters out speculative projects by imposing monthly minimum billing and exit fees, ensuring only serious developments proceed. However, critics like the Ohio Manufacturers Association challenge AEP’s initial demand estimates as inflated and argue the tariff unfairly targets data centers, potentially increasing costs for other ratepayers; they have taken the issue to the Ohio Supreme Court. This dispute highlights tensions between utility planning, industry regulation, and the economic impact on Ohio’s energy consumers.

Washtenaw County Circuit Court Judge Julia Owdziej said the amount of work already done on the site near Ann Arbor and the billions of dollars spent made the case untimely. The resident, Kathryn Haushalter, argued in her motion to intervene in the lawsuit that township officials violated the Open Meetings Act by voting to settle a lawsuit with the landowners during a closed session and signing the consent judgment between the parties without public deliberation or board approval. She also argued that, as a nearby landowner, she has the right to intervene in the underlying zoning dispute. Haushalter's lawyer, Robby Dube, argued that because the vote to settle with the landowners was not recorded in the township's meeting minutes, it effectively didn't happen, since the township "spoke through its minutes." He said that previous rezoning efforts took place in public votes, so this went against how the township typically conducted business. Saline Township's lawyer, David Landry, argued that this was a simple clerical error. Haushalter filed her motion in the hopes of slowing the active construction of the Oracle and OpenAI's "hyperscale" data center that was announced on Oct. 30. Related Digital, a company tied to the real estate development firm of billionaire Stephen Ross' Related Cos., plans to build the data center on a sprawling 250-acre campus across three single-story buildings, each totaling 550,000 square feet. Title: Columbia Planning and Zoning Commission recommends data center regulations | Local
Summary_AI: The Columbia Planning and Zoning Commission unanimously recommended new regulations defining data centers and restricting their construction to industrial districts, pending approval by the City Council. This move, initiated after resident concerns in November 2025, aims to subject data center projects to higher scrutiny through public hearings and to mitigate potential negative impacts such as strain on local utilities. The city plans a two-step regulatory process, with further standards to be developed in collaboration with departments like Columbia Water and Light, ensuring clearer guidelines for data center development and distinguishing them from other industrial projects.
Article_Text: Skip to main content 31° Facebook Twitter YouTube Instagram Vimeo Log In Subscribe E-EDITION NEWS SPORTS OPINION OBITUARIES VOX VISUALS SPECIAL SECTIONS CLASSIFIEDS PREV NEXT TOP STORY Columbia Planning and Zoning Commission recommends data center regulations Nico Berlin, Columbia Missourian Feb 20, 2026 Facebook Twitter Email Facebook Twitter Email Print Copy article link Save The Planning and Zoning Commission unanimously recommended a city proposal that defines what a data center is and limits their construction to industrial districts. Data center projects would have to be reviewed on a case-by-case basis in public hearings if the Columbia City Council adopts the commission’s recommendation. Adopting the definition and zoning district is the first step in a two-step process of regulating construction of data centers in Columbia, according to a city staff report. The commission will still need to review and recommend standards of use that would help reduce possible negative outcomes associated with data centers, like strain on local utilities. Commission Chair Sharon Geuea Jones explained the rationale behind the city’s approach to data centers. “By defining them, we are actually subjecting them to a higher level of scrutiny than they would get without the definition,” she said. City staff combined preexisting definitions of data centers from similar municipalities across the country. The recommended definition excludes facilities that use data centers as a means to support primary operations, which city Senior Planner David Kunz said isn’t as common in other places. “We believe that to be necessary here because we know that IT infrastructure needs it, hospitals need it, internet service providers need it,” he said. “Basically, anyone who has a lot of digital data that they need to work with is going to need servers.” MISSOURI NEWS NETWORK Join the MNN Newsletter for a behind-the-scenes look at how the Columbia Missourian, KOMU, KBIA, MBA and Vox magazine build c

Title: Gov. Shapiro's GRID program addresses mounting concerns about data centers
Summary_AI: Pennsylvania Governor Josh Shapiro announced the GRID (Governor’s Responsible Infrastructure Development) program to guide the rapid growth of data centers in the state, emphasizing responsible development that balances innovation with community and environmental protection. The program mandates that developers generate or fund their own power, adhere to strict environmental and water conservation standards, engage transparently with local communities, and provide local jobs and benefits. With major investments like Amazon Web Services’ $10 billion data center campus in Salem Township and over a dozen projects underway in Northeastern Pennsylvania, GRID aims to position Pennsylvania as a national leader in AI and data center infrastructure while addressing public concerns about costs, environmental impact, and community involvement.

DAF exploring lease opportunities for data centers in Alaska
Summary_AI: The Department of the Air Force issued a Request for Information on February 17 to gauge commercial interest in developing large-scale data centers at three Alaska military locations: Joint Base Elmendorf-Richardson, Eielson Air Force Base, and Clear Space Force Station. The proposed facilities would include associated utility infrastructure, energy generation systems, fiber communications, and water-efficient cooling technologies. Interested vendors have until March 13 to respond, with the DAF using submissions to understand market capabilities and industry trends before initiating formal procurement. The initiative supports executive orders aimed at advancing AI innovation and domestic energy objectives.
Article_Text:
Published Feb. 23, 2026
Secretary of the Air Force Public Affairs
ARLINGTON, Va. (AFNS) --
The Department of the Air Force is gauging general interest in offering land for lease for large-scale commercial-use data center construction at Joint Base Elmendorf-Richardson, Eielson Air Force Base or Clear Space Force Station, all in Alaska.
The potential data centers would also include associated utility infrastructure and energy generation such as battery and fuel storage, distribution infrastructure, fiber communications and water-efficient cooling technologies.
The Request for Information, issued on Feb. 17, will help the DAF gather information from potential vendors before initiating a formal procurement process. Authorities will use the information to explore market capabilities, understand industry trends and make informed decisions about land use.
The DAF is taking this step in support of President Trump’s critical national defense and domestic objectives of Removing Barriers To American AI Innovation (Executive Order (EO) 14179), Declaring a National Energy Emergency (EO 14156), and Unleashing American Energy (EO 14154).
Interested parties have until March 13 to submit a response and should email afcec.EUL@us.af.mil indicating their intention to submit a response.

Some studies indicate that economic incentives have little influence on where businesses locate their projects or don’t deliver broad long-term benefits.

Title: Amazon plans $12 billion Louisiana data center buildout | WTVB
Summary_AI: Amazon announced a $12 billion investment to build data centers in northwest Louisiana, which will create 540 full-time jobs and support roles for specialized technicians. The company established a $400 million water infrastructure fund to address local environmental concerns and is partnering with Southwestern Electric Power Company for electrical infrastructure upgrades. This Louisiana project is part of Amazon's broader $200 billion capital expenditure plan for 2024, reflecting major tech industry investment in AI-driven infrastructure buildout.
Article_Text:
SAN FRANCISCO, Feb 23 (Reuters) – Amazon said it expects to invest $12 billion in Louisiana as part of a plan to build data centers in the Pelican State.
The Seattle-based online retailer said the ‌data center buildout, in northwest Louisiana, will create 540 full-time jobs, as well as additional jobs it claims will help support the facilities, such as electricians and heating, ventilation and air-conditioning technicians.
As part ‌of its fourth-quarter earnings report this month, ⁠Amazon announced an expected $200 ⁠billion this year in capital expenditures, a sharp increase from 2025’s $131 billion. Its shares fell after that announcement. They were down 9% year to date and fell another 2.7% on Monday to $204.47.
Tech firms’ outlays have skyrocketed as they work to meet growing demand for ⁠artificial intelligence software, computer chips and ⁠other infrastructure. The companies have committed at least $630 billion this year, and some investors fear a growing AI-led bubble.
Some communities have pushed back against data center ‌development due to strains on local power grids, as well as the need for huge amounts of water needed to the equipment.
Amazon said it will help offset any ⁠impacts of the Louisiana facilities with a $400 million fund aimed at water infrastructure in the area and said it will ⁠only use water ‌for cooling and other needs.
The company said ⁠it will assume “100% of the costs” of the ‌Louisiana data centers and is working with Southwestern Electric ⁠Power Company on any electrical infrastructure needed.
Amazon did not respond to a request for clarification about whether the $12 billion Louisiana commitment is part of the $200 billion it announced earlier this month.
(Reporting by Greg Bensinger; Editing by Chizu Nomiyama and David Gregorio)

On Saturday, Feb. 21, State Sen. Jeff Irwin, D, met with residents at the Washtenaw County Learning Resource Center for a community conversation focused on early literacy funding, rising electric bills and a proposed data center near Saline.

He supports funding for phonics-based literacy programs, teacher training and updated classroom materials. While new reading requirements for teachers take effect next year, Irwin noted that schools need support now.
“The training is so key,” he said. “We should fund the materials and the training so districts can get started.”
He added that he was encouraged that Gov. Gretchen Whitmer recently made literacy funding a central part of her education proposal.
Much of the meeting centered on electricity rates.
“This is not a 2026 problem,” Irwin said. “This has been building for a long time.”
He explained that years of delayed upgrades to power lines and equipment, followed by increased spending after major outages in 2023, have pushed costs higher. Those investments now appear on customer bills.
Irwin also pointed to a change in state law signed about 10 years ago by former Gov. Rick Snyder that altered how electric rates are calculated. Rates are now based largely on peak demand — the times when electricity use is highest. He said that change shifted more costs onto residential customers.
“That shift is why residential rates have gone up so much faster,” he said.

Title: State Regulation of Data Centers: Emerging Trends and Potential Legal Complexities
Summary_AI: More than 200 bills regulating data centers were introduced across U.S. states in 2025, with over 40 enacted into law, addressing energy procurement, water usage, environmental impact, siting, and labor standards as states move to manage externalities from data center growth. California has proposed legislation requiring special tariffs for large-load customers including data centers with demand exceeding 75 megawatts, while Texas and Minnesota have already enacted comparable measures. Governor Hochul in New York is pushing requirements for large-load projects to cover associated costs unless they demonstrate exceptional job creation or other benefits. These state-level regulatory efforts are expected to escalate in 2026 amid the absence of federal oversight, creating potential legal complexities for data center operators.
Article_Text:
Data centers are proliferating across the United States—and across the legislative and regulatory agendas of state and local governments. In 2025, there were more than 200 bills introduced across all 50 states aimed at regulating in-state data centers, which are the backbone of the ongoing artificial intelligence (AI) boom, and more than 40 of those bills were enacted into law.1 This legislation spans the policy gamut, addressing energy procurement and pricing, water usage, environmental considerations, siting issues, labor standards, national-security concerns, and more. We expect these efforts to continue to escalate in 2026, driven in part by the current absence of federal regulatory oversight (which we discussed in detail in a recent client alert) and possibly accelerated by reports of potential imminent action by the federal executive branch. As more of these bills become actual state laws, we anticipate some of these state measures will face important and novel legal challenges, the prospects of which will ultimately hinge on the specific facts at issue. However, general trends in state efforts at regulating data centers provide some initial insight into how the legal landscape may develop. Because of data centers’ pivotal role in the advancement of AI, how these trends unfold may have a significant impact on clients operating within AI and AI-adjacent industries.
Below, we identify bellwether examples of states and localities2 taking aim at aspects of data-center operations within their jurisdictions. We also offer some preliminary thoughts on potential legal issues raised by these laws, especially those that impose targeted burdens on data centers.
I. Key Trends in State Regulation of Data Centers
An initial survey reflects that while many states see opportunities in the data-center boom, they also are considering regulating data centers in an increasing array of ways, directed at perceived externalities created by data-center growth.

Summary_AI: Wells Fargo projects hyperscaler compute capacity will double to 98 gigawatts by 2027 from 49GW at end of 2025, driven by AI demand outpacing supply, with Alphabet, Amazon, and Microsoft controlling approximately 80% of total capacity. Article_Text:
Hyperscaler compute capacity will double over next two years, says Wells Fargo
Mon, February 23, 2026 at 12:46 PM MST 2 min read
Investing.com -- Wells Fargo said hyperscaler compute capacity is set to double to 98 gigawatts by 2027, as artificial intelligence demand continues to outstrip supply. This has also led to a surge in capital spending.
The brokerage estimates total capacity reached 49GW at the end of 2025, with Alphabet, Amazon and Microsoft accounting for about 39GW, or 80% of the total. It expects the three to maintain roughly 75% share through 2027.
Industry capacity additions are projected to rise to 22GW in 2026 and 27GW in 2027, exceeding the combined 20GW added in 2024 and 2025. By 2028, Wells Fargo sees total hyperscaler compute capacity reaching more than 125GW.
Google parent Alphabet is forecast to remain the leader, adding 19GW to reach 35GW by year-end 2028. Amazon is seen adding 16GW to 29GW, and Microsoft 16GW to 27GW. Meta Platforms is expected to expand capacity to 21GW by 2028, while Oracle scales to 11GW.
Wells Fargo said compute capacity is becoming the key constraint in cloud models, with shortages spanning chips, power and data center infrastructure. It estimates hyperscaler capital expenditures will nearly double to more than $860 billion by 2027, from $427 billion in 2025, with total spending of $2.47 trillion over 2026 to 2028, about 8% above consensus.
The firm raised its Azure revenue estimates and said its forecasts for AWS and Google Cloud Platform are above Street expectations for 2026 and 2027.
Wells Fargo said access to compute capacity will be a critical success factor in AI, citing strong demand following the launch of ChatGPT by OpenAI and subsequent AI product updates across the industry.
"Compute capacity is key success factor as AI-driven demand to materially exceed supply near to medium term. Cloud industry to grow meaningfully faster than consensus view," analyst said.

Title: Without AI spending, U.S. corporate capex would be negative, Pantheon analyst says
Summary_AI: U.S. corporate capital expenditure grew 2.6% in Q4 2025, driven almost entirely by AI infrastructure investments, with computer and communications equipment spending surging 61% and intellectual property/software spending rising 7.4%, according to Pantheon Macroeconomics. All other investment categories declined sharply, with equipment spending falling 17%, leading analyst Oliver Allen to conclude that corporate capex would be negative without AI-related spending. The concentration of capex growth in AI infrastructure now represents a significant driver of U.S. GDP growth.
Article_Text:
Spending on AI infrastructure now forms a significant part of U.S. GDP growth, and corporate capital expenditure (capex) would be negative without it, according to a recent research note from Pantheon Macroeconomics.
“We’ve got a situation where large sums of money are pouring into AI infrastructure, providing a meaningful boost to GDP,” Pantheon analyst Oliver Allen told Fortune recently.
Overall capex rose by 2.6% in Q4 2025, Allen wrote in a research note this morning. Within that, intellectual property and software spending (i.e., spending likely linked to AI) was up 7.4%, and computer and communications equipment was up 61%. But all other segments declined: “Investment in other equipment plunged by 17%, a decline that was worryingly broad-based,” he wrote.

Title: Report: Data center construction delays grow - E&E News by POLITICO
Summary_AI: Sightline Climate's report found that 30-50 percent of the 140 data center projects representing 16 gigawatts of new energy demand planned to open this year will face delays until at least 2027 or may never be built. The delays, up from 26 percent in the prior year, stem from power constraints, community opposition, and grid equipment shortages. Sightline research analyst Olivia Wang noted that delays often signal permanent project cancellations.
Article_Text:
About one-third of the data centers slated to open this year will be delayed — or may never be built at all, according to a new report from an energy research firm.
Sightline Climate found that developers plan to complete 140 projects this year, representing 16 gigawatts of new energy demand. But in a report published Tuesday, the firm estimated that between 30 and 50 percent of those projects will be delayed until at least 2027, thanks to power constraints, community opposition and grid equipment shortages.
That would be an increase from the 26 percent of planned data centers not completed last year, according to the report.
Delays are often the clearest public indication that a development is dead, said Olivia Wang, a research analyst at Sightline who authored the report.

Title: Community members discuss regulations for hypothetical AI data centers in Vermont
Summary_AI: Royalton, Vermont is preparing for a town meeting vote to amend local zoning regulations that would restrict AI and cryptocurrency data centers, citing concerns about high water and electricity demand with limited community benefits. Vermont state lawmakers are simultaneously developing regulatory legislation for data centers despite no current proposals or developments in the state. The debate reflects broader national tensions between climate advocates seeking precautionary measures and technology supporters who view data centers as essential infrastructure for AI advancement.
Article_Text:
ROYALTON, Vt. (WCAX) - It’s a quiet Monday night in Royalton, but come next Tuesday, there’s expected to be a robust debate on AI data centers in the small town.
“This is what we’re going to vote on in our town meeting, to amend our town plan to protect us,” said Royalton resident Jane Philbrick.
Philbrick moved to Royalton in 2024 from Connecticut to be around more environmentalists.
“I feel very strongly that here will be the closest I get to the Black Church of the civil rights movement for the climate movement,” said Philbrick.
She says she’s fighting climate change full-time, and her current project is advocating against AI and cryptocurrency data centers in Royalton and across the country.
“It’s very, very heavy in its water demand, very, very heavy in its electricity demand, and the impacts of that are so onerous for communities with very little community benefit,” said Philbrick.
Right now in Vermont, there are no plans or proposals for a new data center. But state lawmakers are working on a bill to regulate them, just in case. A change to a town plan wouldn’t outlaw data centers, but Philbrick says it’s to send a message to developers.
“Let’s first have a caution, let’s have a protection, and see what happens,” said Philbrick.
Royalton resident Ronald Brown, on the other hand, likes AI. He says he’s used it before to counsel him, and thinks that with more data centers, AI can improve and be more helpful to those who need it.
“I feel like we shouldn’t stand in the way of technology, it’s only gonna get more and more, and why try to hinder what makes it better?” Brown said. “I had a discussion with a friend of mine, and it didn’t go very well, and believe it or not, I asked AI what I could’ve said better, and it gave me a few paragraphs, and you know, it made her melt,” said Brown.
Town Meeting Day is the first Tuesday in March.

Title: Hood County to consider another data center moratorium despite potential legal concerns
Summary_AI: Hood County commissioners are reconsidering a moratorium on large-scale data center development following a narrow 3-2 vote against the measure earlier this month, but face significant legal obstacles under Texas state law that limits county regulatory authority compared to municipalities. The county is now considering requesting a formal opinion from the Texas Attorney General on whether it can impose such restrictions under the Local Government Code, while state Senator Paul Bettencourt has challenged county-level authority to mandate development moratoriums. The debate centers on House Bill 2559, which clarified municipal moratorium powers but notably excluded counties, suggesting state lawmakers intended to restrict local control over data center expansion in rural areas.
Article_Text:
Energy & Environment
Hood County to consider another data center moratorium despite potential legal concerns
KERA | By Emmanuel Rivas Valenzuela
Published February 24, 2026 at 5:01 AM CST
Emmanuel Rivas Valenzuela
Hood County Commissioners listen to residents concerns regarding the expansion of data centers in the rural county.
Hood County leaders will once again weigh whether to hit pause on large-scale data center development.
But even if they move forward, state law may stand in the way.
After narrowly voting 3-2 earlier this month against a proposed moratorium on new industrial projects, county commissioners are now reconsidering the idea Tuesday amid mounting public pressure and new procedural moves that could test the limits of local authority.
At the center of the debate is House Bill 2559, passed during the latest legislative session, which clarifies how cities can impose development moratoriums.
"There clearly is a legal difference of opinion between some of these localities and state government over what the boundaries of county regulatory authority are," said Southern Methodist University political science professor Matthew Wilson.
He said that counties are "entirely subservient to state government," meaning if lawmakers in Austin choose to restrict local authority, they generally can.
Unlike the previous vote, commissioners are now considering directing the county attorney to formally request an opinion from the Texas Attorney General on whether Hood County can act beyond the expressed provisions of the state's Local Government Code.
They are also weighing a resolution asking Gov. Greg Abbott to call a special legislative session to address the rapid growth of data centers in Texas.

Article_Text:
Gigantic Form Energy battery to power Google data center in Minnesota
The startup has struck a deal with Xcel Energy to build a 30 gigawatt-hour battery plant that will deliver clean energy, round the clock, to a Google facility.
Google’s data center campus in New Albany, Ohio (Google)
Form Energy invented a novel iron-air battery to store clean energy for much longer timeframes than conventional lithium-ion batteries can. The startup is still constructing its first commercial project, in Minnesota, but today revealed it has clinched a potentially game-changing follow-up in the same state to support a Google data center.
The utility Xcel Energy will install 300 megawatts of Form’s batteries in Pine Island, Minnesota. It’s a big battery installation for the Midwest, but developers have built several grid storage plants elsewhere with more megawatt capacity. What shoots this project into the energy-storage stratosphere is that it will dispatch energy for up to 100 hours straight — enough to pump clean energy through multiday weather patterns that would limit renewable production. That unique capability means the Pine Island Form plant, fully charged, will hold 30 gigawatt-hours of energy, an astonishing amount for the grid as we know it.
The deal is also notable in that it proves Form has found commercial traction even before its first installation for a utility customer is complete. That outcome was possible because Xcel has seen Form develop its technology for years, said Form CEO Mateo Jaramillo, who co-founded the firm in 2017.
“Xcel in particular has been with us through every step of the journey — when the chemistry was in a very small bucket, essentially, to complete deployed systems,” Jaramillo said. “They saw the challenging things that we worked through. They saw us solve hard problems. They saw us come out the other side.”

Title: Infrastructure fundraising roars back as data centers, energy transition reset investor priorities
Summary_AI: Global infrastructure fundraising rebounded sharply to over $250 billion in 2025, more than doubling 2024's $99 billion low, with $474 billion currently being sought by closed-ended funds globally according to S&P Global's Infrastructure Outlook 2026. Data centers have become the central investment thesis, driven by AI demand, with 144 real asset funds targeting nearly $200 billion in data center exposure since 2020 and a record 604 data centers built in 2024. Article_Text:
Infrastructure fundraising roars back as data centers, energy transition reset investor priorities
With $474B sought globally, managers chase AI data centers, secondaries liquidity and energy mandates.
Infrastructure fundraising has shifted from drought to deluge in just two years, as investors re-engage with the asset class on the back of surging demand for data centers and accelerating energy-transition spending.
The newly released Infrastructure Outlook 2026 from With Intelligence by S&P Global shows the sector rebounding sharply in 2025 after a weak 2024, as large flagship funds returned to market and allocators increased commitments to strategies tied to power, digital infrastructure and liquidity solutions.
That shift is visible in the fundraising numbers. The report estimates $474 billion is currently being sought by closed-ended infrastructure funds globally, with $143 billion of that attributable to the top 10 managers.
“Infrastructure is set for another year of sustained momentum, following a strong rebound in fundraising in 2025, which more than doubled the previous year’s total,” said Viola Caon, global infrastructure research lead at With Intelligence.
“A confluence of megatrends, including AI-driven data center expansion and energy transition, are helping to drive this growth, but we’re also seeing increased interest in infrastructure investments as source of liquidity and portfolio protection amid market volatility.”
The outlook describes a market where capital is consolidating among large managers even as new firms try to break through. Infrastructure fundraising rose from $99 billion in 2024, a six-year low, to more than $250 billion in 2025, excluding co-investments, which the report says marked the strongest year since 2015 and was helped by several funds larger than $10 billion reaching a close.

Alcoa looks to sell 10 aluminum smelting plants to data center developers
Metals company sees opportunity to monetize idle plants
February 25, 2026 By Dan Swinhoe Have your say
US aluminum company Alcoa Corp is looking to sell many of its smelting sites to data center developers.
In comments first reported by Reuters, Alcoa CEO Bill Oplinger this week told the BMO Global Metals, Mining and Critical Minerals Conference in Florida that the company is in the process of closing several deals for idle or curtailed sites.
“We have 10 sites that we're focused on selling into that space," Oplinger said. "We think we'll have the first sale in the first half of this year. There are two that could follow quickly after that."
Full details of which plants are up for sale and which companies might buy them haven’t been shared.
Brownfield industrial sites, including smelting plants and steelworks, often make good candidates for redevelopment into data centers due to their existing transmission infrastructure and favorable zoning.
One former Alcoa smelting plant is already in the midst of being converted into a data center park. In Maryland, TPG Real Estate Partners (TREP) is developing a 2,100-acre, gigawatt-scale data center park some 25 miles (40.2km) north of Ashburn in Adamstown, Frederick County.
In development since 2021, the Frederick development is based on the site of the former Alcoa Eastalco Works aluminum plant. Aligned, Amazon, and Rowan Digital are known customers of the park.
In Kentucky, another aluminum plant is set to become a data center campus. Century Aluminum Company recently sold its site in Hawesville to an affiliate of US data center developer TeraWulf. The 750 acre site has approximately 480MW of existing power capacity, with the potential to expand further over time. The plant was acquired for a reported $200 million, with Century retaining a non-controlling minority equity stake in the project of approximately 6.8 percent.

PACIFIC, Mo. (First Alert 4) - A special Pacific Planning and Zoning Commission meeting in Pacific ended within minutes Wednesday night after the developer behind a proposed $16 billion data center withdrew its rezoning application, drawing shouts of frustration from a packed high school auditorium.
Beltline Energy pulled its request to rezone land south of the city limits, citing an inability to finalize a funding agreement. The company has proposed building the data center off Highway O in Franklin County, with the Meramec River along the northern edge of the site.

“You just wasted all these people’s time,” one attendee at the meeting said.

Nicodemo family lives near the proposed site and also said she’s been trying to reach her local elected state leaders.
Twila Ashworth, whose property borders the western edge of the proposed data center site, said she came to raise concerns about the project’s environmental impact.

Title: Hollow-Core Fiber and the Data Center Networking Impact
Summary_AI: Hollow-core fiber (HCF) technology offers significant latency advantages for distributed AI data center clusters by enabling light to travel approximately 50% faster than in traditional silica-core fiber, reducing latency by roughly 30%. With HCF's lower refractive index of around 1 compared to silica-core fiber's 1.5, network operators can increase the maximum distance between data centers by 50% and expand the overall data center footprint by 125%, providing greater flexibility for siting facilities in areas with lower real estate costs and better access to power and water resources. As GPU clusters scale across multiple metro and regional data centers for AI training and inference workloads, latency performance in fiber transmission becomes an increasingly critical differentiator for network operators offering data center interconnect services like wavelength, dark fiber, and managed optical networks.
Article_Text:
Insight and analysis on the data center space from industry thought leaders.
Will Hollow-Core Fiber Change the Latency Rules of Data Center Networking?
Low latency is becoming increasingly important for AI inference needs. Can hollow-core fiber save the day?
Industry Perspectives,Industry Perspectives
In the AI era, latency is an increasingly critical metric for data center networking. For AI training, low latency helps ensure that all graphics processing units (GPUs) and nodes in the training cluster are used effectively, minimizing idle time and maximizing overall training efficiency. Low latency is even more critical for AI inference, which requires real-time or near-real-time responses.
As GPU clusters scale to hundreds, thousands, and even millions of GPUs, power, space, and availability limitations are forcing AI clusters to be distributed across multiple metro and regional data centers. The term “scale across” is now entering the lexicon to describe AI networking between data centers; however, the locations of these data centers and the distances between them are limited by latency.
Optical Fibers and Latency
A key component of latency in a data center is the time it takes light to travel through fiber-optic cables. As distances increase across metro, regional, long-haul, and submarine networks, this becomes the dominant factor increasingly, with latency in the networking equipment becoming negligible. This fiber latency is therefore a function of distance and the fiber latency per kilometer. Network operators selling data center interconnect services, such as wavelength services, dark fiber, and managed optical fiber networks (MOFN), differentiate themselves from their competitors by having the shortest fiber route.

Developers are not requesting tax increment financing or grant funding for the project.
Meanwhile, city officials say they are also addressing common concerns surrounding data centers, including high water and electricity usage.

Title: Metro Connect 2026: Fiber on the rise, data centers under fire - RCR Wireless News
