Article_Text:
Metro Connect 2026: Fiber on the rise, data centers under fire
Morgan Lewis’ Andrew Lipman surfaces the top trends to watch in 2026 at Metro Connect
With the show floor packed with fiber connectivity players, data center providers, and software folks with all their financial backers, Metro Connect 2026 was a full house.
The keynote sessions and panel discussions were loaded with commentaries and insights, bold predictions, and sometimes light-hearted banter (“If AI is sexy, data centers are X-rated”) which were great on their own, but they also somewhat made up for the interminably long wait to get into the venue on Day1.
Andrew Lipman, partner and head of telecom practice at Morgan Lewis, a law firm, and who is many years associated with the event, set the stage by surfacing the most prominent trends to watch in 2026.
Lipman said that 2026 presents an “optimistic macro environment” for broadband. “It looks like President Trump’s aspirational soft-landing story is happening,” he said. “Our tax structure is also very favorable for the industry with bonus depreciation, R&D tax credits. The antitrust environment is much better than it has been.”
BEAD, a $43 billion opportunity
Lipman segued to the Broadband Equity, Access, and Deployment (BEAD) program — a $42.45 billion federal initiative for pushing broadband access to underserved areas in the United States — which has remained a topic of much speculation.
Although funds were pledged early, BEAD money was long in the coming. The program suffered multiple bottlenecks, including delay in deployment of dollars, federal approvals, and regulatory hurdles — driving up demand and cost of fiber network buildouts.
Finally, late last year, Louisiana received funding approval for $1.36 billion, becoming the first state to see BEAD infrastructure dollars. In February, it disbursed $43 million in BEAD contracts,10% of the $430 million it has signed so far.

Title: Data Center Boom Concentrates Industrial Pipeline in Five Markets - Globest
Summary_AI: Data center starts surged to 30.8 million square feet in 2025, driven by hyperscaler AI investment and exceeding the combined 2020-2022 total. Five markets—Washington D.C. (6.1M sq ft), Dallas (3.2M), Phoenix (2.9M), Atlanta (2.8M), and Columbus (2.6M)—captured 17.6 million square feet, or 57% of the annual total, reflecting sustained capital concentration in established power and fiber infrastructure hubs. These same five markets dominated 2024 with 20.3 million of 34.8 million square feet of starts, underscoring consistent clustering among developed data center corridors.
Article_Text:
Industrial fundamentals are continuing to normalize in early 2026, but one segment of the development pipeline is accelerating at a markedly different pace: data centers.
While trade policy volatility has created turbulence for ports and logistics operators, another shift within industrial real estate is being driven by generative AI investment, according to Yardi Matrix. Hyperscaler demand pushed data center starts to 30.8 million square feet in 2025, exceeding the combined total recorded between 2020 and 2022.
The expansion remains highly concentrated. Of the 30.8 million square feet of data center starts last year, 17.6 million were delivered in five markets, according to Yardi Matrix. They include Washington, D.C., with 6.1 million square feet; Dallas with 3.2 million; Phoenix with 2.9 million; Atlanta with 2.8 million and Columbus with 2.6 million. These same metros also led in 2024, when they accounted for 20.3 million of the 34.8 million square feet of starts nationwide, underscoring sustained capital clustering in established power and fiber-rich hubs.
Overall, 355.7 million square feet of industrial space is under construction, representing 1.7% of existing stock. Outside of data centers, however, development activity has moderated as supply delivered during the pandemic-era boom continues to push vacancies higher, Yardi Matrix reported.

Title: Data center developers promise to be 'good neighbors' in Abernathy - KCBD
Summary_AI: Aligned Data Centers held a community meeting in Abernathy, Texas to address resident concerns about a planned data center development off Interstate 27 and FM 54 north of the city. The developer emphasized its long-term commitment to the community and highlighted plans for a closed-loop cooling system to minimize water consumption compared to conventional data centers. Concerns raised by residents and property owners centered on infrastructure impacts, job creation, electricity usage, water availability, and developer accountability. Abernathy's mayor pledged to protect residents' interests, including potential municipal annexation and preservation of drinking water supplies.
Article_Text:
LUBBOCK, Texas (KCBD) - Residents and area property owners gathered Thursday night to question the impact of a planned data center off Interstate 27 and FM 54 north of Abernathy.
The developer, Aligned Data Centers, said it is aware of community concerns. Executive Vice President David Robinson said he wanted to meet with neighbors in person.
“This is a big project that’s coming to their community,” Robinson said. “Aligned Data Centers is a developer, operator and owner, so we are not selling our data centers. We will continue to be in the community, and we do want to be a good neighbor.”
Community raises questions and Developers respond
Residents raised concerns about infrastructure, job opportunities, electricity use, water consumption and developer accountability.
Robinson and Cardell Andrews, vice president for land enablement, said Aligned is focused on resource conservation. Andrews said the project will use a closed-loop cooling system designed to limit water use compared to other data centers.
“Data centers are a part of the solution. We make the grid more resilient. Data centers are also investing those infrastructure improvements,” Robinson said. “I’ve built and designed them myself, I know that they do that — the new technology, as Dave mentioned, sustainability is a key to Aligned, that is something we focus on, something we take pride in," Andrews said.
Mayor pledges to protect residents
Abernathy Mayor Harold Bufe said he would work to ensure the project does not harm residents.
“I own a home in Abernathy, we are not going to do anything that’s going to make our home worthless, we are not going to jeopardize us to not have drinking water,” Bufe said. “And if we are able to annex this, it’s going to be a huge impact on the city — those are things we got to think about.”
Transparency on project

Title: Moris Media & Consulting: Data centers in the driftless – a community conversation
Summary_AI: Community organizers in southwestern Wisconsin are hosting a public forum on March 8 in Potosi to educate residents about data center development risks and benefits, with the Cassville area identified as a potential site due to its proximity to the Cardinal-Hickory Transmission Line, existing substations, and fiber optic connectivity. The event will feature technology executive Prescott Balch discussing tax implications, water usage, utility impacts, and job creation related to data center projects. Article_Text:
POTOSI, Wis. – Citizens and taxpayers from across SW Wisconsin have asked for a public meeting to discuss potential risks and rewards of data centers being developed in our area. Volunteer organizers have secured the Holiday Gardens Event Center in Potosi to host this community conversation on Sunday, March 8.
The event will be headlined by Prescott Balch, a recently retired technology executive from Caledonia, Wisc. who has traveled across Wisconsin and the Midwest to help educate communities about the rapid proliferation of data centers. Mr. Balch is well versed in a variety of topics surrounding data centers, including tax implications, water usage and utility rate impacts, as well as short and long-term job creation. He has also done extensive research on other economic and environmental considerations surrounding these projects.
“The national debate over data centers has arrived at our doorstep in the Driftless Region,” said Pete Moris, a Mt. Hope native who is one of the volunteer organizers of the Potosi event. “We are incredibly grateful that Prescott Balch and others have agreed to make the trip to Grant County to share their expertise. In addition to a formal presentation, there will be ample time devoted to answering questions from those in attendance.”
The Cassville area has been publicly mentioned as a potential site for a data center in Grant County. Given its location along the Cardinal-Hickory Transmission Line, its proximity to existing substations and a high-voltage electrical transmission line that runs north to Genoa, Wisc., as well as fiber optic connectivity, the area has much of the infrastructure in place that a data center would require.
“I’m an outdoor advocate who believes this kind of ecological devastation is immoral,” said local volunteer Melodie Betts. “Our farmland is disappearing at an alarming rate, and the Driftless Area needs to be preserved for generations to come.”

Title: City Council Approves Zoning Updates for Data Centers and Digital Asset Mining • Lawton, OK
Summary_AI: Lawton, Oklahoma's City Council approved zoning ordinance amendments on February 24, 2026, establishing a two-tier framework for data centers and digital asset mining facilities within city limits. Facilities with peak electrical demand of 2 megawatts or less are permitted uses in the I-1 industrial district, while those exceeding 2 megawatts require conditional approval in the I-4 Heavy Industrial District and must undergo Planning Commission and City Council review with binding site plan requirements. Both categories require written electrical provider clearance confirming sufficient infrastructure capacity. No data center proposals are currently under consideration in Lawton.
Article_Text:
Home NewsFlash 2026 City News
City Council Approves Zoning Updates for Data Centers and Digital Asset Mining
Posted on February 27, 2026
Madalynn ElKouri | Multimedia Specialist
During the February 24, 2026, Lawton City Council meeting, Council held a public hearing and approved an ordinance amending the City’s zoning code related to data centers and digital asset mining facilities.
The ordinance updates Chapter 18 of the Lawton City Code to clarify where these uses may be allowed in industrial zoning districts and what approval process applies based on a facility’s peak electrical demand (measured in megawatts). This ordinance applies only within the City of Lawton city limits and does not establish regulations for Comanche County.
Is There a Data Center Proposal Right Now?
At this time, there is no application and no company currently seeking approval to build a data center or digital asset mining facility in the City of Lawton.
What Are Data Centers?
Data centers are facilities that house large numbers of computer servers and related equipment. These servers store, process, and transmit digital information used for things like cloud storage, websites, streaming services, financial systems, and other online platforms.
Digit asset mining facilities use similar infrastructure and rely on large amounts of electrical power to run specialized computer equipment.
What the Ordinance Does
The approved changes establish a two-tier approach:
Facilities at or Below 2 Megawatts
Data centers and digital asset mining facilities with a peak electrical demand not greater than 2 megawatts are allowed as a permitted use within the I-1 Restricted Manufacturing and Warehouse District, subject to key requirements. This provision keeps our zoning code aligned with Oklahoma Statutes.
Written clearance from the local electrical provider confirming there is sufficient infrastructure to safely support the use without negatively impacting service to existing customers.

Title: 'Least worst option': Carbon County community faces data center challenges - WVIA
Summary_AI: Penn Forest Township in Carbon County is moving forward with a zoning amendment to allow data centers on approximately 500 acres of residential land on Route 903 and Maury Road, where developer Mele Brothers Realty plans to build a data center campus. The township's vice chairman characterized the overlay zoning in a residential area as "the least worst option" due to limited availability of suitable commercial or industrial land, citing state-owned and authority-controlled properties that cannot be developed. Mele Brothers, a Montgomery County company, has stated the project is in feasibility stage and indicated it will pursue development either through the new ordinance or via a validity challenge to the current zoning restrictions. The township supervisors are scheduled to vote on the amendment on Monday, with a Zoning Board hearing on the validity challenge set for March 23.

'Least worst option': Carbon County community faces data center challenges
WVIA | By Kat Bolus | WVIA News
Published February 28, 2026 at 5:00 AM EST
Linda Christman, president of Save Carbon County, speaks during a Penn Forest Twp. hearing about the municipalities data center zoning amendment.
Penn Forest Twp. reviewed a final draft of a curative amendment to its zoning ordinance to allow data centers in an area that many residents say is not suitable for the industry.
"We determined that it is simply the least worst option,” township Vice Chairman Christian Bartulovich said during a zoning hearing earlier this week.
If approved, the amendment will add a zoning overlay to around 500 acres of land in a residential area of the township on Route 903 and Maury Road. It’s the same location where Mele Brothers Realty plans to build a data center campus.
Mele Brothers challenged the township’s zoning ordinance for excluding data centers before it had defined data centers. However, the property owners have not submitted any plans to the borough to build a data center.
During a Jan. 14 meeting, Attorney Matthew McHugh, who represents the Montgomery County company, said the project is in the feasibility stage right now.
“One way or the other, we’re going to pursue a data center development on this site, whether it’s through this ordinance or through the validity challenge,” he said.
He said their preference is to work with the township.
“And come to a regulatory framework in the form of this ordinance that will allow us to understand what the rules of the game are, allow for reasonable development, have an appropriate public process in place, have appropriate standards, performance standards, studies, etc., and remedies for concerns that the township and residents may have,” he said
Supervisors will vote on the amendment on Monday at 6 p.m. at Penn’s Peak. A Zoning Board Hearing is scheduled for March 23 on the substantive validity challenge.

Title: Meta's data center ownership transfer doesn't concern Louisiana power regulators
Summary_AI: Meta transferred an 80% ownership stake of its $28 billion Hyperion artificial intelligence data center in Richland Parish, Louisiana to Blue Owl Capital's Beignet Investor entity while retaining 20% through a separate real estate holding company, a complex financing structure that prompted consumer advocates to request state utility investigation. Entergy Louisiana built a new natural gas power plant with three turbines to supply electricity to the facility under a 15-year agreement with Meta's real estate entity, protected by a parent guarantee making Meta liable if obligations aren't met. Louisiana Public Service Commission regulators rejected the request to investigate the ownership transfer, with Entergy affirming the contract terms remain unchanged and irrevocable despite Meta's financial restructuring.
Article_Text:
Meta’s data center ownership transfer doesn’t concern Louisiana power regulators
Consumer and science advocates want assurances Entergy customers are still protected if Meta walks away massive NE Louisiana facility
From left, Louisiana Public Service Commissioners Foster Campbell, Eric Skrmetta, Mike Francis, J. P. Coussan, and Davante Lewis hold a meeting in Plaquemine, La., on Aug. 22, 2025. (Photo credit: Wes Muller/Louisiana Illuminator)
State utility regulators rejected a request this week to investigate whether Meta’s decision to place its data center in Northeast Louisiana under new ownership could leave Entergy Louisiana customers footing the bill to power it.
Meta, the parent company of Facebook and Instagram, reached an arrangement in October with Blue Owl Capital to transfer an 80% share of its Richland Parish data center to Beignet Investor, a new company the private equity firm created. The remaining 20% is now the property of a separate real estate holding company Meta owns indirectly from which it will lease the Hyperion artificial intelligence data center.
A recent Wall Street Journal report described the new data center ownership structure as “Frankenstein financing.” By relinquishing ownership of its $28 billion Hyperion data center, Meta moved billions in debt off its balance sheet. Its auditor, Ernst & Young, approved the arrangement while describing the maneuver as a “critical audit matter” that warrants investor attention.
The complex transaction prompted the Alliance for Affordable Energy and Union of Concerned Scientists to ask the Louisiana Public Service Commission to look into the deal. The groups are concerned that customers of Entergy Louisiana, which is building a new power plant to provide electricity for the Hyperion campus, wouldn’t be protected if Meta decides to walk away from its data center

For Immediate Release

Sierra Club, Sustain South Jersey, and Local Farmers Oppose Massive Vineland Data Center and New Dirty Gas Generation
Vineland, NJ - In June of 2024, Vineland Planning Board approved a proposal for a 2.4 million-square foot, 300 megawatt (MW) AI data center. Northeast Precast is now a minority shareholder with DataOne.

Community opposition has grown over the many environmental, noise, and energy affordability concerns, and is being spearheaded by the grassroots organization, Sustain South Jersey.

Title: US Data Center Builder Goes Off-Grid to Speed Deployments | BloombergNEF
Summary_AI: Joule Capital Partners is developing a 4,000-acre data center campus in Utah with 1.5 gigawatts of captive power generation in the first phase, using Caterpillar natural gas engines beginning next month to bypass grid connection delays. The company secured $60 million in Series B funding from Clear Lake Investments and Foulger Pratt and plans to bring temporary compute structures online later this year with permanent facilities operational by end of 2026, positioning itself to compete for hyperscaler clients in the competitive race for power-constrained data center capacity. Utah's regulatory environment allowing large power users to establish captive power plants, combined with available water resources and low-latency fiber infrastructure, drove the location selection over traditional data center hubs like Virginia and Texas.
Article_Text:
US Data Center Builder Goes Off-Grid to Speed Deployments
How quickly can you get me power? That’s the key question Conor Andrus keeps hearing as his firm, Joule Capital Partners, builds data centers at a sprawling facility in the US state of Utah.
“Right now, it’s a very hotly contested race of who can get the most compute. The most compute is driven by the most amount of power,” Andrus, managing partner and head of capital markets, said in an interview.
“That bodes well for our project because we know when we can deliver the power and we can be really creative around the structures that are going to house the equipment,” he said.
Joule is building data centers across a 4,000-acre campus in Utah which will be fed by its own power plants tallying 1.5 gigawatts in the first phase. Securing a new power connection from the grid is a time-consuming process and there is a long queue for equipment to set up captive power plants. Joule has already secured kit it needs and will be ready to host clients later this year, according to Andrus.
Caterpillar’s natural gas engines for power generation will be delivered from next month, he said. Temporary structures to house compute power will be ready soon, while halls will start coming online by the end of next year. “Speed is the name of the game,” Andrus said.
The company secured $60 million in series B funding last month from Clear Lake Investments and Foulger Pratt. It expects to sign its first client soon. Whether that will be a large company – a hyperscaler – seeking a big footprint within the development or a clutch of smaller neoclouds remains to be seen. “We are talking to customers in both groups,” he said.
Utah was one of the first states to enable large power users like data centers to set up their own power plants. “The site was selected because of availability of power, water and fiber. Low latency fiber,” Andrus said, referring to networks with superfast data transmission.

Title: Dryden says no to data centers, cryptocurrency mines - WSKG
Summary_AI: Dryden, a rural town in Tompkins County, New York, became the first municipality in the state to permanently ban data centers and cryptocurrency mining facilities after amending its local zoning code. The move follows over a year of temporary moratorium and comes amid controversy over a proposed data center in neighboring Lansing. New York state legislators have introduced parallel legislation to impose a three-year moratorium on data centers consuming over 20 megawatts of electricity, reflecting broader concerns about environmental and economic impacts as the state hosts over 130 of the nation's 5,000-plus data centers.
Article_Text:
Dryden says no to data centers, cryptocurrency mines
WSKG | By Aurora Berry
Published February 27, 2026 at 4:29 PM EST
Smokestacks at an upstate New York cryptocurrency mining facility.
Dryden’s town board voted to ban data centers and cryptocurrency mining facilities at a meeting last week.
There are currently over 5,000 data centers in the United States, according to the Brookings Center. Over 130 of those are in New York, with more on the way.
Lawmakers amended the local zoning code last week to define and disallow data centers and cryptocurrency mines in the rural Tompkins County town.
Deputy Town Supervisor Dan Lamb said that Dryden was “the first town in New York to ban data centers and cryptomining.” He called it a “nice distinction.”
This comes as controversy over a proposed data center next door in the town of Lansing has spiraled into loud meetings and legal action.
There has been a temporary moratorium on both data centers and cryptocurrency mines in Dryden for over a year.
Local elected officials are not the only ones with bans on the brain. Some New York state and national lawmakers have expressed concerns about the potential environmental and economic impacts of a data center deluge.
Recently introduced state legislation would put a three-year moratorium on data centers that use over 20 megawatts of electricity. The bill is sponsored by State Senator Lea Webb and Assemblymember Anna Kelles, whose districts include Tompkins County.

Title: Thermal management system for data centers boosts cooling efficiency and shrinks footprint
Summary_AI: Karman Industries launched its Heat Processing Unit (HPU), a 10-MW modular cooling system that uses supercritical carbon dioxide refrigeration to reduce data-center cooling energy consumption by recovering 30% of compression work through a turbine. The shipping-container-sized platform eliminates water consumption, reduces mechanical infrastructure footprint by 60 to 80%, and repurposes GPU waste heat for lower-temperature cooling loops or electricity generation, addressing thermal management constraints in AI-driven data centers where power densities are rapidly increasing. The HPU design incorporates aerospace and electric-vehicle innovations including metal 3D-printing, high-speed motors, and silicon-carbide power electronics. Cooling currently accounts for 20 to 40% of data-center electricity use, making Karman's efficiency gains significant for infrastructure operators.
Article_Text:
Thermal management system for data centers boosts cooling efficiency and shrinks footprint
March 1, 2026 | By Scott Jenkins
Fast-growing power densities within the server racks at new data centers often generate enough heat to quickly overwhelm legacy air- and liquid-cooling methods, and managing the thermal load is becoming a constraint for the data-center infrastructure that supports AI.
A new thermal management system launched recently by Karman Industries (Los Angeles, Calif.; www.karmanindustries.com) offers a way to lower energy consumption for data-center cooling while shrinking the mechanical footprint of cooling architecture significantly.
In January, Karman released its Heat Processing Unit (HPU), a modular, shipping-container-sized, 10-MW cooling platform that increases cooling efficiency, speeds deployments and eliminates water consumption. At the heart of the HPU is a compression-based refrigeration system using supercritical carbon dioxide as the refrigerant. “The HPU cuts energy waste by recovering about 30% of the compression work through a turbine, instead of a standard expansion valve,” explains CJ Kalra, a thermal-fluids engineer and co-founder of Karman Industries. “The thermodynamic properties of CO2, including a critical temperature near ambient (32°C), make it ideal for efficient heat transfer and partial power recovery,” Kalra adds.
The compressor design approach took inspiration from aerospace engineering and electric-vehicle technology. Karman engineers borrowed recent innovations in rocket turbomachinery, including metal 3D-printing and electric-vehicle innovations like high-speed motors and silicon-carbide power electronics technology, the company says.

Title: A Mississippi subdivision struggles with the data center next door | The Arkansas Democrat-Gazette
Summary_AI: Amazon is constructing a data center campus in Madison County, Mississippi, one of two locations the company selected in the region, with development also underway in Lauderdale, Rankin, and Vicksburg counties alongside xAI's planned facility in DeSoto County near Memphis. Residents of the Idlebriar subdivision near Canton report significant quality-of-life impacts from ongoing construction, including increased traffic congestion, noise from heavy machinery, and dust accumulation that has exacerbated health issues such as asthma in nearby families. The data center boom is driven by artificial intelligence and large language model demand, with developers projecting tens of billions of dollars in economic investment, job creation, and tax revenue for Mississippi, though communities closest to the facilities face environmental concerns over energy consumption, water usage, and pollution with limited control over siting decisions.
Article_Text:
CANTON, Miss. -- Less than 200 yards from Johnny Sims' home on the outskirts of Canton is a sprawling industrial park housing a partially completed data center campus.
It's one of two Madison County locations selected by Amazon to store, process and distribute huge amounts of digital data.
The arrival of the data center and adjacent development has transformed conditions in Idlebriar, the serene, tree-filled subdivision that Sims and his wife settled in three decades ago. An influx of traffic around the site has created bottlenecks in the neighborhood and lengthened commute times. Sims said the sounds of vehicles and heavy machinery have kept his family up at night.
Ongoing construction has also caused dust to accumulate in the subdivision, covering houses, cars and vegetation and raising alarm bells about degraded air quality. The dust has proven especially damaging for Sims' teenage daughter, who suffers from asthma and has had to wear a face covering outdoors to keep her symptoms from worsening.
She still wound up in the emergency room multiple times last year because of severe asthma flare-ups, her father said.
"Amazon brought a lot of baggage with them," said Sims, who has been a Madison County constable for almost 30 years and previously served as Canton's fire chief. "When I moved out here, it was a peaceful, quiet neighborhood. But once all that (development) came, everything went to mess."
Amazon's Madison County complexes are part of a boom in information technology infrastructure development around Mississippi, fueled by the escalating demands of artificial intelligence and large language models like ChatGPT. Last year, the state announced additional data center projects in Lauderdale and Rankin counties, as well as a third Amazon facility in Vicksburg, Miss. Local officials also confirmed in January that billionaire Elon Musk's company xAI is building a mammoth data center in DeSoto County just south of Memphis.

Summary_AI: Peachtree City officials denied circulating claims that the city is pursuing a data center project, stating no proposal has been submitted and current zoning does not permit such facilities. Article_Text:
Peachtree City officials say no data center proposal is before the city, pushing back on claims circulating online as a newly approved annexation study moves forward.
The dispute began after the City Council voted unanimously on Feb. 12 to commission an annexation study — a planning tool leaders say is intended to evaluate boundaries, traffic, zoning, and tax implications as the city approaches buildout.
Former City Council candidate James Clifton has publicly argued that the vote, combined with recent land activity outside city limits, signals an effort to bring a data center to Peachtree City. City officials and the Fayette County Development Authority (FCDA) say that is not the case.
What has actually happened
On Feb. 12, the Peachtree City Council approved an annexation study. No annexation application has been submitted, no zoning amendment permitting data centers has been introduced, and no vote on a data center has occurred.
Mayor Kim Learnard said the study is the first of its kind in 12 years and is meant to provide updated data.
“In our meeting February 12, City Council voted unanimously to conduct an Annexation Study,” Learnard wrote in a Feb. 18 email response to Clifton. She cited three reasons: the city is largely built out after 66 years, it has not conducted such a study in more than a decade, and leaders need updated information on boundaries, traffic, zoning, and service delivery.
Learnard also referenced a cost-of-services study conducted with the Georgia Tech Center for Economic Development Research showing residential areas receive $1.04 in services for every tax dollar paid, while industrial properties receive $0.76 in services per tax dollar.
“Council members would like to expand our industrial base, thereby providing tax relief for residential property owners,” she wrote.
Council Member Clint Holland said he has supported annexation since first running for office in 2022.

Title: PJM requests approval from FERC for new behind-the-meter generation rules for data centers - DCD
Summary_AI: PJM Interconnection filed with FERC to approve new behind-the-meter generation rules for data centers, proposing a 50MW threshold above which new loads would be ineligible for netting their onsite generation against grid consumption, with a three-year transition period for implementation. The proposal also introduces three new transmission services tailored to colocated loads and aims to balance AI-driven data center infrastructure buildout with grid reliability and cost allocation. The filing follows FERC's December order directing PJM to overhaul its colocation framework in response to rapid growth in large data center loads and a rejected behind-the-meter agreement between AWS and Talen Energy for Pennsylvania's Susquehanna nuclear plant. Industrial groups have opposed the changes, arguing the 50MW threshold could render hundreds of megawatts of existing onsite generation uneconomic and deter new projects.
Article_Text:
PJM requests approval from FERC for new behind-the-meter generation rules for data centers
Would establish a 50MW threshold for behind-the-meter facilities
March 02, 2026 By Zachary Skidmore Have your say
PJM Interconnection, the US’s largest Regional Transmission Operator (RTO), has asked the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission to approve changes to its retail behind-the-meter (BTM) generation rules, part of a broader effort to address the rapid growth of colocated generation and large data center loads.
The filing was made in response to FERC’s December order, which directed PJM to overhaul its colocation and BTM framework, which has been in effect since 2004. The rules had allowed facilities with onsite generation to “net” their load, effectively offsetting consumption from the grid with self-supply, in turn reducing transmission and other grid-related costs.
The new proposal would establish a 50MW threshold for BTM facilities. The RTO said it intends to bring the new rules in over a three-year transition period. Under the proposal, new loads larger than 50MW would be ineligible for netting, with backup generation not counting towards the cap.
PJM also proposed three new transmission services tailored to colocated loads. These include an interim network-integration transmission service, a firm contract demand transmission service, and a non-firm contract demand transmission service. The rates, terms, and conditions for those offerings are expected to be submitted in a later filing.
The proposal also specifically referenced that the RTO was working with members to support the buildout of AI-driven data center infrastructure while maintaining reliability and ensuring equitable cost allocation among grid users.

Title: 'The digital colonization of flyover states': how datacenters are tearing small-town America apart
Summary_AI: Rapid datacenter expansion across rural America is creating friction between local governments and residents, with municipalities accepting major projects from companies like Amazon Web Services and EdgeConneX in exchange for tax abatements and infrastructure funding while facing grassroots backlash. In small towns including Wilmington, Ohio; Port Washington, Wisconsin; and Ashville, Ohio, residents are accusing local officials of prioritizing corporate interests over community concerns, leading to confrontations at city council meetings and in some cases resignations of experienced administrators. The tension reflects a fundamental shift in rural governance, where datacenter development now rivals traditional municipal concerns like zoning and infrastructure maintenance.
Article_Text:
A sign placed by opponents of a new datacenter in Saline Township, Michigan, on 8 December 2025. Photograph: Sarah Rice/The Guardian
View image in fullscreen
A sign placed by opponents of a new datacenter in Saline Township, Michigan, on 8 December 2025. Photograph: Sarah Rice/The Guardian
‘The digital colonization of flyover states’: how datacenters are tearing small-town America apart
The rapid rollout of datacenters across the US is creating a divide between municipal governments and residents
Wilmington, Ohio, resident Quintin Koger Kidd was so concerned last June with his local public officials’ alleged misdoings – open meeting violations and other discrepancies – that he filed a complaint in court to have the mayor and city council members removed from their posts.
When Koger Kidd later heard that the city supported plans by Amazon Web Services to build a $4bn datacenter on 500 acres (200 hectares) south of town, he was aghast. Amazon has sought a tax abatement that would see its datacenter exempt from paying property taxes for 30 years in exchange for the funding of local schools and infrastructure projects.
“The people up on city council are, for the most part, good people. They care about the community, [but] they have been taken advantage of by these companies,” he says, referencing the multinational giant. “They’re in over their heads … It’s the digital colonization of flyover states.”
For decades, administering small towns and communities in the US largely centered on zoning amendments, fixing roads and ensuring that trash was collected. But today, the emerging presence of datacenter developments is creating a vicious new divide between local administrators, who play an essential role in rural America, and the residents they are elected to represent.

Title: Amazon data center unit acquires George Washington University Virginia campus | Reuters
Summary_AI: Amazon Data Services is acquiring George Washington University's Virginia Science and Technology campus in Ashburn for $427 million to build a data or information technology center, supporting the company's rapid AI infrastructure expansion. The acquisition aligns with Amazon's 2023 commitment to invest $35 billion by 2040 in Virginia data centers, supplementing the $35 billion already deployed in northern Virginia through 2020. The university will retain an option to maintain programs at the site for up to five years and plans to use proceeds to address structural financial deficits.
Article_Text:
A technician works at an Amazon Web Services AI data center in New Carlisle, Indiana, U.S., October 2, 2025. REUTERS/Noah Berger for AWS Purchase Licensing Rights
WASHINGTON, March 2 (Reuters) - Amazon (AMZN.O)
unit Amazon Data Services is acquiring George Washington University's Virginia Science and Technology campus for $427 ‌million, the university said on Monday, part of a rapid expansion of infrastructure to support artificial intelligence.
The campus student newspaper said the deed authorizes Amazon to build a data or information technology center on the site in Ashburn, Virginia.
Amazon did not ⁠immediately comment.
The company said in 2023 it planned to invest $35 billion by 2040 to expand data centers in Virginia, adding to $35 billion it had spent on data centers in northern Virginia over the 10 years to 2020.
Tech firms' outlays have skyrocketed as they work to meet growing demand for AI software, computer chips and other infrastructure. The companies have committed at least $630 billion this year, and some ‌investors ⁠fear a growing AI-led bubble.
George Washington University, which is based in Washington, said the deal allows the university the option to keep programs at the site for up to five years.
The university said the sale was "part ⁠of a broader strategy to strengthen GW’s long-term financial health and to invest more deeply in our academic mission and community" but did not resolve ⁠the underlying causes of its structural deficit.
GWU took actions last year to cut costs, including axing some jobs, curbing travel, reducing ⁠capital spending, and temporarily cutting salaries of the university’s leadership team. It added it will likely still need to get smaller to address financial issues.

Bipartisan House legislation would halt all data center approval in Michigan until April 1, 2027.
The legislation aims to address concerns about massive energy and water demands, impacts on ratepayers, and threats to climate goals.
Several southeast Michigan communities have already enacted local data center moratoria, including Howell Township, Sterling Heights, and Pontiac.
A bipartisan group of Michigan lawmakers introduced legislation last week to place a one-year moratorium on data centers in the state.
Three state House bills would prevent the Michigan Department of Environment, Great Lakes, and Energy and local governments from issuing permits or other needed approvals for data centers until April 1, 2027.
The legislative package would bar the Michigan Public Service Commission (MPSC), which regulates utilities, from approving agreements with data centers over the same period.
A pause on data centers is needed to determine the best locations for the facilities and how the state will meet their massive power demands, State Rep. Jennifer Wortz (R-Quincy), who led efforts on the moratorium legislation, told Planet Detroit.

Title: Maryland legislature could ban data center on Baltimore Peninsula - The Banner
Summary_AI: Maryland Senate President Bill Ferguson is filing legislation to block data center development on Baltimore Peninsula and increase regulatory scrutiny of a $537 million BGE/Exelon transmission infrastructure project designed to support the site. The bill would require the Public Service Commission to evaluate whether proposed underground transmission lines through South Baltimore neighborhoods are necessary and cost-effective, addressing concerns that utilities are incentivized to overbuild without adequate state oversight. The transmission project would require excavation through historic Baltimore neighborhoods including Otterbein and Ridgely's Delight to connect substations.
Article_Text:
Maryland Senate President Bill Ferguson is seeking to prevent Baltimore Peninsula’s next owners from constructing a data center there, part of a legislative proposal aimed at curbing rising power bills in the region.
The Baltimore Democrat said Monday that he would file the bill this week, which also expands the state’s regulatory oversight of costly utility projects. Baltimore Gas and Electric Company and its parent company, Exelon, have a $537 million plan to build new distribution and underground transmission lines throughout South Baltimore in order to meet future power demands at Baltimore Peninsula.
Ferguson said the project comes with “innumerable questions” that the utility has largely ignored.
“BG&E has created a scenario with this transmission line that is wholly unacceptable,” he said.
Private utilities are able to recoup costs and earn a rate of return, essentially a margin of profit, on infrastructure projects. The Public Service Commission, Maryland’s utility regulator, is supposed to scrutinize the rate of return, but some lawmakers and critics say utilities are incentivized to overbuild and have questioned the BGE project.
Ferguson’s bill would require the Public Service Commission, the state’s utility regulator, to evaluate whether proposed transmission lines are needed and if the project is cost-effective. As it stands, the Public Service Commission doesn’t weigh in on underground or underwater transmission projects and the federal body that does regulate them often acts as a rubber stamp.
Del. Elizabeth Embry, also a Baltimore Democrat, filed an almost identical measure in the House last month. Her bill, which is sponsored by two delegates in Ferguson’s district but does not have a Senate sponsor, has had one hearing so far.
“I’m happy the Senate president is putting in legislation and I very much look forward to working with his office,” Embry said.

Title: Grassroots Resistance to Data Centers Rises in Pennsylvania - Inside Climate News
Summary_AI: Pennsylvania faces opposition to 52 planned data center projects from grassroots community groups and bipartisan lawmakers citing concerns about electricity demand, water consumption, diesel backup generators, and rural industrialization. A Facebook group opposing data centers surpassed 500 members within weeks, with activists noting stronger public resistance than previous campaigns against coal and natural gas extraction. Major projects include Amazon Web Services' deal with Talen Energy for up to 1.92 gigawatts of nuclear power supply through 2042, illustrating the scale of infrastructure development driving community pushback.
Article_Text:
Plans to build more than 50 data centers in Pennsylvania face opposition from a growing network of community groups, environmental activists and state lawmakers from both major parties.
Driven by concerns about the industry’s huge electricity demands, heavy water consumption, use of polluting diesel generators for backup power and industrialization of rural areas, an increasing number of grassroots groups are mobilizing in an attempt to stop or at least delay the massive buildout.
The opponents say the popular outcry against data centers is stronger than previous community campaigns against the resource-rich state’s extractive industries, which include coal and natural gas.
“It’s been unbelievable,” said Karen Feridun, co-founder of Better Path Coalition, a nonprofit that advocates for clean energy and protests against data centers and other sources of climate pollution. “Having done this for 19 years, and worked on fracking, I have never seen the kind of response where everybody is opposed.”

Title: Public comment rules adopted to keep data center discussions more controlled
Summary_AI: Wood County Planning Commission adopted formal public conduct rules limiting speakers to three minutes, requiring name and address identification, and restricting repetitive comments during its Tuesday meeting, a move that significantly reduced attendance from 150 citizens in January to just six speakers. The new rules were implemented as the region grapples with data center expansion, including Meta's construction in Middleton Township, with residents expressing concerns about industrial facilities in residential areas and questions about county authority to issue moratoriums similar to Fulton County's recent action. The commission clarified it can only make zoning recommendations to townships, which hold final authority on data center development requests.
Article_Text:
Smaller crowd attended Tuesday's Wood County Planning Commission meeting.
POSTED BY: JAN LARSON MCLAUGHLIN MARCH 3, 2026
Chris Coultrip has reluctantly come to the conclusion that people are going to have to coexist with data centers. But neighbors should at least know what to expect and how to protect themselves as much as possible.
Coultrip, of Perrysburg Township, was one of six people to speak at the Wood County Planning Commission meeting Tuesday evening. Though data centers were not on the agenda, the planning commission allowed citizens to speak during the public comment portion of the meeting.
“We have to learn to live with these data centers,” Coultrip said. But citizens also need to push for oversight of the massive sites like the Meta center under construction in Middleton Township.
“In my opinion, they belong in industrial areas. They don’t belong in residential areas,” she said.
Unlike the county planning commission meeting in January, which turned into an angry shouting match at times with nearly 150 citizens present, Tuesday’s meeting was much more controlled.
Too controlled, for some of the citizens.
Prior to the public comment portion of the meeting, the commission adopted rules for public conduct. “Please understand we want to hear what you have to say,” said John Musteric, president of the planning commission.
Those rules required people to give their name and address, limited them to three minutes each, and noted that those offering repetitive comments will be asked to move along. The rules are similar to those used by many governmental entities.
Musteric made it clear that commission members would not be engaging in discussion, but may clarify or correct information at the end of the meeting. He warned against poor behavior – something which Musteric himself was accused of at the last meeting.
“There will be no outbursts or other disrespectful action,” Musteric said.

Title: State Data Center Water Usage Legislation Gains Momentum in 2025 - MultiState
Summary_AI: Multiple states are advancing data center water usage legislation in 2025, with California, Iowa, and Michigan proposing bills requiring operators to submit regular water consumption reports to state energy or natural resource commissions. Several states including South Carolina and Kansas are exploring mandates for closed-loop cooling systems that eliminate water evaporation, while Virginia is incentivizing reclaimed wastewater use through conditional grant funding and Georgia is prohibiting nondisclosure agreements to increase transparency on energy and water usage. The legislative focus on water consumption reflects concerns about data center environmental impact, with reporting requirements varying from monthly submissions to state energy commissions to quarterly or annual filings with natural resource agencies.
Article_Text:
State Data Center Water Usage Legislation Gains Momentum in 2025
March 3, 2026 | Kim Miller
States are introducing data center water usage legislation to address concerns about water consumption, with California, Iowa, and Michigan considering bills that require operators to submit regular reports on their water use.
Several states are exploring state data center water regulations that would mandate closed loop cooling systems, including South Carolina and Kansas, which would require data centers to use cooling technologies that don't rely on water evaporation.
The data center reporting requirements that states are considering vary in approach, with some requiring monthly reports to energy commissions while others place the reporting burden on state agencies like public service commissions.
Virginia's approach to data center environmental legislation in 2025 focuses on incentivizing the use of reclaimed wastewater in cooling systems rather than mandating specific technologies, conditioning grant funds on using treated wastewater instead of potable water.
Georgia is working to increase transparency by prohibiting local governments from entering nondisclosure agreements with data center operators that would hide information about energy and water usage from the public.
Water use is one of the primary environmental considerations that state legislators are addressing in data center legislation. Water is used in data centers to help cool computing equipment, but how much water is used depends on the type of cooling technologies. As such, lawmakers have been slower to enact legislation targeting water use by data centers in a similar manner to energy consumption. Here are some ways states are considering measuring and regulating water used by data centers.
State Water Usage Reporting Requirements for Data Centers

Title: Alliant Energy Must Unredact Beaver Dam Data Center Negotiations - Daily Dodge
Summary_AI: Alliant Energy must refile its proposed Individual Contract Rate Agreement for a large data center facility in Beaver Dam, Wisconsin with fewer redactions, following a Public Service Commission directive responding to public pressure. The original filing had withheld critical details including the facility's energy requirements, and during a Feb. 24 PSC hearing, 86% of verbal commenters objected to the redactions while 93% called for contract rejection. The PSC extended the public comment period to March 9, though advocacy groups argue the timeline remains insufficient for adequate review of the newly disclosed information.
Article_Text:
(MADISON – WISC-TV) The Public Service Commission is requiring Alliant Energy to refile its data center negotiations with the Beaver Dam data center following concerns by advocates and residents.
The PSC currently is considering an Individual Contract Rate Agreement in which Alliant Energy is attempting to gain approval of a contract with a data center developer building a large facility in Beaver Dam.
Power Wisconsin Forward released the following statement highlighting the win for residents:
“Power Wisconsin Forward is encouraged by the PSC’s decision to require Alliant Energy to refile its proposed data center contract with fewer redactions. This is a meaningful win for the residents, small businesses and advocates who spoke up to ensure the public has the information needed to evaluate whether this agreement protects Alliant customers.”
The company stated the original filing limited publicly available information, including the amount of energy needed.
During the PSC’s Feb. 24. virtual public hearing on Alliant’s proposed data center contract, 86% of the reported verbal commenters raised concerns about the amount of redactions and 93% of commenters reportedly called for a rejection of the contract.
Additionally, the PSC has extended the public comment period from Tuesday to Monday, March 9. Power Wisconsin said the March 9 deadline remains insufficient as the updated, less-redacted information had only just been made available to the public, limiting advocates ability to fully review and meaningfully comment on the revised contract in a shortened timeframe.

, the largest U.S. electricity company, expects to build between 15 and 30 gigawatts of new generation capacity ‌for U.S. data centers in the next nine years, it said in a presentation on Tuesday.

Title: Onsite Power, New Geographies Will Dominate Data Center Market by 2028 - Bloom Energy
Summary_AI: Data center developers are increasingly installing onsite power and relocating to new geographies to bypass grid constraints, with McKinsey projecting data center power demand will nearly double from 80 GW in 2025 to 150 GW by 2028 driven by AI workloads. Texas is expected to capture 40 GW of capacity and 30% of national data center electrical demand by 2028, ahead of traditional markets, as companies prioritize states with access to natural gas, rapid permitting, and water. According to Bloom Energy's 2026 Data Center Power Report, the primary limiting factor for data center expansion is time to power from the grid, which can take utilities up to two years to supply new facilities. By 2028, the landscape will shift dramatically toward off-grid deployments using entirely onsite power and new high-voltage and DC architectures within data centers.
Article_Text:
If your plans for a multibillion-dollar data center are on hold while you wait for electrical power from the grid, you’re not just losing time, you’re losing revenue and market share. That’s why data center developers and hyperscalers are not waiting — they’re moving fast.
“Energy dominance is extremely important to AI dominance,” Bloom Energy CEO KR Sridhar said in a conversation with Maria Bartiromo during the World Economic Forum. “People are migrating to where the power is.”
The century-old power grid system was not built for the digital age. And the strain is showing. It can take utilities up to two years to supply new data centers with the power they need. Today, the primary limiting factor for data center growth is time to power.
Companies moving at the speed of AI require power at the speed of AI, and they are increasingly taking matters into their own hands, Bloom Energy’s 2026 Data Center Power Report reveals. They are choosing new geographies where time to power is shorter and bringing in onsite power to meet their needs.
These massive changes are happening at breakneck speed. In two years — by 2028 — the energy market is expected to look radically different: New geographies. New high-voltage and DC architectures within data centers. And many more data centers running off-grid, using entirely onsite power.
It’s going to be a whole new world.
Demand for power is driving change
McKinsey and Company predicts that the total power load of data centers will nearly double in just three years, from about 80 gigawatts in 2025 to about 150 GW in 2028, driven almost entirely by AI. That’s twice the growth rate predicted just one year ago.
With this fierce demand for power, Bloom’s Power Report found that data center leaders are concentrating their growth plans on a small number of top hubs, expanding existing campuses and adding new locations where they have the greatest ability to get power on the timelines they need.

Title: BlackRock's GIP and EQT announce acquisition of AES Corporation for $33.4bn
Summary_AI: BlackRock's Global Infrastructure Partners and Swedish investment firm EQT are acquiring AES Corporation, a global power company operating across 15 countries, in a $33.4 billion deal including assumed debt. AES currently supplies 11.8 GW of signed clean energy agreements to major technology firms including Microsoft and Google, and operates regulated utilities in Indiana and Ohio alongside competitive clean energy assets in the US and Latin America. The acquisition provides AES improved access to capital for future infrastructure investments while positioning the consortium to capitalize on surging global power demand driven by artificial intelligence and electrification needs. AES ranked as a top corporate clean energy seller in the US and Americas for the fifth consecutive year as of 2025.
Article_Text:
BlackRock’s GIP and EQT to acquire AES Corporation for $33.4bn
Finance & Investment
Artificial Intelligence
BlackRock’s GIP and EQT to acquire AES Corporation for $33.4bn
Posted on: 4 March 2026
Consortium to acquire the Fortune 500 global energy company, which supplies energy to AI tech giants like Google and Microsoft.
Andrés Gluski, Chief Executive Officer, AES Corporation / Credit: AES Corporation
AES Corporation, a global power company operating across 15 countries, is set to be acquired by a consortium in a deal valuing approximately $33.4 billion.
The consortium is led by BlackRock-owned Global Infrastructure Partners (GIP) and the Swedish EQT Infrastructure VI fund (EQT) and includes the assumption of existing debt.
Without this transaction, say the companies in a release, funding for AES’ future growth investments would likely require a reduction or elimination of the dividend to their shareholders, or significant new equity issuances.

Title: Zeolite 'thermal batteries' could cut data center cooling power up to 86% - Tech Xplore
Summary_AI: NYU Tandon researchers propose using zeolite "thermal batteries" to reduce data center cooling electricity consumption by up to 86% by storing and transporting waste heat from industrial facilities like chemical plants and refineries. The concept leverages zeolites, inexpensive crystalline materials already used in water treatment and oil refining, which absorb water vapor and release heat when charged at industrial sites, then reverse the process on-site to provide cooling without electricity-intensive compression chillers. Charged zeolite would be transported via truck or rail to data centers where warm server room air triggers the cooling cycle. The approach addresses the rapidly growing electricity demands of AI-driven data center proliferation while creating a symbiotic relationship between industrial waste heat sources and cooling-intensive facilities.
Article_Text:
Zeolite 'thermal batteries' could cut data center cooling power up to 86%
by NYU Tandon School of Engineering
edited by Sadie Harley, reviewed by Robert Egan
Add as preferred source
Conceptual schematics of the baseline and proposed cooling systems. Credit: ChemRxiv (2026). DOI: 10.26434/chemrxiv-2026-28wv2
Data centers—the warehouse-sized buildings that store photos, stream movies and train artificial intelligence—are voracious consumers of electricity. A surprisingly large share of that power never reaches a microchip. Instead, it is spent on cooling, hauling away the heat generated by millions of tightly packed servers.
As data centers proliferate thanks to the AI boom, their electricity needs are colliding with a grid already under strain. One solution is to rethink the basics of cooling. In a new study, researchers at NYU Tandon explore an alternative solution: use waste heat from nearby factories to cool data centers, by storing that heat in a material that can later deliver cooling on demand.
"While the electricity needs for data centers are still a small slice of the total U.S. electricity market, it is rapidly growing," says Dharik Mallapragada, Assistant Professor of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering and lead author of the paper published on the preprint server ChemRxiv. "This is an opportunity to 'bend the curve' and aim for a much more sustainable future, in a way that is beneficial to everyone involved."
At the heart of the concept are minerals called zeolite. Zeolites are crystalline materials riddled with microscopic pores, giving them a remarkable ability to soak up water vapor. When a dry zeolite encounters water vapor, it adsorbs the vapor and releases heat. When the zeolite is heated to sufficiently high temperatures, it releases the water again, resetting the cycle.
Importantly, zeolites are inexpensive materials that are already in use for a wide range of applications, including water treatment and oil refining. "Zeolite and its interaction

Article_Text:
An entrance to the Stargate artificial intelligence data center complex in Abilene, Texas on Monday, Sept. 22, 2025. (Matt O'Brien/Associated Press)
Maine lawmakers voted Thursday to advance a bill that would stop the development of new data centers in the state for more than a year.
The bill, LD 307, would create a temporary limitation on data centers with electric loads of at least 20 megawatts by preventing the state, local governments and quasi-governmental agencies from issuing permits or other approvals until 90 days after the first session of the 133rd Legislature adjourns.
That will likely be around October 2027, the bill’s sponsor, Rep. Melanie Sachs, D-Freeport, said.
That would give a new “Data Center Coordination Council,” also created by the bill, time to study and review the potential impacts of building the centers in Maine. The Department of Energy Resources would be responsible for convening the body.
Sachs has said the restriction is designed to address future growth without impeding projects that are already well underway, such as one pegged for the former Loring Air Force Base.
Members of the Maine Legislature’s Energy, Utilities and Technology Committee have debated the bill for weeks.
On Thursday, they voted 8-5 in favor of passing the bill with amendments, sending it to the broader Legislature. The committee vote fell along party lines, with Democrats supporting the measure.
The bill drew attention last month after Sen. Matt Harrington, R-Sanford, said it would effectively kill a project in his district. That proposal — a technical and industrial campus in Sanford that would contain a data center and onsite power generation — had not been previously announced publicly.
“This bill will kill that project,” Harrington said Thursday. “This bill will kill a massive investment in Maine.”

LOS ANGELES, March 05, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Researchers have shown that data can be sent at more than 100 terabits per second (Tb/s) through a single optical fiber over 2,000 kilometers, a first for this class of long-haul transmission. High-capacity, long-haul optical transmission systems are critical for building the next generation of backbone networks that can carry vastly more data over long distances.
To accomplish the ultra-high-capacity optical fiber transmission, the researchers combined a 200-GBaud-class ultra-high-speed signaling technique with a low-noise optical amplification method they developed.

Title: New York data mining company to open data center in Grand Island - KSNB
Summary_AI: Fortitude Mining, a New York-based data-mining company, will build a data center in Grand Island, Nebraska north of the JBS facility with an $18,000 annual land lease to the city. The facility will operate on an interruptible power arrangement tied to a nearby solar farm, allowing Grand Island Utilities to cut power to the data center if needed for peak city demand without requiring additional generation capacity. The utility can balance its load factor through the arrangement while retaining the ability to redirect all power from the data center to other parts of the system during high-demand periods.
Article_Text:
HASTINGS, Neb. (KSNB) - A New York-based data-mining company is set to build a data center in Grand Island, and city utilities officials say the project will not add costs to ratepayers.
Fortitude Mining will construct the data center just north of the JBS facility. The company will cover all construction costs. The site is near an electric substation and a solar farm.
Fortitude Mining will pay the city $18,000 per year to lease the land, with a 3% annual escalator.
Grand Island Utilities Director Ryan Schmitz said the energy supply to the center will be interruptible, which sets it apart from other data center arrangements.
“As a city, we’re required to carry enough generation to supply the city’s peak needs, and being interruptible, we’re able to take this offline so it does not set new requirements for those peak needs, which is different than some data centers people read about in the news and other places that drive generation demand,” Schmitz said.
Schmitz said the arrangement is designed to benefit all utility customers.
“It helps us balance our load factor on our system, which is good for all the customers. It was a long process that we analyzed over a couple of years. We had a lot of demand, so it had to be the perfect situation that didn’t add any burden to the taxpayer,” Schmitz said.
Schmitz said the proximity to the solar farm adds a utility advantage to the project.
“It gives us the ability to utilize the solar farm out there. So we added the solar farm, which will basically provide power directly to this without utilizing substation transformers for large parts of the day,” Schmitz said.
Schmitz also said the city retains the ability to cut all power to the data center if additional supply is needed elsewhere in the system.

Title: US Data Centers Could Require as Much Water as New York City by 2030, Study Shows
Summary_AI: A University of California, Riverside study projects that U.S. data centers will require between 697 million and 1.45 billion gallons per day of new peak water capacity by 2030—equivalent to New York City's daily water supply—due to intensive liquid cooling demands for AI workloads. Large state-of-the-art facilities using evaporative cooling can exceed 1 million gallons per day, with some planned facilities potentially reaching 8 million gallons daily. The infrastructure upgrades needed to meet this demand could cost between $10 billion and $58 billion, with communities hosting data centers bearing much of the financial burden as tech companies partner to fund water infrastructure improvements.
Article_Text:
AI is incredibly thirsty. The data centers that run these models already use massive amounts of water, and by 2030, those in the U.S. could require enough additional water capacity to rival New York City’s daily supply.
That’s according to a new study led by Shaolei Ren, an associate professor of electrical and computer engineering at the University of California, Riverside. The findings—which have not yet been peer reviewed but are publicly available on the preprint server arXiv—show that limited public water capacity is emerging as a critical bottleneck to data center growth.
To avoid burdening local ratepayers, tech companies are partnering with communities to fund water infrastructure upgrades, often spending hundreds of millions of dollars. “Those companies are profit driven, right? So I think clearly there is something wrong,” Ren told Gizmodo.
Data centers operate continuously, generating lots of heat from dense concentrations of servers, networking equipment, and other forms of IT infrastructure. Liquid cooling techniques are the most efficient way to prevent overheating and system failure, but they tend to be highly water intensive.
Tech companies will often argue that by using “closed-loop” cooling systems, their data centers recycle most of the water they use and minimize consumption. But even these systems can consume huge amounts of water because many rely on evaporative cooling towers to transfer heat outside of the facility.
For example, peak daily water demand—the amount required during the hottest days of the year—for a large state-of-the-art data center using evaporative cooling can often exceed 1 million gallons per day, and for some planned facilities it may reach 8 million gallons per day, according to the study.
The water bottleneck
Public water systems are engineered to reliably meet maximum demand at all times, so a data center’s peak water usage is a critical factor in infrastructure planning, system resilience, and operational reliability.

Title: Legislation would codify 'bring your own power' mandate for data centers, large power users
Summary_AI: Connecticut legislators have advanced House Bill 5469, which would require large electricity users with peak demand of at least 50 megawatts—including data centers—to supply their own power through colocated generators or new generation sources rather than drawing from the state's strained grid. The legislation, supported by Gov. Ned Lamont's administration, takes effect July 1, 2026, and mandates that colocated suppliers produce electricity covering 100% of customer demand plus a regulator-set reserve margin using new generation. Large customers connecting to the grid would face similar obligations through service agreements, and all projects must post financial guarantees covering at least 85% of requested service for a minimum of 10 years to protect existing ratepayers.
Article_Text:
EnergyPolitics & Policy
Legislation would codify ‘bring your own power’ mandate for data centers, large power users
A rendering shows ReNew Developers’ proposed 4-megawatt data center in Bristol, which would include on-site fuel cell generation. Contributed Image
State lawmakers are advancing legislation that would require large electricity users — including data centers — to supply their own power rather than draw from Connecticut’s already strained electric grid.
House Bill 5469, introduced by the legislature’s Energy and Technology Committee, would require any electric customer with a peak demand of at least 50 megawatts to either contract with a “colocated electric supplier” — a generator physically connected behind the customer’s meter — or demonstrate it can supply the equivalent of its full anticipated load from a new generation source.
The proposal comes about a month after Gov. Ned Lamont signaled a potential policy shift in Connecticut’s approach to data center development when he told a joint session of the General Assembly that the state would “slow down new data centers, unless they add more generation as well.”
Rob Blanchard, the governor’s communications director, said the administration supports the legislature’s approach.
“Connecticut welcomes innovation and the growth of technologies, but we need to do so responsibly,” Blanchard said. “Large new energy users should bring new power generation with them, so we can grow our economy without driving up electricity costs for residents and small businesses.”
At the time of Lamont’s address, the governor’s office said no specific legislation had been drafted, though discussions were underway.
The newly published bill would take effect July 1, 2026, and would require the Public Utilities Regulatory Authority to launch a proceeding to implement the framework by Jan. 1, 2027.

Title: A new map for connectivity - DCD
Summary_AI: AI-driven network demands are forcing data centers to rethink connectivity architecture as fiber density and interconnection speeds accelerate dramatically, requiring a shift from traditional approaches to support higher volumes of simultaneous connections. Leviton Network Solutions addresses this challenge by segmenting data center operations into four distinct subsystems—front-end, back-end, data center interconnect (DCI), and entry point—each requiring tailored cabling and connectivity solutions. The industry faces pressure to balance rapid deployment with standardization, as mismatched standards across interconnected infrastructure increase bottleneck risks and compromise reliability at scale.
Article_Text:
A new map for connectivity
As modern data centers become more congested and interconnected, keeping traffic moving demands a fundamental rethink of how the digital roads are built and managed
March 06, 2026 By Erika Chaffey, DatacenterDynamics Have your say
At the network level, AI has been the catalyst for an explosive leap in fiber density and connectivity speeds – triggering a series of interlinked changes and challenges across the data center.
The gap between traditional networks and those fit for the AI era isn’t a gentle acceleration – it’s the difference between a country road and a multi-lane superhighway. As a result, typical go-to products and approaches simply can’t keep traffic flowing in modern network environments.
These new realities mean that driving efficiency across every layer of the data center is mission critical for managing the dramatic rate of change, and ensuring operations can continue to scale quickly enough to match the pace of technological advancement. But speed is nothing without quality.
Amidst this fast-moving, complex, and competitive landscape, it’s often easy to push standards to one side in favor of faster deployment. Establishing standardization for new solutions is no small task – and it may feel like an inconvenient pump on the brakes. Yet, it’s more critical than ever if the industry is to deliver sustainable growth at scale, without compromising performance.
Against this backdrop, Leviton Network Solutions is helping operators to navigate these crossroads not only via its suite of innovative cabling solutions, but by redefining how modern data centers should be viewed today.
Rather than treating facilities as one daunting machine, Leviton views it as four distinct subsystems – each having evolved in different ways, demanding a tailored approach. These include: the front-end, back-end, data center interconnect (DCI), and the entry point subsystem.

CEDAR RAPIDS — Driving along Edgewood Road near its intersection with 76th Avenue SW, it’s easy to feel like a small fish in a very large pond.
A common passenger car easily is eclipsed by the stream of semi-trailer trucks and far outnumbered by lightweight haulers and other commuters headed toward the two data center campuses under construction in the area, north of The Eastern Iowa Airport.
Thousands of workers converge there each day to work on some of the biggest construction projects in Cedar Rapids history.
“You sit there and it’s just an endless stream of cars, and so many of them are from out of state,” said Mike Daly, a City Council member for the nearby town of Fairfax. Looking at the license plates, “you see Texas, Louisiana, Virginia, really from all over.”
Mike Daly, Fairfax City Council
It’s enough to make a passerby wonder, where do all those workers go at the end of the day?
The most recent Maxfield Research housing study commissioned by the city of Cedar Rapids posed that same question in attempting to outline the impacts from the massive construction projects on the housing market.
The two data center projects — one from Google and another from QTS — are expected to support thousands of construction jobs in coming years, all of which come with some sort of ticking clock. But until that clock runs out, those workers will need a place to stay — and that’s having effects far beyond the construction sites.

Title: Pa. Lawmakers Advance Bills Targeting Data Center Impacts - MyChesCo

Title: An Ohio suburb approved a data center; Now a gas plant is going up next to the elementary school
Summary_AI: Amazon's 2022 data center approval in Hilliard, Ohio has been followed by plans for a natural gas fuel cell facility built by American Electric Power under a 2025 state law that fast-tracks behind-the-meter power plant approvals, expected to emit 241,000 tons of CO2 annually near residential areas and Beacon Elementary School. Ohio's House Bill 15 transferred jurisdiction over these projects from local governments to the state Power Siting Board, allowing approval in as little as 45 days and effectively removing local communities' ability to block the facilities. The development highlights the infrastructure challenge posed by data center power demands straining regional grids, though Ohio lawmakers acknowledge the problem and have introduced various management bills.
Secondary_Categories: Data Center Development; Regulatory & Community Pushback
Article_Text:
By Anna Staver, cleveland.com
COLUMBUS, Ohio -- On the west side of Columbus, past new subdivisions and shopping centers, sits an Amazon data center that Hilliard approved in 2022.
It looked like a straightforward economic development deal for the suburban community. Four years later, the project includes something city leaders didn’t anticipate: a power plant.
Iran: Israeli airstrikes hit Tehran as Iran launches new missile barrage toward Israel
A 2025 state law fast-tracked the approval process for large customers to build on-site power plants. Amazon and American Electric Power are using that process to build a natural gas fuel cell facility.
The plant will sit down the road from homes, a park, and Beacon Elementary School. It’s expected to emit up to 241,000 tons of carbon dioxide each year—roughly the output of 66,000 cars.
City leaders say they have little power to stop it.
House Bill 15 put control over the locations of these behind-the-meter projects in the hands of the Ohio Power Siting Board, not local governments.
“What I want to say to other communities is this,” Hilliard resident Cathy Cowan Becker said. “If you’re approving a data center, you’re probably going to get a gas plant. And you will have no say. None.”
B.Y.O.P. (Bring Your Own Power)
America is in the middle of a power surge. Artificial intelligence, cloud computing and online services all rely on massive data centers that run day and night.
Inside are thousands of computer servers, along with large cooling systems to keep those machines from overheating. Together, they consume enormous amounts of electricity.
They’re straining our regional electric grid and raising utility costs.
Ohio lawmakers from both parties say it’s a problem. They’ve introduced bills to manage data center growth, including one to create a statewide study commission.
They also passed HB 15, a sweeping energy law that cleared the General Assembly with near-unanimous support. The measure ended certain utility subsidies an

Title: Plug Power Plans Hydrogen Offering in Top US Power-Grid Auction - Energy Connects
Summary_AI: Plug Power is planning to offer up to 250 megawatts of hydrogen-generated electricity in a potential emergency auction being considered by PJM Interconnection, which operates the 13-state power grid serving the eastern and Midwestern US. The supplier is seeking contracts lasting at least seven years and is already in discussions with hyperscalers, data center companies, and utilities to address surging power demand driven by AI infrastructure.
