The deal calls for Perry Township to receive $2.75 million upfront in exchange for foregoing future property tax payments from the project. It still receives 25% of the expected property tax revenue over three decades.
A resolution on the 30-year abatement was passed by a 2-1 vote by Perry trustees on March 24 during the board's regular meeting. The action followed a lengthy closed-door session related to the data center and other township issues.
The agreement is to go to Stark County commissioners for final consideration.
However, Canton is next in line to consider the project due to an existing Joint Economic Development District agreement with Perry Township, Stark County Administrator Brant Luther said.
Tax abatement is a tool used to encourage redevelopment and expansion. The incentives are designed to promote job creation or retention, as well as investment in new construction.
Perry Administrator Gerrie Cotter said Stark County led negations on the deal with the data center. Title: Proposed amendment to ban huge data centers in Ohio can move to next step
Summary_AI: Ohio Attorney General Dave Yost certified a petition from five southwest Ohio residents proposing a statewide constitutional amendment to ban data centers consuming more than 25 megawatts monthly, clearing the way for review by the Ohio Ballot Board. The group, Ohio Residents for Responsible Development, must gather approximately 413,487 valid signatures from half of Ohio's 88 counties by July 1 to reach the November ballot, though organizers anticipate needing close to 700,000 signatures to account for invalid submissions. The Data Center Coalition opposed the amendment, arguing it would place Ohio at "a very competitive disadvantage" and deter future development, while approximately 15 Ohio communities have already enacted local moratoriums against data center construction.
Article_Text:
Proposed amendment to ban huge data centers in Ohio can move to next step
The Statehouse News Bureau | By Karen Kasler
Published March 26, 2026 at 11:32 PM EDT
Statehouse News Bureau
A sign opposing a data center in Pickaway County, Ohio sits in front of a house.
A proposed amendment to stop future construction of huge data centers in Ohio can move to the next step, as backers of the idea try to bring it to voters this fall.
Attorney General Dave Yost certified the petition filed by five southwest Ohio residents, who want to ban data centers using more than 25 megawatts a month. That allows the process to move to the Ohio Ballot Board, which will review the proposal to determine if it's a single ballot issue. Then the group can gather signatures.
"It's on like Donkey Kong," said Austin Baurichter, an attorney in Georgetown in Brown County who co-wrote the petition and is one of the five people who filed it. "As far as we're concerned right now, the thing that we were most concerned with, we're passed through that. So we're stoked and ready to roll it out."
Baurichter said the residents working on the amendment in a few mostly rural counties have come together as Ohio Residents for Responsible Development. And he said they know after the Ballot Board decision, they have a huge job ahead: gathering 413,487 valid signatures from half of Ohio's 88 counties by July 1. Because a number of signatures are likely to be tossed out as invalid, it's expected they'll need to turn in close to 700,000.
"We've been planning this entire time. We've got maps of Ohio. We've got maps of people who have already reached out to us during this process who we've told, hang on for a second, we gotta get to Phase One, and then we'll connect with you for Phase Two," Baurichter said. He said the group is working on designating county captains and other organizing, and also hopes to have discussions with institutions that might support their efforts.

Title: Sen Sanders, Rep Ocasio-Cortez Announce AI Data Center Moratorium Act
Summary_AI: Senator Bernie Sanders and Representative Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez introduced the Artificial Intelligence Data Center Moratorium Act (S.4214), legislation that would impose a federal moratorium on construction of new AI data centers until safeguards are established regarding AI safety, worker benefits from AI gains, and environmental protections including electricity and utility costs. The proposed moratorium aims to slow AI development and allow policymakers time to establish regulatory frameworks before new data center infrastructure development proceeds.
Article_Text:
Skip to main content
Sen Sanders, Rep Ocasio-Cortez Announce AI Data Center Moratorium Act
Sen Bernie Sanders (I-VT) and Rep Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-NY) announced the Artificial Intelligence Data Center Moratorium Act, legislation that would enact a reasonable pause to the development of AI to ensure the safety of humanity. The legislation would slow down the development of AI to give democracy a chance to catch up. The AI Data Center Moratorium Act of 2026 would do that by instituting an immediate federal moratorium on AI data centers until strong national safeguards are in place to ensure that:
AI is safe and effective—preventing executives in the AI industry from releasing harmful products into the world that threaten the health and well-being of working families, our privacy and civil rights, and the future of humanity.
The economic gains of AI and robotics will benefit workers, not just the wealthy owners of Big Tech.
AI does not increase electricity or utility prices, harm communities, or destroy the environment.

Title: Google AI compression technology saves data center energy | Mashable
Summary_AI: Google unveiled TurboQuant, a compression algorithm that reduces large language model memory usage by up to six times, enabling more efficient data center operations and potentially reducing energy consumption and infrastructure demands. The technology allows AI models to run with significantly less computational resources, addressing power generation and transmission constraints that have slowed the industry's data center expansion plans. By improving AI efficiency, TurboQuant could reduce the need for new data center builds, which currently face delays from permitting, environmental opposition, and power infrastructure limitations.
Article_Text:
Credit: Thomas Fuller/NurPhoto via Getty Images
We have seen the future of AI via Large Language Models. And it's smaller than you think.
That much was clear in 2025, when we first saw China's DeepSeek — a slimmer, lighter LLM that required way less data center energy to do its job and performed surprisingly well on benchmark tests against heftier American AI models. (Ironically, it was built atop an open source U.S. model, Meta's Llama).
DeepSeek may have foundered on privacy concerns, but the trend towards smaller and smarter AI isn't going away. The evolution is on display again in TurboQuant, a compression algorithm that Google quietly unveiled this week via a Google Research paper.
The resurrected Dell XPS 14 is the PC MacBook alternative you're looking for.
The paper itself is pretty impenetrable if you're not an AI nerd who talks tokens and high-dimensional vectors. We'll get into a more detailed explanation below. But here's the TL;DR: The TurboQuant algorithm can make LLMs' memory usage six times smaller.
What does that mean? Less energy usage, perhaps to the point where running a powerful AI model on your powerful smartphone becomes possible. Less RAM usage, right on time for the ongoing RAM shortage.
Certainly, algorithms like this can help LLMs make more efficient use of the data centers they're hosted in — either by using the extra space to run more complex models, or, hear me out, by allowing us not to rush into building so many unpopular new data centers in the first place.
And that, paradoxically, could be a problem for the AI economy, at least as it's currently structured.
Why smaller and smarter will mess up NVIDIA
For the past three years, tech stocks have been riding ever higher on the back of one company alone: NVIDIA. Title: Meta pledges $10bn investment for El Paso, Texas, data center - DCD
Summary_AI: Meta has increased its planned investment in an El Paso, Texas data center campus to $10 billion, with the facility designed to reach 1GW of capacity across multiple phases of approximately 800,000 sq ft each. The project, which broke ground in October 2025, will deploy 813 modular gas generators totaling 366MW at a cost of $473 million, expected to come online by 2027, and is expected to create 300 permanent jobs upon completion. Meta's El Paso campus will be its third data center location in Texas, complementing existing facilities in Fort Worth and Temple, as well as nearby operations in New Mexico and Arizona.
Article_Text:
Meta pledges $10bn investment for El Paso, Texas, data center
Project broke ground in 2025
March 27, 2026 By Georgia Butler Have your say
Meta has upped its planned investment in a data center development in El Paso, Texas.
In a blog post shared by the company, Meta revealed that the company will invest $10 billion in the campus, which will have up to 1GW of capacity.
News that Meta was targeting a site in El Paso surfaced in late 2023, with the company operating behind the WurldWide LLC affiliate to acquire more than 1,000 acres of land in the northeast of the city. Originally, the company said it would invest $1.5 billion in the first phase, with up to five phases expected.
Each would see the construction of around 800,000 sq ft (74,320 sqm) of data center space.
According to the company, along with the increased investment, the site's construction needs will also grow, and an expected 4,000 construction workers will be on site at the peak of development. Once completed, 300 full-time jobs are expected to be created at the site.
Meta has also pledged to provide a $500,000 grant that will support a new workforce development effort in partnership with El Paso public schools, to restore 200 percent of the water consumed by the data center to local watersheds, and to add enough clean and renewable energy to the El Paso grid to match 100 percent of the electricity used by the data center.
Ground was broken on the project in October. In February 2026, the company revealed it would deploy 813 modular gas generators, slated to come online by 2027, to power the site. This would total 366MW, and cost $473m.
El Paso, located in the west of Texas and close to the borders of Mexico and New Mexico, is not traditionally a major data center hub. Dallas, Austin, and sometimes Houston, all to the east of El Paso, are more popular with data center developers in Texas. Phoenix in Arizona, to the west of El Paso, is also a large data center market.

Title: Aligned secures $2.58bn credit facility to fund US data center build-out - DCD
Summary_AI: Aligned Data Centers closed a $2.58 billion revolving credit facility secured by six US data center assets to fund its portfolio expansion across existing campuses in Chicago, Dallas, Salt Lake City, Phoenix, and Northern Virginia, as well as sites under development in Maryland, Ohio, Illinois, Pennsylvania, and Virginia. The three-year facility, backed by insurance, pension funds, and institutional capital, includes two one-year extension options and allows for future borrowing capacity increases. This represents Aligned's latest major financing following its $40 billion acquisition by a consortium of AIP, MGX, and GIP last year and previous credit facilities from Blackstone and BXCI totaling $1.6 billion.
Article_Text:
Aligned secures $2.58bn credit facility to fund US data center build-out
Credit secured by six assets
March 26, 2026 By Dan Swinhoe Have your say
US operator Aligned Data Centers has secured new financing to support its data center build-out.
The company this week today announced the closing of a new $2.58 billion credit facility to support the continued expansion of its US data center portfolio.
Supported by insurance, pension fund, and other institutional capital, the new revolving credit facility (devco facility) is secured by an initial collateral pool of six assets within Aligned’s US portfolio.
The facility features an initial term of three years, with two one-year extension options. Aligned said it intends to increase this facility’s borrowing capacity in the future to support its delivery plans.
“This innovative devco facility unlocks substantial additional borrowing capacity, serving as a powerful catalyst to drive Aligned’s continued growth,” said Meghan Baivier, Aligned Data Centers' CFO. “At Aligned, we are deeply focused on cultivating strong partnerships that are enduring in nature. We are incredibly excited to continue our relationship with the institutional lending community as we look to grow in the future. This structure represents a completely new tool in our toolkit, giving us enhanced flexibility as we scale our operations to meet growing customer demand.”
Aligned has campuses in Chicago, Illinois; Dallas, Texas; Salt Lake City, Utah; Phoenix, Arizona; and Northern Virginia. The company has further sites in development in Maryland, Ohio, Illinois, Pennsylvania, and Virginia.
Outside of the US, Aligned acquired LATAM-based OData in May 2023 and has a footprint in Brazil, Chile, Colombia, and Mexico. It has also invested in Canadian firm QScale.
Macquarie Asset Management sold Aligned to a consortium comprising the AI Infrastructure Partnership (AIP), MGX, and BlackRock-owned Global Infrastructure Partners (GIP) last year for $40bn.

Title: Google to provide financial backing for multi-billion-dollar Anthropic data center in Texas - report
Summary_AI: Google is providing financial backing, potentially including construction loans through a consortium of banks, for a multi-billion-dollar data center project in Texas operated by Nexus Data Centers for Anthropic, with the project valued at up to $5 billion and expected to deliver 500MW of capacity later this year with potential expansion to 7.7GW. Article_Text:
Google to provide financial backing for multi-billion-dollar Anthropic data center in Texas - report
Would include a loan for operator Nexus Data Centers
March 30, 2026 By Georgia Butler Have your say
Google is reportedly planning to provide financial backing for a multi-billion-dollar data center project in Texas to be used by Anthropic.
The backing could include construction loans for Nexus Data Centers, the company that will operate the data center for Anthropic, reports the Financial Times citing people familiar with the matter.
– Nexus Data Centers
According to the FT, a consortium of banks is competing to provide financing by the middle of 2026 for the project, which could be as much as $5bn. With Google's backing, this will enable the financing to have a lower cost as it will have a better credit rating.
Plans to provide financial backing come as Anthropic is involved in a lawsuit with the US Department of Defense (now known as the Department of War) after the department designated the company a supply chain risk over a contractual dispute in which Anthropic refused to allow its AI technology to be used for mass surveillance and autonomous weapons. Anthropic has warned that this status could cost it multiple billions of dollars and that "if investors opt not to invest in Anthropic in the future, Anthropic will be unable to train the next generation of models, further eroding its commercial position and investor confidence."
The data center campus, being developed by Nexus Data Centers, will span 2,800 acres. Anthropic signed a lease for the project earlier this month.
The campus is aimed to offer 500MW of capacity later this year, and could eventually be expanded to around 7.7GW. The site is located near a major gas pipeline, which would enable the site to be powered with gas turbines owned by Nexus.
Limited information about Nexus is available online, but the company is headed by CEO Ivan Van der Walt, who has a background in the energy industry.

LAS VEGAS, March 30, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Hyperscale Data, Inc. Title: How a Tiny Farm County Fought a Data Center Complex and Won | Civil Eats
Summary_AI: Talen Energy sought to rezone over 800 acres of farmland in Montour County, Pennsylvania to develop a data center in partnership with Amazon Web Services, leveraging a nearby natural gas power plant for energy. The proposal would have displaced farmers and further reduced the county's agricultural land, following Talen's recent sale of a nuclear-powered data center to Amazon in nearby Berwick that is being expanded to 960 megawatts. A grassroots opposition campaign led by local farmers and residents successfully pressured county commissioners to unanimously reject the rezoning request in February. The case demonstrates growing community resistance to data center expansion on agricultural land driven by AI infrastructure demand.
Article_Text:
How a Tiny Farm County Fought a Data Center Complex and Won
Talen Energy tried to get 800 acres of Pennsylvania farmland rezoned so it could develop the center with Amazon. A grassroots effort defeated the plan.
Farm fields in Lycoming County, Pennsylvania. (Photo credit: Hal Bergman, Getty Images)

On a bright day in early February, farmers Mark Shultz and Mike Leighow drove along the ruler-straight roads of Montour County in north-central Pennsylvania. From Shultz’s new Chevy Silverado, they gazed at the snow-covered farmland with a sense of nostalgia. These fields were at the heart of a proposal to rezone more than 800 agricultural acres to make way for a data center that residents had been fighting for months. Shultz and Leighow were just two out of hundreds of opponents.
Shultz slowed the truck as they passed the area where residents expected the data center would go, just a short distance from Talen Energy’s Washingtonville natural-gas power plant.
The company aimed to partner with Amazon Web Services for a data center buildout that would kick farmers off the land they leased from Talen and further reduce the county’s dwindling farmland. Even though the rezoning hadn’t yet been approved, workers dotted the open fields, surveying them for the anticipated development.
As tech companies and developers seek to power the artificial intelligence boom by building data centers anywhere they can, farmland is increasingly in high demand. Title: As AI data centers rise, N.J. town looks to fight development - SILive.com
Summary_AI: Monroe Township, New Jersey is pursuing an ordinance to ban data center development on a 159-acre redevelopment site, with plans to extend the prohibition township-wide. The move was triggered by a January planning board approval of a preliminary site plan for two 1.6 million square-foot warehouses in the area. Mayor Greg Wolfe emphasized the township is crafting the ordinance carefully to withstand legal challenges, while Councilman John Valentine argued the proposal doesn't go far enough to prevent future data center projects.
Article_Text:
By Jillian Delaney | jdelaney@siadvance.com
STATEN ISLAND, N.Y. — A New Jersey council is attempting to ban a redevelopment site from turning into an AI data center.
During a March meeting in Monroe Township, officials proposed an ordinance that would prevent a 159-acre stretch of land from ever developing into a data center, according to Advance/SILive.com sister site, NJ.com.
It is an initial move to set a stage in which data centers would be barred from coming to fruition in the township altogether.
Monroe Mayor Greg Wolfe said, “Do we think that is the end-all? Absolutely not. We are committed to figuring out a legal way to ban data centers altogether, but it has to be done the right way.”
Wolfe said, “The last thing we want to do is rush an ordinance, get sued, lose, and end up with a data center here anyway.”
If approved by the council during their next meeting on April 8, all data centers, “including traditional facilities and newer AI data centers,” would be pulled off the table.

Title: Oregon communities envision 9100 acres for new data centers, quadrupling the industry's footprint
Summary_AI: Oregon communities have identified 9,100 acres for new data center development that would quadruple the state's current footprint of approximately 2,900 acres, including Amazon's planned "exascale" facility near Boardman that will occupy 1,300 acres. Oregon's data center industry, already the third largest in the nation, currently consumes 11% of the state's electricity—a share expected to double in coming years—raising concerns about grid capacity and potential rate increases. Meta began the state's first major data center construction in Prineville 15 years ago, and while individual facilities employ only dozens to hundreds of workers, they have provided significant economic benefits to small communities despite consuming vast amounts of water and land.
Article_Text:
Oregon communities envision 9,100 acres for new data centers, quadrupling the industry’s footprint
Amazon already has 800 acres of data centers operating or under construction around Boardman. Earlier this month, it purchased 1,300 more acres for Oregon’s first “exascale” data center. (Photo by Mike Rogoway/The Oregonian)
By MIKE ROGOWAY/The Oregonian/OregonLive
Oregon communities are making way for 9,100 acres of new data centers, including the state’s first “exascale” project — a gargantuan multibillion-dollar installation the size of 1,000 football fields that Amazon hopes to build near the small city of Boardman.
The Oregon Department of Land Conservation and Development tallied up the potential new acreage last month for an inventory of new data centers in the pipeline. The records show communities are maneuvering to make enough land available to quadruple the data center industry’s footprint in Oregon.
Data centers are already straining Oregon’s power grid, raising fears they could prompt steep increases in electricity rates or temporary blackouts amid surging energy demand. They’re also consuming vast amounts of water in small communities like The Dalles.
By some estimates, Oregon’s data center industry is the third largest in the nation. It already occupies about 2,900 acres statewide, according to records collected by The Oregonian/OregonLive. Industry analysts say data centers consume about 11% of the state’s electricity, with that share set to double in the coming years.
Individually, data centers aren’t major employers. Some have just a handful of workers. Even billion-dollar installations typically employ no more than a couple hundred.

Title: Inside the Push to Bring DC Power to Data Centers
Summary_AI: The Current/OS Foundation and Open Direct Current Alliance signed a memorandum of understanding in March to align technical work on DC power distribution standards for data centers across Europe and globally. Both alliances argue that converting alternating current to direct current at the building edge rather than at individual servers eliminates redundant conversion stages and reduces energy losses, with a 1% efficiency gain on a 1 GW campus avoiding roughly 10 MW of losses. OCP's Mt. Diablo initiative has already demonstrated ±400 VDC rack distribution supporting 1 MW racks with reduced conversion losses, derived from electric vehicle infrastructure standards. The collaboration formalizes plans to present coordinated positions to international standards bodies for adopting open DC distribution as a common industry standard.
Article_Text:
ENERGY & POWER SUPPLY
DATA CENTER HARDWARE
Inside the Push to Bring DC Power to Data Centers
Two European alliances are collaborating to advance open DC distribution standards, aiming to improve energy efficiency in facilities by eliminating redundant power conversions.
Sean Michael Kerner,Contributor
Nvidia and the Open Compute Project have already explored DC distribution, with OCP's Mt. Diablo initiative demonstrating ±400 VDC rack distribution derived from electric vehicle infrastructure, supporting 1 MW racks with reduced conversion losses.ALAMY
Every server in a modern data center runs on direct current (DC). Yet the electricity that reaches those servers still travels most of the way as alternating current (AC), undergoing several conversions before it reaches the chip. Two international industry alliances argue that this conversion chain is one of the largest sources of avoidable energy waste in today’s facilities – and they have now formalized a plan to change it.
In March, the Current/OS Foundation and the Open Direct Current Alliance (ODCA) signed a memorandum of understanding (MoU) to align their technical work on DC power distribution and present coordinated positions to international standards bodies. The agreement formalizes collaboration that, by both groups’ accounts, was already underway. Current/OS is focused on DC distribution in commercial buildings, while ODCA’s approaches emerged from industrial settings. Both are Europe-based initiatives with global ambitions.

Alphabet Taps Debt Markets With 100-Year Issuance
Author: Thomas Monteiro | Photos: Shutterstock
Alphabet goes long—issuing rare 100-year bond to lock in funding for AI, data centers, and cloud ambitions.
Alphabet Inc., Google’s parent company, has issued a rare 100-year bond as part of a roughly $32 billion multi-currency debt offering. The move comes as the Mountain View, California-based company attempts to ramp up spending on AI and data-center capacity.
Ultra-long maturities remain highly unusual among technology companies, with Motorola’s 1997 century bond commonly regarded as the closest precedent in the sector.
Alphabet’s century bond was denominated in British pounds and ultimately sized at approximately £1 billion. “They’ve chosen sterling bonds not only to expand the investor pool but also because they may believe the real value of what they must repay will fall over time,” said Michael Brown, an analyst at Pepperstone.
Beyond the century tranche, Mountain View, California-based Alphabet issued a range of shorter-dated bonds in dollars, pounds and Swiss francs, with maturities ranging from three years to several decades.
The financing package comes as Alphabet accelerates investment in artificial intelligence, including data centers, custom chips, and expanded cloud computing capacity.
In its most recent earnings call, the company indicated capital expenditures could approach roughly $175 billion to $185 billion in 2026, underscoring the scale of computing resources required to support increasingly complex AI systems.
Against this backdrop, issuing ultra-long debt enables Alphabet to preserve liquidity while locking in financing costs over an extended time horizon. “It is one of the few corporates that can credibly issue at that tenor,” said Bruno Schneller, managing partner at Erlen Capital Management.
The century bond also signals Alphabet’s push beyond traditional equity investors, tapping long-horizon institutional

Title: Gov. Bob Ferguson has a critical choice to make on data centers

Article_Text:
Gov. Bob Ferguson has a critical choice to make on data centers
(Stock photo by Imaginima/Getty Images)
Washington has long been a global leader in technology. From cloud computing and e-commerce to clean energy innovation, the state has played a central role in defining our modern digital economy. But in today’s rapidly evolving technology landscape, Washington has a critical choice: stay ahead of the curve or get left in the dust.
While Washington’s tech sector continues to grow, policies emerging from Olympia will determine the fate of the very infrastructure that underpins our digital future: data centers.
Data centers are the backbone of our modern digital society that keep hospitals operating, small businesses processing transactions, students connected with their teachers, and so much more. Virtually every element of the 21st century economy is tied to a reliable fleet of data centers.
But as equipment ages, retaining the tax exemption for data centers to upgrade and refurbish outdated components of our digital infrastructure is vital to ensuring all of these daily services remain online, reliable, and efficient as technology advances.
Senate Bill 6231, which recently passed both chambers in the state Legislature, removes this exemption and discourages data center companies and their tenants from upgrading servers and energy infrastructure with the most modern and energy-efficient technology.
Simply put: data centers will keep old equipment, rather than purchase new equipment. Washington will fall behind on data center development, and the long-term job creation and green infrastructure commitments that data center companies have made will be challenging to meet, causing investment to leave the state.

The market is no longer centered only on Austin but is spreading across a broader corridor from Temple to San Antonio where land, utilities, and entitlements align.

But data centers are changing the shape of that map. What is happening now across Central Texas is not simply a technology buildout. It is a land rush for power, fiber, and entitlements, one that is beginning to reorder how certain parcels are valued, how industrial sites are underwritten, and which communities will define the region’s next phase of growth.
Austin has already crossed the threshold from emerging curiosity to serious market. Colliers now classifies Austin as a secondary U.S. data center market, with 645 megawatts of market size, 120 megawatts of annual absorption, a 0.6 percent vacancy rate, and a projected market size of 3,250 megawatts. That alone would make it one of the more notable infrastructure growth stories in the country. But the more revealing detail is that Austin is no longer being discussed as an Austin-only market. It is increasingly part of a broader Central Texas corridor, one stretching from Temple through Round Rock, Pflugerville, Hutto, Georgetown, Taylor, San Marcos, and down toward San Antonio.

Title: Hollow Core Fibre Pushes Networks Toward Lower Latency - Environment+Energy Leader

(STL) has launched India's first Hollow Core Fibre (HCF) cable, designed to reduce latency in data center and AI-heavy networks by transmitting light through air rather than solid glass, enabling faster data transmission at the microsecond level. The technology addresses growing performance constraints in hyperscale computing environments where traditional optical fiber limits are increasingly evident. STL's entry into the HCF market signals that production scaling challenges are easing, though widespread ecosystem adoption depends on integration capabilities across existing infrastructure. The company is positioning hollow core fiber as a critical component of AI-ready infrastructure where network performance has become as important as compute capacity in data center interconnects.

Hollow Core Fibre Pushes Networks Toward Lower Latency
STL targets AI and data centers with faster fibre design
(Credit: Unsplash.com)
Posted Tuesday, March 31, 2026 4:00 pm
The push to cut latency across digital infrastructure is picking up pace, and fiber innovation is back in focus. Sterlite Technologies Ltd. (STL) has introduced India’s first Hollow Core Fibre (HCF) cable, aiming to address growing performance demands from data centers and AI-heavy workloads.
As hyperscale computing and real-time applications expand, traditional optical fiber is starting to show its limits. The conversation is shifting from simply increasing bandwidth to rethinking how data physically moves. STL’s launch reflects that broader shift, where infrastructure upgrades are no longer just about scale, but speed at a fundamental level.
Rethinking the Physics of Transmission
Conventional optical fiber transmits light through a solid glass core. Hollow core fibre changes that equation by guiding light through air. The difference matters: light travels faster in air than in glass, which can translate into lower latency and reduced signal distortion over distance.
This isn’t a marginal gain. In environments like AI model training or high-frequency trading, even microsecond improvements can have measurable impact. By addressing latency at the material level, hollow core fibre introduces a new lever for network performance that goes beyond software optimization or routing efficiencies.
That said, the technology is still emerging. Industry-wide, hollow core fibre has been in development for years, but scaling production and integrating it into existing infrastructure remains complex. STL’s entry suggests those challenges may be easing, though widespread adoption will depend on how quickly the ecosystem can adapt.

NM state land commissioner rejects application for gas pipeline to power Project Jupiter data center
A map of the proposed pipeline Energy Transfer submitted in its January 2026 application to the U.S. Federal Energy Regulatory Commission. (Via FERC)
The New Mexico State Land Office has denied a request by a Texas energy company to build a segment of a 17-mile pipeline to fuel the Project Jupiter data center in Southern New Mexico.
The $60-million project, dubbed the “Green Chile Project,” would pipe 400,000 dekatherms of gas from El Paso daily to the private power plants fueling the proposed Project Jupiter, according to documents filed with the U.S. Federal Energy Regulatory Commission.
GET THE MORNING HEADLINES.
While most of the pipeline would pass through federal and private lands, the Dallas-based company Energy Transfer, which owns the Transwestern Pipeline Company, applied to the State Land Office for access to a parcel of state lands in Doña Ana County.
The Feb. 14 application requested a 5-year lease for 0.63 miles of state trust lands and said the company would install the pipeline, several buildings and a backup generator.
The State Land Office sent a letter on March 20 denying Energy Transfer’s application and barred construction. New Mexico Commissioner of Public Lands Stephanie Garcia Richard “determined approving the applications would not be in the best interest of the state’s trust,” Assistant Commissioner of Communications Joey Keefe said in a statement to Source NM.
“Applicants will need to find a route that does not include state trust lands,” Keefe added.
Officials for Energy Transfer told Source NM in a statement Tuesday that the pipeline remains in the “planning stages,” and said the final route will be determined by the FERC, which oversees cross-state pipelines.

Title: Enabling Long-Haul 400G Optical Networks - Bioengineer.org
Summary_AI: Coherent 400G optical transmission technology is enabling long-haul networks to transmit data reliably over distances exceeding 1,500 kilometers without frequent signal regeneration, addressing the growing demand for higher throughput and energy efficiency in backbone infrastructure. The technology leverages quadrature phase-shift keying (QPSK) modulation and advanced optical components capable of sustaining 130 gigabaud symbol rates and 12 terahertz optical bandwidth to maintain signal integrity across extended geographic distances. Recent innovations have achieved approximately 0.08 nanojoules per bit efficiency in optical modules, reducing the power consumption per transmitted bit while improving spectral efficiency and robustness against fiber nonlinearities and noise distortions.
Article_Text:
In the rapidly evolving domain of telecommunications, the demand for higher data throughput combined with energy efficiency has become paramount. With the exponential growth of internet-based services, cloud computing, and multimedia streaming, the pressure on backbone networks to transmit colossal amounts of data over long distances with minimal loss and power consumption is intensifying. The advent of coherent 400G optical transmission technology emerges as a game-changing solution, poised to redefine long-haul optical communications by vastly improving capacity while simultaneously reducing the power consumed per transmitted bit.
At the heart of this technological breakthrough lies the ability to effectively push optical data transmission rates to 400 gigabits per second, supporting distances that traditionally posed severe challenges. Conventional methods were limited by signal degradation, bandwidth constraints, and excessive power requirements as data rates scaled. Coherent detection methodologies, coupled with advanced modulation formats and state-of-the-art optical components, have paved the way for this new realm, enabling reliable transmission over spans exceeding 1,500 kilometers without the customary need for frequent regeneration.
One critical factor enabling this leap is the judicious selection of modulation formats. Quadrature phase-shift keying (QPSK) has been identified as an optimal balance of spectral efficiency and transmission resilience for such systems. By encoding information in both the amplitude and phase of the lightwave, QPSK enables doubled spectral efficiency relative to traditional binary schemes, while exhibiting robustness against fiber non-linearities and noise distortions encountered over extended transmission links. In practice, this means that a single coherent 400G channel utilizing QPSK modulation can transmit data across vast geographic distances while preserving signal integrity.

The information published about potential revenue for the school district was from an information sheet that was provided by the development team for the proposed data center. There has been no information shared with the District about any potential revenue from a data center.
It has been shared by staff from the City that negotiations regarding a potential CRA agreement for the proposed data center project have not commenced. In addition, if and when negotiations are initiated regarding a potential CRA agreement, they will reach out to the school district.
The Urbana Director of Administration, Spencer Mitchell, has contacted the District about participating in a Data Center Oversight Committee. Superintendent Thiel has agreed to represent the District on this committee.

Article_Text:
L. MACUNGIE TWP., Pa. – The Lower Macungie Township Board of Commissioners is looking to make changes to its zoning ordinances to address data centers.
The board on Thursday night approved advertising the proposed amended regulations ahead of conducting a public hearing.
The proposed ordinance permits data centers as a conditional use in three districts: Industrial, Highway Enterprise and Office, Research, Light Industrial Center. It requires the applicant to submit an annual report to the township "providing detailed metrics" of the water used by the site, the amount of water dispersed from the site into the public sewer system, electricity drawn from the power grid for regular operations, and the amount of electricity drawn from emergency or supplemental generators.

The legislation defines the phrase "data center," amends the use table, and creates additional requirements for data center land uses. The document attempts to distinguish data centers and data center equipment, establishes definitions for land development and provides buffer distance requirements.

Title: Washington County Commissioners say they don't approve data center tax abatement -
Summary_AI: Washington County Commissioners clarified they do not directly approve or disapprove a proposed data center tax abatement in Waterford, as the state provides a 75% abatement on real estate outside local jurisdiction. Title: Neocloud Storm Gathers as Data Center Deals Stall Over Credit Risk
Summary_AI: Colocation providers are increasingly rejecting data center deals from well-funded AI neocloud providers based on credit quality rather than price, marking a structural shift in capacity allocation. A recent example involved a neocloud provider offering $155-$160 per kW pricing with a 15-year term and prepayment of infrastructure costs, yet the deal failed after colocation providers deemed the company's creditworthiness insufficient. According to TRG Datacenters, investment-grade credit has become a minimum requirement to secure capacity, displacing pricing as the primary negotiating factor. The tightening reflects a market transition where colocation underwriting increasingly resembles project finance, with operators prioritizing long-term viability and balance sheet durability over aggressive pricing amid historically low vacancy rates.
Article_Text:
Neocloud Storm Gathers as Data Center Deals Stall Over Credit Risk
Even at $160 per kW, with 15-year leases, and upfront cash, some AI cloud providers are being turned away as creditworthiness replaces price as the gatekeeper for capacity.
Shane Snider,Senior News Writer,Data Center Knowledge
In today’s constrained data center environment, aggressive pricing and favorable terms are no longer enough for neocloud providers to secure capacity.IMAGE: ALAMY
A well-funded AI neocloud provider recently came to market with what brokers would typically consider a sure bet.
The company sought an initial 2 MW deployment, with plans to scale to 12 MW within 18 months. It offered a 15-year term, agreed to prepay six months of recurring charges, and committed to covering millions in upfront liquid cooling infrastructure. Pricing landed between $155-$160 per kW.
Despite attractive pricing and substantial commitments, the deal failed.
“The deal was still scrutinized heavily by colocation providers and will not close,” wrote Robert West, head of revenue at TRG Datacenters, in a recent market commentary based on broker and customer conversations.
Across the market, similar outcomes are emerging. In today’s constrained data center environment, aggressive pricing and favorable terms are no longer sufficient to secure capacity.
The breakdown of high-priced deals signals a deeper change in how capacity is allocated.
Related:Veritone Leans Into Oracle Cloud to Scale AI Data Pipelines
After two years defined by supply shortages and rising rates, colocation providers are no longer prioritizing price alone. Instead, they are filtering deals based on credit quality, long-term viability, and confidence confidence in utilization throughout lease terms.
West framed it bluntly: “[Investment Grade] credit is the price of admission right now. It is not a negotiating point. It is the minimum requirement to get to the table.”

Title: What AI Data Centers Can Learn from High-Frequency Trading
Summary_AI: AI data centers supporting real-time inference workloads are adopting performance requirements similar to those developed in high-frequency trading environments, including microsecond-level latency, deterministic networking, and high-throughput processing capabilities. The article notes that HFT infrastructure techniques—such as collocation near critical hubs, performance-focused network infrastructure, and powerful compute capacity—offer proven approaches for AI deployment without requiring wholesale infrastructure redesign. While HFT and AI are distinct use cases, time-critical AI applications like low-latency recommendations and autonomous control systems benefit from the same infrastructure principles that have supported trading platforms for years.
Article_Text:
What AI Data Centers Can Learn from High-Frequency Trading
Some AI workloads now demand microsecond-scale responsiveness, deterministic networking, and high-throughput processing – requirements honed over decades in HFT.
Christopher Tozzi,Technology Analyst
ALAMY / DATA CENTER KNOWLEDGE
Preparing data centers for artificial intelligence often means operating at the edge of performance. Certain AI workloads, such as real-time inference, require microsecond-level latency, deterministic networking, and high-throughput processing. Those expectations push infrastructure far beyond traditional enterprise norms.
Fortunately, they’re not entirely new. High-frequency trading (HFT) has grappled with similar challenges for years, and the techniques pioneered in HFT environments offer a practical starting point for AI infrastructure.
What Is High-Frequency Trading, and How Do Data Centers Support It?
HFT is about reacting faster than competitors, using automated strategies to execute trades at extreme speed. To work, these systems must meet three technical requirements:
Ultra-low latency. Market-moving events unfold in microseconds, so systems must move data and react within that same scale – far faster than the millisecond-level latency tolerated by most enterprise applications.
Highly reliable, deterministic networking. Packets must arrive in order and on time, without loss or jitter, so decisions remain valid when orders hit the exchange.
High-capacity data processing. Large data volumes typically flow into and out of HFT platforms, demanding ample bandwidth and sufficient compute and memory to ingest, analyze, and act in near real time.
Related:How Stadium Data Centers Power Fans, Operations, and Broadcast

Title: Opposition to data centers grows in Mass. cities and towns | WBUR News
Summary_AI: Lowell, Massachusetts recently enacted a one-year moratorium on data center development, becoming the first city in the state to implement such a pause, while Everett residents are campaigning for a similar ban. The move reflects growing opposition from over 140 activist groups across 24 states concerned about data centers' massive electricity consumption, noise, and air pollution from fossil fuel generators, with U.S. data centers consuming as much energy as Pakistan annually. Lowell is home to The Markley Group's existing facility, which the operator plans to expand, and the moratorium gives city officials time to review zoning regulations to protect residents from environmental and health impacts.
Article_Text:
The Markley data center in Lowell, Mass. (Robin Lubbock/WBUR)
Two Massachusetts cities are taking steps to prevent data centers from developing within their borders, joining a growing chorus of communities concerned about noise, air and climate pollution from the facilities.
Lowell recently passed a yearlong moratorium on data center development, and some Everett residents are hoping to follow suit, campaigning for a ban.
The rise of cloud computing and artificial intelligence has ballooned demand for data centers across the U.S. These server hubs process and store data, and require massive amounts of water and electricity to operate and cool equipment. They're typically built close to population centers, where they have easy access to the grid and to customers that benefit from faster service.
But they make unpopular neighbors.
More than 140 activist groups in 24 states are campaigning to stop or slow down data center development. Vermont Sen. Bernie Sanders has called for a nationwide pause on data center construction, citing environmental and ethical concerns about AI.
Data centers' demand for power is straining the electrical grid in some places and spurring the development of fossil fuel power plants in others (around 39% of natural gas-fired power plants built last year served data centers). In 2024, these facilities in the U.S. consumed as much energy as the entire country of Pakistan, demand that's projected to more than double by 2030.
People who live near data centers have complained about noise and air pollution, including from the use of fossil fuel-powered generators at the facilities. Title: Georgia lawmakers fail to pass data center bills, leaving tax breaks and power costs unchanged
Summary_AI: Georgia lawmakers failed to pass legislation addressing rapid data center growth, leaving existing tax incentives and utility cost-sharing rules unchanged. Key proposals included Senate Bill 34, which would have prohibited utilities from passing data center infrastructure costs to residential customers for facilities with 100+ megawatt peak demand, and House Bill 1063, focusing on utility contract terms to protect ratepayers from incremental service costs. A proposed moratorium on new data center construction and measures to eliminate state tax credits for the industry also did not advance before the legislative session ended. The failure to act leaves counties as the primary decision-makers on future data center development.
Article_Text:
Georgia lawmakers fail to pass data center bills, leaving tax breaks and power costs unchanged
Lawmakers debated ratepayer protections, tax incentives and a moratorium, but none became law.
FORSYTH, Ga. — Georgia lawmakers ended this year’s session without passing any major new data center regulations, leaving existing tax breaks and utility rules in place as counties across the state continue to deal with new development proposals.
The debate centered on several bills that took different approaches to the same issue: how to handle the fast growth of large data centers, who should pay for the power infrastructure they require, and whether the state should continue offering tax incentives to the industry.
One of the main bills was Senate Bill 34, which targeted electric rates. The measure would have prohibited utilities from passing costs tied to commercial data centers onto other customers unless those charges were recovered solely or mostly from the data centers themselves.
The bill defined a commercial data center as a facility or group of interconnected facilities used to process, store, retrieve or transmit data and with a peak demand of 100 megawatts or more.
SB 34 moved through the Legislature but did not become law before sine die. Supporters said the bill would have protected residential customers from paying for grid costs linked to large industrial loads. Opponents and utility interests argued the issue already fell under Public Service Commission oversight.
House Bill 1063 took a similar approach but focused on utility contracts instead of statute. The bill required new agreements with large-load customers, including data centers, to include terms meant to prevent other customers from absorbing the incremental cost of serving those facilities. It also aimed to protect residential and retail customers from costs tied to data center construction and service.
The House passed the bill, but it did not clear the Senate before adjournment.

Title: Building Trades View Data Centers as 'Infrastructure of the Future' - Business Journal Daily
Summary_AI: Building trades leaders in Warren, Ohio view data center construction as a significant economic opportunity, with the local construction council president noting that modern facilities employ closed-loop cooling systems and behind-the-meter power generation that mitigate traditional concerns about water and energy consumption. A single data center project typically requires approximately 1,700 trade workers across multiple disciplines including electricians, pipefitters, HVAC specialists, and ironworkers, generating substantial construction jobs and tax revenue for communities. According to union data, 93% of major U.S. data center projects have used union contractors, with trades workers seeing pay increases of 25% or more on such projects.
Article_Text:
WARREN, Ohio – The drive to construct data centers could have immediate positive effects across the Mahoning Valley, say members of the local building trades, despite apprehension from those who are concerned about these centers’ impact on utility costs and the environment.
“I think there’s a misconception,” said Martin Loney, president of the Western Reserve Building Trades & Construction Council and business agent for Plumbers and Pipefitters Local 396.
Loney said data centers’ reputation for using massive amounts of energy and water to sustain their operations has led many communities to conclude that consumer utility rates would skyrocket should one of these projects locate nearby.
“When data centers first started being built in the 1990s, that was all true,” Loney said. “Too much water, too much power.”
Since then, these projects have adopted technologies that mitigate any impact on water and energy used within a community, he said. “What they’ve done now is that they’ve designed closed-loop cooling systems” that would essentially recycle water throughout the campus, he said. Net water usage, on average, would be the equivalent of two new hotels, he added.
To offset energy burdens on the grid, many of these companies have opted to construct behind-the-meter power sources – a small modular reactor, a gas-fired power plant or a small turbine, for example – that would provide electricity directly to a data center campus, Loney said. Any excess power would be sold back into the electrical grid.
The result would be lower electricity costs, Loney suggested. “Now they’re providing electricity instead of draining it,” he said.
Loney added that construction of new data centers is vital to satisfy demand as more industries adopt the use of technological advances such as artificial intelligence. AI is today used across a wide array of business sectors, including aerospace, defense, finance, medicine, manufacturing, communications and transportation.

Once divided, Sand Springs residents band together to demand recall of elected officials
By Phillip Jackson of Tulsa Flyer
Published April 6, 2026 at 5:00 AM CDT
Opposition to the Sand Springs government’s handling of a massive data center came to a head last week when critics gathered over 1,600 signatures to recall three city councilors.
Cody Worrell of Ward 1, Matt Barnett of Ward 2 and Mike Burdge of Ward 3 are facing the possibility of a recall election after protesters delivered proof of signatures Tuesday. Their chief critique: The council’s vote enabling Project Spring, a data center project that had already attracted lawsuits and national media attention.
City Council voted 6-1 in February to rezone 827 acres belonging to the family of Alan Ringle, enabling the facility to be built on the site. Worrell, Barnett and Burdge were a part of the majority who approved. Burdge recently told NBC News he supports the data center because it will bring more industry and jobs to the town of 20,000.
Residents pushed for the recall of Mayor Jim Spoon as well but were not able to gather enough signatures.
Kyle Schmidt, president of the board of directors for the Protect Sand Springs Alliance, organized the recall. Tulsa Flyer interviewed Schmidt as he clutched several thick folders of signatures demanding the recall Tuesday. He was headed to give them to a clerk inside City Hall.
A group of 40 demonstrators were gathered there to protest the city’s decision on the data center.
“I would like to see change,” Schmidt said. “Right now I do think the deck is stacked against the citizens.”
The fallout centers around the Sand Spring government’s agreement last year to annex land to the developer White Rose Partners for the construction of Project Spring, a data center expected to be run by Google for cloud storage.

Title: New software could cut cooling energy use by 25% in data centers - Tech Xplore
Summary_AI: Penn State researchers developed AI-powered software that could reduce data center cooling energy consumption by 25% by dynamically adjusting power usage based on real-time climate and economic data. The physics-based AI model analyzes facility-specific conditions to optimize cooling recommendations, addressing cooling systems that currently account for approximately 40% of total data center electricity use. The software uses a virtual data center simulation to train an AI agent that learns to shift peak power usage to periods of favorable weather and affordable electricity pricing. The research will be presented at the IEEE ITherm Conference in Orlando in May.
Article_Text:
New software could cut cooling energy use by 25% in data centers
by Ty Tkacik, Pennsylvania State University
edited by Lisa Lock, reviewed by Andrew Zinin
Add as preferred source
The team’s software visualizes power usage and efficiency information of a given data center in real time, offering operators a comprehensive look at the facility, how energy is being used and recommended cooling optimizations. The above photo is a snapshot of just several of the insights provided by the software. Credit: Wangda Zuo
Data centers consume millions of homes' worth of electricity each year, with much of that electricity simply powering the cooling systems that keep the facilities operational. Researchers at Penn State are addressing this inefficiency by using artificial intelligence (AI) to dynamically adjust data centers' power usage to peak when the weather is favorable and electricity is affordable.
The team developed software, powered by a new physics-based AI learning model, that analyzes real-time climate and economic data to optimize data center cooling. The software works by simulating a virtual data center that serves as a training space for an AI agent—a system that can make highly complex decisions and learn over time. A trained agent can provide cooling recommendations personalized for the climate and economic market of a data center, specifically optimized for a facility's location.
The new approach is detailed in a paper accepted and set to be presented by the researchers at the IEEE ITherm Conference in Orlando, Florida, in May.
The art of chilling out
Cooling the systems in data centers costs taxpayers and companies millions of dollars in electricity annually, according to Wangda Zuo, professor of architectural engineering at Penn State and corresponding author on the paper. He explained that the intricate cooling systems inside a data center are a main reason these facilities are so power-hungry.

Title: Army plans to host new commercial data centers on at least 4 bases - Task & Purpose
Summary_AI: The U.S. Army is leasing underutilized land on military bases to private developers to build and operate commercial data centers for Pentagon AI and cloud computing access, with conditional selections announced in late March for hyper-scale facilities at Fort Bliss, Texas and Dugway Proving Ground, Utah, plus planned centers at Fort Bragg, North Carolina and Fort Hood, Texas. The Army's contracting documents indicate some proposed sites are located within one-half to one mile of civilian residential and commercial areas, and officials have requested proposals include local engagement plans to assess community opposition, acknowledging potential backlash similar to nearly $64 billion in delayed or cancelled data center projects nationwide due to water consumption and energy cost concerns.
Article_Text:
The Army is leasing land to private companies to build data centers that the service can use for AI and cloud computing needs. Army photo by Sgt. Dominick Smith.
The Army plans to host civilian-run data centers on its bases, according to recent federal contracting documents and official statements.
In late March, Army officials announced that it “conditionally selected” global investment firms to build, operate and manage commercial data centers on “underutilized” land at Fort Bliss, Texas and Dugway Proving Ground, Utah. The data centers are being built under an agreement with private developers to use available acres of Army property in exchange for access to computing power. According to the Wall Street Journal, Secretary of the Army Dan Driscoll said the data center at Fort Bliss would be “the first hyper-scale data center that the Pentagon has ever done.”
Additionally, federal contracting documents show plans for centers at Fort Bragg, North Carolina and Fort Hood, Texas. The documents show specific parcels of land that the service is considering for the projects. This includes an area “within ½ mile” of residential and commercial properties at Fort Hood and several potential spots at Fort Bragg within one mile of civilian areas and one-half mile of civilian housing. Often described as the physical backbone of the digital world, data centers are the physical facilities that house hardware for networks and IT equipment that processes and stores the data that underpins cloud computing and artificial intelligence servers.
As projects in civilian communities have faced major opposition, Army officials also acknowledged the potential for backlash. The federal notice to private contractors said commercial proposals should describe plans for “local outreach and engagement for its potential deployment of the data center on the Properties to assess local sentiment and any risks or opposition to execution of the Project.”

Title: Boulder City residents push back on proposed AI data center at developer's first public meeting
Summary_AI: Skylar Opportunities presented plans for Townsite Solar 2 (TS2), an 88-acre AI data center project in Boulder City, Nevada, at its first public meeting, projecting $2.6 million in annual revenue and 200-300 construction jobs despite resident pushback over environmental concerns. The developer revised its cooling strategy from evaporative to air-cooled closed-loop systems in response to community and regulatory concerns, though it still plans to purchase up to 900,000 gallons per day of waste effluent for dust control during construction. The facility, located 2.8 miles from the nearest Boulder City residence, will not connect to the city's power grid and pledged no impact on local water systems or Lake Mead. Boulder City residents remain skeptical and have called for independent environmental studies to validate the developer's claims.
Article_Text:
LAS VEGAS (FOX5) — Dozens of Boulder City residents showed up to push back against a proposed AI data center on 88 acres of open desert as the developer held its first public meeting Tuesday evening.
The developer of the Townsite Solar 2 project, or TS2, said it would bring economic benefits to Boulder City, which is facing a budget gap. The project would bring in $2.6 million in the first year and about $1.5 million each year after that, according to the developer. It would also bring 200-300 jobs during build out and 25-40 permanent jobs paying more than $100,000.
“We’re going to listen, and we’re going to adapt and try to make this project the best data center in the entire country,” said Richard Lammers, manager of development for Skylar Opportunities, owner of the Townsite Solar 2 project.
Residents raise concerns
Last week, about 20 residents opposed to the project met FOX5 at the site where it would be built. They had concerns about the environmental impact and their electric bills going up.
Tuesday, the developer said the facility will not connect to the city’s power system.
“They say it won’t use any water. It won’t affect our energy costs. There’s no significant heat footprint. All these things are false, in my opinion,” said George Rhee, a Boulder City resident. “We’re going to have to face the music if they build this thing… so I think I’m very skeptical.”
Developer makes changes to plan
The developer said it has already made changes to the plan. Previously, the developer looked into purchasing effluent, or liquid wastewater, from the city to cool the building. Because of concerns raised by the city and some citizens, and the ban on evaporative cooling in southern Nevada, the developer adjusted its plan.
“Rather than try to continue to push that issue, even though we think it would be better for the project and create more revenue for the city, we elected to pivot to an air-cooled closed-loop system,” Lammers said.

Market interest surrounding the AI investment boom has so far focused on high-speed interconnections within data centers. The growing demand for 800G and 1.6T optical modules has been widely recognized as a central theme in the optical communication sector.
However, as hyperscalers accelerate their AI investments, changes are occurring in the very structure of computing power deployment. Factors such as power supply, land constraints, and cooling capacity have started to show limits to expansion within a single data center. As a result, computing power is gradually shifting from being centralized at a single location to being distributed and coordinated across multiple locations.
This change elevates Data Center Interconnect (DCI) from traditional backbone infrastructure to a core element in establishing AI infrastructure. In other words, AI infrastructure is advancing from 'scale-up' and 'scale-out' to 'scale-across.'
DCI (Data Center Interconnect), the next pillar of AI infrastructure?
Traditionally, DCI was often limited to use cases such as backup, disaster recovery, or standard cloud connectivity. However, with the advancement of distributed computing, its role is changing significantly.
To make multiple data centers function as a single logical system, high-bandwidth and low-latency communication is essential. Especially for large-scale AI model distributed training and inference, the efficiency of inter-site communication determines overall performance.

Voter turnout was 129% higher than the April 2025 election. All four challengers won by large margins.

“They felt like their voices weren’t heard through this process with the data center. And they let their voices be known yesterday in this election,” Moore said.

Erica Carter is one of the leaders of a community movement to stop the data center.
“We spoke loud and clear yesterday that we don’t want this,” Carter said.
Rozilyn Daniels’ home is one of around a dozen slated to be bought out to make way for the data center.
“I’m so proud of Festus and the residents for coming out and voting. And showing them that we as a city do not want this data center,” Daniels said.

Festus Mayor Sam Richards said the four incumbents who lost are disappointed. He said the new council members cannot stop the project.
“That contract has already been signed with CRG. I signed it three or four days ago and CRG signed it,” Richards said.
Moore said he disagrees.
New council members said attorneys have given a different opinion to them.
“The council can vote to suspend the agreement. Any money given to the city would then be returned to the developer,” Belleville said.

Title: Downtown Cleveland's Largest Data Center Plans Major Expansion

Downtown Cleveland’s Largest Data Center Plans Major Expansion
Project would displace small businesses and demolish a row of storefronts along a key Downtown corridor.
BY KEN PRENDERGAST, NEOTRANS | APR. 8, 2026 | 2:15 PM
This article was published through an exclusive content-sharing agreement with neo-trans.blog.
Amid the growing controversies surrounding the expansion of data centers in Ohio, Downtown Cleveland’s largest data center is about to get bigger. And, as part of that expansion, it’s evicting small businesses and proposing to demolish a row of storefronts along a major Downtown street.
H5 Data Centers’ Cleveland Data Center, 1625 Rockwell Ave., already owns 2.4 acres of land at the east edge of Downtown for its 351,000-square-foot facility. It has been adding generators along the south side of St. Clair Avenue but utilizing existing parking lots, until now.
It seeks to demolish five retail spaces after acquiring another 0.75-acres of land, divided among a retail property at 1536-40 St. Clair and a second, larger property at 1616-36 St. Clair, each of which house a number of active businesses that had to quickly find new accommodations.
That’s despite H5 affiliate CLE-Infrastructure LLC of Beverly Hills, CA acquiring those properties more than a year ago in January 2025 for an undisclosed sum. The prior owner, Lakewood-based Green OZ LLC, bought them a year earlier for just under $1.5 million, county property records show.
“H5 Data Center has acquired adjacent buildings along Emerald Court (an alley parallel to and south of St. Clair) and will demolish those buildings to expand their generator yard,” wrote Dale Grieder, the project’s architect at The Foundation Architecture in Valley City in Medina County, in a permit application to the city.

Title: Opinion | The anti-data center movement is growing — and becoming more unhinged
Summary_AI: Maine's House of Representatives passed a bill this week imposing the nation's first statewide data center moratorium through 2027, while Wisconsin voters approved a referendum restricting future data centers in Port Washington and Ohio is pursuing a similar statewide ban. Opposition to data center development is growing across the country amid concerns about water usage and utility costs, though hundreds of billions of dollars have been invested by major tech companies including Microsoft, Alphabet, and Amazon, with over 700 data centers currently under construction in the U.S. The article frames these regulatory efforts as obstacles to technological progress and economic competitiveness.
Article_Text:
The mob-like movement against data centers that’s been gaining traction across the country took a dark turn this week. Indianapolis Councilor Ron Gibson (D), who supports a project to build such a facility in his district, woke up early Monday to the sound of 13 gunshots fired at his home. The gunman left a note on the lawmaker’s doorstep: “NO DATA CENTERS.”
The conversations animating the Editorial Board, delivered to your inbox every Tuesday
No one was injured, but the incident illustrates how opposition to artificial intelligence can metastasize into an irrational frenzy. It wouldn’t be the first time in history that deranged Luddites turn to violence to fight the advancement of frontier technology.
Maine is at the forefront of this movement. Its House of Representatives passed a bill this week that would impose the nation’s first statewide moratorium on data centers. The measure, which would last through 2027, is expected to sail through the Senate. Gov. Janet Mills (D) — who is lurching leftward as she trails in a U.S. Senate primary — now supports the temporary ban.
Meanwhile, in Wisconsin, the voters of Port Washington overwhelmingly passed a referendum on Tuesday to restrict future data centers. It was the first such referendum in the country.
Activists see this as a blueprint for opposing developments across America. In Ohio, an effort is gaining steam to place a statewide ban on the ballot this fall to halt new construction.
Each of these fights carries similar themes. Data-center opponents wildly exaggerate claims about water usage. They spread fears that these facilities will drive up utility bills, even though many projects include plans to fulfill their own energy needs. Others simply tap into general anxiety about what AI will mean for the future of work.

Title: East Manchester Township approves 2 new data center ordinances | fox43.com
Summary_AI: East Manchester Township in Mount Wolf, Pennsylvania approved two new data center ordinances that regulate siting, design, construction, and operation of data centers and establish a data center overlay district near Gravel Hill and Saginaw Road. The ordinances include requirements for 55-foot building height limits, 500-foot setbacks, and sound studies, though township supervisors indicated Pennsylvania state law compelled them to designate suitable locations for data center development. Local residents expressed concerns about environmental impacts to wildlife habitats, with some neighbors organizing to petition state lawmakers for a data center moratorium.
Article_Text:
East Manchester Township approves 2 new data center ordinances
The first ordinance regulates the design and operation of data centers in the township, while the second establishes a new data center overlay district.
MOUNT WOLF, Pa. — The East Manchester Township Board of Supervisors approved two new data center ordinances during a meeting Wednesday night. The vote was held after a public hearing regarding the proposal.
The first ordinance will regulate the siting, design, construction, and operation of data centers within the township. Meanwhile, the second one would establish a data center overlay district on a plot of land near Gravel Hill and Saginaw Road.
“Some of the concerns that residents have sometimes fall on deaf ears," Adam Wolf told FOX43.
Wolf said his family's farm sits right next to the new data center district and worries that any development would disrupt the ecosystem near the property.
“It’s definitely going to put a strain on the deer population, turkey populations—it’s really going to put a strain on the natural habitats there," he said.
Mike Schriefer said the board of supervisors were able to address a few concerns that were raised by a few neighbors. The data center ordinance would require developers to limit building height to 55 feet, have a setback of 500 feet, and have them submit to sound studies. However, he said the ordinance will still be unpopular throughout the township.
“Their hands are tied; our hands are tied. If a vote were held tomorrow, I'm sure 90% of the township doesn't want this here," Schriefer said.
During the meeting, township supervisors emphasized that Pennsylvania law required them to find a location in the township that would be conducive for data center development. Dean Kohr added that the board would vote to reject the motion if they were able to.
“There’s only so much we can do and we’re trying to make it, I’ll say, as unattractive as possible, but still remain legal so that we don’t get challenged," Kohr said.

CyrusOne gets go-ahead for data center in Sangamon, Illinois
Will offer up to 634MW of capacity at buildout
April 09, 2026 By Jason Ma Have your say
CyrusOne has got the go-ahead to build a data center in Sangamon, Illinois.
Sangamon County Board members voted on Tuesday to allow the construction of a $500m, 280-acre, four-building data center at the 13000 Block of Thayer Road, Waverly.
CyrusOne has stated that the campus will use up to 634MW of capacity at full buildout, use a closed-loop cooling technology, and generate a property tax revenue of around $100 million over a 20-year period.
This is a victory for the data center developer, who applied for planning permission last October. A substantial number of local residents came out in opposition to the proposal, citing concerns about the data center’s environmental impact, proximity to residential neighborhoods, and the procedural manner of the planning process.
But CyrusOne officials were questioned as to whether they had requested local unions sign up early for the meeting’s public comment session in order to try to take the opposition’s spots in the order, according to the Illinois Times.
This was because of a leaked email attributed to David Wells, the Business Manager and Financial Secretary of the International Brotherhood of Electrical Workers, who said that “the CyrusOne folks have asked us to sign up early for this to try and take the opposition’s spots in the order. That being said, hopefully none of us will have to speak,” reads the email.
“Once again, please show up in force … We all need this project.”
CyrusOne, which is jointly owned by private equity firms KKR and Global Infrastructure Partners, operates more than 50 data centers across the US and Europe. AI data center project breaks ground near Abernathy
Lubbock Avalanche-Journal
Updated April 9, 2026, 4:34 p.m. CT
An AI data center has broken ground outside of Abernathy, according to a company news release issued Thursday.
Aligned Data Centers, a developer and operator of data centers, officially broke ground on the 540MW facility named Project Caprock. The project will cover 313 acres in Hale County and have six facilities with a total square footage of 1.65 million.
“We are proud to bring next-generation infrastructure to Hale County,” said Andrew Schaap, CEO of Aligned, in a news release. “Our expansion into Northwest Texas proves we can meet both the increased capacity demands from customers while also meeting community demands for the responsible construction of AI infrastructure.”
It is estimated to have a $5 billion impact on the region's economy.
This article continues after the photo gallery.

The project will use the company's DeltaFlow liquid cooling, an air-cooled "heat rejection system" and a closed-loop design to recirculate water - a feature the company says will help protect the Ogallala Aquifer and ensure "the campus will not compete with the community’s agricultural or residential water needs."
In the release, Abernathy City Manager Julie Arrington said Aligned's decision to build the facility in Hale County is a transformative moment for her city's economic future.

Title: AI's Next Bottleneck: Capital Crunch and Bridge Loans - Data Center Knowledge
Summary_AI: Forum Markets is deploying up to $50 million in short-term bridge loans for Nvidia GPU purchases, targeting annualized returns in the mid-teens on 60- to 120-day facilities tied to AI chip deployments, with plans to tokenize those loans on-chain. The financing model addresses growing capital strain in AI infrastructure funding, where credit concerns have slowed deals for newer operators lacking long-term customer contracts. The bridge loan strategy reflects a broader market challenge: only 22.8% of enterprise AI projects launched in the past 12 months have reached production and met ROI goals, creating a gap between GPU supply and the infrastructure execution required for viable deployments.
Article_Text:
AI’s Next Bottleneck: Capital Crunch and Bridge Loans
Short-term GPU bridge loans and tokenization highlight growing strain in AI funding, with just 22.8% of enterprise AI projects meeting ROI goals.
Shane Snider,Senior News Writer,Data Center Knowledge
The race to build AI infrastructure is no longer just a supply challenge – it’s becoming a capital markets problem. A new financing model from Forum Markets, which plans to deploy capital into short-term loans for Nvidia GPU purchases and potentially tokenize those loans on-chain, highlights how far the industry is moving beyond traditional funding structures.
The company is targeting annualized returns in the mid-teens, on 60- to 120-day bridge loans tied to AI chip deployments. That mix of high yields, short durations, and alternative distribution via tokenization signals a market still struggling to align surging demand for AI compute with stable, long-term financing.
The shift builds on emerging signs of strain in AI infrastructure funding. In a previous Data Center Knowledge report, lenders and brokers described neocloud deals slowing or stalling as credit concerns mounted, particularly for newer operators without long-term customer contracts. The rise of short-term, high-yield bridge loans suggests the market is adapting to those constraints rather than resolving them.
Related:Hyperscaler Capex Snowballs Toward $700B as Firms Stage AI Builds
That financing strain is unfolding alongside what HyperFrame Research CEO Steven Dickens describes as an “execution gap” in enterprise AI.
“We are currently witnessing what we call the ‘execution gap’ – the chasm between having the raw silicon to experiment and the infrastructure velocity required to reach production,” Dickens told Data Center Knowledge.
His firm’s recent analysis found only 22.8% of enterprise AI projects launched in the past 12 months have reached production and met their original return-on-investment goals.
That gap is beginning to reshape how infrastruc
