Title: Neoclouds gain momentum in a supply-constrained world
Summary_AI: Neocloud revenues reached $9 billion in Q4 2024, up 223% year-over-year, with full-year 2025 exceeding $25 billion according to Synergy Research Group, driven by demand for GPU-centric infrastructure supporting AI workloads. Key players include startups CoreWeave, Lambda Labs, Crusoe, and Nebius, alongside crypto miners transitioning to AI services like Applied Data Centers and TeraWulf. Synergy forecasts the neocloud market will reach approximately $400 billion by 2031 at a 58% compound annual growth rate, with CoreWeave emerging as the most direct challenger to traditional hyperscalers. Both neoclouds and traditional cloud providers face component supply constraints due to competing demand for AI data center hardware, though Synergy expects supply to gradually balance with demand over time.
Article_Text:
Neocloud revenues are rapidly scaling -- reaching $9 billion in Q4, according to Synergy Research Group
Credit: ArtemisDiana / Shutterstock
Neoclouds are starting to gain significant market share, some of the coming at the expense of traditional data center infrastructure providers.
Neoclouds are defined as specialized cloud computing platforms that provide high-performance, GPU-centric infrastructure, primarily to support artificial intelligence (AI) workloads.
They tend to fall into two categories: relatively new startups, or crypto mining firms that are transitioning to being providers of high-performance AI computing services. Among the startups is CoreWeave, Lambda Labs, Crusoe, and Nebius, while the cryptominers-gone-neocloud include Applied Data Centers, IREN, and TeraWulf.
New data from Synergy Research Group shows that neocloud revenues are scaling at an unprecedented pace, reaching $9 billion in Q4—up 223% year-over-year—and exceeding $25 billion for full-year 2025. Synergy forecasts the market will approach $400 billion by 2031, representing a sustained 58% compound annual growth rate.
At that rate of growth, it will come at the expense of traditional cloud services, says John Dinsdale, chief analyst and research director at Synergy. “They are all competing for a slice of the same large pie,” he said. “Neoclouds are more specialized and focused, but in essence they are not doing anything that more traditional cloud providers could not do. And the demand is so big that there is room for both.”
The hardware isn’t all that different between a neocloud and a traditional data center. Neoclouds are GPU-oriented, but still use the same CPU, memory, and storage as a traditional cloud provider. The real difference is that GPU-oriented data centers are much more power dense and all the issues that go with that.

Title: Frederick County residents to hold their own data center forum - The Winchester Star
Summary_AI: Frederick County residents organized an independent data center forum for April 15 in response to dissatisfaction with the county's February 24 informational meeting, which was criticized for poor audience engagement and dismissive treatment of concerns. The community-led event near Stephens City will feature experts in industrial hygiene, geology, land use, and economics to address resident concerns about water usage, groundwater impacts, diesel generator pollution, and air quality. Frederick County currently hosts one data center in Middletown and is reviewing a new proposal in the Kernstown area after rejecting several proposals last year amid public opposition.
Article_Text:
Some concerned Frederick County residents have organized a meeting on data centers that will be held Wednesday, April 15, from 6:30 to 9 p.m. at Trumpet Vine Farm near Stephens City.
"There's a huge disconnect [between] the county government and... the people of Frederick County," said Samantha Armel, one of the organizers.
The group's meeting was put together after a county-organized informational meeting on the pros and cons of data centers on Feb. 24 at Sherando High School was widely criticized, leading the Frederick County Board of Supervisors to postpone a second meeting that had been scheduled for the same week at James Wood High School. Some supervisors referred to the audience at the first forum as an "angry mob" and "agitators." And many attendees were frustrated by a lack of clear answers from panelists and a lack of microphones for audience members to ask questions or make comments in real time, leading some to shout from their seats. Others left out of frustration. A new date for the county's second data center forum has not been announced.
Data centers are a hot-button issue across the country, and Frederick County is no different. Concerns include water and electricity usage and pollution and noise from diesel generators.
Frederick County has one data center in the Middletown area that has been there for years, but last year the Board of Supervisors rejected several data center proposals amid public outcry. A new data center proposal in the county's Kernstown area south of Winchester recently began the review process.
Armel, a longtime county resident who works in real estate and founded Frederick County Homesteaders (FCH) in 2022 to highlight and educate people about agriculture in the county, said FCH members started approaching her about a year ago with concerns regarding how data centers might impact livestock, groundwater, private wells, and the air.

Enhances high-capacity fiber footprint with new on-net presence at Flexential data centers in Dallas and Plano, Texas and Minneapolis–St. Paul, Minnesota, strengthening network options for carriers, cloud providers, and enterprises
THE WOODLANDS, Texas--(BUSINESS WIRE)--Fidium, a leading provider of high-capacity fiber network solutions, today announced an expanded relationship with Flexential, a premier provider of data center colocation and hybrid IT solutions. Through the Flexential Marketplace, part of the recently launched FlexAnywhere® platform, Fidium will extend its reach and visibility within Flexential’s data centers in Dallas and Plano, Texas and Minneapolis–St. Paul, Minnesota, enabling enterprises, carriers, and hyperscalers to scale connectivity faster than ever before.
The agreement builds on Fidium’s rapidly expanding national data center footprint and recent partnerships, providing the infrastructure enterprises need for AI, cloud, and edge workloads with direct access to 100G and 800G Wavelength services, Ethernet, Dark Fiber, and Construction-as-a-Service (CaaS) offerings.
“In an era where connectivity defines possibility, our customers increasingly view connectivity as a strategic layer of digital transformation. They want choice, speed, reach and simplicity when building out their network edge,” said Sean Baillie, SVP of Carrier, Channel, Data Center and Hyperscale at Fidium. “By partnering with Flexential, we’re giving enterprises and carriers the ability to scale capacity at the speed their businesses move.”
With more than $1.9 billion invested in fiber infrastructure since 2020, Fidium offers the most unique routes and most dense metro in its 20+ state service area. Title: Report details Arizona data centers' challenges | News | ahwatukee.com
Summary_AI: Arizona's Governor's Office of Resiliency released a report identifying large-load customers, particularly data centers operating continuously, as challenges to grid reliability and electric rates, noting that the pace of facility construction is exceeding historical growth patterns and limiting utility maintenance windows. The report was prompted in part by a 1-million-square-foot data center-office campus being developed by California-based Menlo Equities on 40.6 acres at 48th Street and Thistle Landing Drive in Ahwatukee, a project that has faced neighborhood opposition. Arizona utilities are being called to adopt customized approaches and greater coordination across service territories to accommodate large-load growth while maintaining grid reliability and cost fairness for residential ratepayers.
Article_Text:
Homeowners in neighborhoods near the 1 million-square-foot data center-office campus that’s going up on 48th Street and Thistle Landing Drive are continuing to try to stop the project. They maintained a booth at the Ahwatukee Kiwanis Club’s Spring Festival last Saturday to raise awareness about the project and drum up support for delaying it pending further study.
As a planned 1-million-square-foot data center-office campus continues to roil Ahwatukee, a new state report cited such facilities and other “large-load” customers as a challenge to grid reliability and electric rates.
In a lengthy report released last week, the Governor’s Office of Resiliency’s Arizona Energy Promise Taskforce called for “a suite of policy considerations to enable Arizona to accommodate economic growth without compromising grid reliability or unfairly increasing costs for existing customers.”
“The volume and pace of large-load customer growth require new, customized approaches to accommodate economic development while protecting residential and other ratepayers,” it says.
It adds that the pace of large-load facility construction is exceeding historical growth patterns and challenging how utilities make investment and planning decisions.
It also warns, “The operating profiles of large-load customers, particularly data centers that operate continuously, pose unique challenges for grid operations and maintenance.
“Arizona utilities traditionally schedule maintenance during periods of lower demand, such as winter months with reduced air conditioning use. Continuous demand from large load facilities can limit these windows, requiring changes to maintenance practices, redundancy planning, and outage management to preserve reliability.
“Large-load growth is occurring across utility service territories and impacting regional infrastructure systems, creating a need for greater coordination.”
Scores of business leaders and professionals were on the panel that produced the report.

Title: U.S. Hyperscale Investment Shifts Decisively Inland | Synergy Research Group
Summary_AI: U.S. hyperscale data center investment is shifting decisively inland, with Texas and Midwest states projected to account for 53% of new hyperscale capacity coming online over the next few years, up from 33% of current operational capacity. While Northern Virginia remains the largest existing hub, major cloud providers including Amazon, Google, Meta, and Microsoft are deploying gigawatt-scale campuses in non-traditional locations such as Abilene, Mount Pleasant, South Bend, and Kansas City, driven by greater power availability. Synergy's analysis of 21 major cloud and internet service firms shows 437 new U.S. hyperscale data centers in the pipeline out of 803 globally, reflecting continued expansion of larger-capacity facilities. Amazon, Microsoft, and Google collectively account for 58% of global hyperscale data center capacity.
Article_Text:
U.S. Hyperscale Investment Shifts Decisively Inland
RENO, NV, April 13, 2026
New data from Synergy shows that the focus of US hyperscale developments has shifted substantially away from coastal regions and towards the center of the country, with Texas and Midwestern states being the main beneficiaries. At the end of 2025, Texas and the Midwest accounted for 33% of operational US hyperscale data center capacity, while Synergy's pipeline of future hyperscale data centers shows that they will account for 53% of new capacity coming online over the next few years. Northern Virginia has, and will continue to have, the single biggest concentration of data centers, but as AI has supercharged investment in infrastructure, the geographic focus has moved more inland, seeking out areas where power is more readily available. By a wide margin Texas is the most prominent state in the pipeline. In the Midwest, Wisconsin, Indiana, Michigan and Missouri will all grow rapidly in importance, as they have attracted multiple major projects from Amazon, Google, Meta, Microsoft, OpenAI and CoreWeave.
The research is based on an analysis of the data center footprint and future plans of 21 of the world’s major cloud and internet service firms, including the largest operators in SaaS, IaaS, PaaS, search, social networking, e-commerce and gaming. Together they had 1,360 operational hyperscale data centers at the end of 2025, of which 580 are in the US. Synergy's known pipeline of future hyperscale data centers has another 803 data centers that will come online in the next few years, of which 437 are in the US. While there are more operational data centers than there are in the pipeline, the pipeline actually has a bigger overall capacity, reflecting the fact that hyperscale data centers have continued to increase in size in terms of their IT capacity. The companies with the broadest data center footprint are the leading cloud providers – Amazon, Microsoft and Google. In addition to a huge data

Title: Hyperscale Data Centers to Reach 67% of Global Capacity by 2031 - Broadband Breakfast
Summary_AI: Hyperscale operators including Google, Microsoft, and AWS are projected to control 67 percent of global data center capacity by 2031, up from 48 percent currently, as enterprise on-premises infrastructure declines from 56 percent in 2018 to an expected 19 percent by 2031. These hyperscale firms operated approximately 1,360 large data centers globally by end of 2025—nearly triple the 2018 count—and are investing more than $500 billion collectively in infrastructure to support AI deployment. Microsoft alone plans to increase its total AI capacity by over 80 percent this year and double its data center footprint over the next two years, reflecting surging demand for AI computing resources. The rapid expansion is creating significant pressure on power systems, with the U.S. Energy Information Administration projecting potential electricity price increases of up to 79 percent in parts of Texas by 2027.

WASHINGTON, April 10, 2026 – Hyperscale operators including Google, Microsoft and Amazon Web Services are projected to account for 67 percent of global data center capacity by 2031.

Enterprise on-premises data centers accounted for 56 percent of global capacity in 2018, compared to 32 percent today, and are projected to fall to 19 percent by 2031. Non-hyperscale colocation providers make up 20 percent.

“Cloud and consumer-oriented digital services have been driving changes in data center deployment patterns for many years now, but over the last three years AI technology has accelerated those changes,” said chief analyst at Synergy Research Group John Dinsdale.

BY HAILEY MENSIK/PHOENIX BUSINESS JOURNAL
A controversial data center project in the West Valley is moving ahead.
Maricopa County’s Planning and Zoning Commission unanimously approved a military special use permit for Project Baccara – a massive data center campus and accompanying gas power plant during an April 9 meeting.
The developer on the project, Takanock LLC, is a Michigan-based data center firm with four sites in three states. Across those sites it is investing $36 billion to prepare the land and build out massive new campuses, according to the company.
The 160-acre site it’s working on in the West Valley is adjacent to Luke Air Force Base and just outside the city limits of Surprise and Glendale.

Title: Nationwide boom in AI data centers stirs resistance - AOL.com
Summary_AI: Archbald, Pennsylvania, a community of 7,000 in northeastern coal country, is facing proposed development of half-a-dozen data centers, sparking local resistance over environmental and utility cost concerns despite strong industry demand for AI infrastructure. Residents, organized through neighborhood associations, successfully blocked at least one 18-campus data center project at a contentious March 10 borough meeting, reflecting broader tensions between tech companies seeking land, water, and power-rich locations and communities wary of landscape disruption and rising electricity bills. The pushback contrasts with established data center hubs like Loudoun County, Virginia's "Data Center Alley," where over 4,000 data centers operate nationwide, with the sector valued at hundreds of billions of dollars.
Article_Text:
Updated Sun, April 12, 2026 at 5:26 PM MDT
Archbald is a little corner of northeastern Pennsylvania where coal used to be big business. But now, there is a new boom: data centers.
Kayleigh Cornell, a teacher, and Sarah Gabriel, an ICU nurse, run a neighborhood association in this community of 7,000, where a half-dozen data centers have been proposed. "It is a really nice small town," said Gabriel. "It feels like home."
Cornell said, "I just really love it, and I can't imagine being anywhere else."
But they, and others, are alarmed about the possible impact of data centers on the environment, their electric bills, and the town's character.
Gabriel said, "It's gonna just completely change the landscape. Anywhere there's trees, there's probably not going to be any any longer."
Cornell says they are determined: "We'll stop it if we could help it."
Correspondent Robert Costa with Kayleigh Cornell and Sarah Gabriel, of the Archbald Neighborhood Association, at the site of proposed data centers in their small Pennsylvania community. / Credit: CBS News
Stopping it won't be easy. Demand is huge: tech companies say they need these massive structures, full of computing power, to fuel the AI revolution. So, developers are rushing to build them in places like Archbald – rich in land, water and power. But there is an intensifying debate over the scope of jobs and revenue they bring. And many communities are on edge over rising electric utility bills.
A March 10 community meeting, at Valley View High School in Archbald, Pa., was not welcoming to those proposing the construction of data centers. / Credit: CBS News
In Archbald, the push to slow things down has upended local politics. At a March 10 borough meeting, where many held up signs reading "No data centers," residents yelled at representatives of the proposed centers to "get out of here."
"We made up our minds: go home!" One woman shouted.
One project, an application for a campus of 18 data centers, has hit a roadblock.

Title: When data centers bring their own power supply - DCD
Summary_AI: Global data center electricity consumption is projected to double to 945 TWh within four years, with U.S. construction spending on data centers reaching $74 billion in 2024, but hyperscalers including Meta, Google, Microsoft, and others are bypassing traditional grid connections due to seven-year interconnection timelines and instead deploying independent power infrastructure. This "bring your own power" trend shifts grid infrastructure costs to residential ratepayers, increases per-kilowatt energy expenses, and reduces overall grid resilience, while construction firms face pressure to accelerate project timelines despite a shortage of approximately 300,000 workers needed to meet demand. Permitting bottlenecks are a critical constraint on both grid modernization and data center deployment, with the Department of Energy warning that outdated infrastructure could increase blackouts 100-fold by 2030.
Article_Text:
When data centers bring their own power supply
Hari Vasudevan is the founder and CEO of KYRO AI.
Why utilities and construction CFOs need a new tech playbook
April 12, 2026 Have your say
Global electricity consumption for data centers is set to double, reaching approximately 945 TWh in the next four years. Scaling data centers to meet this insatiable appetite for power is expected to cost nearly $7 trillion. But there is a huge reliability concern: half of data centers experienced at least one impactful outage in the past three years.
This data center boom, thirst for power, and its reliability concerns mean a ‘second wind' for construction, representing about $74 billion in spending on data centers in the U.S. in 2024 alone.
Construction firms are under pressure from data center demands for more efficient project execution, faster technology adoption, and a shrinking workforce. The construction labor shortage means that about 300,000 workers are needed to meet project demand, and fast. This is a massive problem for an industry that is historically slow to adopt technology and still heavily relies on manual processes that are hard to scale and often inefficient.
These converging constraints from both grid infrastructure and construction technology reticence in the age of AI require a complete rethink of these sectors’ role in the data center gold rush.
Why hyperscalers are bypassing the grid
Hyperscalers, such as Meta, Google, Microsoft, xAI, OpenAI, and Oracle, are not waiting for the grid to catch up. As current infrastructure stands, connecting large-scale data centers to the electric grid is dragging out timelines to seven years. This is untenable for hyperscalers that are racing against the clock to get data centers up and running as quickly as possible.

Applied Digital (NasdaqGS:APLD) is separating its cloud business and combining it with Exo Bionic Holdings to form a focused GPU-optimized AI infrastructure platform.

Operations at Delta Forge 1 are targeted to begin by mid 2027, framing a multi-year build-out for Applied Digital's AI cloud ambitions.
For investors following NasdaqGS:APLD, these moves come as AI infrastructure needs expand and data center power capacity becomes a key constraint across the sector. Applied Digital has been positioning itself as a provider of high-performance computing capacity, with an emphasis on AI and GPU-centric workloads. The new structure and campus are intended to sharpen that focus while addressing growing demand from AI and hyperscaler customers.
Looking ahead, the separation of the cloud business and the Delta Forge 1 build-out create two parallel stories to monitor: a dedicated AI infrastructure platform and a scaled physical campus under development. How the company executes on capital allocation, customer commitments, and timing milestones around the 300 MW site will likely be central to how investors assess NasdaqGS:APLD over the next several years.

Area Development News Desk

Title: Columbia establishes rules on data centers, with more coming
Summary_AI: Columbia City Council established zoning and permitting rules for data centers in the city by amending its unified development code during an April 6 meeting, following a November rezoning request from a Boone County landowner seeking to convert agricultural land to general industrial zoning for data center development near Route B/Paris Road. The council denied the rezoning request but proceeded to codify local definitions and regulations governing data center development to address future projects. The city indicated additional data center rules are forthcoming as part of ongoing regulatory efforts.
Article_Text:
Columbia establishes rules on data centers, with more coming
Columbia Daily Tribune
April 13, 2026, 4:04 a.m. CT
A local definition of data centers, their zoning and how they are permitted in Columbia is now in place.
The Columbia City Council at its Monday evening meeting, April 6 made this change. This follows an attempt in November by a Boone County landowner to get agricultural land rezoned into general industrial (IG) for the purpose of data center construction in the area of Route B/Paris Road. That rezoning request was denied by the council, leading to the business April 6 amending the city's unified development code.

Title: Rexroth explains why data center is perfect for his Conewago Twp. land
Summary_AI: Jim Rexroth is proposing a data center development on 541 acres of farmland in Conewago Township, Pennsylvania, arguing the land is economically unviable for farming and claiming a data center would have less community impact than alternative developments like housing or warehouses. The proposal has faced significant opposition from residents citing environmental damage, noise, and loss of rural character, with the township planning commission developing a zoning ordinance to address data center projects. Rexroth is scheduled to present his case to the planning commission on April 27, 2026, after being unable to speak at a previous meeting attended by over 100 residents.
Article_Text:
Farmer explains why a data center is perfect for his Conewago Twp. land
Updated April 13, 2026, 8:51 a.m. ET
A farmer's proposal to sell 541 acres for a data center has sparked fierce opposition from residents in Conewago Township.
Farmer Jim Rexroth argues the land is not prime for farming and a data center would be less impactful than a housing development.
Residents cite environmental damage, noise, and the loss of the area's rural character as reasons for their opposition.
The Conewago Township planning commission is working on a zoning ordinance to address data center development.
On Oct. 27, 2025, more than 100 people packed into the pews at Bible Baptist Church for a meeting of the Conewago Township Planning Commission.
Among those in the church was Jim Rexroth. The rest of the crowd, it seemed, was there to talk about him. Specifically, they were there to oppose the notion that his 541 acres of farmland on Bowers Bridge Road could one day be the site of a massive data center, believing that the noise and environmental damage caused by the facility would change the character of the rural area and that its massive power consumption would cause their electricity rates to skyrocket.
Rexroth, who farms some 5,400 acres in York County, was there to explain that the property was not prime farmland, that it was challenging to even break even on the corn, wheat and soybeans grown there, that in the future “nobody is going to farm that land just to break even,” and that it was inevitable that it would be developed.
In fact, when he bought the land 17 years ago, there was a plan to develop a 540-home subdivision on the acreage.
Rexroth wanted to tell them that, when he was approached by developers of data centers, he looked into it and was proceeding with caution. He'd previously worked with a company that proposed a solar farm on the hilltop between the Conewago Creek and the Little Conewago, a deal that fell apart.

The AI boom is moving at lightning speed. The U.S. power grid is not. Data center developers are running into multi-year interconnection queues that threaten to stall AI capacity expansion just as demand is inflecting. On-site firm power offers a practical path forward. Purpose-built generation can bypass the interconnection queue, deliver megawatts in months rather than years, and transition into long-term resiliency infrastructure once grid service arrives, all without adding upward pressure on consumer electricity rates.
The AI-driven power gap
U.S. data center power demand is expected to nearly triple by 2030, rising from 62 GW to more than 134 GW (S&P Global), against a backdrop of essentially flat total U.S. electricity consumption over the past two decades. Utilities were never sized to absorb this kind of concentrated, regional load growth, and it has resulted in multi-year interconnection queues, extended substation upgrade timelines, and delayed project starts — even on fully entitled sites.
The 2025 Uptime Institute Global Data Center Survey found that 63 percent of operators now cite power availability as a top concern, ranking it alongside cost and capacity forecasting as the industry’s most pressing challenge. In the speed-to-compute market, certainty of power is the new competitive advantage.

Title: It's a win-win: Applying industrial lessons to AI infrastructure - DCD
Summary_AI: AI infrastructure operators are increasingly adopting methodologies from mature industrial sectors to optimize the growing complexity of data center operations, particularly as AI factories demand greater power capacity and interconnected systems at unprecedented scale. Aveva's Arti Garg highlights how digital twins and industrial software solutions are becoming critical for managing the electrics, power, cooling, and computing infrastructure layers that now characterize modern AI facilities. Article_Text:
It’s a win-win: Applying industrial lessons to AI infrastructure
Aveva’s Arti Garg explores why knowledge sharing between sectors is central to continued data center optimization – sustainably and at scale
April 13, 2026 By DatacenterDynamics Have your say
AI has been the catalyst for a huge shift in the scale of digital infrastructure. The era of advanced compute is characterized by greater power and capacity, alongside bigger, more complex, interconnected components that now make up extensive AI factories – far surpassing the data halls of just the last decade.
These hubs of connectivity have been firmly cemented as the foundations of the digital economy, and this high-stakes reality – combined with the growing complexity of today’s facilities and operations – is driving the need to look beyond the data center industry itself for next-gen solutions and insights.
Increasingly, operators are recognizing that the answers to some of their most pressing challenges may already exist in more mature, adjacent sectors.
With a background in astrophysics and aerospace engineering, Arti Garg, data scientist and chief technologist at Aveva – a key strategic partner of Schneider Electric – explores the value of applying industrial lessons to AI infrastructure, and why digital twins are becoming mission critical for operating data centers at scale, in a recent DCD>Talks episode.
“Today’s powerful AI gigafactories are changing how AI intersects with the industrial sector,” says Garg. “That’s because models are becoming more capable and advanced, which means we can bring in more diverse data types.”
Garg is confident that the industrial sector is rich with opportunities for AI applications. From monitoring to optimizing operations, AI-powered industrial software presents a natural partnership for delivering the next generation of digital transformation and smarter, more efficient manufacturing.

Title: PJM targeting up to 15GW of new generation as part of emergency auction to meet growing ...
Summary_AI: PJM Interconnection is targeting up to 15GW of new generation capacity through an emergency auction running from September 2026 through March 2027, responding to unprecedented data center demand across its 13-state Mid-Atlantic and Midwest footprint. The auction was initiated by President Trump and 13 governors, with data center companies bidding on 2 to 15-year contracts backed by over $15 billion in baseload power generation investment. PJM's summer peak load is projected to grow 3.6 percent annually to 222GW by 2036, driven primarily by data center expansion, though the RTO currently faces a potential 60GW shortfall with a reported 6.5GW deficit in its latest capacity auction. The plan will be voted on in May and submitted to FERC for approval in early June.
Article_Text:
PJM targeting up to 15GW of new generation as part of emergency auction to meet growing data center demand
Process expected to take place from September through March 2027
April 13, 2026 By Zachary Skidmore Have your say
PJM Interconnection, the largest Regional Transmission Operator (RTO) in the US, is targeting up to 15GW of new generation capacity as part of an emergency auction to power growing data center demand across its footprint.
PJM's power needs just got political
President Trump calls for an emergency capacity auction for data center companies across the country's biggest transmission operator
19 Jan 2026 By Zachary Skidmore
Earlier this year, US President Donald Trump, alongside 13 governors, called for the RTO to hold the auction, which would see data center companies bid on 15-year contracts for new electricity generation capacity. The bipartisan group also pushed the RTO to build more than $15 billion of reliable baseload power generation.
According to the RTO, the process will take place from September through March 2027, with PJM expected to ask how many suitors on either side have an interest in participating in bilateral negotiations. Full details of the proposal are expected to be reviewed next week, with a vote to be held in May. Following this, the plan will be submitted to the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission for approval in early June. The contracts are expected to last between two and 15 years.
The plans emerge as PJM grapples with an unprecedented power shortfall. According to reports, the RTO could potentially face a power shortfall of up to 60GW. The RTO reported a 6.5GW shortfall in its latest capacity auction.
Despite the potential shortfall, there have been growing concerns about the impact that massive generation and interconnection buildouts could have on electricity bills for ratepayers in its service area. Title: Protesters call to cancel $19 billion data center project planned for Genesee County farmland
Summary_AI: Stream Data Centers is planning a $19 billion data center complex spanning 2.2 million square feet in the Town of Alabama, Genesee County, New York at a site known as STAMP Park. Local protesters gathered Monday at Batavia City Hall to oppose the project, citing concerns over the loss of farmland, while developers argue it would generate jobs and tax revenue for the region. A public hearing on the proposal is scheduled for Thursday at 4 p.m. at the Alabama Fire Hall.

Opposition voiced to data center plan in Genesee County
ALABAMA, N.Y. — Protesters are calling to cancel a $19 billion project to build one of the largest data centers in the nation in Genesee County.
The Stream Data Centers complex would be built in a field known as the STAMP Park in the Town of Alabama. Protesters gathered on Monday at Batavia City Hall to oppose the project.
Developers say the project would bring new jobs and increased revenue to the area. But protesters argue that the data center campus, totaling 2.2 million square feet according to the project’s website, would take away farmland for the town.
A public hearing on the project is set for Thursday at 4 p.m. at the Alabama Fire Hall on Judge Road.

Title: Data center battle in Archbald continues after months of frustration and fierce opposition
Summary_AI: The Wildcat Ridge data center project in Archbald, Pennsylvania continues facing intense community opposition at a conditional use hearing, with residents expressing concerns about traffic impacts on local roads and potential health risks from environmental contamination. The developer maintains the project's traffic impact will be negligible, while former county officials and a former judge called for either a community ballot vote or a multi-year moratorium on the project. The hearing highlighted a divide between the developer's promotional messaging and local residents' safety and quality-of-life concerns regarding the proposed facility.
Article_Text:
Data center battle in Archbald continues after months of frustration and fierce opposition
Another data center meeting in Archbald drew a full house of residents, a majority of whom oppose the project. Newswatch 16's Adi Iyer was on hand.
ARCHBALD, Pa. — After months of frustration and fierce opposition, the fight against data centers continues to rage on within the borough of Archbald.
Folks at Monday night's data center conditional use hearing were angry about a local ad agency, Ryan Leckey Media, sending out mailers on behalf of the developers of the proposed Wildcat Ridge data center.
One of the developer's mailers was called "Myth vs. Fact, revealing the truth and dispelling rumors about data centers." Residents returned those mailers to the developers within the first 10 minutes of the meeting.
"Since you're so into myths vs. fact, I'll tell you some facts: Ten of my neighbors, 10, have either died from or are living with cancer, and you are going to introduce another carcinogen in my area," says Mary Therese McKane of Archbald.
The focus of the meeting was on the impact that the data center would have on traffic. The data center developer says the impact will be "negligible," but some folks disagree.
"I drove down the Wildcat in ice storms, and I have to two wheels in a ditch, that's how dangerous it is, and you wanna put trucks down that road," says McKane.
Current and former county officials also asked the borough council if it's possible to have residents vote on whether Wildcat Ridge gets built by putting it on a ballot.
Lackawanna County Controller Gary DiBileo says, "I believe the future of Archbald and its residents should be determined by the people of Archbald."
"Unless you agree to a moratorium for a couple of years and let these people alone, or agree to put it on the ballot, pack your bags, pack your bags, get out of this place and don't come back!" says former Judge Thomas Munley.

Title: Data center proposed in Newton Twp. - Scranton Times-Tribune
Summary_AI: A Pennsylvania developer is seeking zoning approval to build a 76-acre data center campus in Newton Township with two buildings of 145,200 square feet each at a projected cost of $280 million, though the township's zoning officer denied the application in February citing incompatibility with the rural resource district. The proposal represents the second data center project in the Abingtons region, following a competing application from Scranton Materials LLC in nearby Ransom Township less than a mile away, and would be the first such facility within the Scranton-Abingtons Planning Association collaborative. The application lacks specification on power sourcing, cooling systems, and noise levels, though it indicates the property contains no wetlands or flood plain constraints.
Article_Text:
The property owned by Popca LLC on Newton Road in Newton Twp. is seen Monday, April 13, 2026. (Sean McKeag/Staff Photo)
By CHRISTINE LEE | clee@scrantontimes.com | The Times-Tribune
PUBLISHED: April 13, 2026 at 4:50 PM EDT | UPDATED: April 13, 2026 at 6:15 PM EDT
The data center proliferation in Lackawanna County is expanding in the Abingtons as a developer proposes a campus in Newton Twp.
Mark Gawron is seeking a zoning permit to build two data center buildings, each 35 feet high, 660 feet long and 220 feet wide, on his nearly 76-acre property on Newton Road. Each of the proposed buildings has a footprint of 145,200 square feet, according to the application.
The plans, which Gawron filed with the township under the name Popca LLC, also call for two generation yards, a well, septic tank, substation, guard building and two stormwater management facilities. He stated in the application the project will cost $280 million.
It does not specify how the proposed data center campus will be powered or cooled, or how loud it will be. The property does not contain wetlands and is not in a flood plain, the application states.
The plans are the second data center proposal in the Abingtons, following those filed late last year by Scranton Materials LLC for a data center campus at 819 Newton Road in Ransom Twp. — less than a mile from the Newton Twp. site. The plans are the first proposed in a municipality that is part of the Scranton-Abingtons Planning Association, a zoning collaborative that allows municipalities to share land uses. It is composed of Clarks Green, Clarks Summit, Dalton, Dunmore, Newton Twp., Scranton, South Abington Twp. and West Abington Twp.
Application rejected, appeal filed

Title: AWS launches "Project Houdini" to speed up data center construction - report - DCD
Summary_AI: AWS is launching "Project Houdini," an initiative to accelerate data center construction using pre-fabricated modular "skids" that are 45 feet long and shipped on double drop trailers with racks, power, cabling, and security systems pre-installed. The program aims to reduce server installation time from 15 weeks to two to three weeks and is expected to be operational by August, with initial module production planned in Topeka, Kansas; Houston, Texas; and Salt Lake City, Utah, in partnership with Cupertino Electric Inc. AWS states the modular approach could eventually support the development of more than 100 data centers annually, enabling faster delivery of AI infrastructure at lower cost.
Article_Text:
AWS launches "Project Houdini" to speed up data center construction - report
Company is turning to pre-fabricated data centers
April 13, 2026 By Georgia Butler Have your say
Amazon Web Services (AWS) is exploring pre-fabricated modular data centers to help speed up construction timelines.
According to Business Insider, citing internal documents accessed by the publication, an initiative dubbed "Project Houdini" is aiming to move a significant portion of the construction process inside the factory with the aim of bringing capacity online faster.
BI reports that the initiative is aiming to reduce delivery time by "months" and the company is exploring solutions to take "various DC build scopes to a factory setting."
"Our innovations in data center construction enable us to deliver AI infrastructure faster and at lower cost, which is why customers turn to AWS to run their most demanding workloads," an AWS spokesperson told BI.
The documents note that the current data hall build system is "stick-built" on-site, with workers installing racks, wiring power systems, and cabling, and that it can take up to 15 weeks before servers can be installed.
The Houdini effort will see large sections of the data halls prebuilt and known as "skids," delivered with racks, power, cabling, lighting, and fire and security systems already installed.
Each module is around 45 feet long and is shipped on double drop trailers, where it can be dropped into place and connected. The documents state that servers can, when using this method, instead be installed in just two or three weeks from construction starting. The initiative is expected to be ready by August and could eventually be used for the development of more than 100 data centers annually.
AWS is working with Cupertino Electric Inc on the initiative, and initial module production is planned in Topeka, Kansas; Houston, Texas; and Salt Lake City, Utah.

Title: Rezoning sought for data center campus in Clear Brook - The Winchester Star
Summary_AI: Pennsylvania-based real estate developer Equus Capital Partners Ltd. filed a rezoning application in Frederick County, Virginia to convert 220.06 acres in Clear Brook from Rural Areas to Technology Manufacturing for the proposed Virginia Technology Park, a data center campus featuring 10 buildings and three substation pads. The site's strategic advantage is its adjacency to NextEra Energy's planned Woodside substation, which will connect to a 500-kiloVolt transmission line upgrade, eliminating the need for on-site power infrastructure development. This represents the developer's third rezoning attempt for the parcels after previous applications in 2023 and September 2024 were withdrawn or rejected by the Board of Supervisors.
Article_Text:
A Generalized Development Plan map for the proposed Virginia Technology Park in Clear Brook. The Woodside substation is slated to be built to the left of the area in green. Ridgefield Estates is above the substation site.
Pennsylvania-based real estate developer Equus Capital Partners Ltd. announced on Monday that it has filed a rezoning application for a data center development adjacent to NextEra Energy's planned Woodside substation in Frederick County's Stonewall District.
The application seeks to rezone three parcels totaling 220.06-acres south of Rest Church Road off Interstate 81 and west of Zachary Ann Lane in Clear Brook from Rural Areas (RA) to Technology Manufacturing (TM).
The proposed development, called Virginia Technology Park, calls for 10 data center buildings and includes three substation pads, according to a generalized development plan in the rezoning application. The land is owned by DTS LC and William O. Minor, according to online county maps.
Over the last four years, Equus Capital Partners has been involved with efforts to try and rezone these same parcels.
In 2023, an application for a mixed-used development called Fruit Hill, with warehouses and 300,000 acres set aside for a data center, passed through the Planning Commission, but it was withdrawn by the developer in August 2023 before the Board of Supervisors could vote on it. According to previous reporting, the attorney representing applicant John Foote later told the supervisors that "discussions" with a member of the board had contributed to the application being pulled.
That same proposal was brought back in September 2024, with the board rejecting it on a 4-3 vote. Some supervisors downplayed the need for warehousing in the area and questioned the possibility of a data center.
Prior to the latest rezoning application being filed with the county, Equus Capital Partners, Ltd. Senior Vice President Daniel M. DiLella Jr. talked with The Winchester Star about his company's proposal.

Title: Oracle Agrees to Buy Power From Bloom for AI Data Centers - Yahoo Finance
Summary_AI: Oracle has contracted 1.2 gigawatts of fuel-cell power from Bloom Energy, with an option for up to 2.8 gigawatts total, to supply AI data centers across US projects in 2026-2027. Bloom's modular fuel cells enable faster deployment than traditional gas turbines, addressing the power supply bottleneck that constrains data center expansion, with the company delivering a full system 35 days ahead of schedule. Oracle is investing $50 billion in capital projects this fiscal year to build AI infrastructure for clients including OpenAI and xAI, with cloud services for AI generating $4.9 billion in quarterly revenue. The agreement underscores how hyperscale AI demand is driving gigawatt-scale power procurement directly from specialized energy providers rather than traditional grid infrastructure.
Article_Text:
Oracle Agrees to Buy Power From Bloom for AI Data Centers
Brody Ford and Naureen S. Malik
Tue, April 14, 2026 at 8:05 AM MDT 2 min read
(Bloomberg) -- Oracle Corp. agreed to purchase as much as 2.8 gigawatts of fuel-cell power from Bloom Energy Corp. to supply data centers for artificial intelligence work.
An initial 1.2 gigawatts of capacity has already been contracted and will be used this year and in 2027 at Oracle projects in the US, the companies said Monday in a statement. A gigawatt is enough electricity to supply about 750,000 US households at any one time.
“The expanded agreement with Oracle is a clear signal that hyperscale AI demand is translating directly into gigawatt-scale,” Evercore ISI analyst Nicholas Amicucci said after the announcement.
Bloom shares jumped as much as 18% Tuesday, after closing at $176.67 in New York Monday. The energy company’s stock has more than doubled in value this year as data center demand has fueled an energy supply crunch. Shares of Oracle rose more 7% Tuesday from the previous day’s closing.
Oracle has embarked on a massive construction program to build AI data centers for such clients as OpenAI and Elon Musk’s xAI, and has said it expects to spend $50 billion on capital projects in the fiscal year that ends in May. The push into providing cloud services for AI companies generated revenue of $4.9 billion for Oracle’s infrastructure business in the quarter that ended in February.
Bloom relies on modular fuel cells that can be used to scale up a data center more quickly than relying on gas turbines alone, which can take months or years to put in place due to supply chain delays. Power is often the biggest chokehold on opening new data centers.
Oracle is expanding this relationship after Bloom delivered a fully operational fuel-cell system in 55 days, more than a month ahead of an anticipated 90-day schedule, the companies said in the statement.

Title: LSC Fuels AI Innovation with 400 Miles of New Dark Fiber, 7 ILAs Connecting Amarillo, OKC ...
Summary_AI: Light Source Communications announced a 400-mile dark fiber route connecting Amarillo, Oklahoma City, Stillwater, and Tulsa, anchored by a hyperscale tenant and reinforced with seven in-line amplifiers, with construction expected to complete in Q1 2027. The project is the third new dark fiber build LSC commenced in 2026 and will integrate with the company's concurrent 500-mile Tulsa-St. Louis route and existing 130-mile Tulsa metro ring to create regional AI infrastructure capacity across the Central U.S. LSC is simultaneously building dark fiber networks in Las Vegas, Phoenix, Indianapolis, and Kansas City, positioning itself as a carrier-neutral provider of GPU-dense computing infrastructure for hyperscalers and neoclouds.
Article_Text:
Expansion Feeds Into Previously Announced Tulsa-St. Louis Long-Haul Route
SOUTHGATE, Mich., April 14, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Light Source Communications (LSC), a leading provider of high-capacity, future-proof dark fiber networks, is laying the groundwork for AI innovation with its newest dark fiber route bridging Amarillo, Texas, with Oklahoma City, Stillwater, and Tulsa, Oklahoma. This represents the third new dark fiber build LSC has commenced this year, underscoring its emergence as a key infrastructure partner in the AI era.
Anchored by a hyperscale tenant, the 400-mile route will deliver high-capacity, diverse connectivity between these markets to support hyperscalers, neoclouds, Machine Learning (ML) and other High-Performance Computing (HPC) applications. It is reinforced by seven in-line amplifiers (ILAs) to prevent degradation and ensure a robust signal. Additional Points-of-Presence (PoPs) are forthcoming.
Construction is expected to be complete in the first quarter of 2027 and is running concurrently with the building of LSC's long-haul route linking Tulsa and St. Louis. The St. Louis-Tulsa route is 500 miles of AI-generation dark fiber supported by eight ILAs and anchored by a hyperscale tenant. Both long-haul routes will connect with LSC's existing 130-mile dark fiber metro ring in the Tulsa area.
"Bridging these markets is a critical new catalyst for next-generation computing," said Debra Freitas, CEO of LSC. "The combination of this route, along with our St. Louis-Tulsa corridor, creates a powerful regional interconnection opportunity for the Central U.S., delivering the massive capacity and diverse pathways required for the AI era."
LSC is a carrier neutral, customer agnostic provider singularly focused on designing, building, and operating custom dark fiber infrastructure purpose-built to deliver massive capacity for GPU-dense clusters, minimal latency for real-time processing, and maximum diversity to ensure network resilience. Title: Data centers, pricey power await candidates for Public Service Commission - Louisiana Illuminator
Summary_AI: Louisiana's Public Service Commission faces a critical regulatory test as artificial intelligence data centers drive unprecedented electricity demand, with two commissioner seats up for election this year. The regulatory board must balance utility companies' needs to build power infrastructure for data centers against protecting residential and small business ratepayers from escalating costs, a challenge that has sparked broader discussions about utility deregulation in the state where Entergy and Cleco dominate. District 1's election includes five Republican primary candidates competing to replace term-limited Commissioner Eric Skrmetta, with differing views on how strictly to regulate the utilities' expansion plans.
Secondary_Categories: Data Center Development; Regulatory & Community Pushback
Article_Text:
Data centers, pricey power await candidates for Public Service Commission
Two seats up for grabs on board that sets electricity rates
WESLEY MULLER AND GREG LAROSE
The Louisiana Public Service Commission is one of the few in the nation where there are no restrictions on when, where and how often commissioners can communicate with the utilities they regulate. (Louisiana Public Service Commission YouTube channel)
This year’s elections for two replacements on the state Public Service Commission unfold as utility regulation in Louisiana approaches a crossroads. The rapid development of artificial intelligence data centers and their high demand for electricity have put a spotlight on the regulatory board.
The current commissioners attempted to strike a balance between granting utilities approval to meet the immense power needs of AI infrastructure while ensuring the cost of building new power plants isn’t eventually borne by household and small business ratepayers.
This data center dilemma awaits the election winners, as does the perennial quest for lower utility bills and the pressing need for more resilient and reliable power transmission networks.
These issues have prompted increased discussion of utility deregulation in Louisiana, where Entergy and Cleco, both stockholder-owned utility corporations, provide power to the large majority of customers. The alternative would be to open the door to more utility competition, with Texas as an example of an open marketplace.
The semi-closed primary for District 1 is open only to Republican and no-party voters.
On the Democratic side, only one candidate qualified: Connie Norris, a 73-year-old grandmother from Slidell who’s been an advocate for utility customers safe drinking water. That makes her the default nominee for the general election in November, so registered Democrats and third-party voters must wait until then to choose between Norris and whoever wins the upcoming Republican primary.

Title: Seattle considers ban on new data centers after reported expansion interest | king5.com
Summary_AI: Seattle city officials announced plans for a one-year moratorium on new data center development after reports that three companies—including Prologis, Equinix, and Sabey—have explored potential sites in the City Light service area with a combined maximum demand of 317 megawatts, roughly double the city's existing inventory. The moratorium will be paired with impact studies on infrastructure, water usage, utility rates, and public health, though city officials noted that none of the companies have submitted formal service requests or committed to construction. Additionally, Pacland filed early-stage permits for a $220 million data center site at 3625 1st Ave S on behalf of an unidentified stakeholder.
Article_Text:
Seattle considers ban on new data centers after reported expansion interest
Seattle weighs pause on new data centers as proposals emerge, though officials say plans are early and it’s unclear how many will move forward.
The roof of a data center in Hillsboro, Ore. Seattle is home to roughly 30 small data centers, many of which service the internet, not just AI.
SEATTLE — Seattle officials are considering a one-year moratorium on new data centers after reports of interest in expansion that remains unclear in its scope and extent.
Thursday evening, Seattle councilmembers announced plans to introduce a 365-day moratorium on the selection of locations for new data centers. The proposal will be paired with a resolution calling for impact studies on city infrastructure, water usage, utility rates, land use, jobs and public health.
Councilmember Debora Juarez (District 5), Councilmember Eddie Lin (District 2) and Council President Joy Hollingsworth (District 3) introduced the plan.
“It is essential to put necessary safeguards in place for our communities, our infrastructure, and our environment from the impacts of large-scale data centers,” Hollingsworth said.
'A small number of developers'
Seattle City Light told KING 5 that three companies have explored potential data center development in its service area, though none have begun an approval process that takes several years to complete once started.
"We were approached by a small number of developers to study the possibility of locating within the City Light service area," City Light added.
"The City Light process for connecting large load customers begins with a service request followed by design, engineering, and construction on both the developer and City Light sides before power can be delivered.
"The timeline is variable depending on a number of factors and would be several years.

Title: Data center bill targets rates, water, incentives - Smoky Mountain News
Summary_AI: North Carolina filed House Bill 1063, the "Ratepayer and Resource Protection Act," which would require large data centers with peak demand of at least 40 megawatts or annual water use exceeding one billion liters to pay full infrastructure costs and meet new environmental standards. The legislation, filed by Rep. Lindsey Prather (D-Buncombe), mandates that data centers obtain certificates of operation from the Utilities Commission and install on-site clean energy generation to offset at least 25% of their projected peak electricity demand, rejecting renewable energy credits or off-site agreements. The bill addresses growing concern among North Carolina constituents about hyperscale data center development and was inspired by a 12-month moratorium passed by the Town of Canton in February 2026.
Article_Text:
Data center bill targets rates, water, incentives
WRITTEN BY CORY VAILLANCOURT THURSDAY, APRIL 30, 2026
A bill filed in the General Assembly would require large data centers to pay the full cost of their electricity, water use and infrastructure, while meeting new on-site clean energy and environmental standards.
Cory Vaillancourt photo
As North Carolina braces against a surge in large-scale data center development, a new bill filed by Rep. Lindsey Prather (D-Buncombe) aims to redraw the rules governing how those facilities use electricity, consume water and tap into public subsidies.
In filing the bill, Prather noted that she was inspired by a 12-month moratorium passed in the Town of Canton in February, but also that the bill was “crowd-sourced.”
“I continue to get emails and phone calls from constituents that are worried and want to know what the state is doing about it,” Prather told The Smoky Mountain News April 29. “There's just a lot of fear and misunderstanding, and when there's a lack of data and transparency, it's easy for that stuff to spread.”
House Bill 1063, titled the “Ratepayer and Resource Protection Act,” proposes sweeping changes to state utilities law, environmental regulation and economic development policy. Its central premise is simple but far-reaching — data centers should pay the full cost of the infrastructure they require rather than shifting those costs onto everyday utility customers.
At the heart of the legislation is a new regulatory framework for what the bill defines as “large data centers,” facilities with projected peak electricity demand of at least 40 megawatts or annual water use exceeding one billion liters. Those thresholds capture the kind of hyperscale operations increasingly drawn to North Carolina for its land availability, tax structure and growing energy capacity.

Title: AVK launches modular power system for data center market - DCD
Summary_AI: UK-based power solutions provider AVK has launched the PowerPod, a modular power system designed for hyperscale and AI data centers, now available for order in the UK and EU. The fully integrated unit comprises an engine, switchgear, UPS, controls, and enclosures, capable of connecting to the grid or operating completely off-grid with customizable configurations for individual customer requirements. The product completes AVK's end-to-end data center power ecosystem offering, following the company's 2024 microgrid agreement with a European data center provider and partnership with renewable energy developer Wärtsilä.
Article_Text:
AVK launches modular power system for data center market
PowerPod system is available for order in UK and EU
April 30, 2026 By Zachary Skidmore Have your say
UK-based power solutions provider AVK has launched a modular power system for the hyperscale and AI data center sector.
The AVK PowerPod is a fully integrated, pre-engineered power solution delivered in a transportable modular unit. The unit comprises an engine for power delivery, switchgear, UPS, controls, and enclosures. According to the company, it can connect to the grid and operate completely off-grid, providing flexibility for data center customers looking to power their operations.
AVK claims that the product can be delivered as bespoke units to meet the application-specific requirements of individual customers. In addition, according to the company, the solution is technologically agnostic, which it claims means that customers won’t be locked into a single solution.
The product is immediately available for order for both UK and EU customers.
Commenting on the launch, Ben Pritchard, CEO of AVK, said: “The launch of the AVK PowerPods reinforces our position as one of the few businesses capable of designing, delivering, and supporting the entire data center power ecosystem – at scale, with true flexibility and with the engineering depth that critical infrastructure demands. Large data centers need partners who genuinely understand the full energy picture, and we now own the full power train.
“PowerPods complete our proposition to the market. They bring together our extensive critical power expertise with our technology-agnostic model to deliver a complete power train solution that makes us a reliable, long-term energy partner for data center operators. With our ready-to-deploy PowerPods model, we are perfectly positioned to support the next wave of hyperscale data centers and AI infrastructure.”

Title: Rise of data centers, energy worries expected to boost results of Lancaster County's ...
Summary_AI: Lancaster County-based Armstrong World Industries expects significant growth from data center construction and rising energy efficiency demands, with CEO Mark Hershey highlighting the company's Templok ceiling panels that reduce energy costs by up to 15% and meet sustainability standards eligible for tax credits. The mineral fiber segment, which includes Templok, generated $257 million in first-quarter sales, up 4.9% year-over-year, as data center builders and building owners increasingly adopt the product to address grid stability concerns and carbon reduction regulations. Armstrong is positioning itself as a one-stop provider for data center and building projects, leveraging speed, labor efficiency, and complete system installation capabilities to win contracts in the expanding data center construction market.
Article_Text:
Andy Trostle, uses a table saw to cut pieces of mineral fiber board for testing at Armstrong World Industries 2500 Columbia Ave., in Manor Township Friday, May 24, 2024.
BLAINE SHAHAN | Staff Photographer
Data center construction and the focus the centers’ rise put on energy efficiency are expected to be substantial drivers of growth at Lancaster County-based Armstrong World Industries, which has nearly 1,000 jobs based here.
Growing concerns about energy efficiency, sustainability and construction timelines are addressed by the ceiling, wall and suspension makers’ offerings, CEO Mark Hershey explained this week in his first earnings call since taking the top spot at one of the county’s oldest major employers in April.
The latest generation of the company’s Templok ceiling panels, which cut energy costs by as much as 15%, meet the highest sustainability standards and are eligible for tax credits, are being adopted across new building and retrofitting efforts. Recent projects as diverse as office, school, healthcare and small business applications contribute to a growing number of real-world case studies that prove the product’s value, Hershey told investors and analysts during the call to discuss first quarter results.
Armstrong World Industries brings energy savings through ceiling tiles
ELIZABETH DEORNELLAS | Staff Writer
Armstrong’s expectation for increasing Templok deployment “is bolstered by the urgent need for energy efficiency and grid stability as demands from AI, cloud computing and data centers …,” Hershey said. “In addition, … regulations introduced over the last several years present real challenges for building compliance with carbon and energy reduction mandates. With few new solutions coming to market to tackle these challenges, Templok is appealing for building owners facing these new regulations and even utilities looking for ways to protect the grid during peak usage hours. We believe this is a multiyear macro-driven opportunity.”

Title: MARA Holdings to buy Long Ridge Energy in $1.5 billion AI data center push - CoinDesk
Summary_AI: MARA Holdings is acquiring Long Ridge Energy & Power for $1.5 billion, gaining a 505 MW combined-cycle gas plant and 1,600 acres in Hannibal, Ohio with over 1 GW of total power capacity potential to support AI and critical IT infrastructure. The deal expands MARA's owned-and-operated power capacity by 65% and increases its pipeline to roughly 2.2 gigawatts across PJM, ERCOT, SPP and international markets, with construction on initial AI buildout expected to begin in the first half of 2027. The acquisition is anticipated to add $144 million in annualized adjusted EBITDA and close in the second half of 2026, with first capacity targeted for mid-2028.
Article_Text:
MARA Holdings to buy Long Ridge Energy in $1.5 billion AI data center push
The deal includes a 505 MW gas plant and 1,600 acres in Ohio, offering over 1 GW power capacity for future AI and IT buildout.
By Francisco Rodrigues|Edited by Stephen Alpher
Apr 30, 2026, 6:44 a.m. 1 min read
(Taylor Vick/Unsplash)
MARA Holdings (MARA) is acquiring Long Ridge Energy & Power for $1.5 billion to power its AI data center expansion.
The deal includes a 505 MW gas plant and 1,600 acres in Ohio, offering over 1 GW power capacity for future AI and IT buildout.
The acquisition increases MARA's capacity by 65%, adds $144M in annualized adjusted EBITDA, and is expected to close in the second half of 2026.
MARA Holdings (MARA) has agreed to buy Long Ridge Energy & Power in a deal valued at about $1.5 billion. MARA will also assume at least $785 million of debt backstopped by a bridge loan.
The seller, FTAI Infrastructure (FIP), is up 12% in pre-market trading. MARA is ahead 3%.
The deal includes Long Ridge’s 505-megawatt combined-cycle gas plant in Hannibal, Ohio, along with more than 1,600 acres of land, water access, fiber links, fuel supply and grid connections, according to a Thursday filing.
MARA said the site could support more than 1 gigawatt of total power capacity over time.
MARA said the acquisition would raise its owned-and-operated power capacity by about 65% and expand its operating and development pipeline to roughly 2.2 gigawatts across PJM, ERCOT, SPP and international markets.
MARA plans to start construction on an initial AI and critical IT buildout in the first half of 2027, with the first capacity targeted for mid-2028. The company said it does not expect to cut Long Ridge’s current power supply to the PJM grid.
The company expects the Long Ridge assets to add about $144 million of annualized adjusted EBITDA. The deal is expected to close in the second half of 2026.

Title: Data center in Columbus, Ohio, sold to occupier - DCD
Summary_AI: Five 9s Digital sold a 6MW single-tenant data center in Columbus, Ohio to its long-term tenant Expedient, with the transaction closing in March 2026. The facility at 5700 Innovation Drive in Dublin, Ohio, originally developed as a build-to-suit in 2016, comprises 29,210 sq ft with 4.8MW across 10,500 sq ft of raised floor space. Expedient now operates three facilities across the Columbus area totaling 13.4MW of capacity. The deal terms were not disclosed, though Expedient is backed by InfraBridge, a division of infrastructure investment firm DigitalBridge.
Article_Text:
Data center in Columbus, Ohio, sold to occupier
Five 9s sells facility, likely to Expedient
April 30, 2026 By Dan Swinhoe Have your say
Real estate firm Five 9s Digital has sold a data center in Columbus, Ohio. Colocation firm Expedient is the likely buyer.
“Five 9s Digital announced the successful sale of a 6MW single-tenant data center in Columbus, Ohio, to its long-term tenant,” the company said this week. Terms of the deal were not shared.
Originally developed by Five 9s Digital in 2016 as a build-to-suit facility, the tenant exercised its option to acquire the asset, with the transaction closing in March 2026.
Five 9s didn’t provide further details, but the image accompanying the release shows 5700 Innovation Drive in Dublin, Ohio, which is occupied by Expedient. The company is yet to comment on the news.
According to Expedient’s website, the 29,210 sq ft (2,715 sqm) CMH2 offers 4.8MW across 10,500 sq ft (975 sqm) of raised floor space. The company announced the facility in 2015.
Across the wider Columbus area, Expedient operates three facilities totaling 13.4MW across 59,600 sq ft (5,535 sqm) of raised floorspace. Its latest facility in the area was acquired in 2024.
Founded in 2001, Expedient offers colocation space from more than a dozen locations across the US. The company is owned by InfraBridge, a division of digital infrastructure investment firm DigitalBridge.
DigitalBridge became the investment manager for Expedient after acquiring AMP Capital’s global infrastructure equity investment management business in 2022. AMP acquired Expedient in 2019, in a deal reportedly valued at more than $500 million.
DCD has reached out to Five 9s and Expedient.

Title: Common-sense guardrails for new data center development benefit people, the planet

Common-sense guardrails for new data center development benefit people, the planet | GUEST COLUMN
By Colorado Politics 05/01/2026 | updated 7 days ago
Colorado’s business leaders are right about one thing: Colorado is at an inflection point. In a recent letter to Gov. Jared Polis, members of the technology and business community argued Colorado’s innovation economy must not only grow, but “serve the long-term prosperity and well-being of the people and communities of Colorado.”
Signal Tech Coalition represents Colorado tech and business leaders who believe tech is future innovation, and climate change is future risk. We recognize both the promise and the challenges we currently face. Which is why we support the common-sense guardrails for new data center development in Senate Bill 26-102.
Tech is the fastest-growing industry in Colorado, and there is no question data centers, driven by the growth of artificial intelligence and cloud computing, present meaningful economic opportunities for Colorado. They can bring investment, support innovation and contribute to our state’s economic development in a time of widespread uncertainty. We welcome this growth and believe Colorado can be a leader in responsibly integrating this industry into our broader economic future. However, these benefits must not come at the expense of Colorado ratepayers, state resources and our long-term climate targets.
Data centers are particularly power-intensive and are causing a massive increase in projected new demand for electricity throughout the country. Xcel Energy estimates new data centers could lead to more than a 30% increase in the utility’s peak power demand by 2035. Without accountability, new data centers will lead to construction of new gas-burning power plants and the delay of coal plant retirements. Title: Neighbors uneasy about South Annville data center proposal - Altoona Mirror
Summary_AI: A proposed data center in South Annville Township, Lebanon County, Pennsylvania is facing significant community opposition over concerns about wildlife impacts, property values, and resource consumption, with residents particularly worried about effects on a nesting eagle population in the area. The proposal has sparked broader debate across Pennsylvania, where data center development decisions remain largely unregulated at the state level despite Governor Josh Shapiro's proposed "GRID" framework and multiple bills pending in the legislature. Local opposition is intensifying statewide, with communities in Limerick Township and other counties reporting raucous public meetings, online threats, and growing calls for development moratoriums as residents express uncertainty about data center impacts.
Article_Text:
Neighbors uneasy about South Annville data center proposal
Nesting eagles, threats, lack of information all stir discussion
Rick Pyles of South Annville Township in Lebanon County is worried about the impact of a proposed nearby data center on his property and local wildlife. Ford Turner / The Center Square
Many neighbors have seen the local eagles soaring above the gently rolling fields and stands of trees in their part of southeastern Pennsylvania, and Rick Pyles has particularly enjoyed watching the secluded nest near his home get bigger each year.
Pyles is uneasy about the data center proposal that will affect his swath of South Annville Township, Lebanon County, where the birds live and hunt. Both the township and the proposer have indicated steps will be taken to protect the eagles, but Pyles’ love of the landscape — the deer, the seasonal planting and harvesting of crops, and the majestic eagles — cannot be put aside.
“I would rather not see it,” he said of the data center.
He is one of a huge number of Pennsylvania property owners trying to figure out what the future might look like after having a data center proposed near their homes. They worry about wildlife, property values, electricity and water use, and quality of life, among other things.
Of the various levels of government — state, county and local — nearly all the data center action in Pennsylvania appears to be at the local level. County governments have little involvement, and there has been no bipartisan agreement in Harrisburg on comprehensive regulations for data centers.
An avalanche of opposition has appeared in South Annville to the proposal there. Online threats have been made in connection with proposals in Limerick Township, Montgomery County. In other counties, there have been raucous meetings, misunderstandings, and a general sense that the topic is a no-man’s-land in terms of solid information.

Title: Utah has an 'obligation' to allow the building of massive data centers, Gov. Cox says
Summary_AI: Governor Spencer Cox endorsed a proposed hyperscale data center in Box Elder County, Utah, backed by investor Kevin O'Leary, stating that states have an "obligation" to permit such projects. The facility is expected to require three gigawatts of power initially and nine gigawatts when fully operational—exceeding Utah's current average consumption of four gigawatts—while also generating power and treating water for the Great Salt Lake. The project received initial approval from the state's Military Installation Development Authority, which agreed to significantly limit taxes as an incentive. Box Elder County commissioners delayed a final decision until the following Monday amid public controversy over the accelerated timeline.
Article_Text:
Utah has an ‘obligation’ to allow the building of massive data centers, Gov. Cox says
“I’m so tired of our country taking years to get stuff done,” the governor said. “It’s the dumbest thing ever.”
(Kristin Murphy | Pool) Gov. Spencer Cox speaks at a monthly news conference in Salt Lake City on Thursday, April 30, 2026.
By  Addy Baird  and  Robert Gehrke
| April 30, 2026, 2:24 p.m.
| Updated: 4:25 p.m.
Add as Preferred Source
Gov. Spencer Cox said he supports the installation of a new “hyperscale” data center in Box Elder County, a project expected to both generate and consume more power than Utah currently uses.
He also bristled against criticism that the proposed project has been rushed.
“This whole idea of being rushed — I’m so tired of our country taking years to get stuff done,” he said at a news conference Thursday morning. “It’s the dumbest thing ever. We think that taking time makes things better or safer. It absolutely does not.”
And though Cox said he has been “pretty neutral on data centers,” the governor said every state, including Utah, “has an obligation to allow for these types of data centers to be built.”
Cox’s comments on the proposed project came after a week of public frenzy and just days after the Box Elder County commissioners delayed making a final decision on approving the data center until next Monday. It has already received initial approval from the state’s Military Installation Development Authority (MIDA), which agreed to significantly limit taxes in an effort to help lure the project to northern Utah.
Cox noted that MIDA is a state entity, but not one that he controls.
The data center, he said, “is one that I think is going to help our country long term, and that’s something that we all have to support.”
Backed by celebrity investor Kevin O’Leary of “Shark Tank” fame, proponents of the center say it will create thousands of jobs, power itself and clean the water it uses in order to send it to the Great Salt Lake.

Bernt Nelson and Autumn Lankford Higgins American Farm Bureau Federation

The facilities form the backbone of the modern economy, powering the internet and enabling the applications and services relied upon by businesses, governments and everyday consumers. Those facilities, often requiring hundreds of megawatts of power, are increasingly sited in rural areas where land availability, energy access and proximity to transmission infrastructure make development feasible.

The price tag for larger campuses can reach tens of billions of dollars. Those projects are not just industrial developments; they are long-term infrastructure commitments that shape local economies for decades.
