Global investment could potentially hit $5 trillion by 2030. This capital supercycle is the largest since the railroad boom of the 1880s, and it has already dwarfed the recent Infrastructure and Jobs Act. In today's dollars, the top hyperscalers are spending nearly as much as the entire global oil industry did at its 2014 peak. I remember that time fondly because I was working in project controls for a major oil company at the time. This new capital cycle makes me very optimistic about the current moment, both for the civil contractors we work with at HCSS and for my own son, who is just now starting his career in the construction industry. This is our time!

Non-residential investment grew at a 10.4 percent annual rate, adding 1.39 percentage points (p.p.) to growth in the quarter. Government spending was also a major contributor to growth, growing at a 4.4 percent rate and adding 0.73 p.p. to growth. Consumption grew at just a 1.6 percent annual rate, adding 1.08 p.p. to growth.
Consumption Was Driven by Health Care
Increased spending on health care accounted for 47.2 percent of the growth in consumption in the quarter. Nominal spending on health care services increased at an 8.3 percent rate in the quarter, while real spending grew at a 4.5 percent rate. Nominal spending is up 7.9 percent from its year-ago level. It is likely that most people don’t distinguish between health care inflation as measured by the statistical agencies and what they are paying for health care, so this rise in nominal spending might be the better measure of the perceived burden of health care expenses.
Financial services was the only other component of consumption showing rapid growth, increasing at a 4.6 percent rate. Durable goods consumption was flat, while consumption of non-durable goods fell at a 0.2 percent rate. Car purchases rose at a 5.2 percent rate, but that followed sharp declines in the prior two quarters.
Spending at hotels and restaurants fell at a 2.8 percent rate after falling at a 1.2 percent rate in the fourth quarter. The fall in these discretionary expenditures likely indicates that people are feeling financially stressed.

Title: The Trump admin is trying to stop state climate lawsuits. It isn't working. - POLITICO Pro
Summary_AI: The Trump administration's Department of Justice has filed suit against Minnesota to block state climate liability lawsuits against oil companies, arguing such cases undermine federal energy policy authority. This represents an escalation of the DOJ's strategy to protect the oil industry from climate litigation, following similar unsuccessful efforts to stop comparable lawsuits in Michigan and Hawaii, where federal courts rejected the administration's arguments. If successful, state climate cases could cost the oil industry billions in damages for contributions to climate change impacts.
Article_Text:
The Trump admin is trying to stop state climate lawsuits. It isn’t working.
Federal courts aren't buying DOJ's argument that climate liability lawsuits undermine U.S. energy policy.
| 05/05/2026 06:18 AM EDT
Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche.J. Scott Applewhite/AP
CLIMATEWIRE | The Trump administration has escalated its bid to protect the oil industry from costly climate litigation — even after its earlier efforts faced decisive setbacks in federal court.
In its latest push, the Department of Justice on Monday sued Minnesota in federal court, claiming the state's lawsuit seeking payment from oil companies for contributing to climate change undermines federal authority and burdens U.S. energy development.
The argument has fallen flat with judges in two other federal courts, who rejected DOJ lawsuits that sought to prevent Michigan and Hawaii from filing climate liability cases. Like Minnesota’s, those lawsuits seek to hold the oil industry financially responsible for the effects of climate change. If the states' cases are successful, they could cost the oil industry billions.
“This naked political intimidation tactic should meet the same fate,” said Richard Wiles, president of the Center for Climate Integrity, which backs climate lawsuits against the oil and gas industry. “This is a desperate effort to shield the architects of Big Oil’s decades-long climate deception from facing accountability.”

Title: Gallego's energy plan: Pull the Democrats to the center - E&E News by POLITICO
Summary_AI: Sen. Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.) advocates for Democrats to adopt an "all-of-the-above" energy policy approach emphasizing affordability and energy costs over climate messaging to improve midterm election prospects. Gallego argues that focusing on lowering fuel and electricity prices addresses voters' immediate concerns more effectively than climate change appeals, which he contends cause many constituents to disengage from the message. He suggests that outcome-focused energy policies, rather than climate-centric rhetoric, will ultimately advance decarbonization goals and strengthen Democratic electoral performance.
Article_Text:
Adopting an “all-of-the-above” energy policy message will help Democrats show voters the party is serious about tackling the affordability crisis — and ultimately boost clean energy and the fight against climate change, Sen. Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.) said in Monday’s episode of the POLITICO Energy podcast.
Gallego’s calls for Democrats to pivot to the more centrist position comes as soaring costs for gasoline and electricity are pushing up inflation and hitting voters in the wallet, and raising the party’s chances of winning control of Congress in the midterm elections in November.
That energy message should replace the climate change appeals that Gallego said causes many voters to shut off, since soaring fuel prices represent a more urgent problem than rising temperatures — even in Arizona, which suffers the nation’s worst heat waves and has been hard hit by climate-influenced drought.
“Honestly, it’s just so loaded,” Gallego said. “If our goal is to bring down our carbon footprint — try to restrain climate change — we need to win. And focusing on words versus outcomes, I think, is a real good pathway to losing.”

Title: Expanding Pathways for Thermal Energy and Geothermal Development | Brownstein
Summary_AI: Colorado's Senate Bill 26-142 expands geothermal development pathways by increasing allowable community-scale project sizes from 5 MW to 25 MW and requiring investor-owned utilities to identify small-scale geothermal projects for development. The legislation directs the Colorado Energy and Carbon Management Commission to compile statewide geothermal resource data and consider creating a geothermal development authority to facilitate public-private partnerships and secure federal funding for exploration and thermal energy network infrastructure. Expanding Pathways for Thermal Energy and Geothermal Development
BROWNSTEIN CLIENT ALERT, MAY 4, 2026
Colorado lawmakers are advancing a bipartisan proposal aimed at accelerating thermal energy resource development across the state, including geothermal energy and industrial waste. The bill reflects Colorado’s broader push toward decarbonization and electrification and could potentially impact municipalities, utilities, industrial operators and energy developers.
Senate Bill 26-142, “Concerning the Development of Thermal Energy Resources,” would significantly expand opportunities for geothermal energy development by creating clearer pathways for both distributed and utility-scale projects.
Most notably, the bill increases the allowable size of community-scale geothermal projects from 5 MW to 25 MW and contemplates that investor-owned electric utilities “shall identify one or more potential small-scale geothermal projects” for development. At the same time, the legislation lays the groundwork for large-scale geothermal deployment by directing utilities and state agencies to identify and evaluate geothermal resources and opportunities.
SB 26-142 also contains a set of provisions that would expand the role of the Colorado Energy and Carbon Management Commission (ECMC) and related agencies in advancing geothermal development. The bill directs ECMC, in coordination with the Colorado Geological Survey, to collect, analyze and publish data on geothermal resources statewide, including leveraging existing subsurface information such as oil and gas well data. This effort is intended to address a major barrier to geothermal investment—limited access to reliable geological data.
In addition, the legislation requires ECMC to develop recommendations to support safe and effective geothermal development and report those findings to the General Assembly by Nov. 15, 2026. Mayor Kate Gallego, W’12, City of Phoenix, Arizona
Josué Tanaka, visiting professor in practice at the LSE Grantham Research Institute and principal finance advisor at C40 Cities
Mayor Gallego has positioned Phoenix, Arizona as a global hub for bioscience, advanced manufacturing, and climate resilience, including securing $165 billion in foreign investment from TSMC, the largest such investment in U.S. history. Under her leadership, Phoenix established the nation’s first Office of Heat Response and Mitigation, advanced smart water use strategies, and strengthened critical infrastructure—making it one of the most sustainable desert cities in the United States.
Dr. Tanaka has more than four decades of leadership in development finance, environmental policy, and urban infrastructure. As the founding leader of the European Bank for Reconstruction and Development’s climate finance initiative, he oversaw more than $42 billion in green investments across nearly 2,000 projects. He also launched the flagship €3 billion Green Cities program, now active in 46 cities across three continents, which advances large-scale climate mitigation and adaptation efforts.

Title: The Cloud Already Ate Your Hardware Lunch - BigDATAwire - HPC Wire
Summary_AI: Hyperscalers' $700 billion spending commitment this year has created severe supply constraints for enterprise hardware, with lead times extending to 8-10 months and storage costs rising 472% year-over-year, fundamentally shifting economics away from on-premise infrastructure. The top five cloud providers now control critical component supply through multi-year agreements, spending $410 billion in 2025 compared to the Big Seven enterprise vendors' combined $145 billion, effectively monopolizing GPU and compute capacity needed for AI workloads. This supply-side squeeze has transformed cloud adoption from a strategic choice to an operational necessity for enterprises seeking timely access to compute resources, forcing CIOs to prioritize speed of deployment over traditional infrastructure placement decisions.
Article_Text:
The Cloud Already Ate Your Hardware Lunch
by Brandon Whitelaw | May 4, 2026
For years, the decision was simple: workload variability dictated placement. Highly variable workloads favored the cloud’s elasticity while predictable, steady workloads stayed on-premise for cost efficiency. Today, hardware costs and lead times are leaving customers scrambling to access cloud capacity as quickly as possible. A demand-side shock, driven by hyperscaler commitment for AI buildouts, has fundamentally altered the economics of infrastructure.
The Hardware Squeeze
Hyperscalers have cornered the market on critical data center components with $700 billion in spending this year (+70% YoY). The largest cloud providers have locked in multi-year supply agreements for DRAM, NVMe, and GPU capacity. Back in 2021, the top five hyperscalers spent about $100B, on par with the Big Seven Enterprise hardware vendors. In 2025, the top five had jumped to $410B, and their year-over-year increase to $700B – that is double the entire Big Seven’s spend at $145B.
The impact is stark, especially for companies trying to expand on-premise environments. They’re now facing eight to ten month lead times and significant price increases. All-flash storage systems alone are more than 472% more expensive than a year ago. In most cases, the total cost of ownership has inverted. What was once cheaper on-premises is not only more expensive than the cloud, but it’s also only available with four to six month lead times.
(FOTOGRIN/Shutterstock)
Don’t look for this to be a temporary disruption. That’s because hyperscalers are no longer just competing on services and price. They are competing on access to compute itself, and their scale allows them to secure supply chains in ways even the largest enterprises cannot match. To de-risk the tens of billions required to bring new fabs online, the hyperscalers have made multi-year, escalating commitments, justified by their combined $1.4 trillion in committed backlog. North Carolina lawmakers are moving to require large data centers to pay the full cost of the power, water, and infrastructure they consume, advancing legislation that would also limit their access to public incentives.
The legislation reflects a growing trend among regulators to address the perceived imbalance between hyperscale demand and infrastructure capacity, as AI-driven workloads push utilities to their limits.
The proposed Ratepayer and Resource Protection Act targets what lawmakers describe as cost-shifting from large data centers onto households and small businesses, as AI-driven demand accelerates power and water consumption across the state.
The measure shifts from using incentives to attract data center projects to a full cost recovery model, placing the burden of grid expansion and resource use directly on data center operators.
It follows similar action in Wisconsin, where regulators recently overhauled a proposed data center tariff to ensure large-load customers cover the full cost of generation and grid infrastructure. These developments signal a broader shift toward stricter frameworks to safeguard existing ratepayers while managing the rapid growth of hyperscale facilities.

The US data center electrical equipment market is projected to grow to $65 billion by 2030, from approximately $20 billion in 2026, driven by rising demand for transformers, switchgear, and power distribution systems as hyperscale construction accelerates, according to a new Wood Mackenzie report.
The data center sector, once a marginal slice of electrical equipment demand, is becoming a dominant buyer – large enough to reshape production capacity, pricing, and lead times across the supply chain.
Ben Boucher, research director at Wood Mackenzie, told Data Center Knowledge that both capacity growth and pricing pressure are driving the expansion. US data center capacity is expected to scale from roughly 24 GW to 100 GW between 2026 and 2030, increasing competition with utilities for the same pool of electrical equipment.
The report comes as hyperscalers, including Microsoft, Amazon, Google, and Meta, report surging AI demand, driving record capital expenditures and cloud growth. Recent earnings highlight the widening gap between infrastructure supply and demand, with power, cooling, and permitting emerging as key constraints.

Crown Castle has officially closed the books on one of the most significant strategic transformations in U.S. telecommunications history, completing its $8.5 billion sale of fiber and small cell businesses on May 1, 2026, receiving aggregate cash proceeds of approximately $8.4 billion after preliminary purchase price adjustments.
Crown Castle sold its fiber solutions business to Zayo Group Holdings Inc., an investor group led by DigitalBridge, while Arium Networks, an EQT Active Core Infrastructure fund company, acquired its small cells assets. Each business was valued equally, with Zayo acquiring the fiber solutions business for an enterprise value of $4.25 billion and EQT acquiring the small cells business for an enterprise value of $4.25 billion.
A Strategic Pivot Years in the Making

The company’s board of directors approved the sale following a strategic review that assessed offers from both financial and strategic buyers proposing a variety of transaction structures.
The sale includes 90,000 route miles of fiber located in the largest U.S. markets, along with Crown Castle’s focus on its 40,000 U.S. towers. Former CEO Steven Moskowitz acknowledged that fiber “has a different business model and different customer base than towers and requires different operational capabilities.”

Title: Data Centers Have a PFAS Problem | Sierra Club
Summary_AI: Data centers in Virginia's Loudoun County are facing scrutiny over water contamination risks, including potential pollution from PFAS chemicals, despite being marketed as closed-loop systems that recycle water. The region, known as Data Center Alley and home to the world's largest data center hub supporting AI applications like ChatGPT and Gemini, consumes up to 2 million gallons of water daily and has driven electricity costs up 267 percent in five years, prompting environmental groups to flag the lack of safeguards against water pollution. Data Centers Have a PFAS Problem
While air and noise pollution are common concerns, little is known about PFAS contamination from the AI buildout
High-voltage transmission lines provide electricity to data centers in Ashburn, Loudoun County, Virginia. | Photo by Ted Shaffrey/AP
In suburban Virginia, a cluster of monumental gray buildings rise up around the unincorporated settlement of Ashburn in Loudoun County. Ringed by fencing and humming with AC, this is the new front of the digital economy—a place that has become known as Data Center Alley, the biggest data center hub in the world.
Popular large language models such as ChatGPT, Gemini, and MidJourney—marketed as artificial intelligence—need data centers to work. This has caused a boom in the construction of colossal compounds that require a massive amount of electricity and water. The companies building these compounds have brought billions of dollars in investments to Virginia, along with promises of jobs and improvements to the local economy.
For the Virginians living nearby, however, those promises have largely failed to deliver. Data centers take over the landscape, bring air and noise pollution, and guzzle as much as 2 million gallons of water a day. They also hit people in the pocketbook. According to one recent report, energy prices in states like Virginia have skyrocketed by up to 267 percent in the last five years as utilities have scrambled to build out the infrastructure needed to accommodate the data center boom.
All of this is changing local communities in ways that deeply impact quality of life, says Julie Bolthouse, director of land use at the Piedmont Environmental Council, a nonprofit dedicated to land and water conservation in Virginia. “[Data centers] are located in close proximity to houses and schools,” she says. “You're really changing those communities. These are huge boxes surrounded by fencing. They are not conducive to a walkable, livable environment.”

Title: US Utility Threatens to Quit Power Grids on Data Center Rift | Financial Post
Summary_AI: American Electric Power Co., one of the largest US utilities, is threatening to withdraw from PJM Interconnection and Southwest Power Pool over lengthy interconnection timelines for AI data centers. CEO Bill Fehrman cited the need to solve connection speed issues, noting the company is in early evaluation stages regarding membership in both grids, which serve 67 million and 20 million people respectively across eastern and central US regions. Article_Text:
(Bloomberg) — American Electric Power Co., one of the largest US utilities, is threatening to remove itself from two of the country’s biggest electricity grids over the length of time it takes to connect new AI data centers.
“We have to solve the speed to market issue,” Chief Executive Officer Bill Fehrman said in a Tuesday earnings call. “This is an area that clearly has to get fixed.”
AEP is in the early stages of evaluating membership in PJM Interconnection LLC, the largest regional grid in the US, as well as Southwest Power Pool Inc., Fehrman said. The move comes as the Ohio-based utility giant seeks to meet booming demand from power-hungry data centers.
PJM operates a regional power grid that serves more than 67 million people in 13 eastern states, extending from Illinois to North Carolina. SPP’s grid serves about 20 million people in central US from North Dakota to part of Texas.

Frustrations over PJM have been growing because wholesale electricity costs are climbing while reforms are moving too slowly to both bring on new supplies and serve increasing demand by artificial intelligence. Problems that have been festering for years for connecting and procuring supplies have now become urgent because data centers are expected to expand electricity consumption at a record clip following two decades with little growth.
“AEP is expressing frustration with the time to connect new generation in PJM,” said Paul Patterson, a utility analyst for Glenrock Associates. “It sounds like their patience has been tried by the perception that PJM and others are not moving as fast enough as utilities would like.”
AEP stock rose 1.8% in New York, extending the year-to-date gain to 19%. Earlier Tuesday, the company said it was boosting its five-year capital plan by 8.3% to $78 billion and was eyeing a further $10 billion in investment opportunities, including SoftBank Group Corp.’s data center project in Piketon, Ohio.

Title: Silicon Valley bets $200M on AI data centers floating in the ocean - Ars Technica
Summary_AI: Panthalassa secured $140 million in funding led by investors including Palantir co-founder Peter Thiel to build ocean-based AI data centers powered by wave energy, with plans to establish a pilot manufacturing facility near Portland, Oregon. The company's floating nodes, resembling large steel spheres with vertical tubes that convert wave motion into electricity, would house AI chips cooled by seawater and transmit results via satellite links rather than relying on land-based infrastructure. The latest prototype, Ocean-3, measures approximately 85 meters in length and is scheduled for testing in the northern Pacific Ocean in 2026, following successful earlier tests of Ocean-1 and Ocean-2 models. The approach addresses growing constraints for deploying AI data centers on land by shifting energy transmission into data transmission through satellite connectivity.
Article_Text:
Silicon Valley investors, such as Palantir co-founder Peter Thiel, have bet hundreds of millions of dollars on deploying AI data centers powered by waves in the middle of the world’s oceans—a move that coincides with the mounting challenges tech companies are facing in building AI data center projects on land.
The latest investment round of $140 million is intended to help the company Panthalassa complete a pilot manufacturing facility near Portland, Oregon, and speed up deployments of wave-riding “nodes” designed to generate electrical power, according to a May 4 press release. Instead of sending renewable energy to a land-based data center, the floating nodes would directly power onboard AI chips and transmit inference tokens representing the AI models’ outputs to customers worldwide via satellite link.
“Panthalassa’s idea transforms an energy transmission problem into a data transmission problem,” Benjamin Lee, a computer architect and engineer at the University of Pennsylvania, told Ars. “Performing AI computation on the ocean would require transferring models to the ocean-based nodes and then responding to prompts and queries.”
Each node resembles a huge steel sphere bobbing on the water with a tube-like structure extending vertically down beneath the surface. The wave motions drive water upward through the tube into a pressurized reservoir, where it can be released to spin a turbine generator that produces renewable energy for the AI chips on board.
How Scientists Respond to Science Deniers
Panthalassa claims the node’s AI chips would also get cooled using the surrounding water, which could offer another advantage over traditional data centers. “Ocean-based compute might offer a massive cooling advantage because the ambient temperature is so low,” Lee said. “Land-based data centers use a lot of electricity and fresh water for cooling.”

Title: Bill would forbid New Hampshire towns from enacting regulations specific to data centers
Summary_AI: New Hampshire's House Committee on Municipal and County Government voted along party lines to recommend amendments to Senate Bill 439 that would prevent towns from regulating data centers more restrictively than other commercial uses and establish data centers as permitted land uses in commercially or industrially zoned areas. The bill's original provisions, which required utility grid capacity verification and allowed local zoning restrictions, were significantly weakened in a Senate amendment in January, prompting the bill's Democratic sponsor to request restoration of stronger statewide requirements during House hearings. Republicans on the committee argued data centers should face no different regulations than other enterprises, while Democrats raised concerns about the state's insufficient energy and water resources to support large data center development. The competing positions reflect broader national debate over balancing economic development with infrastructure capacity and environmental protection.
Article_Text:
ENERGY + ENVIRONMENT
Bill would forbid New Hampshire towns from enacting regulations specific to data centers
Rep. Diane Pauer (left), chair of the House Committee on Municipal and County Government, describes an amendment to Senate Bill 439 on Tuesday, May 5. Pauer said a goal of her amendment was to ensure that data centers are not regulated differently than other industries in the state. (Photo by Molly Rains/New Hampshire Bulletin)
Opposition to data centers is becoming increasingly bipartisan in states across the U.S., but the opposite was true in the New Hampshire House on Tuesday.
The House Committee on Municipal and County Government split on party lines in their vote on a bill that would set guidelines for how towns can regulate data center development.
The committee’s Republican majority voted to recommend an amended version of Senate Bill 439 that would prevent towns from regulating data centers in a manner “more restrictive” than other uses allowed in the same district. The bill would also make data centers a permitted land use “by right” in commercially or industrially zoned areas.
“A data center is an enterprise, and it shouldn’t be treated any differently than any other type of enterprise that seeks to do business here,” said Brookline Republican Diane Pauer, the committee chair and an author of the amendment.
But opponents said data centers raise novel and emerging concerns that may require unique regulations.
“I am very much concerned that we are opening the doors to large data mining centers in the state of New Hampshire, where we cannot afford to host them because we do not have sufficient energy or water to do so,” said Rep. Laurel Stavis, a Lebanon Democrat.

Shane Snider,Senior News Writer,Data Center Knowledge
IMAGE: OVERVIEW ENERGY
A week after Meta said it would pursue solar power beamed from space to run its data centers, the company’s energy strategy is coming into sharper focus – as a long-term capacity bet rather than a near-term fix for AI-driven power demand.
The social media company has outlined partnerships with Overview Energy and Noon Energy that target technologies intended to extend renewable generation and store energy for days at a time. But both efforts are still several years from deployment, with initial demonstrations planned for 2028.
That timing highlights a broader challenge for data center operators, as the accelerating demand for power outpaces the development of firm, continuous energy sources, which remain constrained by grid limitations and lengthy development timelines.
Long-Term Capacity, Not Immediate Relief
Meta has framed its partnership with Overview Energy as a way to increase the output of existing solar infrastructure by transmitting energy collected in orbit to ground-based facilities – a concept broadly known as ‘space-based solar power’ (SBSP). In theory, SBSP could enable solar generation beyond daylight hours by beaming energy to large ground receivers. However, the system has yet to be demonstrated at grid scale, and Meta’s first orbital tests remain years away.

Panthalassa’s solution is an autonomous, fully integrated system called a node that houses both AI infrastructure and power-generating hardware in a single offshore unit. Each lollipop-shaped node consists of a buoyant spherical head connected to a long, submerged vertical tube and structural frame.
As ocean waves pass, the node bobs up and down, but, crucially, the surrounding water moves only in small orbital paths. This relative motion between the structure and the water column induces oscillations within the tube, effectively driving seawater up and down through the system.
That oscillating flow is channeled into the spherical chamber through a high-pressure jet, where it is converted into usable mechanical energy. The water then passes through internal turbines, generating electricity before recirculating back into the tube to repeat the cycle. The system is designed as a closed hydraulic loop that continuously extracts energy from wave-induced motion.

Title: America's AI Boom Has a Trade Policy Blind Spot | Coalition For A Prosperous America
Summary_AI: U.S. data center equipment imports reached $653 billion in 2025, more than double the 2020 level, with $580 billion in computing hardware and $70 billion in power infrastructure, but while tariffs have redirected computing supply chains away from China, the power supply chain remains dangerously exposed and heavily dependent on foreign sources. Lead times for large power transformers have stretched to five years, and approximately 40% of planned U.S. data center capacity faces delays due to equipment and power availability constraints. Article_Text:
AMERICA’S AI BOOM HAS A TRADE POLICY BLIND SPOT
U.S. imports of data center equipment reached $653 billion in 2025, more than double their 2020 level. That total breaks into two distinct supply chains: nearly $580 billion in computing hardware (servers, chips, networking, cooling) and more than $70 billion in power infrastructure (transformers, switchgear, lithium-ion batteries). Tariffs and export controls have reshaped the first. The second remains dangerously exposed.
Shortages of transformers, switchgear, and batteries are already threatening to delay the buildout. Lead times for large power transformers have stretched to as long as five years, and roughly 40% of planned U.S. data center capacity faces delays tied to equipment and power availability. The equipment the United States cannot manufacture fast enough is the same equipment it increasingly sources from China.
The power supply chain tells the opposite story. China’s share of U.S. lithium-ion battery imports peaked at 71% in 2023 and remains at 59%. China controls 99% of global lithium iron phosphate (LFP) cathode production, the battery chemistry of choice for data centers and grid-scale storage. Transformer imports show signs of rerouting through Southeast Asia to circumvent tariffs.
Tariffs proved they can redirect trade flows. They have not yet proved they can rebuild domestic production. The computing stack needs to be reshored, not just rerouted. The power stack needs both: strengthening 45X production incentives, enforcing FEOC restrictions at the processing stage, investing in allied cathode processing networks, and investigating transformer rerouting through Southeast Asia.
The AI data center buildout is reshaping the U.S. trade balance. Between 2020 and 2025, U.S. imports of nine categories of data center equipment grew from $312.7 billion to $653.1 billion, an increase of 109% (Figure 1). Title: Fortifying US national security with a hidden energy advantage - Atlantic Council
Summary_AI: The article discusses how geothermal energy and naturally occurring geologic hydrogen could strengthen US national security by providing resilient, domestic power sources for military installations and critical infrastructure. With natural gas price increases of over 25 percent and a global recession on the table, now is the time to diversify domestic energy resources like geothermal and geologic hydrogen to ensure American energy security.
For decades, US energy strategy has focused on what can be readily deployed—oil, natural gas, coal, wind, and solar—with relative ease across a large, interconnected grid. Yet recent conflicts in Eastern Europe and the Middle East, along with rising trade tensions with China regarding critical minerals and other key inputs underscore a hard truth: Reliance on centralized grids exposes military installations and critical infrastructure to risks from adversary targeting, cyberattacks, natural disasters, and blackouts. As threats to fuels and energy infrastructure proliferate, ensuring reliable power becomes a national security imperative.
Beneath US soil lie untapped resources that could reshape this equation: geothermal energy and naturally occurring geologic hydrogen. These domestic, continuous sources offer resilient power that reduce dependence on external infrastructure while leveraging US expertise in oil and gas. Advancing them would strengthen both energy security and national defense, particularly on federal lands and military bases—if policymakers move quickly to enable exploration, pilot projects, and deployment.

Title: U.S. gasoline prices rise 50% since the start of the Iran war | PBS News
Summary_AI: Gasoline prices in the U.S. have surged 50% since the Iran war began, reaching an average of $4.48 per gallon as of May 5, 2026, driven by the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz through which approximately 20% of the world's crude oil normally flows. The disruption has constrained global oil supply, pushing crude prices as high as $112 per barrel in early April and creating persistent upward pressure on fuel costs despite brief optimism around mid-April ceasefire announcements. The International Energy Agency characterized the Strait of Hormuz closure as the largest supply disruption in oil market history, with crude oil costs representing roughly 51% of gasoline prices at the pump.
Article_Text:
Cathy Bussewitz, Associated Press
U.S. gasoline prices rise 50% since the start of the Iran war
Economy May 5, 2026 5:38 PM EDT
NEW YORK (AP) — The price of a gallon of regular gasoline climbed 31 cents in the past week, spiking to an average of $4.48 per gallon Tuesday, according to AAA, hitting the wallets of drivers after rising 50% since the war with Iran began.
The main reason drivers are paying more at the pump is because of the global energy crisis caused by the Iran War. The price of crude oil, which is the main ingredient in gasoline, has been climbing for most of the past two months because the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow passage of the Persian Gulf through which a fifth of the world's crude oil normally passes, has effectively been shut, and oil tankers have been stranded there unable to deliver crude.
READ MORE: UN resolution proposal would hit Iran with sanctions for halting freedom of navigation
Many drivers were hopeful in mid-April, amid signs that the conflict could be winding down, and gasoline prices fell daily for almost two weeks.
"After the announcement of the initial ceasefire, there was kind of optimism that this really could be the beginning of the end of the conflict," said Rob Smith, director of global fuel retail at S&P Global Energy. "And so crude prices came down correspondingly, gasoline spot prices followed, and so on and ... the retailers lowered prices as well."
But as the war continued, gasoline prices reversed course and began increasing again.
"There's a fundamental shortfall that will exist globally or fundamental struggle to meet that demand that will drive up price," Smith said. "No matter what a government says or what any market person thinks, there is a true kind of upward pressure that's being exerted on prices every day the Strait of Hormuz is constrained. And it is still severely constrained."
Who sets gasoline prices
Gas station owners set prices at the pump, but a lot of factors go into what they decide to charge.

Today, more than a year into the second Trump administration, many of those policies have been reversed.

Can these upgrades be delivered cost-effectively?

To enable the Northern Link Route, Ziply Fiber is taking advantage of so-called coherent optical technology from Ciena and its high-speed connectivity.
Commercial customers also benefit from the route's fully subterranean design, which provides greater immunity to wildfires or storms that have historically disrupted commodity routes and forced traffic rerouting, according to a Ziply news release.

Title: Are the New Jersey Pinelands safe from data centers?
Summary_AI: The New Jersey Pinelands Commission determined that the 1981 Comprehensive Management Plan adequately protects the region from hyperscale data center development through strict controls on development intensity and water withdrawals from the Kirkwood-Cohansey aquifer, with only Monroe and Manchester Township having amended zoning to permit data centers on limited lots. However, gaps remain in the regulatory framework, including the ability of municipalities to allocate existing water supplies to large data center users and the absence of noise regulations addressing the low-frequency sound produced by these facilities. Monroe Township reversed course in April, passing an ordinance to ban all data centers after initially permitting them, joining other municipalities like Pemberton Township in taking definitive action against such development.
Article_Text:
As concerns grow about the rise of data centers, a central question has emerged: is the New Jersey Pinelands adequately protected?
After a recent presentation by staff to the New Jersey Pinelands Commission, the answer is clearer than many expected. The Pinelands Comprehensive Management Plan (CMP), adopted in 1981, remains remarkably strong, even in the face of technologies its authors could never have imagined.
Pinelands Commissioner Rittler-Sanchez captured this perfectly: the CMP “holds up pretty well” because it was designed not around specific threats, but around enduring values: protecting land, water, and ecological integrity. That approach is now proving its worth. While data centers pose new challenges, the framework governing the Pinelands is not starting from scratch. In many ways, it is already doing its job.
Consider development intensity. Hyperscale data centers require vast amounts of land, but the CMP tightly restricts where that level of development can occur. Only a small portion of the Pinelands—primarily Regional Growth Areas and Pinelands Towns—could even accommodate such facilities. And even there, data centers are not automatically allowed.
Municipalities must actively amend their zoning, and so far, only two have done this: Monroe and Manchester Township. For both, data centers are permitted as a use on only one or two lots in the entire municipality. On April 22, Monroe Township reversed this decision and passed an additional ordinance to ban all data centers. Monroe joins a growing list of municipalities, such as Pemberton Township, that have taken definitive action to prevent data center development within their borders.
Water protections in the Pinelands are similarly robust. The CMP limits new large-scale withdrawals from the Kirkwood-Cohansey aquifer, one of the most important groundwater systems in the region. Title: Councilman Glass proposes 6-month moratorium on data center permits
Summary_AI: Montgomery County Council member Evan Glass proposed a six-month moratorium on new data center permits, the first formal attempt to pause their construction in the county. The moratorium would impact the planned Atmosphere Data Centers facility in Dickerson, which has generated local controversy over concerns about water and air pollution and energy costs. Atmosphere Data Centers CEO Chuck McBride stated the company remains committed to collaborative development, while other council members including Natali Fani-González are pursuing alternative regulatory approaches such as zoning restrictions and developer submission requirements.
Article_Text:
This story has been updated to reflect a new comment from Atmosphere Data Centers, the company that aims to build in Dickerson.
County Council member Evan Glass on Tuesday proposed a six-month moratorium on new permits for data centers, in Montgomery County officials’ latest attempt to control their development.
It’s the first formal attempt, though, to pause their construction.
Glass has previously tried to rein in data centers. He filed a bill to create a task force to assess the potential impacts of future centers, but that bill did not make it out of committee.
Some critics called it unnecessary, given existing ways for the public to weigh in. Others said it could delay more substantive action on the issue, such as zoning policy.
If Glass’ new bill passes, it will apply only to new data center permits and could impact the planned center in Dickerson that has been at the center of controversy.
“We have to have meaningful community engagement,” Glass said as he introduced the bill during Tuesday’s council meeting. “This legislation allows that dialogue to happen. This is not about not having any data centers. It’s about doing it right.”
Data centers, in Montgomery County and across the nation, have alarmed communities.
Locally, residents and county officials worry that they will increase water and air pollution and raise energy bills. There are at least four existing data centers in Montgomery County, according to the planning board.
The prospect that the county might attract more of them has made the issue a talking point for the three council members running for county executive in the June 9 Democratic primary — Glass and council members Andrew Friedson and Will Jawando.
Other attempts to regulate data centers

Microsoft confirms plans for data center in Tyrone, Georgia
Will hold a community information session later this month
May 05, 2026 By Georgia Butler Have your say
Microsoft has confirmed that it is behind a data center project in Tyrone, Georgia.

Tyrone is in the Atlanta metropolitan area, part of Fayette County, Georgia.
– Fayette County Development Authority

A substation will also be developed at the site.
The project itself has been underway since November 2023, with a request made to annex the site in March 2024.

Title: Wamego community fights proposed Beltline Energy data center - KSNT
Summary_AI: Georgia-based Beltline Energy held an open house in Wamego, Kansas on May 6 to present plans for a proposed data center in Pottawatomie County, highlighting potential economic benefits including tax revenue and construction jobs. The company stated it has not yet purchased land and would use non-potable water in a closed-loop cooling system for the facility. However, the proposal faced significant community opposition, with residents expressing concerns about water usage, environmental impacts, and noise and light pollution, citing threats to the shared Flint Hills Aquifer that extends across county lines.
Article_Text:
WAMEGO (KSNT) — A proposed data center in Pottawatomie County drew a large crowd of residents during an open house hosted by Georgia-based Beltline Energy in Wamego on Wednesday, May 6.
The company is exploring plans for a data center project in the county, an idea that has surfaced in multiple Pottawatomie County Planning Commission meetings in recent months.
During the open house, representatives from Beltline Energy outlined potential economic benefits of the proposal, including increased tax revenue and high-paying construction and operations jobs.
However, many residents attending the meeting voiced strong opposition to the project, citing concerns about water use, environmental impacts, noise and light pollution.
Community members gathered throughout the evening to ask questions and share concerns about how the proposed development could affect the area in the long term.
Johnny Evans, a Wamego resident, said he was struck by the level of unity among attendees.
“Whether you’re left or right, I’ve seen this community divided on multiple subjects over the years, but I’ve never seen a community more united than I have tonight, and that was impressive,” Evans told 27 News.
Amber Starling, another resident, said the proposal could have impacts beyond Wamego and Pottawatomie County.
“I have heard a couple of people say, ‘Well, if you don’t live in Wamego, then this doesn’t affect you.’ And the answer to that is that’s objectively false,” Starling said. “The Flint Hills Aquifer is all of our aquifers, and aquifers do not give a solitary single care about our county lines.”
Representatives from Beltline Energy repeatedly told attendees they have not purchased land for the project. Company officials also said the proposed facility would use non-potable water in a closed-loop cooling system.

Title: Developer yanks plan for huge Johnson County data center after resident backlash
Summary_AI: Beale Infrastructure withdrew its proposal to build a 16-building hyperscale data center campus on 300 acres in Gardner, Kansas after the city declined to offer financial incentives and residents voiced health and environmental concerns at a City Council meeting. The San Francisco-based developer submitted the application in April for the project near 191st Street and South Clare Road in Johnson County, but the city's policy decision not to grant incentives prompted the withdrawal. Mayor Todd Winters stated the city remains committed to pursuing responsible economic development, while residents celebrated the outcome after mobilizing community opposition to the rural facility.
Article_Text:
Beale Infrastructure submitted a proposal to build a hyperscale data center campus on 300 acres off 191st Street and South Clare Road in Gardner. Kaela Eisenbarger
After days of worrying that a massive data center might be her new nextdoor neighbor, Gardner resident Kaela Eisenbarger celebrated the fact that the proposal will no longer be moving forward.
“I am surprised that it happened this quickly, but I am so thankful just to know that our home won’t be immediately affected by something like this,” Eisenbarger said.
In April, San Francisco-based data center developer Beale Infrastructure announced a proposal to Gardner to build a 16-building campus across 300 acres near the northwest corner of 191st Street and South Clare Road. Gardner residents came out in droves against the application, sharing concerns about potential health and environmental effects of a data center moving into the rural Johnson County community.
And it appears that Gardner heard its constituents’ concerns. Almost a week later, city officials said in a statement that the company withdrew its application “based on the City of Gardner communicating that no incentives would be granted.”
Gardner spokesperson Daneeka Marshall-Oquendo said in an email that Beale made an initial request for incentives, but no formal agreement was drafted. Because the city didn’t approve a site plan, no official project cost was determined.
The decision to not offer incentives didn’t occur during a public portion of a City Council meeting, she said. Rather, it was a “policy decision related to this specific development.”
A Beale spokesperson said in a statement that the company appreciates the input from the Gardner community and city leaders.
“Beale Infrastructure is committed to delivering projects that create real tangible value for communities” the spokesperson said. “We look forward to continuing to grow our investments and community partnerships across other locations in Kansas.”

Title: Las Vegas data center research explores impact of state's 60+ facilities
Summary_AI: Nevada's 60+ data centers consumed 22% of the state's electricity generation capacity in 2024, with consumption projected to reach 35% of forecasted generation by 2030 at an estimated annual cost exceeding $2.8 billion, according to a Desert Research Institute study released Wednesday. New facilities under development are expected to consume 25,590 gigawatt hours by 2033—approximately 56% of Nevada's current total net generation—while water consumption from 12 studied data centers matches annual usage of 31 golf courses or drinking water for 24.1 million people. NV Energy's integrated resource plan acknowledges potential summer power shortfalls as the data center industry continues expanding in the state, though researchers identified opportunities in efficiency improvements including direct liquid cooling and load flexibility technologies.
Article_Text:
LAS VEGAS (KLAS) — Nevada’s abundant land, low-cost energy, and attractive tax environment have ranked the state among the top five sought-after regions for prospective data centers, according to the latest comprehensive study.
The Desert Research Institute updated its overview of data center water and electricity consumption in Nevada on Wednesday, attaching a map of the 60+ data centers in the state. The study highlighted future power challenges, water challenges, and opportunities.
“This is kind of a double-edged sword,” Erik Henzl, a DRI scientist, said. “There are opportunities moving forward: more efficient cooling technologies, direct liquid cooling, and load flexibility.”
Henzl, and his colleague Sean McKenna presented their findings about how Nevada was quickly becoming a data center hub and the consequences of success.
“In their most recent integrated resource plan, NV Energy recognized that there is a potential for shortfalls, particularly during summer months moving forward,” he said.
The study found Nevada’s data centers consumed 22% of the state’s electricity generation capacity in 2024. With more construction expected, the industry is estimated to grow more than 35% of the forecasted electricity generation by 2030, with an estimated cost of more than $2.8 billion each year.
“The new facilities we’re looking at will consume 25,590 gigawatt hours by 2033,” Henzl said. “If we compare that to Nevada’s 2024 net generation, that’s about 56% of what we generate.”
As for water, the study outlined consumption is based on different cooling methods and size of the facility. However, when looking at the 12 data centers from the study, they were on pace to consume the same amount of water per year as 31 average golf courses—or drinking water for 24.1 million U.S. adults.
More from the study can be found at the Desert Research Institute website.

Title: Douglas County residents ask commission to consider moratorium on battery systems, data centers
Summary_AI: Douglas County commissioners held a work session on potential regulations for battery energy storage systems and data centers following public concerns about fire risks and water consumption. Planner Karl Bauer presented research showing these uses are currently undefined in county code, meaning battery systems could already be permitted alongside solar installations, while data centers could require hundreds of thousands of gallons of water daily and potentially increase electric bills by $50 monthly or more. No projects have been proposed, but the county initiated a text amendment process in 2024 that would create a legal pause on such developments while regulations are finalized. Commissioner Erica Anderson suggested a formal moratorium may be appropriate as the county's zoning study continues.
Article_Text:
Jacob Rice / Lawrence Times
Attendees listen during the Douglas County Commission's work session on May 6, 2026. Some members of the public asked commissioners to consider a moratorium or ban on battery energy storage systems and data centers in the county.
Douglas County commissioners on Wednesday faced public calls for a moratorium or ban on battery energy storage systems and data centers, following a work session where they heard research on fire risks and rural water limits.
No local projects have been proposed, but county commissioners in July 2024 asked staff members to look into how those land uses should be addressed in county codes.
Planner Karl Bauer presented research Wednesday showing these uses are undefined in the county’s code outside of solar accessory rules, meaning that battery energy storage systems, or BESS, would already be allowed in the county alongside a solar farm to store extra energy harvested during the day to output during peak usage.
The county’s current zoning restrictions would subject BESS to restrictions to avoid fires and noise disturbances.
Jacob Rice / Lawrence Times
Karl Bauer speaks to Douglas County commissioners during the work session.
Bauer also cited BESS fire risks like thermal runaway — a rapid increase in temperature that can lead to a fire — as well as massive water and electric demands of data centers, and construction impacts for all.
Public commenters voiced infrastructure fears during the commission’s business meeting.
“It looks like it’s probably a minimum, at least $50 a month bump up in electric bills, if it’s a big, big data center, at least double to triple what your water bill is going to be,” economist Tim Hamilton said. “That’s pretty significant.”
Jacob Rice / Lawrence Times
Resident Kincaid Dennett pressed the commission to be bold.
“If we’re developing regulations for how these can exist without a moratorium, we’re just kind of slow walking into what kind of data centers can be allowed, rather than stati

Title: Huge data center gets approvals in Middlesex Township — a likely legal challenge, too
Summary_AI: Middlesex Township supervisors approved final and preliminary land development plans for the Pax-1 Data Center, a 700-acre hyperscale facility near Carlisle that represents south central Pennsylvania's first project of this scale. The approval faced immediate legal challenges, with an attorney announcing plans to contest the township's data center overlay zoning district as illegal spot zoning on behalf of surrounding residents. Concerns raised included local fire department capacity for lithium battery storage, impacts on the nearby Appalachian Trail viewshed, and questions about the independence of the environmental impact assessment. The development, a partnership between Virginia-based PowerHouse Data Centers and local investment group Pennsylvania Data Center Partners, will proceed through three proposed campus phases pending final approvals.

Middlesex Township supervisors gave several important approvals to the Pax-1 Data Center outside Carlisle Wednesday night.
But a possible legal hurdle to the project - south central Pennsylvania’s first hyperscale data center - also emerged.
Attorney Nathan Wolf told the board that, on behalf of residents surrounding the 700-acre tract off Country Club Road, he will contest the validity of the township’s data center overlay zoning district as a case of illegal spot zoning.
Meanwhile, the development plan review process lurched forward over the objections of a roomful of concerned residents.
Supervisors, in a series of unanimous votes, approved final land development plans for the electrical infrastructure serving the site, and preliminary land development plans for the first of three proposed data center campuses.
The latter still faces final land development plan review.
Wednesday’s votes came after a long night of impassioned pleas from project neighbors with questions about:
Middlesex’s readiness to handle a project of this scale and scope.
Teaberry Drive resident Beth Legore questioned the capability of local volunteer fire departments to handle a fire involving lithium batteries stored on the site.
Township zoning officer Mark Carpenter said the industry standard is to keep those materials in a separate fire-containment room to protect the vast arrays of computing equipment.
Middlesex Fire Chief Ed Beam said all the fire companies serving Middlesex will also get specialized training on the centers as development proceeds.
Impacts on the Appalachian Trail, the iconic East Coast hiking trail that passes about one-third of a mile from the data center site.
Earlier Wednesday, the Appalachian Trail Conservancy issued a statement announcing its opposition to the project because of negative impacts on the viewshed, and the local ecology.

Title: Neighbors raise concerns about proposed Cleveland data center
Summary_AI: Lakeland Equity Group is proposing a 300,000-square-foot, two-story hyperscale data center on East 55th Street in Cleveland's Slavic Village neighborhood that would consume 150 megawatts of power daily and require new water, sewer, and power infrastructure. Ward 15 Councilman Charles Slife introduced a moratorium ordinance to halt all data center permits as neighbors express concerns about proximity to residential areas and impacts on utilities and traffic. Mayor Justin Bibb pledged transparency and promised to implement environmental protections and safeguards against rising utility costs. The proposal remains unapproved, with a potential ballot measure to ban large-scale data centers in Ohio potentially appearing in November.
Article_Text:
Add as a preferred source on Google
CLEVELAND, Ohio (WOIO) - A Cleveland city councilman introduced an ordinance to halt all data center permits and applications as neighbors raise concerns about a proposed hyperscale facility in Slavic Village.
Westlake-based Lakeland Equity Group is proposing to build Cleveland’s first hyperscale data center on East 55th Street at the old Morabito Enterprises property. The facility would be 300,000 square feet and two stories.
The building would consume 150 megawatts of power per day, enough to supply about 100,000 homes. It would also require new water, sewer and power connections.
Ward 15 Councilman Charles Slife introduced the moratorium ordinance Monday.
“Right now, there’s a real concern that one of these large, invasive data centers could be co-located within very close proximity to people’s houses which we’re hearing from the community is not something that they want,” Slife said.
“We need to take a pause. We need to kind of come to terms with this new reality and make sure that we are looking for a way to build our community in a sustainable way,” he said.
Dorothy Blackwell and Witold Sztalkopr have lived in the Slavic Village neighborhood since 1985. They said they want to know how a project this size would affect water, power and traffic.
“Is this beneficial for the area or causing more trouble? How is it going to impact utilities?” Blackwell said.
“How is this going to impact the whole Slavic Village — financially?” Sztalkopr said.
Mayor Justin Bibb released a video on social media saying he understands the concerns and promises to be transparent with the project.
“We are doing the work right now to make sure we have strong policies and real safe guards in place to protect our environment and address rising utility costs,” Bibb said.
The proposal has not yet been approved.
An amendment to ban large-scale data centers in Ohio could appear on the ballot in November.

Title: Bunkers, Mines, and Caverns: The World of Underground Data Centers
Summary_AI: Underground data centers are emerging as a proven niche in the global market, with operators converting Cold War-era bunkers and disused mines in North America and Northern Europe rather than building new facilities from scratch. Iron Mountain's Boyers, Pennsylvania facility, located over 200 feet underground in a former limestone mine, leverages natural rock temperature and a 35-acre underground lake to optimize cooling and achieve lower power usage effectiveness while providing resilience against extreme weather and external threats. Norway's Lefdal Mine Data Centers repurposes a former olivine mine on the west coast, utilizing nearby fjord water as part of its cooling system. According to Uptime Intelligence research, underground data centers continue to appeal for their combination of security, sustainability, and infrastructure reuse, though they remain a small but growing segment of the broader data center market.
Article_Text:
DATA CENTER SITE SELECTION
Bunkers, Mines, and Caverns: The World of Underground Data Centers
Underground facilities promise physical and efficient cooling, but operators face unique engineering and logistic challenge.
Nathan Eddy,Contributor
Lefdal Mine Data Centers on Norway’s west coast repurposes a former olivine mine, using nearby fjord water as part of its cooling system.LEFDAL MINE DATA CENTERS
Underground facilities represent a small but growing niche in the global data center market, driven largely by adaptive reuse of retired mines and bunkers in North America and Northern Europe.
Rather than constructing new facilities from scratch, many operators are converting Cold War-era bunkers or disused mines, repurposing hardened, abandoned sites that offer unique advantages for modern deployments.
Iron Mountain’s Boyers, Pa., campus, exemplifies this approach. Located more than 200 feet underground, the facility demonstrates how repurposed industrial infrastructure can be engineered to support modern, high-density deployments while meeting the most stringent global compliance standards.
“The underground advantage is built on a foundation of resilience, security, and inherent efficiency,” Doug Titzer, senior vice president, design and construction at Iron Mountain, told Data Center Knowledge.
Related:Riding the Wave: The Rise of Floating Data Centers
By moving hundreds of feet underground, operators gain a natural, impenetrable shield against extreme weather and external threats – a significant benefit for mission-critical workloads in highly regulated sectors, he explained.
“At our Boyers facility, we leverage the natural stable ambient temperature of the rock and a 35-acre underground lake to dramatically optimize cooling,” Titzer said.
For organizations prioritizing disaster recovery or long-term preservation, this creates a strong alignment between lower power usage effectiveness (PUE) and maximum uptime.

Data Centers and Land Use – Public Opinion and Action
Data centers have a global reach, but their development is increasingly shaped by local land use decisions, public opinion, and evolving legislative frameworks. Across the country, state and local governments are introducing moratoria, revising zoning codes, and responding to community concerns in ways that directly affect project timelines and siting strategies.
This alert analyzes current moratorium proposals, zoning restrictions, land use litigation, and community engagement trends to provide practical guidance for data center stakeholders like developers, operators, and investors to navigate this shifting landscape.
Various tools are available to assist developers in minimizing the impact of state or local opposition to their projects, many of which are analyzed in this alert.
Monitor State-Level Moratorium Proposals: With moratorium bills introduced or under consideration in more than 10 states, developers should track legislative activity in all target jurisdictions and assess the potential impact on project timelines. Many proposals include specific energy capacity thresholds that may or may not affect a given project.
Engage Communities Early: Community opposition has proven to be a decisive factor in project outcomes, particularly in Virginia, where it has contributed to project cancellations and political shifts. Proactive, transparent engagement with local stakeholders, including hosting informational sessions and developing community benefits plans, can help build support and reduce permitting risk.
Stay Current on Zoning Changes: As Virginia counties like Loudoun and Montgomery update their zoning frameworks, developers should review applicable zoning ordinances carefully and engage with local planning processes to anticipate changes that could affect project feasibility or require additional approvals.

Summary_AI: CloudBurst Data Centers received approval from Guadalupe Commissioners Court to develop a 706-acre, 1.2GW data center campus near San Marcos, Texas, with a $500 million tax abatement and $14.5 billion investment commitment. The facility will be built across three 400MW phases starting in 2026, with an initial 50MW phase already under construction targeting Q4 2026 completion, spanning 3 million square feet across Hays and Guadalupe counties. The project includes closed-loop cooling and behind-the-meter power generation, with CloudBurst projecting approximately $120 million in annual tax revenue for the region. Article_Text:
706-acre data center approved for development outside San Antonio, Texas
Will offer 1.2GW of capacity at completion
May 07, 2026 By Jason Ma Have your say
A 706-acre data center has been approved for development outside San Antonio, Texas.
Data center firm CloudBurst got the go-ahead for its data center project from the Guadalupe Commissioners Court on Tuesday, April 21, after local authorities approved a tax abatement worth $500 million and a development agreement for the facility.
– CloudBurst Data Centers
CloudBurst is planning to invest $14.5 billion to build between 10 and 12 data center buildings on a land parcel located near the city of San Marcos between Center Point Road, Francis Harris Lane, and Huber Road, spanning a total of 3 million sq ft (278,709 sqm).
According to a presentation given at the April 21 Commissioners Court meeting, the 1.2GW campus will be built in three 400MW phases, with the first commencing in 2026 and finishing by 2027.
The project will be split across Hays County and Guadalupe County, and an initial 50MW phase of development has already broken ground and is set to go live in Q4 2026.
CloudBurst has said that the campus would use closed-loop cooling and get its power from behind-the-meter generation, and the company estimates that the project will provide around $120m in tax revenue annually for the community and the county.
Both the tax abatement and the development agreement were voted through – by a margin of one vote – to the consternation of attendees, the majority of whom spoke against the proposal.
Most expressed concern about the potential impact of the campus on the environment, and some worried that a data center would have adverse health effects.
A petition against the data center has gathered more than 1,200 signatures at time of writing.
Colorado-based CloudBurst Data Centers was founded in 2022. The company is also developing a 200MW campus in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma.

Environment & Climate

County Councilmember says six-month moratorium would apply retroactively to planned Dickerson campus

Montgomery County Councilmember Evan Glass (D-At-large) on Tuesday introduced a bill to temporarily pause the development of data centers in the county — including a project in Dickerson that already has conditional approval. Credit: Benjamin Sky Brandt for the Montgomery Council
Montgomery County Councilmember Evan Glass (D-At-large) on Tuesday introduced a bill to temporarily pause the development of data centers in the county, including a project in Dickerson that already is pending final approval.
During a Tuesday press conference at the council office building in Rockville, Glass cited county residents’ concerns about the potential environmental impacts of future data centers — specifically, a 360-megawatt campus proposed at the site of a former coal-fired power plant in Dickerson — as a driving force behind his decision to push for a moratorium.
“The urgency is real. Our climate is on the line, the cost of utilities are on the line, and these decisions are irreversible,” Glass said. “We have to be very prudent in how we move forward.”
Glass’ bill would prevent the county’s Department of Permitting from accepting, reviewing or issuing building or grading permits for data centers projects for a period of six months. The clock on the moratorium would start as soon as the bill takes effect.
If approved by the council, the moratorium would apply to data centers for which a conditional use application is submitted on or after the effective date of the bill.
It would also apply to data centers that already have a conditional use application pending and those that have received conditional use approval, but have not yet obtained the building or grading permits necessary to begin construction.

Title: Microsoft's AI data center push is colliding with its clean power goals | TechCrunch
Summary_AI: Microsoft is reconsidering its aggressive 2030 goal to match 100% of hourly energy use with clean power on the same grid, as internal discussions reveal tension between its rapid AI data center expansion and clean energy commitments. The hourly matching target is more rigorous than annual matching goals, requiring renewable energy supply to align with demand on a near-instantaneous basis rather than allowing accounting flexibility across the year. While Microsoft has committed to removing more carbon from the atmosphere than its operations produce by 2030, the company is simultaneously pursuing a 5-gigawatt natural gas power plant in West Texas with Chevron and Engine No. 1, indicating a pivot toward fossil fuel infrastructure to support its growing data center footprint.
Article_Text:
Microsoft is weighing whether to delay or scale back one of its most ambitious clean energy goals as its rapid buildout of AI data centers puts pressure on its ability to meet those targets. Microsoft has yet to make any public announcements, but according to Bloomberg the company is having internal discussions over its hourly clean energy matching goal.
The tech company has said that by 2030 it intends to match 100% of its hourly energy use with clean power on the same grid. But Microsoft’s rush to build AI data centers has apparently sparked debate within the company about whether the pledge has become an impediment to its ambitions.
Microsoft declined to comment on the internal debate over the hourly matching goal. Instead, a spokesperson told TechCrunch the company continues “to look for opportunities to maintain our annual matching goal.”
Hourly targets like the kind Microsoft has set for itself are more rigorous than annual targets. Because the grid is a balanced system — the supply and demand of electrons needs to be matched on a near-instantaneous basis — hourly matching helps develop clean energy sources that more closely align with a company’s usage patterns.
Annual targets are more lenient. They are effectively accounting tricks that could, for example, let a company buy more solar power than it might use at midday. Other customers on the grid use that energy, but the company that paid for the solar panels gets to claim the renewable power they make. It’s a tidy arrangement that has sped the deployment of wind, solar, and batteries. But on its own, annual targets won’t eliminate fossil fuels entirely. Hourly targets help foster renewable development that more closely mimics how a true net-zero world would be powered.

Title: Gas power leapfrogs wind for first time in 10 years in Texas' grid connection queue
Summary_AI: Natural gas projects have surpassed wind in Texas' ERCOT interconnection queue for the first time since 2016, driven primarily by data centers seeking reliable 24/7 power for artificial intelligence operations. Gas queue capacity has surged over 400% in three years to nearly 64,000 megawatts as of last month, compared to wind's slower 87% growth to 48,000 megawatts, while prospective data center projects alone represent approximately 360,000 megawatts of power demand. The Texas Energy Fund program, which provides low-interest loans for dispatchable power projects like gas plants, supports about 9,000 megawatts of near-term gas projects in the queue. Article_Text:
Shelby Tauber for The Texas Tribune
The list of power projects that want to connect to Texas’ grid includes a surge of natural gas power plants.
Brandon Mulder is a journalism fellow at the University of Texas Energy Institute.
A decade ago, wind power was surging in popularity and attracting huge investments that made Texas a national leader in renewable energy. But today, gas generation is making a big comeback, driven by a wave of data centers flooding into the state.
For the last six months, the volume of gas generation in the Texas grid's interconnection queue — the yearslong waiting list for electric generators wanting to connect to the grid — has surpassed wind. It's the first time since January 2016 that gas has overtaken wind in the queue, a shift that reflects the policy and economic headwinds facing the wind industry and data centers favoring gas power as they seek to cash in on the artificial intelligence boom.
"The data center explosion and their desire for 24/7 power probably excited a lot of gas developers, and that gas queue got bigger," said University of Texas professor of energy regulation David Spence.
Like every power grid operator in the U.S., the Electric Reliability Council of Texas, or ERCOT, uses the interconnection queue to manage and plan for new power generation coming online. But not all projects in the queue ultimately reach completion. According to the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, or LBNL, only 22% of projects in ERCOT's queue actually get built, which is still the highest percentage of any grid in the nation.
Still, the queue gives an early indication of how the grid is projected to evolve in the future. Solar and battery projects dominate, accounting for 75% of the 458,000 megawatts in the queue, with gas and wind projects making up the rest.

Title: Copper cold plates could slash data-center energy usage - EurekAlert!
Summary_AI: Researchers at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign designed optimized copper cold plates using topology optimization and advanced 3D printing to improve data center cooling efficiency. The new liquid direct-to-chip cooling technology would consume approximately 1.1% of a data center's total energy compared to over 30% for conventional air-cooling methods, addressing a critical bottleneck as data centers are projected to consume up to 12% of the U.S. national grid load by 2028. The copper cold plates feature complex fin geometries optimized for maximum thermal performance and minimal pumping power, manufactured in collaboration with Fabric8 using 3D printing techniques that conventional manufacturing cannot achieve.
Article_Text:
COPPER COLD PLATES CLOSE UP.
CREDIT: CELL REPORTS PHYSICAL SCIENCE, BAZMI ET AL.
Mechanical engineers have designed a more effective and energy-efficient technology for cooling computer chips. Publishing May 7 in the Cell Press journal Cell Reports Physical Science, the researchers used a mathematical algorithm and advanced 3D printing method to produce pure copper cold plates that outperformed conventional cold plates and required less energy to run. If used to cool an entire data center, the technology would contribute only around 1.1% of the data center’s total energy usage compared to more than 30% for conventional air-cooling methods, the researchers estimate.
“Cooling is the bottleneck in computer-chip design,” says first author Behnood Bazmi, a mechanical engineer at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, USA. “By bridging the gap between computational design and manufacturing capability, our approach provides a pathway for more energy-efficient liquid cooling of chips and other electronics.”
Computer chips are becoming increasingly high powered, which means they produce more heat. This, combined with the increase in data centers, is putting a strain on the energy grid—by 2028, it’s predicted that data centers will consume up to 12% of the national grid load in the United States. For the past 40–50 years, computer chips have been cooled by circulating air, but air is insufficient for dissipating the heat produced by modern chips. Liquid direct-to-chip cooling could offer a more effective solution, say the researchers.
Direct-to-chip cooling systems consist of a cold plate that is attached to a computer chip. These cold plates have tightly packed metal “fins” that project out into the cooling liquid to maximize the surface area that is in contact with the coolant. Some direct-to-chip liquid cooling systems are already commercially available, but those systems prioritize manufacturing cost over performance. In this study, the researchers set out to optimi

Title: AI Data Centres Need Big Batteries But Lithium Isn't Fit-For-Purpose - CleanTechnica
Summary_AI: AI data centers present a unique grid challenge beyond generation capacity: their burst-driven power demand is highly volatile and unpredictable, causing them to disconnect abruptly during disturbances in ways that traditional industrial loads do not, thereby destabilizing grid frequency and voltage. While lithium-ion batteries have become the dominant storage solution for grid buffering, they degrade rapidly under the high-frequency cycling required by data center workloads and become economically unfeasible beyond four to six hours of duration. Alternative storage technologies such as flow batteries are emerging as better-suited solutions because they separate energy capacity from power capacity, offering improved cycling resilience and economics for the long-duration buffering that AI data centers require.
Article_Text:
Support CleanTechnica's work through a Substack subscription or on Stripe.
By Dr. Thomas Nann, CEO and Co-Founder of Allegro
The biggest constraint facing AI data centre expansion is not generation. It is storage, and not only storage, but the unique way that AI data centre’s use power.
As AI-driven data centres scale, the grid challenge is no longer simply how much electricity they consume. It is how unpredictably they draw it, and whether the system has sufficient buffering to absorb sudden power surges and dips reliably when required.
This context matters when BlackRock chair Larry Fink warns that data centres cannot realistically run on renewables alone in the near term, and that coal and nuclear will remain necessary. At a high level, he is correct. AI data centres will draw unprecedented amounts of power and on the surface it might seem attractive, even necessary, to return to more conventional generation to meet this need.
But the stress point is not renewable energy suitability per se. It is the volatility of the way AI data centres use energy and the absence of sufficient “buffering” capacity capable of reconciling intermittent supply with increasingly intermittent demand.
Traditional large industrial loads, such as aluminium smelters, draw power in continuous, predictable bands. AI data centres do not. Their demand profile is burst-driven. Compute intensity can surge within minutes as millions of users simultaneously query AI platforms or as automated systems across finance, logistics and defence ramp computation in response to events.
Grid operators are already observing a new phenomenon. When major data centres disconnect during disturbances, they do not ramp down gradually like traditional industrial loads. According to Hitachi Energy, they can disconnect abruptly, triggering voltage and frequency deviations that ripple across the system.
In other words, data centres are becoming one of the most volatile loads on the grid.
