Title: Google hosts data center information fair - WSAZ

At a community information fair, Google representatives outlined plans to cool the facility using either water or air technology, manage water discharge through retention ponds to public sewers, and fund 100% of the electrical infrastructure required while paying full utility rates. SCIOTO COUNTY, Ohio (WSAZ) - Many in Scioto County are protesting and even signing petitions to stop a new Google data center from coming to Franklin Furnace.
Patrick McDavid, a resident and Conserve Ohio volunteer said, “My hope is that the economic aspect of it does play out like we’re being told it will. You know, here recently my concerns are more shifting to the environmental aspect.”

To address concerns, Google set up an information fair going over possibilities including cooling the data center with water or air.

Chadwick, who works with data center operations for Google, said when it comes to powering the data center, Google will pay 100% of the electric required to run the campus along with paying AEP to provide infrastructure locally.

Engineers are actively working to limit impacts like noise pollution, with a goal to leave a positive impact on the local economy.

Title: The data center boom is transforming Georgia. Some residents could lose their land.
Summary_AI: Georgia Power is acquiring easements on residential properties in unincorporated Fayette County, southwest of Atlanta, to construct high-voltage transmission lines supporting the region's data center expansion. The utility's efforts to secure land for infrastructure, including approximately one-third of some homeowners' properties, reflect the power demands created by the data center boom transforming Georgia's landscape. Affected residents like Rachael Maszk are negotiating with Georgia Power over compensation for easements that would replace forested areas with transmission towers and lines.
Article_Text:
Rachael Maszk and her husband ditched their Buckhead apartment four years ago seeking more space to garden, keep chickens and raise a family.
They found what they were looking for in unincorporated Fayette County, 25 miles southwest of Atlanta. Their new home sits on 2 acres and has the seclusion Maszk craved for her young children.
Then, last fall, the couple got a letter from Georgia Power.
It said new, high-voltage transmission lines  may need to cross their property, and invited them to a community meeting to learn more. In March, Georgia Power revealed its intentions: to acquire about a third of their land through an easement to accommodate the power lines.
If they accept the utility’s compensation offer, Maszk said the forested area off their back porch — the reason they bought the home — would be gone, replaced by transmission towers and lines.
The past several weeks have been a “stressful” tug of war, with offers and counteroffers. But Maszk is not alone.

Article_Text:
Edge data center firm Greensparc targets new Alaska deployments
Company eyes sites in Petersburg and Wrangell
May 15, 2026 By Dan Swinhoe Have your say
US Edge computing provider Greensparc is looking to deploy a data center pod in Petersburg, Alaska.
The company this week announced it was proposing a small-scale data center project on the Petersburg waterfront at the former Ocean Beauty Seafoods canning plant.
Greensparc's existing deployment in Alaska
“The existing 2MW transformer on the property has been sitting idle since the cannery closed in 2016,” the company said on LinkedIn. “Greensparc would put it to work.”
Petersburg is located in Petersburg Borough, near the southern tip of Alaska, close to the border with British Columbia in Canada.
The company noted the project is still in the early stages and nothing has been signed or finalized. The Petersburg Municipal Power and Light said the borough has not yet received any building permits or applications. Local press report the deployment could take up around 1,500 sq ft of the 65,000 sq ft building.
Local developer Andrew Mazzella acquired the vacant canning plant in 2024 through his Fierce Allegiance business, buying it from Ocean Beauty. At the time, Mazella said the site was set for a new tenant, with the bunkhouse used for fish processing, a restaurant, and an upstairs dwelling.
The plant was previously used by seafood distributor Ocean Beauty to produce canned salmon. The building was struck by a state-owned ferry and damaged in 2012.
Founded in 2014, Greensparc is headquartered in San Francisco and specializes in the deployment of renewable energy-powered Edge computing resources.
Greensparc has previously delivered a 170kW Edge data center to the Cordova Electric Cooperative (CEC) in Cordova, Alaska. That modular was deployed at and powered by the local Humpback Creek Hydro facility.

Title: Illinois lawmakers weigh new data center regulations
Summary_AI: Illinois lawmakers are considering the POWER Act to mandate transparency from data centers regarding water and energy consumption, though the bill has stalled since its February introduction with less than three weeks remaining in the legislative session. Governor JB Pritzker called for a two-year pause on state tax credits for new data centers and proposed that grid operator PJM Interconnection require developers to fund their own energy infrastructure, but has not publicly endorsed the regulatory proposal itself. Illinois has distributed $983 million in estimated lifetime tax breaks and benefits to at least 27 data centers since 2019, prompting environmental advocates to push for stricter oversight before additional projects advance.
Article_Text:
Illinois lawmakers consider new rules for data centers
UIS Public Affairs Reporting via Capitol News Illinois
May 15, 2026, 4:06 a.m. CT
Environmental advocates are urging Illinois lawmakers to pass the POWER Act, which would require more transparency from data centers.
Governor JB Pritzker has called for a pause on data center tax credits but has been silent on the POWER Act itself.
Community members across Illinois have expressed frustration over a lack of transparency and public input on new data center projects.
SPRINGFIELD — Environmental advocates continue to pressure lawmakers to require more transparency about data centers’ water and energy use as the legislative session creeps toward the finish line.
The POWER Act is the primary vehicle for regulations that address concerns about the effects data centers have on communities, but it hasn’t seen any action beyond subject matter hearings since it was introduced in February.
With less than three weeks left before lawmakers are slated to adjourn, however, it’s unclear whether the wide-ranging bill will come together and whether Gov. JB Pritzker will throw his support behind any specific regulatory proposal.
“We are confused and concerned by the Governor’s lack of engagement on the issue of data centers this spring legislative session,” Kady McFadden, lead lobbyist on behalf of the Illinois Clean Jobs Coalition that has been behind several recent energy reforms, said in a statement.
Pritzker mentioned data centers in his February State of the State address, calling for PJM Interconnection, the electric grid operator that covers all or part 13 states from Illinois to the East Coast, to require data center developers to pay for and provide their own energy.

Title: Fetterman: Democratic opposition to AI, data centers is 'lunacy' - The Hill
Summary_AI: Sen. John Fetterman (D-Pa.) criticized Democratic opposition to AI and data center development as "lunacy," arguing the party should embrace rapid deployment to compete globally rather than pursue moratoriums. Progressive Democrats including Sen. Bernie Sanders and Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez have called for pausing data center expansion until safeguards protect workers, civil liberties, and the environment, while Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer has pushed for a bipartisan regulatory framework ensuring data centers "pay their fair share" and provide consumer protections. Article_Text:
Fetterman: Democratic opposition to AI, data centers is ‘lunacy’
BY ALEXANDER BOLTON - 05/14/26 9:46 AM ET
Centrist Sen. John Fetterman (D-Pa.) is calling out Democratic opposition to the rapid development of AI and data centers around the country as “lunacy” and warning that taking a stance against AI is a “China first kind of policy.”
“You know, people in my party are now becoming anti-AI and data centers. You know, people in my party are calling for a moratorium in our country. That’s lunacy. That’s China-first kind of a policy in our nation,” Fetterman said in an interview with Fox News Channel.
“I’ll be the one Democrat to just stand up and say that’s ridiculous. You know, we have to be the people that build the chassis of AI,” Fetterman argued.
He added that he’s “proud to say, look, ‘I think it’s a country — definitely a country over party’” policy to embrace the potential economic benefits of AI.
Progressive leaders including Sen. Bernie Sanders (Vt.), an independent who caucuses with Democrats, and Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) have called for a  pause in building AI data centers until national safeguards are put in place to protect workers, civil liberties and the environment.
“Bottom line: We cannot sit back and allow a handful of billionaire Big Tech oligarchs to make decisions that will reshape our economy, our democracy and the future of humanity,” Sanders said in a statement earlier this year.
“We need serious public debate and democratic oversight over this enormously consequential issue. The time for action is now. We need a federal moratorium on AI data centers,” he said.
Ocasio-Cortez said Congress has a “moral obligation” to stop the expansion of these data centers “until we have a framework to adequately address the existential harm AI poses to our society.”
